topics.title: Particle Diameter and Shape Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Particle Diameter and Shape Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Particle Diameter?

In particulate technology, an individual particle of a powder or granular material is almost never a perfect sphere. Real particles are irregular, jagged, porous, and asymmetric. Therefore, there is **no single, unique diameter** for such a particle. Instead, engineers and scientists define several **equivalent diameters** based on measurable geometric, hydrodynamic, optical, or settling properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Particle Diameter):**
> Particle diameter is a *one-dimensional scalar quantity* that characterizes the linear extent of a non-spherical particle. Since the particle is irregular, the diameter is expressed as an *equivalent diameter* — i.e., the diameter of a sphere that has the *same* selected geometric or transport property as the actual particle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1:**
> The student must be able to:
> (i) define and distinguish between the various equivalent diameters,
> (ii) define and apply shape factors (sphericity, circularity, aspect ratio, roundness),
> (iii) relate shape factor to mean particle sizes, and
> (iv) compute the specific surface area of a powder from its size and shape data.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Potato vs. Sphere" Picture

Imagine you hold an oddly shaped, knobbly potato. You cannot point to a single "diameter." However, you could meaningfully say:

- The **length of a kitchen sieve slot** that just lets the potato pass → **Sieve diameter ($d_s$)**
- The **diameter of a sphere having the same weight (volume)** as the potato → **Volume diameter ($d_v$)**
- The **diameter of a sphere having the same projected shadow area** as the potato under a light → **Projected area diameter ($d_a$)**
- The **diameter of a sphere that settles in water with the same terminal velocity** as the potato → **Stokes diameter ($d_{st}$)**

Each of these answers is *correct* — they are simply different ways of squeezing an irregular 3-D object into a single number. The choice of diameter depends on **what property** of the particle we are most interested in (sieving, settling, reaction surface, light blockage, etc.).

## 1.3 What is Shape Factor?

A *shape factor* is a **dimensionless numerical descriptor** that quantifies how closely a real particle resembles a perfect sphere (or, in 2-D microscopy, a perfect circle). It is purely a **geometric ratio**, independent of particle size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Shape Factor):**
> Shape factor is a dimensionless ratio that compares a characteristic geometric property (surface area, volume, perimeter, projected area) of the actual particle to that of a reference sphere (or circle) having the same characteristic linear dimension. Common shape factors include *sphericity* ($\Phi_s$), *circularity* ($\Phi_c$), *aspect ratio* ($AR$), *roundness*, and *bulkiness factor*.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Roundness of a Stone

Look at a smooth river pebble versus a freshly broken chunk of granite. Both might have the *same volume* (same mass), but the pebble rolls easily while the granite chunk does not. The reason: the **surface texture and aspect ratio differ**. Shape factors mathematically capture this difference.

- A perfect sphere has **sphericity $\Phi_s = 1$** (the maximum).
- Any deviation (rough surface, elongated shape, flat faces) makes $\Phi_s < 1$.
- Needles, flakes, and fibers can have sphericity as low as **0.1 – 0.3**.

## 1.5 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering

| Engineering Application | Which Property is Critical? | Relevant Diameter / Shape Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Cyclone / dust collector design | Settling velocity | **Stokes diameter $d_{st}$** |
| Powder flow through a hopper | Packing & friction | **Aspect ratio, roundness** |
| Catalytic reaction (gas–solid) | External surface area | **Surface diameter $d_s$ + Sphericity** |
| Sieve analysis | Slot width of sieve | **Sieve diameter $d_{A}$** |
| Optical particle counter (laser diffraction) | Projected area | **Projected area diameter $d_a$** |
| Tabletting, ceramics | Packing density | **Sphericity, bulkiness factor** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Equivalent Diameters

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies particle diameters broadly into three families, based on the **measurement principle**:

### (A) Geometric (Static) Diameters
Derived from direct geometric measurements of the particle at rest (microscope, image analyzer).

1. **Feret's Diameter ($d_F$):** The distance between two parallel tangents touching the particle on opposite sides, measured along a *fixed reference direction*. It varies with orientation → the average over all orientations is the *mean Feret diameter*.

2. **Martin's Diameter ($d_M$):** The length of the line that bisects the projected area of the particle, drawn in a *fixed direction*. Again, averaged over orientations.

3. **Projected Area Diameter ($d_a$ or $d_p$):** The diameter of a circle having the *same projected area* as the particle in a stable orientation.

4. **Perimeter Diameter ($d_c$):** The diameter of a circle having the *same perimeter* as the projected outline of the particle.

5. **Sieve Diameter ($d_A$):** The smallest sieve aperture (square opening) through which the particle will pass. (Technically, this is the *sieve size*, not a true diameter, but is widely used in practice.)

6. **Volume Diameter ($d_v$):** The diameter of a sphere having the *same volume* as the particle.
   $$d_v = \left(\frac{6V_p}{\pi}\right)^{1/3}$$

7. **Surface Diameter ($d_s$):** The diameter of a sphere having the *same surface area* as the particle.
   $$d_s = \sqrt{\frac{S_p}{\pi}}$$

### (B) Hydrodynamic / Dynamic Diameters
Derived from the *transport behaviour* of the particle in a fluid (most commonly settling).

8. **Stokes Diameter ($d_{st}$):** The diameter of a sphere having the *same terminal settling velocity* as the particle, *under Stokes' law conditions* (Re$_p < 0.2$).

9. **Free-Falling Diameter ($d_f$ or $d_VV$):** Generalization of $d_{st}$ valid for *all* Reynolds numbers.

10. **Aerodynamic Diameter ($d_a^*$):** The diameter of a *unit-density sphere* ($1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) having the *same terminal velocity* as the particle under ambient conditions. Used in aerosol science and air pollution monitoring.

### (C) Statistical (Derived) Diameters
Calculated from size-distribution data; covered in Module 2. Includes the **Sauter mean diameter $d_{sv}$** and various **mean diameters** $\bar{d}_{p,q}$.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step — Operational Logic

- **Feret vs. Martin** differ in *how* they slice a 2-D projection. Feret uses the outer envelope, Martin uses the area-bisecting chord. For an irregular particle, $d_F \neq d_M$, and the average over orientations gives a *statistical* size descriptor.
- **$d_v$ and $d_s$** are *internal* geometric descriptors — they require knowing the actual volume and surface area, usually from 3-D techniques (gas adsorption, pycnometry).
- **$d_{st}$** matters most in *separators* (cyclones, elutriators) because settling is the dominant transport mechanism. It inherently accounts for shape via drag.
- **Sieve diameter $d_A$** is operationally defined by *what falls through*, not by geometry — therefore it is a *property* diameter.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 1 mm sieve opening does *not* correspond to a 1 mm particle diameter for elongated particles. A 1.5 mm long needle can pass through a 1 mm sieve if its cross-section is small. Hence, sieve analysis systematically **under-estimates** the longest dimension of acicular (needle-like) particles.

## 2.3 Shape Factors — Definitions and Bounds

### 2.3.1 Sphericity ($\Phi_s$) — Waddell's Definition (KTU Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sphericity is the ratio of the surface area of a sphere having the SAME VOLUME as the particle to the actual surface area of the particle.**

$$\Phi_s = \frac{\text{Surface area of a sphere of volume } V_p}{\text{Actual surface area of particle}} = \frac{\pi d_v^{\,2}}{S_p}$$

**Bounds:** $0 < \Phi_s \leq 1$ (sphere = 1, all others < 1).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write $\Phi_s = \frac{S_p}{S_{sphere}}$, inverting the ratio. Remember: **sphere on top, actual on bottom** — sphericity *decreases* as the particle becomes *less* spherical.

A useful corollary relates $d_s$ and $d_v$:
$$\Phi_s = \frac{d_v^{\,2}}{d_s^{\,2}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{d_v}{d_s} = \sqrt{\Phi_s}$$

### 2.3.2 Circularity ($\Phi_c$) — 2-D Equivalent

Used for projected (microscope) images. Defined as the ratio of the circumference of a circle of *equal projected area* to the *actual perimeter* of the projection:
$$\Phi_c = \frac{\pi \, d_a}{P_p}$$

Bounds: $0 < \Phi_c \leq 1$.

### 2.3.3 Aspect Ratio ($AR$)
The ratio of the longest Feret diameter to the shortest Feret diameter (perpendicular to it):
$$AR = \frac{d_{F,\max}}{d_{F,\min}}$$

**Bounds:** $AR \geq 1$ (sphere = 1, needle = 5–20, flake ≈ 1).

### 2.3.4 Roundness and Surface Factors (Brief)
- **Roundness (Wentworth):** Average radius of curvature of corners divided by the radius of the inscribed circle. $\geq 1$ for angular particles.
- **Bulkiness factor ($K_b$):** Ratio of total volume of solid to the volume of the bounding rectangular prism.
- **Specific surface area ($S_w$):** Total surface area per unit mass, $S_w = S_p / (\rho_p V_p) = 6 / (\Phi_s \rho_p d_v)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Bounds |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_v$ | Volume diameter | $d_v = (6V_p/\pi)^{1/3}$ | m | Sphere of same volume |
| 2 | $d_s$ | Surface diameter | $d_s = \sqrt{S_p/\pi}$ | m | Sphere of same area |
| 3 | $d_a$ | Projected area diameter | $d_a = \sqrt{4A_p/\pi}$ | m | 2-D image analysis |
| 4 | $d_{st}$ | Stokes diameter | $d_{st} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu u_t}{g(\rho_p - \rho_f)}}$ | m | Re$_p < 0.2$ |
| 5 | $d_A$ | Sieve diameter | (smallest aperture passed) | m | NOT a true geometric diameter |
| 6 | $\Phi_s$ | Sphericity | $\Phi_s = \pi d_v^{2}/S_p = d_v^{2}/d_s^{2}$ | – | $0 < \Phi_s \leq 1$ |
| 7 | $\Phi_c$ | Circularity | $\Phi_c = \pi d_a / P_p$ | – | $0 < \Phi_c \leq 1$ |
| 8 | $AR$ | Aspect ratio | $AR = d_{F,\max}/d_{F,\min}$ | – | $AR \geq 1$ |
| 9 | $S_v$ | Volumetric surface area | $S_v = S_p / V_p = 6/(d_v \Phi_s)$ | m²/m³ | Surface per unit volume |
| 10 | $S_w$ | Specific surface area | $S_w = S_v / \rho_p$ | m²/kg | Surface per unit mass |
| 11 | $K_b$ | Bulkiness factor | $K_b = V_p / (L \cdot B \cdot H)$ | – | $0 < K_b \leq 1$ |
| 12 | $\bar{d}_{p,q}$ | Mean diameter | $\bar{d}_{p,q} = \left(\sum d_i^{\,p} \cdot n_i / \sum d_i^{\,q} \cdot n_i\right)^{1/(p-q)}$ | m | Generalized mean |
| 13 | $d_{sv}$ | Sauter mean diameter | $d_{sv} = 1/\sum (x_i/d_i)$ | m | $p-q = 3$–$2 = 1$; for surface-volume mean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latex Pipe Substitution:** All absolute-value bars inside the table are written as `\vert` / `\;` to avoid breaking markdown rendering. Example: $d_v = (6V_p/\pi)^{1/3}$ uses parentheses, not pipes.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

- **Catalyst design:** A catalyst pellet with $\Phi_s = 0.7$ has roughly **30 % more external surface** (per unit volume) than a perfect sphere of the same volume. That extra surface directly increases the external mass-transfer rate.
- **Fluidization:** Geldart's powder classification uses both $d_{sv}$ and $\rho_p$ — so sphericity indirectly affects whether a powder fluidizes smoothly (Group A) or sluggishly (Group B/C).
- **Cement and tabletting:** High sphericity powders flow better; low sphericity causes arching and segregation.
- **Air pollution control:** The aerodynamic diameter $d_a^*$ is the legally defined metric for PM$_{2.5}$ and PM$_{10}$ particles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume Diameter from Particle Volume

**Statement:** For an irregular particle of measured volume $V_p$, the volume-equivalent diameter is the diameter of a sphere with the same volume.

**Step 1:** Write the volume of a sphere of diameter $d$:
$$V_{sphere} = \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$$

**Step 2:** Equate to particle volume $V_p$ (definition of equivalence):
$$V_{sphere} = V_p \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{\pi d_v^{3}}{6} = V_p$$

**Step 3:** Isolate $d_v$:
$$d_v^{3} = \frac{6 V_p}{\pi} \;\Longrightarrow\; d_v = \left(\frac{6 V_p}{\pi}\right)^{1/3}$$

> *Conversion logic:* cube both sides after multiplying by $6/\pi$, then take the cube root. Result is in metres when $V_p$ is in m³.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Diameter from Particle Surface Area

**Step 1:** Surface area of a sphere of diameter $d$:
$$S_{sphere} = \pi d^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Set equal to actual particle surface area $S_p$:
$$\pi d_s^{2} = S_p$$

**Step 3:** Solve:
$$d_s = \sqrt{\frac{S_p}{\pi}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sphericity in Terms of $d_v$ and $d_s$

**Step 1:** From Waddell's definition:
$$\Phi_s = \frac{\pi d_v^{2}}{S_p}$$

**Step 2:** From the surface-diameter formula, the surface area of a sphere of diameter $d_s$ is $\pi d_s^{2}$. But $S_p \neq \pi d_s^{2}$ (the particle's *actual* area exceeds the sphere's *if* the particle is irregular). However, by *definition* of $d_s$, we have $\pi d_s^{2} = S_p$. Wait — this would make $\Phi_s = 1$ for everything, which is a known subtlety. The correct interpretation: the *volume-equivalent* sphere has surface area $\pi d_v^{2}$, while the *area-equivalent* sphere has surface area $\pi d_s^{2} = S_p$. Substituting into the sphericity definition:

$$\Phi_s = \frac{\pi d_v^{2}}{S_p} = \frac{\pi d_v^{2}}{\pi d_s^{2}} = \frac{d_v^{2}}{d_s^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Take the square root:
$$\sqrt{\Phi_s} = \frac{d_v}{d_s} \quad \text{or} \quad d_v = d_s \, \sqrt{\Phi_s}$$

> *Conversion logic:* For a non-spherical particle, $d_v < d_s$ because the same volume must be packed into a shape with *more* surface than a sphere. So $d_v/d_s < 1$, consistent with $\Phi_s < 1$. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sauter Mean Diameter $d_{sv}$

The **Sauter mean diameter** is the *most important* mean diameter in chemical engineering (used in combustion, catalysis, atomization, drying). It is the diameter of a sphere having the **same volume-to-surface-area ratio** as the entire powder.

**Step 1:** For a powder with $n_i$ particles of size $d_i$, total volume:
$$V_{tot} = \sum_i n_i \cdot \frac{\pi d_i^{3}}{6}$$

**Step 2:** Total surface area:
$$S_{tot} = \sum_i n_i \cdot \pi d_i^{2}$$

**Step 3:** The Sauter mean diameter is defined by:
$$\frac{V_{tot}}{S_{tot}} = \frac{\pi d_{sv}^{3}/6}{\pi d_{sv}^{2}} = \frac{d_{sv}}{6}$$

**Step 4:** Equate and solve:
$$\frac{\sum n_i d_i^{3}/6}{\sum n_i d_i^{2}} = \frac{d_{sv}}{6}$$

Cancel the $1/6$ factor:
$$d_{sv} = \frac{\sum_i n_i d_i^{3}}{\sum_i n_i d_i^{2}}$$

In **mass fraction** form (if $x_i$ is the mass fraction of size interval $i$ and $\bar{d}_i$ is the mean size of that interval):
$$d_{sv} = \frac{1}{\sum_i \left(\dfrac{x_i}{\bar{d}_i}\right)}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The numerator is the third moment of the distribution ($\sum n d^{3}$), the denominator the second moment ($\sum n d^{2}$). Their ratio is dimensionally a length. ✓

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Specific Surface Area of a Non-Spherical Powder

**Step 1:** For a single non-spherical particle of volume $V_p$ and surface area $S_p$, volumetric surface area is:
$$S_v = \frac{S_p}{V_p}$$

**Step 2:** Using $V_p = \pi d_v^{3}/6$ and $S_p = \pi d_v^{2}/\Phi_s$ (from $\Phi_s = \pi d_v^{2}/S_p \Rightarrow S_p = \pi d_v^{2}/\Phi_s$):
$$S_v = \frac{\pi d_v^{2}/\Phi_s}{\pi d_v^{3}/6} = \frac{6}{\Phi_s \, d_v}$$

**Step 3:** Mass-specific surface area:
$$S_w = \frac{S_v}{\rho_p} = \frac{6}{\Phi_s \, \rho_p \, d_v}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Sphericity in the denominator shows that for a given $d_v$, irregular particles have *more* surface than spheres. The factor $1/\Phi_s$ is the "shape penalty" in catalysis and drying.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam — Jun 2022 Pattern]:**
A powder consists of three size fractions, each 100 % by mass:
- 40 % of mass is $100\;\mu m$ particles,
- 35 % of mass is $200\;\mu m$ particles,
- 25 % of mass is $300\;\mu m$ particles.

If all particles have sphericity $\Phi_s = 0.75$ and density $\rho_p = 2500\;\text{kg/m}^3$, calculate:
(a) the Sauter mean diameter $d_{sv}$ and
(b) the specific surface area $S_w$ of the powder.

**Solution:**

**(a) Sauter mean diameter** — using mass fractions $x_i$ and the formula $d_{sv} = 1/\sum(x_i/\bar{d}_i)$:

$$\sum \frac{x_i}{\bar{d}_i} = \frac{0.40}{100\times 10^{-6}} + \frac{0.35}{200\times 10^{-6}} + \frac{0.25}{300\times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= 4000 + 1750 + 833.33 = 6583.33\;\text{m}^{-1}$$

$$d_{sv} = \frac{1}{6583.33} = 1.519\times 10^{-4}\;\text{m} = 151.9\;\mu\text{m}$$

**(b) Specific surface area** — using $S_w = 6/(\Phi_s \, \rho_p \, d_{sv})$:

$$S_w = \frac{6}{0.75 \times 2500 \times 1.519\times 10^{-4}} = \frac{6}{284.8} = 0.02107\;\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$$

Converting: $S_w \approx 21.1\;\text{cm}^2/\text{g}$ — a typical value for a fine industrial powder.

> *Interpretation check:* The Sauter mean ($151.9\;\mu m$) lies between the volume mode ($200\;\mu m$) and the surface mode ($100\;\mu m$) — biased toward the finer fraction, as it *should* be, because $d_{sv}$ is dominated by surface-area contributions. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Taxonomy of Particle Diameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Particle Diameter Classification"] --> B["Geometric Static Diameters"]
    A --> C["Hydrodynamic Dynamic Diameters"]
    A --> D["Statistical Mean Diameters"]

    B --> B1["Feret diameter dF"]
    B --> B2["Martin diameter dM"]
    B --> B3["Projected area diameter da"]
    B --> B4["Perimeter diameter dc"]
    B --> B5["Sieve diameter dA"]
    B --> B6["Volume diameter dv"]
    B --> B7["Surface diameter ds"]

    C --> C1["Stokes diameter dst"]
    C --> C2["Free falling diameter df"]
    C --> C3["Aerodynamic diameter da star"]

    D --> D1["Arithmetic mean d1 0"]
    D --> D2["Surface mean d2 0"]
    D --> D3["Volume mean d3 0"]
    D --> D4["Sauter mean dsv d3 2"]
    D --> D5["De Brouckere mean d4 3"]

    B6 --> E["Shape factor link via sphericity"]
    B7 --> E
    E --> F["Sphericity Phi s equals dv squared over ds squared"]
    E --> G["Specific surface area Sw equals 6 over Phi s rho p dv"]
    E --> H["Aspect ratio AR equals dF max over dF min"]
    E --> I["Circularity Phi c equals pi da over P p"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Relation Between Shape Factor and Surface Area

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Same volume Vp given"] --> node2["Compute volume equivalent sphere surface area: pi dv squared"]
    node1 --> node3["Measure actual particle surface area Sp"]
    node2 --> node4["Take ratio pi dv squared over Sp"]
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5["This ratio IS the sphericity Phi s"]
    node5 --> node6["Sphericity less than 1 implies higher Sw than spherical particle of same dv"]
    node6 --> node7["Higher Sw increases external mass and heat transfer rates in catalysis and drying"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Engineering Use-Case Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    app1["Cyclone separator design"] --> param1["Required parameter: Stokes diameter dst"]
    app2["Catalyst pellet selection"] --> param2["Required parameter: Surface area Sw using sphericity"]
    app3["Hopper flow analysis"] --> param3["Required parameter: Aspect ratio AR and roundness"]
    app4["Air pollution PM2.5 and PM10"] --> param4["Required parameter: Aerodynamic diameter"]
    app5["Sieve size distribution"] --> param5["Required parameter: Sieve diameter dA"]
    app6["Cement and tabletting"] --> param6["Required parameter: Sphericity Phi s and bulkiness factor"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define *sphericity* and state its bounds. Why is the surface area of a sphere with the same volume used in the definition?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sphericity $\Phi_s$ is defined as the ratio of the surface area of a sphere having the **same volume** as the particle to the **actual surface area** of the particle:
$$\Phi_s = \frac{\pi d_v^{2}}{S_p}$$
Bounds: $0 < \Phi_s \leq 1$, with $\Phi_s = 1$ for a perfect sphere. The sphere of the *same volume* is chosen as the reference because volume is the invariant, measurable property of the particle (from mass and density), and it provides a unique, size-independent shape descriptor. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023] Differentiate between **Feret's diameter** and **Martin's diameter** with a neat sketch in your description.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Feret's diameter ($d_F$):** the distance between two *parallel tangents* touching the particle on opposite sides, measured along a fixed reference direction. It depends on particle orientation.
- **Martin's diameter ($d_M$):** the length of the line that *bisects the projected area* of the particle, drawn in a fixed direction. Also orientation-dependent.
- The two are *not* identical; $d_F$ typically equals the longest chord in that direction, while $d_M$ cuts the area in half. For an asymmetric projection, $d_F > d_M$. Both are averaged over many orientations to give a statistical size. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive an expression for the **Sauter mean diameter** $d_{sv}$ of a powder in terms of (i) the number-mean form, and (ii) the mass-fraction form.

**(b) [7 marks]** A catalyst powder has the following size distribution (mass fraction basis):

| Sieve size range ($\mu m$) | Mass fraction $x_i$ | Mean size $\bar{d}_i$ ($\mu m$) |
|---|---|---|
| $-75 + 53$ | 0.10 | 64 |
| $-53 + 45$ | 0.20 | 49 |
| $-45 + 38$ | 0.30 | 41.5 |
| $-38$ | 0.40 | 25 |

If the particles have sphericity $\Phi_s = 0.65$ and density $\rho_p = 1800\;\text{kg/m}^3$, compute:
(i) the Sauter mean diameter $d_{sv}$,
(ii) the volumetric surface area $S_v$, and
(iii) the mass-specific surface area $S_w$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation [7 marks]:**

For $n_i$ particles of size $d_i$ in the $i$-th class:

Total volume:
$$V_{tot} = \sum_i n_i \cdot \frac{\pi d_i^{3}}{6}$$

Total surface area:
$$S_{tot} = \sum_i n_i \cdot \pi d_i^{2}$$

By definition, the Sauter mean diameter is the size of a sphere whose **volume-to-surface-area ratio** equals that of the *entire* powder:
$$\frac{V_{tot}}{S_{tot}} = \frac{\pi d_{sv}^{3}/6}{\pi d_{sv}^{2}} = \frac{d_{sv}}{6}$$

Substituting and solving:
$$\frac{\sum_i n_i d_i^{3}/6}{\sum_i n_i d_i^{2}} = \frac{d_{sv}}{6} \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{d_{sv} = \frac{\sum_i n_i d_i^{3}}{\sum_i n_i d_i^{2}}}$$

In **mass-fraction form**, with $x_i$ the mass fraction in size interval $i$ and $M_i$ the mass of one particle of that size ($M_i = \rho_p \pi d_i^{3}/6$):
$$d_{sv} = \frac{1}{\sum_i \left(\dfrac{x_i}{\bar{d}_i}\right)}$$

This is the **most-used** form in industry because sieve analysis directly gives $x_i$ and $\bar{d}_i$. (7 marks)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**(i) Sauter mean diameter** [2 marks]:

$$\sum \frac{x_i}{\bar{d}_i} = \frac{0.10}{64} + \frac{0.20}{49} + \frac{0.30}{41.5} + \frac{0.40}{25}$$
$$= 1.5625\times 10^{-3} + 4.0816\times 10^{-3} + 7.2289\times 10^{-3} + 16.00\times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 28.873\times 10^{-3}\;\mu\text{m}^{-1}$$

$$d_{sv} = \frac{1}{28.873\times 10^{-3}} = 34.63\;\mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Volumetric surface area** [2 marks]:
$$S_v = \frac{6}{\Phi_s \, d_{sv}} = \frac{6}{0.65 \times 34.63\times 10^{-6}} = \frac{6}{22.51\times 10^{-6}} = 2.665\times 10^{5}\;\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$$

**(iii) Mass-specific surface area** [3 marks]:
$$S_w = \frac{S_v}{\rho_p} = \frac{2.665\times 10^{5}}{1800} = 148.1\;\text{m}^2/\text{kg} = 1.481\times 10^{4}\;\text{cm}^2/\text{g}$$

> *Sanity check:* A typical industrial catalyst has $S_w \sim 10^3$–$10^5\;\text{cm}^2/\text{g}$. Our value $1.48\times 10^{4}\;\text{cm}^2/\text{g}$ lies comfortably in the right range. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Define and explain, with appropriate formulas, the following equivalent diameters: (i) volume diameter $d_v$, (ii) surface diameter $d_s$, (iii) projected area diameter $d_a$, and (iv) Stokes diameter $d_{st}$. State the conditions under which each is most appropriately used.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a powder consisting of 200 g of particles having $d_v = 250\;\mu m$, $d_s = 320\;\mu m$ and density $\rho_p = 2200\;\text{kg/m}^3$, calculate:
(i) the sphericity,
(ii) the total number of particles (assuming all identical),
(iii) the total surface area, and
(iv) the mass-specific surface area $S_w$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Definitions and usage [7 marks]:**

(i) **Volume diameter $d_v$:** diameter of a sphere with the same volume as the particle, $d_v = (6V_p/\pi)^{1/3}$. Used when particle volume is known (e.g., from pycnometry, mass + density).

(ii) **Surface diameter $d_s$:** diameter of a sphere with the same surface area as the particle, $d_s = \sqrt{S_p/\pi}$. Used when surface area is known (e.g., from BET, gas adsorption).

(iii) **Projected area diameter $d_a$:** diameter of a circle with the same projected area as the particle's 2-D image, $d_a = \sqrt{4A_p/\pi}$. Used in image analysis, microscopy, and optical particle counters.

(iv) **Stokes diameter $d_{st}$:** diameter of a sphere of the same density having the same terminal settling velocity in the Stokes regime ($\text{Re}_p < 0.2$). Used in sedimentation, elutriation, cyclone design, and air-classifier design:
$$d_{st} = \sqrt{\frac{18\,\mu\,u_t}{g\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)}}$$
(7 marks — about 1.75 per definition)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**(i) Sphericity** [1.5 marks]:
$$\Phi_s = \frac{d_v^{2}}{d_s^{2}} = \left(\frac{250}{320}\right)^{2} = (0.7813)^{2} = 0.6104$$

**(ii) Total number of particles** [1.5 marks]:
Volume of one particle:
$$V_p = \frac{\pi d_v^{3}}{6} = \frac{\pi (250\times 10^{-6})^{3}}{6} = 8.181\times 10^{-15}\;\text{m}^3$$

Mass per particle:
$$m_p = \rho_p V_p = 2200 \times 8.181\times 10^{-15} = 1.800\times 10^{-11}\;\text{kg}$$

Number of particles:
$$N = \frac{M_{total}}{m_p} = \frac{0.200}{1.800\times 10^{-11}} = 1.111\times 10^{10}\;\text{particles}$$

**(iii) Total surface area** [1.5 marks]:
$$S_p = \frac{\pi d_s^{2}}{\Phi_s} = \frac{\pi (320\times 10^{-6})^{2}}{0.6104} = 5.272\times 10^{-7}\;\text{m}^2 \text{ per particle}$$

$$S_{tot} = N \cdot S_p = 1.111\times 10^{10} \times 5.272\times 10^{-7} = 5858\;\text{m}^2 \approx 5.86\times 10^{3}\;\text{m}^2$$

**(iv) Mass-specific surface area** [2.5 marks]:
$$S_w = \frac{S_{tot}}{M_{total}} = \frac{5858}{0.200} = 29290\;\text{m}^2/\text{kg} = 2.93\times 10^{6}\;\text{cm}^2/\text{g}$$

> *Sanity check:* Very fine particles (sub-sieve) routinely show $S_w \sim 10^5$–$10^6\;\text{cm}^2/\text{g}$. Our value (≈ 2.9 m²/g expressed as $2.9 \times 10^{6}$ cm²/g) is consistent. ✓

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Typically Lose Marks on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Inverting the sphericity ratio** (writing $\Phi_s = S_p / (\pi d_v^{2})$) — costs 1 mark minimum and propagates errors in $S_w$ calculations.
> 2. **Mixing up $d_v$ and $d_s$** in $S_v = 6/(\Phi_s d_v)$. The $d_v$ in the denominator is the *volume* equivalent, NOT $d_s$.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency in $S_w$**: giving $S_w$ in m²/m³ (which is $S_v$, not $S_w$) instead of m²/kg. Always divide by $\rho_p$ at the end.
> 4. **Forgetting the averaging orientation** for Feret/Martin diameters — these are statistical averages, not single measurements.
> 5. **Sauter mean formula confusion**: $d_{sv} = \sum n_i d_i^{3}/\sum n_i d_i^{2}$ (number form) is *not* the same as $\sum x_i d_i$. Use the correct form depending on whether $x_i$ is mass or number fraction.
> 6. **Sphericity and circularity confusion**: $\Phi_s$ is for 3-D particles, $\Phi_c$ is for 2-D projected images. The KTU exam will explicitly say "particle" or "projection" — read carefully.
> 7. **Assuming shape factor = 1** without stating it: a sphere is the *only* case where $\Phi_s = 1$; for an irregular powder, always carry $\Phi_s$ through the calculation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Particle diameter is NEVER a single value** for an irregular particle — it is always an *equivalent* diameter based on a chosen property.
- **Geometric diameters** ($d_F, d_M, d_a, d_c, d_A, d_v, d_s$) are measured at rest; **hydrodynamic diameters** ($d_{st}, d_f, d_a^*$) are measured by settling behaviour.
- **Sphericity $\Phi_s = \pi d_v^{2}/S_p$** is the most-used 3-D shape factor; bounds $0 < \Phi_s \leq 1$.
- **Sphericity relates $d_v$ and $d_s$**: $d_v = d_s \sqrt{\Phi_s}$.
- **Aspect ratio $AR = d_{F,\max}/d_{F,\min}$** — measures elongation; $\geq 1$.
- **Circularity $\Phi_c = \pi d_a / P_p$** — 2-D shape factor for projected images.
- **Sauter mean diameter $d_{sv} = \sum n_i d_i^{3}/\sum n_i d_i^{2}$** (number form) **or** $d_{sv} = 1/\sum(x_i/\bar{d}_i)$ (mass-fraction form) — the most engineering-relevant mean size.
- **Specific surface area $S_w = 6/(\Phi_s \rho_p d_v)$** — the "shape penalty" $1/\Phi_s$ appears here, making irregular powders more reactive per unit mass.
- **Sieve diameter $d_A$ underestimates** the longest dimension of elongated particles.
- **Aerodynamic diameter $d_a^*$** is the *legal* metric for PM$_{2.5}$/PM$_{10}$ in air-pollution regulations.
- Always carry **units** ($d$ in m, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $S_w$ in m²/kg) through the full calculation — the most common KTU mark loss.
- The four formulas to memorize: $\Phi_s = \pi d_v^{2}/S_p$, $d_{st} = \sqrt{18\mu u_t/[g(\rho_p - \rho_f)]}$, $d_{sv} = 1/\sum(x_i/\bar{d}_i)$, $S_w = 6/(\Phi_s \rho_p d_v)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Particle Size Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Particle Size Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Particle Size Analysis (PSA)** is the collective name given to the set of laboratory techniques and mathematical procedures used to determine the **size distribution** of particles in a powdered, granular, or particulate solid sample. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, PSA is the quantitative descriptor of how a bulk mass of solids is partitioned across a range of equivalent spherical diameters, typically expressed in **micrometers (µm)** or **millimeters (mm)**.

In strict KTU terminology, particle size analysis is the *experimental and mathematical determination of the equivalent spherical diameter* of irregularly shaped particles, classified into:

1. **Direct Measurement Methods** → microscopy, image analysis.
2. **Indirect Methods** → sieving, sedimentation, elutriation, laser diffraction, electrical resistance (Coulter counter).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The module explicitly demands mastery of *sieve analysis, equivalent diameters (Feret, Martin, projected), mean particle diameters (arithmetic, geometric, surface, volume, mass-mean), specific surface area, and the interpretation of cumulative / differential distribution curves.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you have a **bucket of mixed marbles, ball-bearings, and pebbles** of various sizes. How would you describe this mixture precisely? You would not say "small, medium, big". Instead, you would *sort them through a series of sieves with progressively smaller holes*. Whatever stays on top of a sieve tells you "this particle is bigger than the hole". Doing this across many sieves gives you a complete *distribution* — this is the essence of sieve analysis.

**Particle Size Analysis is the engineering discipline of giving a precise, numerical identity to the phrase "how coarse or fine is my powder?"** Without PSA, phrases like "fine talc", "coarse sand", or "micronized drug" are useless in any engineering calculation involving reaction rates, filtration, dissolution, or fluidization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference Sizes used in KTU problems:**
> - **1 inch = 25.4 mm**
> - **1 mm = 1000 µm = 10⁶ nm**
> - **Mesh number** = number of openings per linear inch of sieve.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Particle Size Analysis is the Backbone of Particle Technology
- **Reaction Engineering**: Surface area per unit mass governs heterogeneous reaction rates.
- **Pharmaceutics**: Drug dissolution rate is governed by particle size (Noyes-Whitney equation).
- **Cement / Civil Engineering**: Fines content dictates hydration kinetics and strength.
- **Mineral Processing**: Liberation size dictates grinding energy & recovery.
- **Powder Handling**: Flowability, packing fraction, and angle of repose are size-dependent.

### 2.2 Classification of Particles by Size Range
| Class | Size Range |
|---|---|
| Colloidal | < 1 µm |
| Ultra-fine / Sub-sieve | 1 – 10 µm |
| Fine powder | 10 – 100 µm |
| Coarse powder | 100 – 1000 µm |
| Granular | 1 – 10 mm |
| Coarse / Lump | > 10 mm |

### 2.3 Equivalent Spherical Diameters (Crucial for KTU Theory)
For an irregular particle, three classical diameters are used:
- **Projected diameter ($d_p$)**: Diameter of a circle having the same projected area as the particle in random orientation.
- **Feret's diameter ($d_F$)**: The perpendicular distance between two parallel tangents to the particle profile.
- **Martin's diameter ($d_M$)**: The line that bisects the projected area of the particle profile.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean (Number Mean) | $\bar{d}_n = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i}{\sum n_i}$ | µm |
| 2 | Length Mean (Mean of $\bar{d}_n \cdot d$) | $\bar{d}_L = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^2}{\sum n_i d_i}$ | µm |
| 3 | Surface Mean (Sauter / SMD, $D_{3,2}$) | $\bar{d}_{sv} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}$ | µm |
| 4 | Volume / Mass Mean ($D_{4,3}$) | $\bar{d}_{vm} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^4}{\sum n_i d_i^3}$ | µm |
| 5 | Geometric Mean | $d_g = (d_1^{x_1} \cdot d_2^{x_2} \cdots d_n^{x_n})^{1/\sum x_i}$ | µm |
| 6 | Surface Area of one sphere | $A = \pi d^2$ | m² |
| 7 | Volume of one sphere | $V = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}$ | m³ |
| 8 | Specific Surface Area (volume basis) | $S_v = \dfrac{6}{d}$ | m²/m³ |
| 9 | Specific Surface Area (mass basis) | $S_w = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p \cdot d}$ | m²/kg |
| 10 | Number of particles per kg | $N = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p \pi d^3}$ | particles/kg |
| 11 | Mass retained on sieve | $m_i = (m_{cumulative} / m_{total}) \times 100$ | % |
| 12 | Cumulative mass passing (smaller than) | $\%P_i = 100 - \sum_{j=1}^{i} \%R_j$ | % |
| 13 | Geometric standard deviation | $\sigma_g = \dfrac{d_{84.13\%}}{d_{50\%}} = \dfrac{d_{50\%}}{d_{15.87\%}}$ | µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the formula sheet above, the symbol `\vert` is intentionally avoided inside the table rows. KTU examiners and PDF parsers break when the pipe character appears inside formulas — always use `\mid` or `\vert` in any LaTeX expression embedded in a markdown table.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility
- **Sauter Mean ($D_{3,2}$)** is used in combustion, mass transfer with chemical reaction, and catalysis because it weights surface area.
- **Volume Mean ($D_{4,3}$)** is used in crystallization, grinding, and product control because it weights mass.
- **Specific Surface Area ($S_w$)** is the *single most critical design parameter* for catalyst selection, dissolution kinetics, and powder reactivity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Specific Surface Area of a Spherical Particle
We derive the relationship between the diameter and the surface-to-volume ratio, which is the foundation of $S_v$ and $S_w$.

**Step 1 — Surface Area of a single sphere:**
$$A = \pi \, d^2$$

**Step 2 — Volume of a single sphere:**
$$V = \frac{\pi \, d^3}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Surface-to-Volume ratio (volume specific surface area):**
$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{\pi d^2}{\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}} = \frac{6}{d}$$

Thus the **volume specific surface area** is:
$$S_v = \frac{6}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Mass specific surface area** (divide by particle density $\rho_p$):
$$S_w = \frac{S_v}{\rho_p} = \frac{6}{\rho_p \cdot d}$$

**Conversion Logic:** $S_v$ has units of $\text{m}^2 / \text{m}^3$, while $S_w$ has units of $\text{m}^2 / \text{kg}$ — the density $\rho_p$ ($\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$) converts the volumetric basis into a gravimetric basis, which is essential when expressing surface area "per kg of powder".

### 3.2 Derivation II — Sauter Mean Diameter (Surface-Volume Mean $D_{3,2}$)
Consider $N$ spherical particles divided into $k$ size classes. Each class $i$ has $n_i$ particles of diameter $d_i$.

**Step 1 — Total surface area of all particles:**
$$A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, \pi d_i^2$$

**Step 2 — Total volume of all particles:**
$$V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, \frac{\pi d_i^3}{6}$$

**Step 3 — We define the Sauter Mean $D_{sv}$ such that a *single* mono-disperse cloud of diameter $D_{sv}$ and total particle count $N_{total} = \sum n_i$ has the same surface-to-volume ratio as the polydisperse system:**
$$\frac{A_{total}}{V_{total}} = \frac{N_{total} \cdot \pi D_{sv}^2}{N_{total} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_{sv}^3}{6}} = \frac{6}{D_{sv}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 and Step 2:**
$$\frac{6}{D_{sv}} = \frac{\sum n_i \, \pi d_i^2}{\sum n_i \, \dfrac{\pi d_i^3}{6}}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\pi$ from numerator and denominator, multiply both sides by $D_{sv}$ and rearrange:**
$$D_{sv} = \frac{\sum n_i \, d_i^3}{\sum n_i \, d_i^2}$$

This is the classical Sauter Mean formula. The physical meaning: $D_{sv}$ is the diameter of an equivalent mono-disperse sphere having the *same mass-transfer / heat-transfer capability* as the actual polydisperse powder.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Relationship Between Number, Surface and Mass Means

Define three general moments:
$$M_r = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, d_i^r$$

Then:
$$\bar{d}_n = \frac{M_1}{M_0}, \quad \bar{d}_L = \frac{M_2}{M_1}, \quad D_{sv} = \frac{M_3}{M_2}, \quad \bar{d}_{vm} = \frac{M_4}{M_3}$$

**Inequality Rule (always true for polydisperse systems):**
$$\bar{d}_n \; < \; \bar{d}_L \; < \; D_{sv} \; < \; \bar{d}_{vm}$$

This hierarchy is tested directly in KTU 14-mark questions as a "state and justify" item.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** A powder has the following sieve analysis. (a) Determine the cumulative % retained, cumulative % passing. (b) Calculate the mass-mean diameter $d_{vm}$. (c) Find the specific surface area $S_w$ assuming $\rho_p = 2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

| Sieve (µm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|---|---|
| 500 | 0 |
| 355 | 50 |
| 250 | 100 |
| 180 | 150 |
| 125 | 120 |
| 90 | 60 |
| 63 | 20 |
| Pan | 0 |

**Total mass = 500 g**.

**Step (a) — Cumulative % Retained and % Passing:**

| Sieve (µm) | Mass Retained (g) | % Retained | Cum. % Retained | Cum. % Passing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 500 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 100.00 |
| 355 | 50 | 10.00 | 10.00 | 90.00 |
| 250 | 100 | 20.00 | 30.00 | 70.00 |
| 180 | 150 | 30.00 | 60.00 | 40.00 |
| 125 | 120 | 24.00 | 84.00 | 16.00 |
| 90 | 60 | 12.00 | 96.00 | 4.00 |
| 63 | 20 | 4.00 | 100.00 | 0.00 |

**Step (b) — Mass-Mean Diameter:**
Using midpoint of each sieve as the representative $d_i$, and fractional mass $x_i = m_i / \sum m_i$:

| $d_i$ (µm) | $x_i$ (–) | $x_i \cdot d_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 427.5 | 0.10 | 42.75 |
| 302.5 | 0.20 | 60.50 |
| 215.0 | 0.30 | 64.50 |
| 152.5 | 0.24 | 36.60 |
| 107.5 | 0.12 | 12.90 |
| 76.5 | 0.04 | 3.06 |

$$\bar{d}_{vm} = \sum x_i d_i = 220.31 \; \mu m$$

**Step (c) — Specific Surface Area:**
$$S_w = \frac{6}{\rho_p \, d_{vm}} = \frac{6}{2500 \times (220.31 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 10.89 \; \frac{m^2}{kg}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Mean for the Right Problem

| Engineering Scenario | Most Appropriate Mean | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Combustion of pulverized coal | Sauter Mean $D_{3,2}$ | Reaction rate ∝ surface area |
| Crystallizer product control | Volume Mean $D_{4,3}$ | Mass-weighted specification |
| Particle counting (Coulter) | Number Mean $\bar{d}_n$ | Each particle counts equally |
| Sedimentation / Stokes settling | Stokes Mean $D_{st}$ | Velocity ∝ $d^2$ |
| Sieve analysis reporting | Mass Median Diameter $d_{50}$ | Convention in mining/civil |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow of Particle Size Analysis Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Particle Size Analysis Techniques] --> B0[Direct Methods]
    A0 --> C0[Indirect Methods]

    B0 --> B1[Optical Microscopy]
    B0 --> B2[Electron Microscopy SEM TEM]
    B0 --> B3[Image Analysis Software]

    C0 --> C1[Sieving 63 micron to mm range]
    C0 --> C2[Sedimentation 1 to 100 micron]
    C0 --> C3[Elutriation]
    C0 --> C4[Laser Diffraction 0.1 to 1000 micron]
    C0 --> C5[Coulter Counter 0.5 to 200 micron]

    C1 --> D1[Coarse Fraction retained on sieve]
    C1 --> D2[Fines through pan]
    C1 --> D3[Median Diameter d50 from cumulative curve]

    D3 --> E1[Mass Mean Diameter d vm]
    D3 --> E2[Number Mean Diameter d n]
    D3 --> E3[Sauter Mean Diameter D sv]
    D3 --> E4[Specific Surface Area S w]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sieve Analysis Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Raw Sieve Stack 500 to 63 micron] --> stepB[Weigh Each Fraction]
    stepB --> stepC[Compute Percent Retained Each Sieve]
    stepC --> stepD[Build Cumulative Table]
    stepD --> stepE[Plot Cumulative Percent Passing vs log d]
    stepE --> stepF[Read d10 d50 d90 from curve]
    stepF --> stepG[Compute Geometric Std Deviation sigma g]
    stepF --> stepH[Compute Mean Diameters d n d L d sv d vm]
    stepH --> stepI[Compute Specific Surface Area S w]
    stepI --> stepJ[Report to Quality Control]
```

### 4.3 Particle Diameter Hierarchy & Equivalent Diameter Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Equivalent Spherical Diameter Concept] --> B1[Feret Diameter dF perpendicular tangents]
    A1 --> B2[Martin Diameter dM bisects projected area]
    A1 --> B3[Projected Diameter dp equal area circle]
    A1 --> B4[Sieve Diameter ds minimum square aperture]
    A1 --> B5[Stokes Diameter dSt settling velocity equivalence]

    B5 --> C1[Used in Sedimentation Analysis]
    B4 --> C2[Used in Sieve Analysis]
    B3 --> C3[Used in Microscopy]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block is a *functional step* in a KTU-quality numerical or theoretical solution. The arrows represent the **information flow** from raw sieve data to the final reported physical quantities ($d_{50}$, $d_{vm}$, $S_w$). KTU examiners expect students to explicitly draw or describe such flows in 14-mark problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 2 Mark Conceptual Questions

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define (i) Feret's diameter, and (ii) Martin's diameter. Why are these called "equivalent diameters"?**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Feret's diameter ($d_F$):** The perpendicular distance between two parallel tangents drawn on opposite sides of the projected particle profile.
- **(ii) Martin's diameter ($d_M$):** The length of the line that divides the projected area of the particle into two equal halves.

These are termed **"equivalent diameters"** because they reduce the irregular, two-dimensional projection of a 3-D particle to a *single representative scalar* that approximates the particle as a sphere of equivalent geometric property (tangent distance, area bisector, area, etc.). This allows comparison and inclusion in distribution calculations.

*(2 marks — 1 mark per definition, 1 mark for explanation of "equivalent")*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the Sauter Mean Diameter ($D_{sv}$ or $D_{3,2}$) in chemical engineering practice?**

**Model Answer:**

The Sauter Mean Diameter is defined as the diameter of an equivalent mono-disperse sphere having the **same surface-to-volume ratio** as the polydisperse powder:
$$D_{sv} = \frac{\sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}$$

**Significance:** It is the *only* mean diameter that preserves the mass-transfer and heat-transfer capability of the actual distribution. Hence it is the *correct* mean to use in design of:
- Combustion systems (reaction rate ∝ surface area)
- Catalytic reactors
- Spray nozzles
- Liquid-liquid and gas-liquid dispersions

*(2 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark application)*

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Module Choice Questions

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the formula for the **volume specific surface area** $S_v$ and the **mass specific surface area** $S_w$ of a powder consisting of spherical particles of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A limestone powder is screened through a nest of standard Tyler sieves. The mass retained on each sieve is tabulated below. Calculate: (i) the cumulative percent passing each sieve, (ii) the mass-mean diameter $d_{vm}$, and (iii) the specific surface area $S_w$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$. Take $\rho_p = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

| Tyler Sieve (mesh) | Aperture (µm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|---|---|---|
| 28 | 589 | 0 |
| 35 | 417 | 30 |
| 48 | 295 | 70 |
| 65 | 208 | 110 |
| 100 | 147 | 130 |
| 150 | 104 | 90 |
| 200 | 74 | 50 |
| Pan | – | 20 |

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Surface area of one spherical particle:**
$$A = \pi d^2$$

**Step 2 — Volume of one spherical particle:**
$$V = \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Surface to volume ratio (volume specific surface area):**
$$S_v = \frac{A}{V} = \frac{\pi d^2}{\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}} = \frac{6}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Mass specific surface area (divide by density $\rho_p$):**
$$S_w = \frac{S_v}{\rho_p} = \frac{6}{\rho_p \, d}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** $S_w$ has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$ and represents the *external surface area exposed per unit mass of powder*. This is the key input to heterogeneous reaction rates and dissolution kinetics.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Total mass** = $30 + 70 + 110 + 130 + 90 + 50 + 20 = 500$ g.

**Step 2 — Compute percent retained, cumulative % retained, cumulative % passing:**

| Aperture (µm) | Midpoint $d_i$ (µm) | Mass (g) | % Retained | Cum % Retained | Cum % Passing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 589 | 503 | 0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 100.0 |
| 417 | 356 | 30 | 6.0 | 6.0 | 94.0 |
| 295 | 251.5 | 70 | 14.0 | 20.0 | 80.0 |
| 208 | 177.5 | 110 | 22.0 | 42.0 | 58.0 |
| 147 | 125.5 | 130 | 26.0 | 68.0 | 32.0 |
| 104 | 89.0 | 90 | 18.0 | 86.0 | 14.0 |
| 74 | 63.0 | 50 | 10.0 | 96.0 | 4.0 |
| Pan | – | 20 | 4.0 | 100.0 | 0.0 |

**Step 3 — Compute mass-mean diameter $d_{vm}$ using midpoint method:**
$$d_{vm} = \sum x_i d_i$$
where $x_i = m_i / \sum m_i$.

| $d_i$ (µm) | $x_i$ | $x_i d_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 503 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 356 | 0.06 | 21.36 |
| 251.5 | 0.14 | 35.21 |
| 177.5 | 0.22 | 39.05 |
| 125.5 | 0.26 | 32.63 |
| 89.0 | 0.18 | 16.02 |
| 63.0 | 0.10 | 6.30 |
| 0 (Pan) | 0.04 | 0.00 |

$$\sum x_i d_i = 150.57 \;\mu m$$
$$\boxed{d_{vm} = 150.57 \;\mu m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute specific surface area:**
$$S_w = \frac{6}{\rho_p \, d_{vm}} = \frac{6}{2700 \times (150.57 \times 10^{-6})}$$
$$\boxed{S_w = 14.76 \;\text{m}^2/\text{kg}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is the **Sauter Mean Diameter** $D_{sv}$? Show step by step how it is derived, starting from the definition that a mono-disperse cloud of diameter $D_{sv}$ must have the same surface-to-volume ratio as the original polydisperse powder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A catalyst powder has the following particle size distribution (mass basis). Calculate: (i) the **number mean diameter** $\bar{d}_n$, (ii) the **Sauter mean diameter** $D_{sv}$, and (iii) the **number of particles per kg** of powder. Take $\rho_p = 1500 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

| Size range (µm) | Mass in range (g) |
|---|---|
| 1 – 10 | 5 |
| 10 – 50 | 20 |
| 50 – 100 | 35 |
| 100 – 500 | 40 |

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition:** $D_{sv}$ is the diameter of a hypothetical mono-disperse powder such that the surface-to-volume ratio of the mono-disperse system equals that of the actual polydisperse system.

**Step 2 — For the mono-disperse equivalent of $N$ particles:**
$$\frac{A}{V}\bigg|_{mono} = \frac{N \pi D_{sv}^2}{N \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_{sv}^3}{6}} = \frac{6}{D_{sv}}$$

**Step 3 — For the actual polydisperse system with $k$ size classes:**
$$A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \pi d_i^2, \quad V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \frac{\pi d_i^3}{6}$$

$$\frac{A_{total}}{V_{total}} = \frac{\sum n_i d_i^2}{\sum n_i d_i^3/6} = \frac{6 \sum n_i d_i^2}{\sum n_i d_i^3}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two surface-to-volume ratios:**
$$\frac{6}{D_{sv}} = \frac{6 \sum n_i d_i^2}{\sum n_i d_i^3}$$

**Step 5 — Invert and simplify:**
$$D_{sv} = \frac{\sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}$$

**Step 6 — Physical meaning:** This diameter preserves *all mass-transfer and heat-transfer phenomena* that depend on surface-to-volume ratio. Hence $D_{sv}$ is the appropriate mean for design of combustion, drying, dissolution, and catalysis systems.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — For each size range, compute midpoint $d_i$ and total mass $m_i$. Assume uniform density, so $n_i \propto m_i / d_i^3$.**

| Range (µm) | Midpoint $d_i$ (µm) | $d_i^3$ (µm³, ×10³) | $m_i$ (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 – 10 | 5.5 | 0.000166 | 5 |
| 10 – 50 | 30 | 0.027 | 20 |
| 50 – 100 | 75 | 0.422 | 35 |
| 100 – 500 | 300 | 27.000 | 40 |

**Step 2 — Compute relative number of particles:** $n_i \propto m_i / d_i^3$

$$n_1 \propto 5/0.000166 = 30,120 \quad (\text{relative units})$$
$$n_2 \propto 20/0.027 = 740.7$$
$$n_3 \propto 35/0.422 = 82.9$$
$$n_4 \propto 40/27.0 = 1.48$$

(Note: actual $n_i$ requires the constant $\frac{6 \rho_{tot}}{\pi \rho_p}$; ratios are sufficient for mean calculations.)

**Step 3 — Number Mean Diameter $\bar{d}_n$:**

$$\bar{d}_n = \frac{\sum n_i d_i}{\sum n_i}$$

$$\sum n_i d_i = 30120 \times 5.5 + 740.7 \times 30 + 82.9 \times 75 + 1.48 \times 300$$
$$= 165,660 + 22,221 + 6217.5 + 444 \approx 194,542$$

$$\sum n_i = 30120 + 740.7 + 82.9 + 1.48 \approx 30,945$$

$$\boxed{\bar{d}_n \approx 6.29 \;\mu m}$$

**Step 4 — Sauter Mean $D_{sv}$:**

$$D_{sv} = \frac{\sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}$$

$$\sum n_i d_i^2 = 30120 \times 30.25 + 740.7 \times 900 + 82.9 \times 5625 + 1.48 \times 90000$$
$$= 911,130 + 666,630 + 466,313 + 133,200 \approx 2,177,273$$

$$\sum n_i d_i^3 = \sum m_i (\text{relative}) \approx 100 \text{ (sum of mass ratios, after normalization)}$$

More rigorously, $\sum n_i d_i^3 \propto \sum m_i = 100$, and $\sum n_i d_i^2 \propto \sum m_i / d_i$:

$$\sum \frac{m_i}{d_i} = \frac{5}{5.5} + \frac{20}{30} + \frac{35}{75} + \frac{40}{300} = 0.909 + 0.667 + 0.467 + 0.133 = 2.176$$

Thus:
$$D_{sv} = \frac{100}{2.176} \approx 45.96 \;\mu m$$

$$\boxed{D_{sv} \approx 46.0 \;\mu m}$$

**Step 5 — Number of particles per kg:**

For each class, $N_i = \dfrac{6 \, m_i}{\rho_p \, \pi \, d_i^3}$.

Converting $d_i$ to meters:

$$N_1 = \frac{6 \times 0.005}{1500 \times \pi \times (5.5 \times 10^{-6})^3} \approx 9.55 \times 10^{9}$$
$$N_2 = \frac{6 \times 0.020}{1500 \times \pi \times (30 \times 10^{-6})^3} \approx 9.43 \times 10^{8}$$
$$N_3 = \frac{6 \times 0.035}{1500 \times \pi \times (75 \times 10^{-6})^3} \approx 1.76 \times 10^{8}$$
$$N_4 = \frac{6 \times 0.040}{1500 \times \pi \times (300 \times 10^{-6})^3} \approx 3.77 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\boxed{N_{total} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{10} \text{ particles per kg}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong representative diameter:** Using *sieve aperture* instead of *midpoint of the size range*. KTU board answer keys penalise this strictly.
> 2. **Cumulative % passing vs. retained mix-up:** Always start the cumulative table at the *top sieve* and work downward. Cumulative % passing decreases monotonically.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in $S_w$:** Diameter MUST be in meters before plugging into $S_w = 6/(\rho_p d)$. Students writing $S_w = 6/(\rho_p d)$ with $d$ in µm lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the "6" factor:** A common error is writing $S_v = 3/d$ (from confusing surface-to-volume with circumference-to-area). The 6 comes from $V = \pi d^3 / 6$.
> 5. **Confusing Sauter and Volume mean:** $D_{sv}$ uses $d^3$ over $d^2$ *without* fourth power. Volume mean $d_{vm}$ uses $d^4$ over $d^3$.
> 6. **Skipping the inequality proof:** In theory questions, KTU expects explicit statement that $\bar{d}_n < \bar{d}_L < D_{sv} < \bar{d}_{vm}$ with one-line justification.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Particle size analysis** is the experimental determination of the equivalent spherical diameter distribution of a particulate solid.
- **Equivalent diameters** (Feret, Martin, projected) collapse the irregular shape into a single scalar for distribution analysis.
- **Tyler sieves** use $\sqrt{2}$ ratio; **US Standard** uses $\sqrt[4]{2}$ ratio between successive sieves.
- **Cumulative % passing** is plotted against $\log d$ on the abscissa to linearize most distributions.
- **Arithmetic mean** weights each particle equally; **Sauter mean** weights surface area; **Volume mean** weights mass.
- **Inequality rule:** $\bar{d}_n < \bar{d}_L < D_{sv} < \bar{d}_{vm}$ always holds for polydisperse systems.
- **Specific surface area** $S_w = 6 / (\rho_p d)$ — the smaller the particle, the larger the surface per kg.
- **Number of particles per kg** $N = 6 / (\rho_p \pi d^3)$ — extremely sensitive to fines content.
- **Median diameter $d_{50}$** is read from the cumulative curve at the 50% passing point.
- **Geometric standard deviation** $\sigma_g = d_{84.13}/d_{50}$ quantifies the spread of a log-normal distribution.
- **Sieve analysis valid range:** ~63 µm to ~10 mm; below this, sedimentation or laser diffraction must be used.
- **KTU 2024 scheme emphasis:** numerical problems on cumulative curves, mean diameter calculation, and $S_w$ derivation carry **14 marks**, while definitional items carry **2 marks**.
- **Always show units** explicitly in the final answer; a numerical answer without units = **0 marks** as per strict KTU valuation.
- **Plotting tip for full marks:** When asked for distribution curves, draw *both* the cumulative (% passing) curve *and* the frequency histogram on the same graph with log-scale x-axis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sieve Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sieve Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sieve Analysis** (also termed **Mechanical Analysis** or **Screen Analysis**) is a fundamental particle size distribution (PSD) determination technique in powder technology wherein a representative sample of granular particulate solids is successively passed through a stack of standard test sieves arranged in descending order of aperture size. The mass of material retained on each sieve is gravimetrically measured, enabling the construction of a quantitative size-frequency distribution that characterizes the bulk particulate system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Sieve analysis forms the foundation for understanding *particle size*, *mean particle diameter*, *specific surface area*, and *distribution laws* (Rosin–Rammler, Gaudin–Schuhmann). It is the most heavily weighted topic of Module 1 for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Kitchen Sieve" Analogy**
> Imagine sieving atta (flour) in your kitchen. When you shake it over a fine muslin cloth, large wheat bran pieces remain on top, while the fine flour passes through. By changing the cloth's tightness, you effectively separate particles by size. Now imagine stacking 8–10 such cloths, each progressively finer, from top to bottom. The largest particles sit on the topmost coarse sieve, the finest powder collects in the bottom pan, and intermediate fractions are trapped in between. This stack-of-sieves is *exactly* what a **Ro-Tap Sieve Shaker** does in a powder technology lab — but with international, ISO-standardized apertures (e.g., 710 µm, 500 µm, 250 µm) rather than cloths.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Standards (Recognized Globally)

| Standard | Country / Body | Aperture Series Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Tyler Standard | USA (W.S. Tyler Co.) | $\sqrt{2}$ or $\sqrt[4]{2}$ ratio |
| ASTM E-11 | American Society for Testing and Materials | ISO R 565 compliant |
| BS 410 | British Standards Institution | ISO 3310-1 |
| IS 460 | Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) | ISO aligned |
| ISO 3310-1 | International Organization for Standardization | $\sqrt[10]{10} \approx 1.259$ ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A standard sieve has an **aperture** (clear opening) of size $d$ measured in micrometres (µm) or millimetres (mm). The **Mesh Number** is the number of openings per linear inch (25.4 mm) of the sieve surface.

## 1.4 Working Principle — The Core "Why"

The technique exploits **size-exclusion geometry**: a particle of diameter $d_p$ will pass through an aperture of size $d$ **if and only if** all three of its orthogonal cross-sectional dimensions are smaller than $d$. The "cube-equivalent" approximation assumes that a particle is statistically likely to be presented to the aperture in its most stable orientation (largest face down), so the **smallest cross-sectional dimension** governs passage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Procedure — The "How"

The standard mechanical sieve analysis protocol is executed as follows:

1. **Sample Collection**: Obtain a representative sample using a *scoop sampler* or *riffle sampler* (minimum mass guided by Riffle's rule: $m \geq 0.02\,\text{kg}$ per ton of bulk for coarse solids, scaled up for fines).
2. **Oven Drying**: Dry the sample to constant mass at **105–110 °C** to remove bound moisture (in hygroscopic powders). For heat-sensitive materials, desiccation under vacuum is preferred.
3. **Weighing**: Record the initial sample mass $M_0$ on an analytical balance (precision $\pm 0.01\,\text{g}$).
4. **Stack Assembly**: Arrange sieves in a **vertical column** with the **largest aperture at the top** and the **smallest at the bottom**, terminated by a **receiving pan (backing dish)**. A **lid** seals the top to prevent dust escape.
5. **Agitation**: Mount the stack on a mechanical **Ro-Tap shaker** (horizontal circular motion + vertical tap) for a **standard duration of 15–20 minutes**, after which asymptotic equilibrium of separation is reached.
6. **Reweighing**: Carefully dismantle the stack, weigh the **retained mass $M_i$** on each sieve plus the pan, recording the cumulative mass.
7. **Mass Balance Check**: Verify that
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} M_i + M_{\text{pan}} = M_0 \pm 1\%$$
8. **Distribution Plotting**: Convert raw masses to cumulative percent undersize / oversize, then plot on semi-log or log-log graph paper.

## 2.2 Mesh Number vs. Aperture — The Inverse Relationship

> [!NOTE]
> A high mesh number = a *small* aperture. A low mesh number = a *large* aperture. This inverse relation is a common KTU MCQ trap.

$$d \approx \frac{25.4\,\text{mm}}{M} - d_w$$

where $M$ is the mesh number (openings per inch) and $d_w$ is the average wire diameter.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass fraction retained on sieve $i$ | $x_i = \dfrac{M_i}{M_0}$ | dimensionless | Always sum to 1.0 |
| 2 | Cumulative mass retained | $X_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} x_j$ | dimensionless | Step plot |
| 3 | Cumulative % undersize (passing) | $D_i = \left(1 - X_i\right)\times 100$ | % | Plotted on Y-axis of PSD curve |
| 4 | Cumulative % oversize (retained) | $R_i = X_i \times 100$ | % | Plotted on Y-axis of complementary curve |
| 5 | Mean aperture of two adjacent sieves | $d_{\text{avg},i} = \sqrt{d_{i}\cdot d_{i+1}}$ | µm or mm | Geometric mean — always used in PSD |
| 6 | Arithmetic mean diameter | $d_{\text{AM}} = \dfrac{\sum x_i d_{\text{avg},i}}{\sum x_i}$ | µm or mm | Coarse approximation, KTU $L_1$ level |
| 7 | Geometric mean diameter | $\ln d_{\text{GM}} = \dfrac{\sum x_i \ln d_{\text{avg},i}}{\sum x_i}$ | µm or mm | KTU favourite — most accurate |
| 8 | Mass-mean (median) diameter | $d_{\text{50\%}} \mid_{D(d)=50\%}$ | µm | From PSD curve directly |
| 9 | Specific surface area (sieve-derived) | $S_w = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p}\sum \dfrac{x_i}{d_{\text{avg},i}}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$ | Assumes spheres |
| 10 | Specific surface area (volume-mean) | $S_v = \dfrac{\sum x_i / d_{\text{avg},i}}{\sum x_i / d_{\text{avg},i}^3}\cdot 6$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$ | For surface-volume mean |
| 11 | Rosin–Rammler equation | $R(d) = 100\,\exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{d}{d'}\right)^{n}\right]$ | % | Distribution law — KTU PYQ repeat |
| 12 | Rosin–Rammler slope (uniformity) | $n$ | dimensionless | Slope of $\ln\!\ln$ plot |
| 13 | Rosin–Rammler size constant | $d'$ | µm | Diameter at $R=36.79\%$ |
| 14 | Gaudin–Schuhmann equation | $y(d) = \left(\dfrac{d}{d_{\max}}\right)^{m}$ | fraction | Power-law distribution |
| 15 | G–S distribution modulus | $m$ | dimensionless | $m=1$: Schuhmann ideal |
| 16 | Effective size $d_{10}$ | $D=10\%$ on PSD curve | µm | Allen Hazen — sand filter design |
| 17 | Uniformity coefficient | $C_u = \dfrac{d_{60}}{d_{10}}$ | dimensionless | Soil mechanics & KTU civil-chem overlap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Geometric Mean Diameter** is the statistically correct mean for log-normally distributed particle populations and is the most frequently demanded KTU numerical. The arithmetic mean is acceptable only for narrow distributions ($d_{\max}/d_{\min} < 2$).

## 2.4 Rosin–Rammler Distribution Law (Detailed)

> [!NOTE]
> The Rosin–Rammler–Sperling–Bennett (RRSB) distribution is **empirically** derived for the breaking of brittle materials (cement clinker, coal, ores) but is widely applied. It is the most-tested distribution law in the KTU ESE.

The equation expresses the **cumulative percent oversize** as a function of particle diameter:

$$R(d) = 100\,\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{d}{d'}\right)^{n}\right]$$

Linearization via double logarithm yields the canonical KTU plot:

$$\ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{100}{R}\right)\right] = n\,\ln(d) - n\,\ln(d')$$

A plot of $\ln\ln(100/R)$ versus $\ln(d)$ yields a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** = $n$ (uniformity number, larger $n$ → narrower distribution)
- **Intercept** = $-n\ln(d')$

> [!WARNING]
> When $R = 36.79\%$, then $100/R = 2.718 = e$, and $\ln(100/R) = 1$, hence $\ln\ln(100/R) = 0$. The corresponding $d$ at this point **equals** $d'$. This is the KTU examiner's "shortcut" trick question!

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cement industry**: Blaine fineness validation.
- **Pharmaceutical powders**: Tablet granulation uniformity.
- **Coal beneficiation**: Washability curves depend on PSD.
- **Catalyst manufacturing**: Active site distribution.
- **Food industry**: Flour grade, sugar granulometry.
- **Filtration / Sand filters**: Effective size $d_{10}$ for bed design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Geometric Mean Diameter Formula

**Premise**: For a log-normal particle size distribution, the probability density of $\ln d$ is normal, so the mean is taken in log-space.

**Step 1** — Define the geometric mean for a discrete set of size fractions.

Let fraction $i$ have a representative diameter $d_i$ and mass fraction $x_i$. The geometric mean of all $d_i$ weighted by $x_i$ is:

$$\ln d_{\text{GM}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \ln d_i$$

**Step 2** — Justify the use of geometric mean aperture $d_{\text{avg},i} = \sqrt{d_i\,d_{i+1}}$ between two adjacent sieves.

For a particle caught between sieve $i$ (aperture $d_i$, larger) and sieve $i+1$ (aperture $d_{i+1}$, smaller), its diameter lies in $[d_{i+1}, d_i]$. Under a uniform distribution assumption within this interval, the central tendency is the **arithmetic mean**, but in a log-normal population the **geometric mean** is correct. Taking the log-centre:

$$\ln d_{\text{avg},i} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\ln d_i + \ln d_{i+1}\right) = \ln\sqrt{d_i\,d_{i+1}}$$

**Step 3** — Combine to give the KTU-standard formula.

$$\boxed{\,\ln d_{\text{GM}} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i\,\ln\!\sqrt{d_i\,d_{i+1}}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i} = \frac{\sum x_i\,\ln d_{\text{avg},i}}{\sum x_i}\,}$$

**Step 4** — Recovery of the linear form by exponentiation.

$$d_{\text{GM}} = \exp\!\left(\sum x_i \ln d_{\text{avg},i}\right) = \prod_i (d_{\text{avg},i})^{x_i}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Specific Surface Area (Sieve Method)

**Premise**: A spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ has surface area $A = \pi d_p^2$ and volume $V = \pi d_p^3/6$. Hence the surface-to-volume ratio is $6/d_p$.

**Step 1** — Mass of particles in fraction $i$ per unit mass of sample.

For $M_0 = 1$ kg of total sample, the mass in fraction $i$ is $x_i$ kg, and the number of particles is $N_i$:

$$N_i = \frac{x_i}{V_i\,\rho_p} = \frac{6\,x_i}{\pi\,d_{\text{avg},i}^{\,3}\,\rho_p}$$

where $\rho_p$ is particle density.

**Step 2** — Total surface area contributed by fraction $i$.

$$A_i = N_i \cdot \pi\,d_{\text{avg},i}^{\,2} = \frac{6\,x_i}{\rho_p\,d_{\text{avg},i}}$$

**Step 3** — Specific surface area $S_w$ (per unit mass).

Summing over all fractions and dividing by total mass $M_0$:

$$\boxed{\,S_w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{A_i}{M_0} = \frac{6}{\rho_p}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\frac{x_i}{d_{\text{avg},i}} \quad \left[\text{m}^2/\text{kg}\right]\,}$$

**Step 4** — Conversion to volumetric specific surface $S_v$.

$$S_v = \rho_p\,S_w = 6\sum_{i}\frac{x_i}{d_{\text{avg},i}}\quad \left[\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3\right]$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Sample Question

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem** [KTU University Exam — May 2022, Modified]: A 500 g sample of crushed quartz is subjected to sieve analysis. Results:

| Sieve Aperture (µm) | Mass Retained (g) | Mass Fraction $x_i$ | $d_{\text{avg},i}$ (µm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| +710 | 25 | 0.05 | 595.0 |
| +500 | 75 | 0.15 | 425.0 |
| +355 | 100 | 0.20 | 305.0 |
| +250 | 150 | 0.30 | 210.0 |
| +180 | 100 | 0.20 | 150.0 |
| +125 | 35 | 0.07 | 105.0 |
| −125 (pan) | 15 | 0.03 | — |

**Required**: (a) Plot the cumulative % undersize vs. $\log d$ (PSD curve), (b) compute $d_{\text{GM}}$, (c) find specific surface area if $\rho_p = 2650\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

### 3.3.1 (a) Cumulative % Undersize Calculation

**Cumulative % retained** $R_i$ and **% undersize** $D_i$ are tabulated step-wise:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 5\% & D_1 &= 95\% \\
R_2 &= 5 + 15 = 20\% & D_2 &= 80\% \\
R_3 &= 20 + 20 = 40\% & D_3 &= 60\% \\
R_4 &= 40 + 30 = 70\% & D_4 &= 30\% \\
R_5 &= 70 + 20 = 90\% & D_5 &= 10\% \\
R_6 &= 90 + 7 = 97\% & D_6 &= 3\% \\
R_7 &= 97 + 3 = 100\% & D_7 &= 0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Plot**: $D_i$ (Y-axis, linear) versus $d_i$ (X-axis, log scale). The $d_{50}$ is read where the curve crosses $D = 50\%$.

### 3.3.2 (b) Geometric Mean Diameter

**Step 1** — Compute $x_i \ln d_{\text{avg},i}$ for the first 6 fractions (the pan fraction has no defined $d_{\text{avg},i}$ and is conventionally assigned a value $= d_7/2$ — here $62.5$ µm).

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 \ln d_{\text{avg}} &= 0.05 \times \ln(595.0) = 0.05 \times 6.3886 = 0.3194 \\
x_2 \ln d_{\text{avg}} &= 0.15 \times \ln(425.0) = 0.15 \times 6.0524 = 0.9079 \\
x_3 \ln d_{\text{avg}} &= 0.20 \times \ln(305.0) = 0.20 \times 5.7203 = 1.1441 \\
x_4 \ln d_{\text{avg}} &= 0.30 \times \ln(210.0) = 0.30 \times 5.3471 = 1.6041 \\
x_5 \ln d_{\text{avg}} &= 0.20 \times \ln(150.0) = 0.20 \times 5.0106 = 1.0021 \\
x_6 \ln d_{\text{avg}} &= 0.07 \times \ln(105.0) = 0.07 \times 4.6539 = 0.3258 \\
x_7 \ln d_{\text{avg}} &= 0.03 \times \ln(62.5) = 0.03 \times 4.1352 = 0.1241
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2** — Sum the weighted logarithms.

$$\sum x_i \ln d_{\text{avg},i} = 0.3194 + 0.9079 + 1.1441 + 1.6041 + 1.0021 + 0.3258 + 0.1241 = 5.4275$$

**Step 3** — Sum the mass fractions (mass balance check).

$$\sum x_i = 0.05 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.07 + 0.03 = 1.00 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 4** — Compute the geometric mean.

$$\ln d_{\text{GM}} = \frac{5.4275}{1.00} = 5.4275 \quad\Rightarrow\quad d_{\text{GM}} = e^{5.4275} = 227.4\,\mu\text{m}$$

**Result**: $d_{\text{GM}} \approx 227.4\,\mu\text{m}$.

### 3.3.3 (c) Specific Surface Area

**Step 1** — Compute $x_i / d_{\text{avg},i}$ for each fraction (convert $d_{\text{avg}}$ to metres: $d_{\text{avg},i}(\text{m}) = d_{\text{avg},i}(\mu\text{m}) \times 10^{-6}$).

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1/d_1 &= 0.05 / (595 \times 10^{-6}) = 84.03\,\text{m}^{-1} \\
x_2/d_2 &= 0.15 / (425 \times 10^{-6}) = 352.94\,\text{m}^{-1} \\
x_3/d_3 &= 0.20 / (305 \times 10^{-6}) = 655.74\,\text{m}^{-1} \\
x_4/d_4 &= 0.30 / (210 \times 10^{-6}) = 1428.57\,\text{m}^{-1} \\
x_5/d_5 &= 0.20 / (150 \times 10^{-6}) = 1333.33\,\text{m}^{-1} \\
x_6/d_6 &= 0.07 / (105 \times 10^{-6}) = 666.67\,\text{m}^{-1} \\
x_7/d_7 &= 0.03 / (62.5 \times 10^{-6}) = 480.00\,\text{m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2** — Sum.

$$\sum \frac{x_i}{d_{\text{avg},i}} = 84.03 + 352.94 + 655.74 + 1428.57 + 1333.33 + 666.67 + 480.00 = 5001.28\,\text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3** — Apply the formula.

$$S_w = \frac{6}{\rho_p}\sum\frac{x_i}{d_{\text{avg},i}} = \frac{6 \times 5001.28}{2650} = \frac{30007.7}{2650} = 11.32\,\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$$

**Result**: $S_w \approx 11.32\,\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$.

## 3.4 Rosin–Rammler Linearization — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2021]: Sieve data for ground limestone gave $R = 25\%$ at $d = 50\,\mu\text{m}$ and $R = 1.5\%$ at $d = 250\,\mu\text{m}$. Find $n$ and $d'$.

**Step 1** — Take double logarithms.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 = 25\% \quad\Rightarrow\quad \ln(100/R_1) = \ln 4 = 1.386 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \ln\ln(100/R_1) = \ln 1.386 = 0.326 \\
R_2 = 1.5\% \quad\Rightarrow\quad \ln(100/R_2) = \ln 66.67 = 4.200 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \ln\ln(100/R_2) = \ln 4.200 = 1.435
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2** — Form linear system in $n$ and $n\ln d'$.

$$0.326 = n \ln(50) - n\ln d' = n(3.912) - n\ln d'$$
$$1.435 = n \ln(250) - n\ln d' = n(5.521) - n\ln d'$$

**Step 3** — Subtract the first equation from the second to eliminate $n\ln d'$.

$$1.435 - 0.326 = n(5.521 - 3.912)$$
$$1.109 = n(1.609) \quad\Rightarrow\quad n = 0.6893$$

**Step 4** — Back-substitute.

$$0.326 = 0.6893 \times 3.912 - 0.6893 \ln d'$$
$$0.326 = 2.697 - 0.6893 \ln d'$$
$$\ln d' = \frac{2.697 - 0.326}{0.6893} = \frac{2.371}{0.6893} = 3.440$$
$$d' = e^{3.440} = 31.22\,\mu\text{m}$$

**Result**: $n \approx 0.69$ (broad distribution), $d' \approx 31.2\,\mu\text{m}$.

**Verification**: At $R = 36.79\%$, $d$ should equal $d' = 31.2$ µm. Quick check: $R(31.2) = 100\,\exp[-(31.2/31.2)^{0.69}] = 100\,e^{-1} = 36.79\%$. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sieve Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Powder Sample] --> B[Drying in Oven 105 to 110 C]
    B --> C[Weighing M0 on Analytical Balance]
    C --> D[Sievestack Assembly Coarse on Top Fine at Bottom]
    D --> E[Ro Tap Shaker 15 to 20 min]
    E --> F[Dismantle and Reweigh Each Sieve Mi]
    F --> G{Mass Balance Check Sum Mi = M0}
    G -->|Within 1 percent| H[Compute Mass Fractions xi and Cumulative D]
    G -->|Exceeds 1 percent| I[Reject Run and Repeat]
    H --> J[Plot PSD Curve D versus log d]
    J --> K[Read d10 d50 d90 from Curve]
    J --> L[Compute dGM via Weighted Log Mean]
    J --> M[Compute Specific Surface Area Sw]
    J --> N[Fit Rosin Rammler Linearize Slope n and Size d prime]
    L --> O[Final PSD Report]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    K --> O
```

## 4.2 Schematic of a Ro-Tap Sieve Shaker Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph stack [Vertical Sieve Column]
        S1[Sieve 1 710 micrometre Aperture Largest] --> S2[Sieve 2 500 micrometre]
        S2 --> S3[Sieve 3 355 micrometre]
        S3 --> S4[Sieve 4 250 micrometre]
        S4 --> S5[Sieve 5 180 micrometre]
        S5 --> S6[Sieve 6 125 micrometre]
        S6 --> S7[Receiving Pan Backing Dish]
    end
    S1 --> L[Lid Sealed Top]
    stack --> RT[Ro Tap Mechanical Shaker]
    RT --> MA1[Horizontal Circular Motion 280 rpm]
    RT --> MA2[Vertical Tapping 150 taps per min]
    MA1 --> EFF[Induced Stratification by Size Largest Top Smallest Bottom]
    MA2 --> EFF
    EFF --> OUT[Equilibrium Separation After 15 min]
```

## 4.3 Cumulative Distribution Curve — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AX[X Axis Log Particle Diameter d in micrometre] --> P1
    AY[Y Axis Linear Cumulative Percent Undersize D] --> P1
    P1[Plot All Data Points Di di] --> R[Read Off Key Diameters]
    R --> R1[d10 Effective Size 10 percent Passing]
    R --> R2[d50 Mass Median Diameter 50 percent Passing]
    R --> R3[d90 Coarse Tail 90 percent Passing]
    R1 --> C[Uniformity Coefficient Cu = d60 divided by d10]
    R2 --> M[Mean Diameter Arithmetic Geometric or Volume Surface]
    R3 --> S[Distribution Width Indicator d90 minus d10]
```

## 4.4 Rosin–Rammler Linearization Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Raw Data Pairs di and Ri] --> T1[Compute y = ln ln 100 by Ri]
    T1 --> T2[Compute x = ln di]
    T2 --> T3[Plot y vs x on Cartesian Axes]
    T3 --> FIT{Best Fit Straight Line}
    FIT -->|Slope m equals n| GETN[Extract Uniformity Number n]
    FIT -->|Intercept c equals minus n ln d prime| GETD[Extract Size Constant d prime]
    GETN --> USE[Engineering Use Predict Percent Retained at any d]
    GETD --> USE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four diagrams above form a complete **operational reference chain**: sample preparation → mechanical separation → distribution plotting → analytical model fitting. Each block in the Mermaid schematics corresponds to a procedural step that a KTU student must be able to describe in a 7-mark answer.
