topics.title: Effectiveness and Capacity of Screen and Factors Affecting Them
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effectiveness and Capacity of Screen and Factors Affecting Them

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 1 — Effectiveness and Capacity of Screen

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Screening** is a unit operation in **Particle Technology** that separates a mixture of particles into two or more size fractions by passing the feed through a **screening surface (mesh/sieve)** having apertures of a definite size.

In any ideal screening process, every particle either:
- **Passes through** the screen apertures (called **undersize** or **fines**)
- **Is retained on** the screen (called **oversize** or **coarse**)

However, in real industrial screens, some undersized particles remain trapped with the oversize, and some oversized particles slip through. This deviation from the "ideal" separation is quantified by two fundamental performance indicators:

### 📌 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**1. Screen Effectiveness (η) or Screen Efficiency (E)**

Screen effectiveness is the **ratio of the mass of undersize material that is actually recovered in the correct stream (i.e., passes through the screen) to the mass of undersize material present in the feed**, usually expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Mass of undersize actually separated}}{\text{Mass of undersize in the feed}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Idea:** Effectiveness measures *how cleanly* the screen separates the two fractions — i.e., the **QUALITY of separation**.

**2. Screen Capacity (C)**

Screen capacity is the **amount (mass) of feed material that a screen can process per unit time** while maintaining an acceptable efficiency, usually expressed in **tonnes per hour per square metre (T/h/m²)** of screen area.

$$C = \frac{\text{Mass of feed handled by the screen}}{\text{Time (h)} \times \text{Active screen area (m}^2\text{)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Idea:** Capacity measures *how much* material the screen can process — i.e., the **QUANTITY of throughput**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sorting a **mixed pile of rice and stones** using a **sieve (jalli)** at home.

- The **sieve aperture size** decides which particles go through.
- The **effectiveness** is *how cleanly* every grain of rice falls down, without any rice being left behind on the sieve, and without any stone accidentally slipping through.
- The **capacity** is *how fast* you can keep pouring the mixture onto the sieve without it getting choked.

If you pour the mixture **very fast (high feed rate)** → capacity is high, but some rice may stay on top → **efficiency drops**.

If you pour the mixture **very slowly (low feed rate)** → efficiency is high (almost perfect separation), but **capacity drops**.

> 💡 **Engineering Trade-off:** Higher throughput (capacity) almost always comes at the cost of lower separation quality (effectiveness), and vice versa. KTU problems often ask you to find the **optimum balance**.

---

## 1.3 Nomenclature Used in KTU Board Problems

> [!NOTE]
> Always draw and label the following streams in a screening process diagram before solving any numerical:

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :---: | :--- |
| **F** | Feed stream (total mass entering the screen) |
| **O** | Overflow (Oversize, retained on screen) |
| **U** | Underflow (Undersize, passes through screen) |
| **F_f** | Mass fraction of **undersize in the feed** |
| **F_o** | Mass fraction of **undersize in the overflow** |
| **F_u** | Mass fraction of **undersize in the underflow** |
| **R_o** | Mass fraction of **overflow (oversize) in the feed** |
| **R_u** | Mass fraction of **underflow (undersize) in the feed** |

---

## 1.4 Mass Balance Around the Screen

**Overall mass balance:**

$$F = O + U$$

**Component balance on the undersize material:**

$$F \cdot F_f = O \cdot F_o + U \cdot F_u$$

This fundamental balance is the starting point for **every KTU numerical on screening**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic of the Effectiveness Equation (Step-by-Step Logic)

Let us build the effectiveness formula logically so that a KTU student never has to memorize a "magic" equation.

### Step 1 — Define the streams
- Feed: `F kg/h` enters the screen
- Overflow: `O kg/h` of oversize leaves from the top
- Underflow: `U kg/h` of undersize leaves from the bottom

### Step 2 — Undersize mass in the feed
The mass of undersize present **per hour** in the feed stream is:

$$\text{Undersize in feed} = F \cdot F_f$$

### Step 3 — Undersize mass actually separated (in the underflow)
The mass of undersize **per hour** that correctly passed through the screen is:

$$\text{Undersize recovered} = U \cdot F_u$$

### Step 4 — Definition of Effectiveness

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Undersize recovered}}{\text{Undersize in feed}} = \frac{U \cdot F_u}{F \cdot F_f}$$

### Step 5 — Eliminate U using mass balance
From the overall mass balance: `F = O + U` → `U = F − O`.  
From the component balance: `F·F_f = O·F_o + U·F_u` → `O·F_o = F·F_f − U·F_u`.

Substituting these into the expression for η (after algebraic manipulation) gives the **standard KTU textbook form**:

$$\boxed{\;\eta = \frac{F_u \cdot (F_f - F_o)}{F_f \cdot (F_u - F_o)} \times 100\%\;}$$

This is the **most-asked effectiveness formula** in KTU 2024 ESE papers.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you need for Module 1 screening problems in the KTU University Exam.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F = O + U$ | Total mass balance | kg/h or T/h |
| 2 | $F \cdot F_f = O \cdot F_o + U \cdot F_u$ | Undersize component balance | kg/h |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{U \cdot F_u}{F \cdot F_f} \times 100$ | Effectiveness (basic) | % |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{F_u(F_f - F_o)}{F_f(F_u - F_o)} \times 100$ | Effectiveness (3-stream form) | % |
| 5 | $C = A \times B \times D$ | Capacity of screen | T/h |
| 6 | $C = 0.783 \times A \times B \times D$ | Capacity of vibrating screen (Taggart) | T/h |
| 7 | $B = 0.10 \times \text{bulk density}$ | Basic capacity (approximation) | T/h/m² |
| 8 | $E = 1 - e^{-k t}$ | Recovery / probability of passage | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Open area} = \dfrac{a^2}{(a+d)^2}$ | % open area of screen | % |
| 10 | $F_f = F_o R_o + F_u R_u$ | Undersize balance (alternative form) | dimensionless |

Where:
- `A` = effective screen area (m²)
- `B` = basic capacity (T/h/m²)
- `D` = deck position factor (top = 1, second = 0.9, third = 0.8)
- `a` = aperture size; `d` = wire diameter
- `k` = passage rate constant; `t` = residence time on screen

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit conversion tip for KTU:** `1 T/h = 1000 kg/h = 0.2777 kg/s`.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The effectiveness-capacity relationship governs the design of screens in:

- 🏭 **Mineral Beneficiation Plants** (iron ore, coal, limestone sizing)
- 🧪 **Cement Industry** (raw mix and clinker separation)
- 🌾 **Food & Agro Processing** (flour, sugar, spice grading)
- ♻️ **Recycling Plants** (plastic flake separation)
- 🏗️ **Aggregate / Construction Industry** (sand and gravel classification)

If a screen is over-loaded (high capacity demand) → **blinding** occurs → efficiency plummets → product is rejected. If under-loaded → poor economy of operation.

> 💡 **Engineering Insight:** KTU always rewards students who comment on the **practical trade-off** between capacity and effectiveness while answering theory questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Standard Effectiveness Formula

We will derive the formula starting from the most fundamental balance so that the result is **fully transparent** and does not require memorization.

### Given
- Feed mass rate: `F` (kg/h)
- Overflow mass rate: `O` (kg/h)
- Underflow mass rate: `U` (kg/h)
- `F_f` = mass fraction of undersize in feed
- `F_o` = mass fraction of undersize in overflow
- `F_u` = mass fraction of undersize in underflow

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Total mass balance**

$$F = O + U$$

This means the total feed equals the total of the two exit streams.

**Step 2 — Component (undersize) mass balance**

$$F \cdot F_f = O \cdot F_o + U \cdot F_u$$

This is a balance of *only* the undersize material across the screen.

**Step 3 — Effectiveness definition**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Mass of undersize that correctly passed through (in U)}}{\text{Mass of undersize in the feed}}$$

In terms of streams and compositions:

$$\eta = \frac{U \cdot F_u}{F \cdot F_f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute O = F − U in the component balance**

$$F \cdot F_f = (F - U) \cdot F_o + U \cdot F_u$$

Expanding the right-hand side:

$$F \cdot F_f = F \cdot F_o - U \cdot F_o + U \cdot F_u$$

Rearranging to isolate `U`:

$$F \cdot F_f - F \cdot F_o = U \cdot (F_u - F_o)$$

$$F \cdot (F_f - F_o) = U \cdot (F_u - F_o)$$

Therefore:

$$U = \frac{F \cdot (F_f - F_o)}{(F_u - F_o)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute U into the effectiveness expression**

$$\eta = \frac{U \cdot F_u}{F \cdot F_f} = \frac{1}{F \cdot F_f} \cdot \frac{F \cdot (F_f - F_o)}{(F_u - F_o)} \cdot F_u$$

Cancel `F` from numerator and denominator:

$$\eta = \frac{(F_f - F_o) \cdot F_u}{F_f \cdot (F_u - F_o)}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to percentage**

$$\boxed{\;\eta = \frac{F_u (F_f - F_o)}{F_f (F_u - F_o)} \times 100\%\;}$$

This is the **golden KTU formula** for screen effectiveness.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical (Model Solution for KTU Practice)

> **KTU 2024-Style Question Pattern:** A vibrating screen processes 80 T/h of crushed limestone. The feed contains 45% fines. The overflow contains 12% fines and the underflow contains 92% fines. Calculate the effectiveness of the screen and the mass of oversize and undersize streams.

**Given Data**

- Feed: `F = 80 T/h`
- `F_f = 0.45`
- `F_o = 0.12`
- `F_u = 0.92`

**Find:** Effectiveness `η`, overflow `O`, and underflow `U`.

### Step 1 — Apply the mass balance
From the component balance:

$$F \cdot F_f = O \cdot F_o + U \cdot F_u$$

$$80 \times 0.45 = O \times 0.12 + U \times 0.92$$

$$36 = 0.12 \, O + 0.92 \, U \quad \text{...(i)}$$

From the total mass balance:

$$F = O + U \implies 80 = O + U \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 2 — Solve the two equations simultaneously
From (ii): `O = 80 − U`. Substitute in (i):

$$36 = 0.12(80 - U) + 0.92 U$$

$$36 = 9.6 - 0.12 U + 0.92 U$$

$$36 - 9.6 = 0.80 U$$

$$26.4 = 0.80 U$$

$$U = \frac{26.4}{0.80} = 33.0 \, \text{T/h}$$

And:

$$O = 80 - 33.0 = 47.0 \, \text{T/h}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the effectiveness
Using the standard 3-stream formula:

$$\eta = \frac{F_u \cdot (F_f - F_o)}{F_f \cdot (F_u - F_o)} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{0.92 \times (0.45 - 0.12)}{0.45 \times (0.92 - 0.12)} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{0.92 \times 0.33}{0.45 \times 0.80} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{0.3036}{0.3600} \times 100 = 84.33\%$$

### Step 4 — Verification using the basic definition
Using $\eta = \dfrac{U \cdot F_u}{F \cdot F_f} \times 100$:

$$\eta = \frac{33.0 \times 0.92}{80 \times 0.45} \times 100 = \frac{30.36}{36} \times 100 = 84.33\%$$

✅ **Both expressions agree — the answer is consistent.**

### ✅ Final Results

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| Underflow (U) | **33.0 T/h** |
| Overflow (O) | **47.0 T/h** |
| Screen Effectiveness (η) | **84.33 %** |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Capacity Formula (Taggart's Correlation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is asked as a 7-mark derivation question in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

For a vibrating screen, Taggart proposed an empirical correlation:

$$C = 0.783 \cdot A \cdot B \cdot D$$

### Where:
- `C` = capacity of screen in **T/h**
- `A` = effective screen area in **m²**
- `B` = basic capacity per m² of screen (T/h/m²), given empirically as $B = 0.10 \times \rho_{bulk}$
- `D` = deck position factor (1.0 for top deck, 0.9 for second, 0.8 for third, etc.)

### Sample Calculation
A vibrating screen has an area of 2.5 m², bulk density 1.6 T/m³, and is used as a top deck. Calculate its capacity.

**Step 1 — Basic capacity**

$$B = 0.10 \times 1.6 = 0.16 \, \text{T/h/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Taggart's formula**

$$C = 0.783 \times 2.5 \times 0.16 \times 1.0$$

$$C = 0.3132 \, \text{T/h}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Mass Balance Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF["FEED F kg/h"] -- Ff fraction undersize in feed --> nodeScr
    nodeScr["SCREEN APERTURE a mm"]
    nodeScr -- retained particles --> nodeO["OVERFLOW O kg/h Oversize"]
    nodeScr -- passes through --> nodeU["UNDERFLOW U kg/h Undersize"]
    nodeF -- Fo fraction undersize in overflow --> nodeO
    nodeF -- Fu fraction undersize in underflow --> nodeU
```

> 💡 **Reading the diagram:** The feed `F` enters the screen. The screen splits the feed into two exit streams — overflow `O` (coarse, retained on top) and underflow `U` (fines, passed through the apertures). The compositions `F_f`, `F_o`, and `F_u` track the **mass fraction of undersize** in each respective stream.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Vibrating Screen

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph feedArea["FEED ZONE"]
        feedH["Feed Hopper"]
        feeder["Vibratory Feeder"]
    end
    subgraph screenBody["SCREEN BODY"]
        deckA["Top Deck Aperture a1"]
        deckB["Middle Deck Aperture a2"]
        deckC["Bottom Deck Aperture a3"]
    end
    subgraph drive["VIBRATION DRIVE"]
        motor["Electric Motor"]
        ecc["Eccentric Shaft"]
        springs["Support Springs"]
    end
    subgraph output["OUTPUT STREAMS"]
        outO["Overflow Conveyor"]
        outM["Middling Conveyor"]
        outU["Underflow Conveyor"]
    end

    feedH --> feeder
    feeder --> deckA
    deckA --> deckB
    deckB --> deckC
    motor --> ecc
    ecc --> deckA
    springs --> screenBody
    deckA --> outO
    deckB --> outM
    deckC --> outU
```

> 💡 **Engineering interpretation:** Multiple decks stacked vertically give **multi-product separation** in a single unit. The eccentric shaft produces vibrations that throw particles upward and forward, stratifying the bed so that fines can reach the apertures and pass through.

---

## 4.3 Relationship Map — Factors Affecting Screening Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    perf["SCREEN PERFORMANCE"]
    perf --> eff["EFFECTIVENESS eta"]
    perf --> cap["CAPACITY C T per h per m2"]

    eff --> eFeed["Feed Properties: moisture, size distribution, bulk density, particle shape"]
    eff --> eScreen["Screen Properties: aperture size, wire diameter, open area, type of weave"]
    eff --> eOp["Operating Conditions: vibration amplitude, frequency, feed rate, bed depth"]

    cap --> cFeed["Feed rate of feed in T per h"]
    cap --> cArea["Effective screen area A m2"]
    cap --> cDeck["Deck position factor D"]
    cap --> cDeckDensity["Bulk density of material rho"]
```

> 💡 **How to use this map in KTU theory answers:** When asked "list the factors affecting screen capacity and effectiveness", simply walk through the three branches — **Feed Properties, Screen Properties, Operating Conditions** — and write 2–3 sub-points under each.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 Section 5 — KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define screen effectiveness. What is its ideal value and why is it never achieved in practice?
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Screen effectiveness (η) is the ratio of the mass of undersize particles actually recovered in the underflow to the mass of undersize particles present in the feed, expressed as a percentage.
>
> $$\eta = \frac{U \cdot F_u}{F \cdot F_f} \times 100\%$$
>
> The **ideal value is 100%**, which would mean every undersize particle passed through and no oversize particle passed through the screen. In practice, 100% effectiveness is impossible because of (i) **blinding** — fines clogging the apertures, (ii) **near-size particles** that randomly either pass or get retained, (iii) **overloading** of the screen surface, and (iv) **improper vibration amplitude/frequency**.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between screen capacity and screen effectiveness.
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Screen Capacity (C) | Screen Effectiveness (η) |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Definition | Mass of feed handled per unit area per unit time | Percentage of undersize correctly separated |
> | Measures | **Quantity** of throughput | **Quality** of separation |
> | Unit | T/h/m² | % (dimensionless) |
> | Typical value | 5–20 T/h/m² for vibrating screens | 70–95% in well-operated screens |
> | Major influence | Screen area, bulk density, deck factor | Aperture size, vibration, moisture |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

### 🎯 Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Model Question]**
> **(a)** A double-deck vibrating screen receives 120 T/h of crushed coal. The feed contains 38% fines. The oversize (overflow from the top deck) contains 8% fines, and the combined underflow (through both decks) contains 95% fines. Calculate:
> 1. Mass of oversize stream
> 2. Mass of underflow stream
> 3. Effectiveness of the screen
> **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Given:**
- `F = 120 T/h`
- `F_f = 0.38`, `F_o = 0.08`, `F_u = 0.95`

**Step 1 — Component balance**

$$120 \times 0.38 = O \times 0.08 + U \times 0.95$$
$$45.6 = 0.08 O + 0.95 U \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 — Total mass balance**

$$O + U = 120 \implies O = 120 - U \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (ii) in (i)**

$$45.6 = 0.08(120 - U) + 0.95 U$$
$$45.6 = 9.6 - 0.08 U + 0.95 U$$
$$36.0 = 0.87 U$$
$$U = 41.38 \, \text{T/h}$$
$$O = 120 - 41.38 = 78.62 \, \text{T/h}$$

**Step 4 — Effectiveness**

$$\eta = \frac{F_u(F_f - F_o)}{F_f(F_u - F_o)} \times 100 = \frac{0.95(0.38 - 0.08)}{0.38(0.95 - 0.08)} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{0.95 \times 0.30}{0.38 \times 0.87} \times 100 = \frac{0.285}{0.3306} \times 100 = 86.21\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- Mass of oversize = **78.62 T/h**
- Mass of underflow = **41.38 T/h**
- Effectiveness = **86.21 %**

---

> **(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting screen effectiveness and capacity** in detail, with at least **eight distinct factors**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Factors affecting screen effectiveness and capacity can be grouped into three categories:**

**1. Feed Properties**
- **Particle size distribution** — a wide distribution forces the screen to handle a broad range of sizes, lowering both capacity and efficiency.
- **Moisture content** — high moisture causes particles to **stick together** (agglomerate) and to the screen surface, leading to **blinding** and a sharp drop in effectiveness.
- **Bulk density** — heavier materials load the screen surface more, reducing throughput.
- **Particle shape** — flaky or elongated particles pass through with difficulty, lowering efficiency.

**2. Screen Properties**
- **Aperture size** — directly determines the cut-point; smaller apertures give cleaner separation but lower capacity.
- **Wire diameter and open area** — thicker wires reduce the open area, lowering capacity but improving strength.
- **Type of weave (woven, perforated, wedge-wire)** — affects near-size passage probability.

**3. Operating Conditions**
- **Feed rate** — too high a rate causes overloading and blinding; too low a rate is uneconomical.
- **Vibration amplitude and frequency** — must be tuned to the particle size; under-vibration causes blinding, over-vibration causes particle ejection.
- **Angle of inclination** — typically 18°–25° for vibrating screens; affects residence time.
- **Bed depth on the screen** — must be maintained at 2–4 times the aperture for proper stratification.

> 💡 **Key engineering conclusion:** Effectiveness and capacity are **inversely related**. The KTU student should always comment on this trade-off in the conclusion.

---

### 🎯 Question Choice B (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 — Model Question]**
> **(a)** Derive the standard KTU formula for **screen effectiveness** starting from the basic mass balance. Also explain the significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Start with the basic mass balances (as derived in Section 3.1 above):**

1. Total mass balance: `F = O + U`
2. Component balance on undersize: `F·F_f = O·F_o + U·F_u`
3. Effectiveness definition: $\eta = \dfrac{U \cdot F_u}{F \cdot F_f}$

Substitute `O = F − U` into the component balance:

$$F \cdot F_f = (F - U) F_o + U \cdot F_u$$
$$F \cdot F_f - F \cdot F_o = U(F_u - F_o)$$
$$U = \frac{F(F_f - F_o)}{(F_u - F_o)}$$

Substitute U into the effectiveness definition:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{F \cdot F_f} \cdot \frac{F(F_f - F_o)}{(F_u - F_o)} \cdot F_u = \frac{F_u (F_f - F_o)}{F_f (F_u - F_o)}$$

In percentage form:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{F_u (F_f - F_o)}{F_f (F_u - F_o)} \times 100\%}$$

**Significance of each term:**

| Term | Significance |
| :--- | :--- |
| `F_f` | Target undersize content of feed — sets the upper bound on what can be separated |
| `F_o` | Undersize leakage into overflow — **lower is better** |
| `F_u` | Undersize recovery in underflow — **higher is better** |

> 💡 **Interpretation:** If `F_o → 0` (no undersize leakage into overflow) and `F_u → 1` (all undersize recovered), then η → 100% — the ideal screen.

---

> **(b)** A vibrating screen of area 3.0 m² is used to screen iron ore of bulk density 2.4 T/m³. The screen is used as the top deck. Calculate the capacity using Taggart's formula. Comment on how the capacity will change if it is used as the second deck of a double-deck arrangement. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- `A = 3.0 m²`
- `ρ_bulk = 2.4 T/m³`
- Top deck → `D = 1.0`

**Step 1 — Basic capacity**

$$B = 0.10 \times \rho_{bulk} = 0.10 \times 2.4 = 0.24 \, \text{T/h/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Taggart's formula**

$$C = 0.783 \times A \times B \times D$$
$$C = 0.783 \times 3.0 \times 0.24 \times 1.0$$
$$C = 0.5638 \, \text{T/h}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of moving to the second deck**

For the second deck, `D = 0.9`:

$$C_{2nd} = 0.783 \times 3.0 \times 0.24 \times 0.9 = 0.5074 \, \text{T/h}$$

**Conclusion:** Capacity **decreases by 10%** when used as the second deck, because the underflow from the top deck now arrives pre-screened, and the bed depth available is less, reducing the effective stratification zone.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose 2–3 marks per question:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up `F_f`, `F_o`, `F_u`** — always remember these are mass fractions of **undersize** (not oversize) in the feed, overflow, and underflow respectively.
> 2. **Forgetting the ×100 in the percentage form** — KTU rubric deducts 1 full mark for an unmultiplied decimal.
> 3. **Not verifying with the basic definition** — KTU examiners love to see a verification step ($\eta = U F_u / F F_f$ used to cross-check).
> 4. **Wrong deck factor in Taggart's formula** — top = 1.0, second = 0.9, third = 0.8. **Do not assume D = 1 for every case.**
> 5. **Skipping the "trade-off" comment in theory answers** — always end with a sentence on the inverse relationship between capacity and effectiveness.
> 6. **Unit mistakes** — bulk density in kg/m³ vs. T/m³. KTU answer keys strictly enforce SI-consistent units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> 📌 **Quick-fire revision points — read before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ Screening separates feed into **oversize (overflow)** and **undersize (underflow)** using an aperture.
- ✅ **Effectiveness (η)** = mass of undersize recovered ÷ mass of undersize in feed × 100.
- ✅ **Capacity (C)** = mass of feed handled per unit area per unit time (T/h/m²).
- ✅ The **golden KTU formula**: $\eta = \dfrac{F_u(F_f - F_o)}{F_f(F_u - F_o)} \times 100\%$
- ✅ Mass balance: `F = O + U` and `F·F_f = O·F_o + U·F_u`.
- ✅ **Taggart's capacity formula**: $C = 0.783 \times A \times B \times D$.
- ✅ Basic capacity approximation: $B = 0.10 \times \rho_{bulk}$.
- ✅ **Deck factor (D)**: top = 1.0, middle = 0.9, bottom = 0.8.
- ✅ **Open area** of screen: $\dfrac{a^2}{(a + d)^2} \times 100$ %.
- ✅ **Capacity ↑ → Effectiveness ↓** (inverse relationship).
- ✅ Main factors: feed rate, moisture, aperture size, wire diameter, vibration amplitude/frequency, angle of inclination, bulk density, particle shape.
- ✅ **Blinding** is the most common industrial problem — caused by moisture, near-size particles, and overloading.
- ✅ For multi-deck screens, each deck has its **own effectiveness and capacity**, calculated independently.
- ✅ Always **state the trade-off** between capacity and effectiveness in any descriptive answer to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Industrial Screens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Industrial Screens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Screening** is a unit operation in particle technology that involves the separation of a mixture of particles of varying sizes into two or more portions, based on the difference in particle geometry and size, by utilizing a surface (the *screen medium* or *aperture*) containing openings of a defined dimension. The fraction passing through the apertures is called the **undersize (or fines)**, while the fraction retained is called the **oversize (or tails/coarse)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Screening is the separation of a mixture of particles according to size alone, using a porous surface (screen) through which the undersize passes and on which the oversize is retained."*

The industrial device used to perform this operation is called an **Industrial Screen** (or *sifter* / *sieve* / *classifier* in older literature). Screens are characterized by their:
- **Aperture size (D₀)** — measured in **mm** or **microns (µm)**
- **Capacity (Q)** — measured in **tonnes/hour (t/h)** or **m³/h**
- **Efficiency (η)** — dimensionless **percentage (%)**
- **Cut-point / Separation size (d₅₀)** — measured in **mm**

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **kitchen flour sieve** your mother uses. She pours wheat flour mixed with bran particles onto a fine mesh. The fine flour falls through (undersize), but the bigger bran flakes stay on top (oversize). The kitchen sieve is essentially a *stationary screen* working in **batch mode**.

Now scale this concept to a cement plant or a coal washery: instead of a 20 cm sieve, you have screens 2 m wide and 6 m long, vibrating 800–1500 times per minute, processing **100–500 tonnes of crushed rock per hour**. The principle is identical — separation by size through a defined aperture — but the engineering is far more sophisticated to handle:
- Moisture & dust
- Abrasive minerals
- Near-aperture particles (the *near-mesh* problem)
- Continuous throughput demand

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Relevance:** The global market for industrial screening equipment exceeds **USD 9 billion**, with applications in mining (coal, iron ore, copper), aggregates (crushed stone, sand), food (sugar, flour, salt), pharmaceuticals (API sizing), and recycling (plastic flakes, e-waste). A single mis-selected screen in a cement plant can cause downstream ball-mill inefficiency costing lakhs of rupees in extra power consumption per month.

### 1.3 Why Screens Matter in Chemical Engineering

Unlike filtration (which separates solid from liquid) or centrifugation (which uses density), **screening is purely geometric**. It is therefore the *cheapest* and *fastest* size-separation method when the feed is **dry**, **free-flowing**, and the **cut-size is larger than ~75 µm**. Below this, screening becomes inefficient due to aperture blinding.

### 1.4 Master Classification of Industrial Screens (Roadmap for Module 1)

For KTU, screens are broadly classified by **mode of motion** as follows:

| Sl. No | Class | Typical Motion | Capacity (t/h) | Cut-Size (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stationary Screens** (Grizzlies) | None — inclined fixed bars | Very High | 50–300 |
| 2 | **Gyratory Screens** | Circular in horizontal plane | Medium | 0.5–50 |
| 3 | **Vibrating Screens** | Linear or elliptical | High | 0.1–100 |
| 4 | **Trommels (Revolving Screens)** | Rotational about axis | Medium | 1–100 |
| 5 | **Shaking Screens** | Reciprocating linear stroke | Low–Medium | 0.5–25 |
| 6 | **Disc Screens** | Rotating discs on shaft | Medium | 5–100 |
| 7 | **Probability Screens** | Multi-deck, multi-aperture | Very High | 0.5–50 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principles — The "Why" Behind Each Screen Type

#### 2.1.1 Stationary Screens (Grizzlies)
- **Construction:** A series of parallel steel bars (called *grizzly bars*) set at a fixed angle (typically 30°–45°) to the horizontal.
- **Mechanism:** Feed is discharged onto the bars by gravity; undersize falls through the gaps between bars, while oversize slides down the inclined surface.
- **Used for:** Primary scalping of large rocks in quarries and mines; aperture size ranges from **50 mm to 300 mm**.
- **Limitation:** No mechanical vibration → prone to blinding (clogging); low efficiency (~50–60%); not suitable for fine separations.

#### 2.1.2 Gyratory Screens
- **Construction:** A circular frame mounted on eccentric weights; the entire screen housing is given a circular horizontal motion.
- **Mechanism:** Particles move in a spiral path over multiple decks; stratification (small particles sinking, large floating) is excellent.
- **Advantages:** Compact, multi-deck, gentle on particles, ideal for sticky/abrasive materials like **fertilizers, salt, sugar**.
- **Capacity range:** 1–50 t/h, cut-size 0.5–50 mm.

#### 2.1.3 Vibrating Screens (Most Important for KTU)
- **Construction:** A screen cloth (woven wire or polyurethane) tensioned on a vibrating frame driven by an **eccentric shaft** or **vibrator motor**.
- **Mechanism:** The screen is subjected to high-frequency, low-amplitude vibration (typically 600–3600 RPM, 2–10 mm stroke).
- **Types of vibration:**
  - **Circular (Eccentric):** Particles move in circles; used for coarse screening.
  - **Linear (Inclined, 15°–30°):** Particles move in a straight line; used for fine dewatering.
  - **Elliptical:** Hybrid motion, combines benefits of both.
- **Industrial application:** Iron ore screening, coal preparation, sand classification. Capacity up to **1000 t/h**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Concept:** The *amplitude* of vibration must be **1.5× to 2.5× the aperture size** for effective de-blinding. If amplitude is too low, near-mesh particles blind the screen; if too high, particles are ejected before contact with the cloth.

#### 2.1.4 Trommel Screens (Revolving Screens)
- **Construction:** A cylindrical or slightly conical drum fitted with screen panels; mounted on rollers and rotated by a gearmotor.
- **Mechanism:** As the drum rotates, feed tumbles inside; undersize passes radially outward through apertures, oversize discharges from the open end.
- **Key parameters:** Trommel diameter (0.5–3 m), length-to-diameter ratio (L/D = 2–4), rotational speed (typically **critical speed = 0.5 to 0.7 × √(g/R)**).
- **Used for:** Municipal solid waste (MSW), compost screening, aggregate washing.

#### 2.1.5 Shaking Screens
- **Construction:** A rectangular screen frame connected to an eccentric drive that imparts a reciprocating motion.
- **Mechanism:** The screen is shaken back and forth; particles stratify and progress along the deck.
- **Limitation:** Lower capacity, higher wear, but excellent for **fine, dry powders** (e.g., cement, flour).

#### 2.1.6 Disc Screens
- **Construction:** A series of parallel shafts, each carrying equally spaced star-shaped discs.
- **Mechanism:** As shafts counter-rotate, the gaps between discs act as apertures; oversized material is carried over the top.
- **Used for:** Sticky/clayey materials in mining (e.g., overburden); cuts down blinding.

#### 2.1.7 Probability Screens
- **Recent innovation:** Multi-deck screen with progressively increasing aperture from top to bottom.
- **Why "probability"?** Only a small fraction of feed needs to pass through any given aperture (since multiple decks offer many chances). Result: blinding is dramatically reduced.
- **Used for:** Difficult-to-screen materials like wet coal, coking coal, sinter.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Memorization Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** KTU examiners almost always test these formulas. Memorize units, not just symbols.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Screen Surface Area (Basic Capacity Equation) | $A = 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{Q}{\rho_b \cdot d \cdot s}$ | $A$ = screen area (m²), $Q$ = capacity (t/h), $\rho_b$ = bulk density (t/m³), $d$ = half-size of aperture (mm), $s$ = slot factor (0.6–0.8) | Used for vibrating screen sizing; $d = D_p/2$ |
| 2 | Screen Efficiency (Recovery Method) | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{us}}{m_{uf}} \times 100$ | $m_{us}$ = mass of undersize in screen underflow (passed product), $m_{uf}$ = mass of undersize in feed | Most direct efficiency definition |
| 3 | Critical Particle Aperture Relation | $D_{50} = 0.5 \cdot D_0$ | $D_{50}$ = cut size, $D_0$ = screen aperture | For a square aperture and spherical particle |
| 4 | Trommel Critical Speed | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$ | $N_c$ = critical speed (rev/s), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $R$ = drum radius (m) | Operating speed = 0.5 to 0.7 × $N_c$ |
| 5 | Grizzly Bar Spacing Aperture | $a = s - w$ | $a$ = clear aperture, $s$ = center-to-center spacing, $w$ = bar width | For grizzly design |
| 6 | Throughput (Mass) | $Q = \dot{m} = \rho_b \cdot A \cdot v$ | $v$ = linear velocity of material (m/s) | For inclined screens |
| 7 | Screen Effectiveness (Sands-Grade Index) | $E = \dfrac{\text{Actual undersize recovered}}{\text{Total undersize in feed}} - \dfrac{\text{Undersize in oversize product}}{\text{Undersize in feed}}$ | Dimensionless | McCabe/Harriott formulation |
| 8 | Surface Moisture Correction (Wet Screening) | $Q_{wet} = Q_{dry} \cdot (1 - M)$ | $M$ = moisture fraction (decimal) | Capacity drops with moisture |
| 9 | Stratification Velocity (Vibrating Screen) | $v_s = \omega \cdot A_v$ | $\omega$ = angular frequency (rad/s), $A_v$ = amplitude (m) | For circular vibratory motion |
| 10 | Probability of Passage (Single Layer) | $P = \left(\dfrac{a}{D_p}\right)^2$ for square weave | $a$ = aperture, $D_p$ = particle diameter | Geometric probability model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on LaTeX in Tables:** I have used `\cdot` for multiplication and `\dfrac` for fractions to prevent markdown table breaks. Never use `|` in table cells (use `\vert` instead).

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why You Will Use This on Day 1 of Your Plant Job

1. **Pre-Crusher Scalping:** Grizzlies remove fines before primary crushing, reducing crusher wear and energy by **15–25%**.
2. **Classification in Closed-Circuit Grinding:** Vibrating screens separate ball-mill discharge into oversize (recycled) and undersize (finished product), forming a *closed circuit* that improves mill throughput.
3. **De-bottlenecking in Coal Washeries:** Probability screens handle **wet, near-aperture coal** with up to **2× the capacity** of conventional vibrating screens.
4. **Quality Control in Aggregates:** Aggregate producers must supply **graded sand** (e.g., Zone-II sand for concrete). Screens are the only practical way to achieve this consistently.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Screen Capacity Equation

This is a **KTU favourite 14-mark derivation**. The starting point is the empirical observation that throughput is proportional to:
- Screen area $A$
- Bulk density $\rho_b$
- A characteristic dimension (the half-aperture $d$)
- An empirical slot factor $s$

#### Step 1: Define the basic proportionality
The mass throughput $Q$ (in t/h) passing through a screen of area $A$ is observed experimentally to obey:

$$Q \propto A \cdot \rho_b \cdot d \cdot s$$

#### Step 2: Convert proportionality to an equation
The empirically determined constant of proportionality from plant data is **0.4** (in the chosen SI-consistent unit system: $A$ in m², $\rho_b$ in t/m³, $d$ in mm):

$$Q = 2.5 \cdot A \cdot \rho_b \cdot d \cdot s$$

> **Conversion logic:** The constant 0.4 in the *alternative form* arises from unit normalization. We will use the *capacity form* in Step 4.

#### Step 3: Rearrange to solve for screen area
Rearranging for $A$ gives the **Sizing Equation** that KTU examiners expect you to derive:

$$A = \frac{Q}{2.5 \cdot \rho_b \cdot d \cdot s}$$

This is the equation used to **size** a vibrating screen given the desired capacity.

#### Step 4: Alternative form (the one in most textbooks)
Re-writing with the constant 0.4 (when $Q$ is in t/h, $\rho_b$ in t/m³, $d$ in mm):

$$A = 0.4 \cdot \frac{Q}{\rho_b \cdot d \cdot s}$$

The two forms are dimensionally equivalent under the SI assumption that 1 tonne = 1 m³ for water-equivalent bulk densities.

#### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example
**Question:** A vibrating screen must process $Q = 100$ t/h of crushed limestone with bulk density $\rho_b = 1.6$ t/m³. The screen half-aperture is $d = 5$ mm. Take slot factor $s = 0.7$. Calculate the required screen area.

**Solution:**

$$A = 0.4 \cdot \frac{100}{1.6 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.7}$$

$$A = 0.4 \cdot \frac{100}{5.6}$$

$$A = 0.4 \cdot 17.857$$

$$\boxed{A = 7.14 \text{ m}^2}$$

So, a screen of dimensions approximately **1.5 m × 4.8 m** would suffice.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Screen Efficiency by Material Balance

This is another **KTU 14-mark hot topic** — often appears as a numerical problem.

#### Step 1: Define the streams
Let:
- $F$ = mass flow rate of **feed**
- $O$ = mass flow rate of **oversize** (rejects)
- $U$ = mass flow rate of **undersize** (passes)
- $x_{uF}$ = mass fraction of undersize in feed
- $x_{uO}$ = mass fraction of undersize in oversize
- $x_{uU}$ = mass fraction of undersize in undersize (= 1 ideally)

#### Step 2: Overall mass balance
$$F = O + U$$

#### Step 3: Component mass balance (for the undersize fraction)
$$F \cdot x_{uF} = O \cdot x_{uO} + U \cdot x_{uU}$$

#### Step 4: Define screen efficiency
Efficiency is the **ratio of the actual mass of undersize that reports to the correct stream** (underflow) to the **maximum possible** (i.e., what was in the feed):

$$\eta = \frac{U \cdot x_{uU}}{F \cdot x_{uF}} = \frac{U \cdot 1}{F \cdot x_{uF}} = \frac{U}{F \cdot x_{uF}}$$

(assuming $x_{uU} = 1$ in the ideal case where all undersize material passes through).

#### Step 5: Substitute from overall mass balance
From Step 2: $U = F - O$. Substituting:

$$\eta = \frac{F - O}{F \cdot x_{uF}} = 1 - \frac{O}{F \cdot x_{uF}}$$

#### Step 6: Eliminate $O$ using oversize composition
Define $x_{oO}$ = mass fraction of oversize in oversize stream = $1 - x_{uO}$. Then $O \cdot x_{oO}$ = total oversize mass recovered. We can also express:

$$O = \frac{F \cdot x_{oF}}{x_{oO}}$$

where $x_{oF} = 1 - x_{uF}$.

#### Step 7: Final form
The most exam-friendly form (using only feed and oversize composition):

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{x_{uF} - x_{uO} \cdot (1 - x_{uF})}{x_{uF} \cdot (1 - x_{uO} - x_{uO} \cdot (1 - x_{uF}))} \times 100}$$

But KTU usually accepts the **simpler** form:

$$\eta = \frac{x_{uU} \cdot U}{x_{uF} \cdot F} \times 100 = \frac{\text{Mass of undersize recovered in underflow}}{\text{Mass of undersize in feed}} \times 100$$

#### Step 8: Worked Example
**Question:** A screen receives 200 t/h of feed containing 35% undersize (by mass). The oversize product contains 5% undersize contamination, and the undersize product contains 2% oversize contamination. Calculate the screen efficiency.

**Solution:**

Step (a): Overall balance: $F = 200$ t/h = $O + U$
Step (b): Undersize mass in feed = $0.35 \times 200 = 70$ t/h
Step (c): Let $U$ = undersize flow. Mass balance: $200 \cdot 0.35 = O \cdot 0.05 + U \cdot 0.98$
Step (d): $70 = 0.05(200 - U) + 0.98U$
Step (e): $70 = 10 - 0.05U + 0.98U = 10 + 0.93U$
Step (f): $0.93U = 60 \Rightarrow U = 64.52$ t/h
Step (g): $\eta = \dfrac{0.98 \cdot 64.52}{0.35 \cdot 200} = \dfrac{63.23}{70} = 0.903$
Step (h): $\eta = 90.3\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Trick:** The 5% undersize in oversize is called **"carryover" or "misplaced material"** — this is what *reduces* efficiency. Always check this first.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Screen

This matrix is directly **exam-relevant** for design-type questions.

| Feed Characteristic | Best Screen Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Coarse (50–300 mm), high tonnage, dry** | Grizzly (stationary) | No moving parts, rugged, cheap |
| **Sticky / clayey, 5–50 mm** | Disc screen or Probability screen | Self-cleaning discs; multiple decks reduce blinding |
| **Fine powder (< 5 mm), dry, food grade** | Gyratory screen | Gentle motion, multi-deck, low dust |
| **Wet coal / iron ore, 0.5–25 mm** | Probability screen or Linear vibrating | Probability screens handle moisture; linear vibrating dewater |
| **MSW / compost, 5–100 mm, variable** | Trommel | Robust against oversize junk, low maintenance |
| **High capacity, 0.1–100 mm, dry** | Inclined vibrating screen | Workhorse of industry; 70% of all installations |
| **Cut-size < 0.5 mm** | **Do not screen** — use classifier (hydraulic or air) | Screening becomes uneconomical; use air classification instead |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Trommel Critical Speed Derivation

This derivation is asked in **Part B 14-mark** questions frequently.

#### Step 1: Set up the system
A particle sitting on the inner wall of a rotating drum of radius $R$ is held by:
- **Centripetal force** = $m \omega^2 R$ (toward center)
- **Gravitational force** = $m g$ (downward)

#### Step 2: Define the *critical* condition
The drum is rotating at speed $N$ (rev/s). The particle is just about to leave the wall when the centripetal force requirement equals the gravitational component at the top of the drum.

At the **top of the drum**, gravity is directed **inward** (toward center), so the condition for the particle to be in contact is:

$$m g \geq m \omega_c^2 R$$

The critical condition (particle just lifts off) is when equality holds:

$$g = \omega_c^2 R$$

#### Step 3: Solve for angular velocity
$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$$

#### Step 4: Convert angular velocity to RPM
Since $\omega = 2\pi N$ (where $N$ is in rev/s):

$$2\pi N_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$$

$$N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$$

#### Step 5: Operating speed rule
Trommels operate at **50–70% of critical** to ensure particles *cascade* and *tumble* (not centrifuge):

$$N_{op} = 0.5 \text{ to } 0.7 \times N_c$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Below 0.5 $N_c$:** Particles simply slide — no screening action.
> **Above 0.7 $N_c$:** Particles centrifuge against the wall — no tumbling, no screening.
> **At exactly $N_c$:** Particle is weightless at top — the drum behaves like a centrifuge.

#### Step 6: Numerical Example
For a trommel of radius $R = 1$ m:
- $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{9.81/1} = \dfrac{3.132}{6.283} = 0.498$ rev/s = 29.9 RPM
- Operating speed: $N_{op} = 0.6 \times 29.9 = 17.9$ RPM
