> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical preset withdrawal times** for $h = 0.10 \; \text{m}$: $t = 60, \; 120, \; 240, \; 480, \; 960, \; 1920, \; 3840 \; \text{s}$. Each successive sample probes a **smaller** Stokes diameter $D_p$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Stokes' Law and write the expression for the equivalent Stokes diameter of a particle settling under gravity in a viscous fluid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stokes' Law gives the **terminal settling velocity** of a spherical particle falling under gravity in the laminar regime ($Re_p < 0.2$):

$$v_t = \frac{g \, D_p^{\,2} \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$$

For a particle falling through height $h$ in time $t$, the equivalent Stokes diameter is:

$$D_p = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, h}{g \, (\rho_s - \rho_f) \, t}}$$

> *(1 mark — statement of Stokes' Law with condition; 1 mark — velocity form; 1 mark — Stokes diameter form.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three assumptions of the Andreasen pipette method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. The suspension is **initially homogeneous** (uniform dispersion of solids throughout).
2. Particles settle **independently** (no hindered settling) — therefore concentration must be low ($\le 1\% \; v/v$).
3. Settling is in the **laminar regime** ($Re_p \le 0.2$) — hence Stokes' law applies.

*(Other acceptable assumptions: spherical rigid particles, no flocculation, no convection currents, known solid density, known fluid viscosity.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Q.A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Stokes diameter $D_p$ of a particle settling under gravity through a viscous fluid from the fundamental force balance on the particle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle, procedure, and analysis steps of the **Andreasen pipette method** for determining the particle size distribution of fine powders. State the conditions under which the method is valid. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Consider a spherical particle of diameter $D_p$ and density $\rho_s$ settling at terminal velocity $v_t$ through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$.

Forces acting on the particle:
- Weight (downward): $F_w = \dfrac{\pi}{6} D_p^{\,3} \rho_s \, g$
- Buoyancy (upward): $F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6} D_p^{\,3} \rho_f \, g$
- Viscous drag (Stokes' drag, upward): $F_d = 3 \pi \mu D_p \, v_t$

At terminal velocity, net force = 0:

$$F_w - F_b = F_d$$

$$\frac{\pi}{6} D_p^{\,3} (\rho_s - \rho_f) \, g = 3 \pi \mu D_p \, v_t$$

Simplify (divide both sides by $\pi D_p$):

$$\frac{1}{6} D_p^{\,2} (\rho_s - \rho_f) g = 3 \mu v_t$$

$$\boxed{v_t = \frac{D_p^{\,2} \, g \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}}$$

For a particle that has fallen through depth $h$ in time $t$: $v_t = h/t$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{D_p = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu h}{g (\rho_s - \rho_f) t}}}$$

Valid for **laminar regime**, i.e., $Re_p = \dfrac{D_p v_t \rho_f}{\mu} \le 0.2$.

*(1 mark – identifying forces; 1 mark – force balance equation; 2 marks – derivation to $v_t$; 2 marks – substitution of $v_t = h/t$ and solving for $D_p$; 1 mark – Stokes regime condition.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Principle:**
The method is based on **Stokes' Law of sedimentation**. A homogeneous suspension of the fine powder in a liquid (usually water + dispersant) is allowed to settle in a graduated cylindrical tube. At predetermined times, a fixed volume of suspension is withdrawn through a side-arm pipette at a fixed depth $h$ below the surface. By measuring the solids concentration in each withdrawn sample, the cumulative mass fraction of particles finer than the corresponding Stokes diameter is computed.

**Apparatus:**
- Andreasen sedimentation cylinder (500–550 mL) with side-arm pipette (10 mL).
- Stopcock on the pipette.
- Sampling depth $h$ — typically **0.10 m**.
- Stopwatch, oven (105–110 °C), analytical balance (0.0001 g), dispersant.

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh accurately the dry powder mass $M_{\text{dry}}$.
2. Disperse in water with a small quantity (~ 0.5 g/L) of **sodium hexametaphosphate** as dispersant.
3. Transfer to the Andreasen tube, make up to the graduation mark $V_{\text{total}}$.
4. Agitate by inversion for ~ 5 minutes to ensure uniform suspension.
5. Start stopwatch immediately; keep tube vibration-free at constant temperature.
6. At preset times $t_1 < t_2 < \ldots$, withdraw 10 mL samples through the side-arm pipette (after discarding 5 mL flush).
7. Dry each sample to constant mass; record $m_{p,t}$.
8. For each sample, compute:
   - Stokes diameter: $D_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu h}{g (\rho_s - \rho_f) t}}$
   - Cumulative % finer: $\% F = \dfrac{C_t}{C_0} \times 100$ where $C_0 = M_{\text{dry}} / V_{\text{total}}$ and $C_t = m_{p,t} / V_p$.
9. Plot cumulative % finer vs $D_p$ on semi-log paper.

**Validity Conditions:**
- $Re_p \le 0.2$ (laminar regime).
- Concentration $\le 1\% \; v/v$ (no hindered settling).
- No flocculation; no convection.
- Particle density $\rho_s$ accurately known.

*(1 mark – principle & Stokes' law; 2 marks – apparatus; 2 marks – procedure; 1 mark – calculation steps; 1 mark – validity conditions.)*

---

### Q.B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain, with neat derivations, how the **cumulative mass percent finer** corresponding to a particular Stokes diameter is obtained from the Andreasen pipette experiment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mineral sample ($\rho_s = 2800 \; \text{kg/m}^3$) is dispersed in water at 25 °C ($\rho_f = 997 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa·s}$) in an Andreasen pipette with sampling depth $h = 0.20 \; \text{m}$ and pipette volume $V_p = 10 \; \text{mL}$. The total dry mass of solids is $M_{\text{dry}} = 4.0 \; \text{g}$ dispersed in a total volume $V_{\text{total}} = 500 \; \text{mL}$. After a settling time of $t = 1800 \; \text{s}$, the dry mass of solids in the pipette sample is $m_{p,t} = 0.028 \; \text{g}$. Determine: **(i)** the Stokes diameter $D_p$ at this time; **(ii)** the cumulative percent of particles finer than $D_p$; **(iii)** verify whether the settling is in the Stokes regime. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Let the original (uniformly dispersed) suspension have dry solids mass $M_{\text{dry}}$ in total volume $V_{\text{total}}$.

**Step 1 — Initial concentration:**

$$C_0 = \frac{M_{\text{dry}}}{V_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 2 — After settling for time $t$ at depth $h$:** All particles of Stokes diameter $> D_p$ have settled below the sampling plane. The pipette withdraws a fixed volume $V_p$, and the dry mass of solids in that pipette sample is $m_{p,t}$.

**Step 3 — Concentration in the pipette sample:**

$$C_t = \frac{m_{p,t}}{V_p}$$

**Step 4 — Relation between $C_t$ and cumulative fraction:** Because the suspension was initially uniform, the **mass of fines per unit volume of clear suspension** at depth $h$ is the same everywhere at $t = 0$. Therefore the cumulative mass fraction of fines is:

$$X(D_p) = \frac{m_{p,t} / V_p}{M_{\text{dry}} / V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{m_{p,t} \cdot V_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{dry}} \cdot V_p}$$

**Step 5 — Cumulative percent finer:**

$$\boxed{\% \, F(D_p) = \frac{m_{p,t} \cdot V_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{dry}} \cdot V_p} \times 100 = \frac{C_t}{C_0} \times 100}$$

**Step 6 — Stokes diameter corresponding to this $t$:**

$$\boxed{D_p = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, h}{g \, (\rho_s - \rho_f) \, t}}}$$

Each successive withdrawal time $t$ yields a corresponding $D_p$ and $\% F(D_p)$. The complete size distribution is plotted as cumulative % finer versus $D_p$ on a semi-log plot.

*(1 mark – defining $C_0$; 1 mark – defining $C_t$; 2 marks – derivation of cumulative percent relation; 2 marks – linking to Stokes diameter; 1 mark – semi-log plot mention.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $\rho_s = 2800 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 997 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\Delta\rho = 2800 - 997 = 1803 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa·s}$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- $h = 0.20 \; \text{m}$, $t = 1800 \; \text{s}$
- $M_{\text{dry}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg}$, $V_{\text{total}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3$
- $V_p = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^3$, $m_{p,t} = 2.8 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{kg}$

**(i) Stokes diameter:**

$$D_p = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20}{9.81 \times 1803 \times 1800}}$$

Numerator:

$$18 \times 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20 = 3.204 \times 10^{-3}$$

Denominator:

$$9.81 \times 1803 \times 1800 = 31{,}834{,}734$$

$$\frac{3.204 \times 10^{-3}}{3.1834 \times 10^{7}} = 1.0065 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$D_p = \sqrt{1.0065 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.003 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{D_p \approx 10.03 \; \mu\text{m}}$$

**(2 marks for the computation.)**

**(ii) Cumulative percent finer:**

$$C_0 = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 8.0 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$C_t = \frac{2.8 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.8 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\% F = \frac{2.8}{8.0} \times 100 = 35 \;\%$$

$$\boxed{\% \, F(D_p) = 35 \;\% \; \text{finer than } 10.03 \; \mu\text{m}}$$

**(3 marks for the computation.)**

**(iii) Stokes regime verification:**

$$v_t = \frac{h}{t} = \frac{0.20}{1800} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m/s}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{D_p \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\mu} = \frac{1.003 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \times 997}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{1.111 \times 10^{-6}}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{Re_p \approx 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \ll 0.2 \;\; \text{✓ Stokes regime is valid.}}$$

**(2 marks for the verification.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting the volume correction** $V_{\text{total}} / V_p$ when calculating cumulative % finer — loses 2 marks easily.
> 2. **Not verifying the Stokes regime** ($Re_p \le 0.2$) at the end of every numerical — examiners explicitly check this; missing it = **–2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing units**: $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu$ in $\text{Pa·s} = \text{kg/(m·s)}$, $h$ in m, $t$ in s, $g$ in $\text{m/s}^2$. Inconsistent units = **entire answer invalidated**.
> 4. **Confusing Stokes diameter with sieve diameter** — they are fundamentally different concepts; pipette analysis reports **Stokes diameter only**.
> 5. **Not stating the dispersant** (sodium hexametaphosphate) in the procedure — losing 1 mark in 7-mark procedure questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Pipette Analysis (Andreasen method)** = sedimentation technique for sub-75 µm fines.
- **Governed by Stokes' Law**: $v_t = \dfrac{g D_p^{\,2} (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$ valid for **laminar regime** ($Re_p \le 0.2$).
- **Stokes diameter formula** (HIGH-YIELD): $D_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu h}{g (\rho_s - \rho_f) t}}$.
- **Sampling depth** $h$ is fixed (typically 0.10 m); **time $t$ increases** to probe **smaller $D_p$**.
- **Cumulative % finer** = $\dfrac{C_t}{C_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{m_{p,t} \, V_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{dry}} \, V_p} \times 100$.
- **Initial concentration** $C_0 = M_{\text{dry}} / V_{\text{total}}$; **instantaneous** $C_t = m_{p,t} / V_p$.
- **Assumptions**: uniform initial suspension, no hindered settling ($\le 1\% \; v/v$), no flocculation, no convection, spherical particles.
- **Dispersant** (sodium hexametaphosphate, ~ 0.5 g/L) is added to prevent flocculation.
- **Sample withdrawal volume** $V_p = 10 \; \text{mL}$ (after discarding ~ 5 mL flush).
- **Practical lower limit** of measurable $D_p \approx 1 \; \mu\text{m}$ (Brownian motion dominates below).
- **Plot**: cumulative % finer (y-axis, linear) vs $D_p$ (x-axis, log scale) on **semi-log paper**.
- **Hindered settling correction** (Richardson–Zaki) becomes important for solids $> 1\% \; v/v$.
- **Equivalent Stokes diameter** ≠ geometric diameter for irregular particles — it is the diameter of a sphere of the same density with the same $v_t$.
- **Always verify** $Re_p \le 0.2$ at the end of any numerical; otherwise the result is invalid under Stokes' Law.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beaker Decantation and Elutriation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Beaker Decantation and Elutriation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Beaker Decantation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beaker Decantation** is a *batch*, *gravity-based sedimentation* technique used in mineral processing and particle technology to separate a coarse (settled) solid fraction from a fine (suspended) fraction. The slurry is allowed to stand undisturbed in a **graduated beaker** until the coarse particles settle to the bottom, after which the supernatant liquid containing the unsettled fines is **carefully poured off** (decanted) without disturbing the sediment. The operation is repeated several times by re-suspending the residue in fresh liquid to improve the grade of separation.

### 1.2 Elutriation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elutriation** is a *continuous*, *hydraulic / pneumatic classification* operation in which a fluid (water, air, or any other carrying medium) flows **upward** through a vertical column against the action of gravity acting on suspended particles. The fluid velocity is so chosen that **particles whose terminal (free-settling) velocity is lower than the upward fluid velocity are carried away as overflow (fines)**, while **particles whose terminal velocity is higher than the fluid velocity settle downward and are removed as underflow (coarse fraction)**. The fluid velocity that just supports a particle in suspension (zero net force) is the **classification velocity** or **cut velocity**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Beaker Decantation like rinsing rice at home.** You pour water and rice into a bowl, swirl it, and let it rest. The unwanted light husk / dust floats or stays in suspension, while the heavy grains sink. You gently pour out the cloudy water — that cloudy water is the *decant*. You repeat the washing. **The principle is identical to that used in the laboratory, only scaled up and standardized.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Elutriation is like a vertical 'particle sorting elevator.'** Imagine a tall glass tube with water rising slowly from the bottom. Tiny sand particles, like balloons in an updraft, cannot resist the upward flow and are flushed out the top. Bigger pebbles, too heavy for the gentle current, sink and accumulate at the bottom. **By precisely controlling the upward water velocity, you can decide EXACTLY which particle size gets washed out and which stays behind.** This is the heart of elutriation — *velocity as a sorting dial*.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Constants and Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $\rho_p$ | Density of solid particle | 1000 – 25000 kg/m³ |
| $\rho_f$ | Density of fluid (water at 25 °C) | **997 kg/m³** |
| $\mu_w$ | Viscosity of water (25 °C) | **8.9 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s** |
| $\mu_{air}$ | Viscosity of air (25 °C) | **1.85 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** |
| $d_p$ | Particle diameter | 1 µm – 10 mm |
| $u_t$ | Terminal settling velocity | m/s |

### 1.5 Engineering Significance in the Chemical Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Beaker decantation is the **qualitative laboratory analogue** of large-scale industrial *thickening* and *clarification* operations. Elutriation, on the other hand, is the **fundamental principle behind hydraulic classifiers, elutriator legs, fluidized-bed separators, and hydrocyclones**. A complete grasp of these two topics is therefore essential for sizing industrial separators.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Beaker Decantation — Underlying Theory

When a slurry stands in a beaker, the suspended particles experience three forces:

1. **Weight of the particle** acting downward: $W = m_p \, g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \, \rho_p \, g$
2. **Buoyancy** acting upward: $F_B = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \, \rho_f \, g$
3. **Drag force** opposing motion (Stokes regime): $F_D = 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, u$

**Net driving force** (effective gravitational force) on the particle:

$$F_{net} = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g$$

**Physical reason for separation:** Particles with **larger $d_p$** and **higher $\rho_p - \rho_f$** settle **faster**. After a fixed waiting time $t$, only particles whose settling distance $u_t \, t$ is less than the liquid depth $H$ remain in suspension. By pouring off this top layer (decant), the **fines are removed preferentially**.

#### 2.1.1 Step-by-step Procedural Logic (KTU Board Examiner's Lens)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Step 1 — Suspension preparation:** The solid–liquid mixture is poured into a tall-form graduated beaker and stirred to obtain a homogeneous suspension.
> - **Step 2 — Quiescent settling:** The beaker is left undisturbed on a vibration-free surface for a calculated time $t$ such that the **smallest particle intended for the coarse fraction** has settled at least the full depth $H$ of the beaker.
> - **Step 3 — Decanting:** A glass rod is held against the lip and the supernatant liquid is poured into a second beaker without disturbing the sediment.
> - **Step 4 — Re-suspension:** Fresh liquid is added to the sediment, the contents are agitated, and steps 2–3 are repeated. Typically **3 to 5 washings** are carried out.
> - **Step 5 — Mass balance:** The mass of coarse and fine fractions is determined after drying, and a separation efficiency / recovery is calculated.

#### 2.1.2 Recovery (η) of the Coarse Fraction

If $M_c$ is the mass recovered in the coarse fraction and $M_0$ is the initial mass of solids whose size is greater than the cut size:

$$\eta_{coarse} = \dfrac{M_c}{M_0} \times 100 \, \%$$

### 2.2 Elutriation — Underlying Theory

In an elutriator, the particle is acted upon by:

1. **Downward gravity:** $F_g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g$
2. **Upward drag due to fluid motion:** $F_D = f(u) \cdot d_p^{\,m} \cdot u^{\,n}$

At **steady state**, these forces balance, and the particle is held in suspension or moves with constant velocity. The fluid velocity at which this balance occurs is the **terminal velocity $u_t$**, which becomes the **classification (cut) velocity $u_c$** of the elutriator.

**Selection rule:**
- If $u_f > u_t$ → particle is entrained upward (overflow / fines).
- If $u_f < u_t$ → particle settles downward (underflow / coarse).

#### 2.2.1 Types of Elutriators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Hydraulic elutriator** — uses water as the fluid. Used for fine particles (down to ~ 10 µm).
> 2. **Pneumatic elutriator** — uses air. Used for dry powders, dust analysis, cement, fly-ash classification.
> 3. **Multi-stage elutriator (Batel, Andrews, Roller classifiers)** — a vertical column divided into sections of successively **increasing diameter**, hence **decreasing upward velocity**. This yields multiple size cuts in a single apparatus.
> 4. **Counter-current elutriator** — particles move downward, fluid moves upward.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Expression | Conditions / Regime | Units |
|---|------------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | $u_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}{18 \, \mu}$ | **Stokes' Law**: $Re_p < 0.2$ (laminar) | m/s |
| 2 | $u_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}{\rho_f}}$ | **Newton's Law**: $Re_p > 1000$ (turbulent) | m/s |
| 3 | $u_t = \dfrac{0.153 \, d_p^{1.14} \, g^{0.71} (\rho_p - \rho_f)^{0.71}}{\rho_f^{0.29} \, \mu^{0.43}}$ | **Intermediate regime** (general) | m/s |
| 4 | $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p \, u_t \, \rho_f}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_D = \dfrac{4 \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}{3 \, \rho_f \, u_t^2}$ | Drag coefficient definition | dimensionless |
| 6 | $u_c = \dfrac{V_{fluid}}{A_{column}}$ | Cut velocity from volumetric flow | m/s |
| 7 | $d_{p,\,cut} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \, \mu \, u_f}{(\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}}$ | Stokes inversion (size from velocity) | m |
| 8 | $\eta_{coarse} = \dfrac{M_c}{M_{0,\,cut}} \times 100$ | Decant recovery | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** The particle Reynolds number $Re_p$ is the **master discriminator**. KTU questions almost always ask: *given a particle size and density, find the regime, then pick the right formula.* Memorize the boundaries — **$Re_p < 0.2$ → Stokes**, **$0.2 < Re_p < 1000$ → intermediate**, **$Re_p > 1000$ → Newton**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Mining & Mineral Dressing:** Hydraulic classification of ore, silica sand, and coal.
> - **Cement Industry:** Pneumatic elutriators separate cement dust from kiln exhaust.
> - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Decantation is used in washing of API crystals during purification.
> - **Wastewater Treatment:** Primary sedimentation tanks function on the same physics as beaker decantation, scaled up by a factor of ~10⁶ in volume.
> - **Powder Metallurgy & Ceramics:** Air elutriation gives narrow size distributions for raw powders before pressing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Law (Terminal Velocity in Laminar Regime)

We start from a force balance on a single spherical particle falling under gravity through a viscous fluid at steady state.

**Step 1 — Identify the three forces acting on the sphere.**

The forces are: weight $F_g$ downward, buoyancy $F_B$ upward, and drag $F_D$ opposing motion (so upward, since the particle falls down).

**Step 2 — Write each force explicitly.**

$$F_g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d_p^{\,3} \, \rho_p \, g$$

$$F_B = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d_p^{\,3} \, \rho_f \, g$$

$$F_D = 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, u_t$$

**Step 3 — Apply steady-state force balance** (net force = 0 at terminal velocity).

$$F_g - F_B - F_D = 0$$

Substituting:

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d_p^{\,3} \, \rho_p \, g \; - \; \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d_p^{\,3} \, \rho_f \, g \; - \; 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, u_t \; = \; 0$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the terms with $u_t$.**

Rearrange so that all terms not containing $u_t$ sit on one side:

$$3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, u_t \; = \; \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d_p^{\,3} \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d_p$.**

$$u_t \; = \; \dfrac{\pi \, d_p^{\,3} \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}{6 \times 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d_p}$$

The $\pi$ cancels, the $d_p^3$ becomes $d_p^2$, and $6 \times 3 = 18$:

$$\boxed{\, u_t \; = \; \dfrac{d_p^{\,2} \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}{18 \, \mu} \,}$$

**Step 6 — State the condition of validity.**

Stokes' law assumes *creeping flow*, which requires:

$$Re_p \; = \; \dfrac{d_p \, u_t \, \rho_f}{\mu} \; < \; 0.2$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cut-Size Equation for an Elutriator

For an elutriator, the fluid velocity $u_f$ is set such that particles of size $d_{p,\,cut}$ are exactly at equilibrium. In the laminar regime, replace $u_t$ in Stokes' law with $u_f$:

$$u_f \; = \; \dfrac{d_{p,\,cut}^{\,2} \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}{18 \, \mu}$$

Solving for the cut diameter:

$$d_{p,\,cut}^{\,2} \; = \; \dfrac{18 \, \mu \, u_f}{(\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}$$

$$\boxed{\, d_{p,\,cut} \; = \; \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \, \mu \, u_f}{(\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}} \,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Higher fluid viscosity, higher fluid velocity, and lower density difference **all increase the cut size**. Conversely, increasing $g$ or density difference **decreases the cut size**, allowing finer particles to settle out.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Style — 7 Mark)

**Problem:** A spherical quartz particle ($\rho_p = 2650 \; kg/m^3$, $d_p = 60 \; \mu m$) settles in water at 25 °C ($\rho_f = 997 \; kg/m^3$, $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \; Pa \cdot s$). Determine (a) the terminal settling velocity and (b) the height fallen in 90 s. Assume Stokes' regime.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Stokes' law** (we are told to assume Stokes' regime; we will verify at the end).

$$u_t \; = \; \dfrac{d_p^{\,2} \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}{18 \, \mu}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.** Convert $d_p$ to meters: $d_p = 60 \times 10^{-6} \; m$.

$$u_t \; = \; \dfrac{(60 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (2650 - 997) \times 9.81}{18 \times 8.9 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Numerator step by step:
- $(60 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 3600 \times 10^{-12} = 3.6 \times 10^{-9} \; m^2$
- $2650 - 997 = 1653 \; kg/m^3$
- $3.6 \times 10^{-9} \times 1653 \times 9.81 = 5.838 \times 10^{-5}$

Denominator:
- $18 \times 8.9 \times 10^{-4} = 1.602 \times 10^{-2}$

Final value:
$$u_t \; = \; \dfrac{5.838 \times 10^{-5}}{1.602 \times 10^{-2}} \; = \; 3.645 \times 10^{-3} \; m/s \; \approx \; 3.65 \; mm/s$$

**Step 3 — Verify Stokes regime.**

$$Re_p \; = \; \dfrac{d_p \, u_t \, \rho_f}{\mu} \; = \; \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.645 \times 10^{-3} \times 997}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$Re_p \; = \; \dfrac{2.18 \times 10^{-4}}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} \; \approx \; 0.245$$

> [!WARNING]
> $Re_p \approx 0.245$ is **slightly above the Stokes limit of 0.2**. In a KTU answer, state that the assumption is **approximately valid** and the result is a close engineering estimate. The intermediate-regime formula would give a more accurate value.

**Step 4 — Distance fallen in 90 s.**

$$H \; = \; u_t \times t \; = \; 3.645 \times 10^{-3} \times 90 \; = \; 0.328 \; m \; \approx \; 32.8 \; cm$$

**Conclusion:** The quartz particle settles about 33 cm in 90 s, and Stokes' regime is approximately valid.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Beaker Decantation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Slurry Preparation: Mix solid and liquid in beaker] --> B[Agitation to ensure uniform suspension]
    B --> C[Quiescent Settling Period: Allow gravity separation]
    C --> D{Coarse sediment has formed at bottom?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Position glass rod at beaker lip]
    E --> F[Decant supernatant liquid carefully into second beaker]
    F --> G[Collect fines suspension for analysis]
    C --> H[Coarse sediment remains in first beaker]
    H --> I[Add fresh liquid and re-suspend sediment]
    I --> J{Number of washings completed?}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Filter, dry and weigh coarse residue]
    K --> L[Compute recovery and grade of separation]
    F --> M[Compute recovery and grade of fines]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow inside a Multi-Stage Elutriator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Fresh Feed: Mixed particle slurry enters at top] --> R[Stage 1 Narrow section: Highest upward velocity u1]
    R --> S[Particles with ut less than u1 carried upward as OVERFLOW FINES]
    S --> T[Stage 2 Wider section: Lower upward velocity u2 less than u1]
    T --> U[Medium sized particles settle out here]
    U --> V[Stage 3 Even wider section: Even lower velocity u3]
    V --> W[Coarser particles settle out here]
    W --> X[Final underflow: Largest particles exit at bottom]
    Y[Fluid inlet at bottom with controlled flow rate Q] --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** A multi-stage elutriator is built so that the **upward fluid velocity DECREASES as one moves downward through the column** (achieved by increasing cross-sectional area). Each 'stage' therefore traps a specific size band of particles, giving **multi-cut classification in a single column**.

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Decantation vs Elutriation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEC[Beaker Decantation BATCH mode]
        D1[Slurry in beaker] --> D2[Wait for settling]
        D2 --> D3[Pour off supernatant]
        D3 --> D4[Re-suspend and repeat]
    end
    subgraph ELU[Elutriation CONTINUOUS mode]
        E1[Fluid enters bottom] --> E2[Fluid flows upward at velocity u_f]
        E2 --> E3{Fines ut less than u_f}
        E3 -- Yes --> E4[Carried out as overflow]
        E3 -- No --> E5[Particles settle to underflow]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, Module 1]**
**Q1.** Distinguish clearly between **beaker decantation** and **elutriation** as particle-separation techniques. Mention at least two industrial applications of each.

**Model Answer:**
Beaker decantation is a *batch*, *gravity-based* operation in which a slurry is allowed to stand in a beaker until coarse particles settle to the bottom; the supernatant liquid carrying fine particles is then poured off manually. It is **qualitative / semi-quantitative** and is used as a laboratory tool or in small-scale washing operations such as API-crystal washing and ore-sample analysis.

Elutriation is a *continuous*, *fluid-velocity-based* classification in which an upward stream of fluid (water or air) separates particles based on their terminal settling velocities. Particles whose $u_t$ is less than $u_f$ are carried out as overflow, while particles with $u_t$ greater than $u_f$ settle as underflow. Industrial applications include **hydraulic classifiers, cement-kiln dust separators, fluidized-bed separators, and hydrocyclone precursors**.

Both techniques exploit the principle of **unequal settling velocities** of particles of different sizes / densities in a fluid medium under gravity.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, Module 1]**
**Q2.** A particle of diameter 50 µm and density 2500 kg/m³ is settling in air at 25 °C ($\rho_f = 1.2 \; kg/m^3$, $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \; Pa \cdot s$). Will Stokes' law be applicable? Justify with a calculation.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Use Stokes' law to estimate $u_t$:

$$u_t \; = \; \dfrac{(50 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (2500 - 1.2) \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$u_t \; = \; \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 2498.8 \times 9.81}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}} \; \approx \; 0.184 \; m/s$$

**Step 2:** Compute the particle Reynolds number:

$$Re_p \; = \; \dfrac{d_p \, u_t \, \rho_f}{\mu} \; = \; \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.184 \times 1.2}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re_p \; \approx \; 0.597$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.

Since $Re_p = 0.597 > 0.2$, the flow lies in the **intermediate regime**, NOT in the Stokes regime. Hence **Stokes' law is NOT applicable**; the intermediate-regime equation must be used for an accurate terminal velocity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **procedure for beaker decantation** for separating a mixture of coarse and fine particles. Explain how the **number of decantations** affects the recovery of fines. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A slurry containing 20 % by mass of limestone ($\rho_p = 2700 \; kg/m^3$) in water ($\rho_f = 1000 \; kg/m^3$, $\mu = 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s$) is allowed to settle in a beaker of liquid depth 25 cm. Calculate the **diameter of the largest particle that will NOT settle completely** in 10 minutes. Assume Stokes' regime. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Answer (a):**

**Procedure (with reference to the schematic in Section 4.1):**

1. The slurry is poured into a tall-form graduated beaker and stirred to obtain a uniform suspension.
2. The beaker is allowed to stand on a vibration-free surface.
3. After a predetermined settling time $t$, the supernatant (containing the unsettled fines) is carefully poured into a second beaker by holding a glass rod against the lip so the liquid flows gently without disturbing the sediment.
4. Fresh water is added to the residue, the contents are re-agitated, and steps 2–3 are repeated.
5. Usually **3 to 5 washings** are carried out; the residue is then filtered, dried, and weighed.

**Effect of number of decantations on fines recovery:**

Each washing recovers a fraction of the fines still suspended in the bulk liquid. If $V_0$ is the liquid volume per wash and $V_r$ is the residual liquid carried with the sediment (assumed to remain constant), then the fraction of fines recovered in $n$ washes is:

$$R_n \; = \; 1 - \left( \dfrac{V_r}{V_0 + V_r} \right)^{\,n}$$

This shows that **fines recovery increases asymptotically with $n$**. In practice, beyond 4–5 washes, the incremental gain is small and the process becomes uneconomical — a fact often tested in KTU theory questions.

**Diagram:** Refer to the block-level functional architecture diagram in Section 4.1 (substitute it during exam time as a labelled sketch of beaker + glass rod + decanted stream).

---

**Model Answer (b):**

**Given:** $H = 0.25 \; m$, $t = 600 \; s$, $\rho_p = 2700 \; kg/m^3$, $\rho_f = 1000 \; kg/m^3$, $\mu = 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s$, $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$.

**Step 1:** The critical (cut) particle will just settle the full depth $H$ in time $t$:

$$u_t \; = \; \dfrac{H}{t} \; = \; \dfrac{0.25}{600} \; = \; 4.17 \times 10^{-4} \; m/s$$

**Step 2:** Apply Stokes' law and solve for $d_p$:

$$d_p \; = \; \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \, \mu \, u_t}{(\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}}$$

$$d_p \; = \; \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.17 \times 10^{-4}}{(2700 - 1000) \times 9.81}}$$

Numerator: $18 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.17 \times 10^{-4} = 7.5 \times 10^{-6}$
Denominator: $1700 \times 9.81 = 16677$

$$d_p \; = \; \sqrt{\dfrac{7.5 \times 10^{-6}}{16677}} \; = \; \sqrt{4.5 \times 10^{-10}} \; = \; 2.12 \times 10^{-5} \; m$$

$$\boxed{\, d_p \; \approx \; 21.2 \; \mu m \,}$$

**Step 3:** Verify Stokes' regime.

$$Re_p \; = \; \dfrac{d_p \, u_t \, \rho_f}{\mu} \; = \; \dfrac{2.12 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.17 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000}{10^{-3}} \; \approx \; 8.84 \times 10^{-3}$$

Since $Re_p \ll 0.2$, Stokes' law is **fully valid**.

**Conclusion:** Particles larger than **21.2 µm** will settle completely in 10 minutes; finer particles remain suspended and are removed with the decant.

---

#### Question B — (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the principle and working of a **multi-stage hydraulic elutriator**. Derive the expression for the **cut-size** in the laminar regime. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** In a hydraulic elutriator, water flows upward through a column of diameter 5 cm at a volumetric flow rate of 4.5 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s. The particles are quartz ($\rho_p = 2650 \; kg/m^3$). Given water properties $\rho_f = 1000 \; kg/m^3$ and $\mu = 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s$, determine the **cut-size** and verify the regime. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Answer (a):**

**Principle:** In an elutriator, an upward fluid stream is passed through a vertical column. A particle is in dynamic equilibrium when the upward drag equals the net downward gravitational force:

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{\,3} (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g \; = \; F_D(u_t)$$

**Multi-stage design (see Section 4.2 diagram):** The column is built in sections of progressively **increasing cross-sectional area** from bottom to top. By continuity ($Q = u \cdot A$), the upward velocity **decreases** with height. Therefore:

- **Bottom stage** (narrow, highest $u_f$): only the very finest particles are washed out.
- **Top stage** (widest, lowest $u_f$): the coarsest fraction settles out at the bottom and exits as underflow.

**Derivation of cut-size:**

In the Stokes (laminar) regime, the drag force is given by Stokes' law. Equating the drag with the net weight:

$$3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d_{p,\,cut} \, u_f \; = \; \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d_{p,\,cut}^{\,3} \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g$$

Solving for $d_{p,\,cut}$:

$$\boxed{\, d_{p,\,cut} \; = \; \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \, \mu \, u_f}{(\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}} \,}$$

**Validity condition:** $Re_p < 0.2$, which is equivalent to requiring $u_f$ small enough (and therefore $d_{p,\,cut}$ small enough) for laminar flow around the particle.

---

**Model Answer (b):**

**Given:** $D = 0.05 \; m$, $Q = 4.5 \times 10^{-5} \; m^3/s$, $\rho_p = 2650 \; kg/m^3$, $\rho_f = 1000 \; kg/m^3$, $\mu = 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s$.

**Step 1:** Compute the cross-sectional area of the column.

$$A \; = \; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, D^2 \; = \; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.05)^2 \; = \; 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute the upward fluid (cut) velocity.

$$u_f \; = \; \dfrac{Q}{A} \; = \; \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} \; = \; 2.29 \times 10^{-2} \; m/s$$

**Step 3:** Apply the cut-size formula (assuming Stokes).

$$d_{p,\,cut} \; = \; \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \, \mu \, u_f}{(\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g}}$$

$$d_{p,\,cut} \; = \; \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.29 \times 10^{-2}}{(2650 - 1000) \times 9.81}}$$

Numerator: $18 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.29 \times 10^{-2} = 4.122 \times 10^{-4}$
Denominator: $1650 \times 9.81 = 16186.5$

$$d_{p,\,cut} \; = \; \sqrt{\dfrac{4.122 \times 10^{-4}}{16186.5}} \; = \; \sqrt{2.547 \times 10^{-8}} \; \approx \; 1.596 \times 10^{-4} \; m$$

$$\boxed{\, d_{p,\,cut} \; \approx \; 159.6 \; \mu m \,}$$

**Step 4:** Verify Stokes' regime.

$$Re_p \; = \; \dfrac{d_p \, u_t \, \rho_f}{\mu} \; = \; \dfrac{1.596 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.29 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000}{10^{-3}}$$

$$Re_p \; \approx \; 3.66$$

Since $Re_p = 3.66 \gg 0.2$, the flow is in the **intermediate regime**, NOT in the Stokes regime. Therefore the computed cut-size of **159.6 µm is an overestimate**. To get a more accurate answer, the intermediate-regime correlation must be used (this is a typical follow-up sub-question in KTU papers).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Students Lose Marks Here!**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units.** A $d_p$ given in µm must be converted to metres BEFORE substitution. Marks are deducted silently for unit inconsistencies.
> 2. **Assuming Stokes without verification.** ALWAYS compute $Re_p$ at the end and either confirm $Re_p < 0.2$ or use the intermediate-regime formula.
> 3. **Mixing up density difference.** It is $\rho_p - \rho_f$, NOT $\rho_p + \rho_f$ or $\rho_f - \rho_p$.
> 4. **Forgetting the $18$ in Stokes' law denominator.** Many students write $u_t = d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g / \mu$, which is off by a factor of 18.
> 5. **Not drawing a labelled diagram** in the elutriator question. A sketch with labelled inlet, outlet, fluid direction, and particle paths is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Beaker Decantation & Elutriation**
>
> ✅ **Beaker Decantation** is a *batch* technique: settle → pour off → re-suspend → repeat.
> ✅ Separation is based on **differential settling velocity** of particles of different sizes / densities.
> ✅ Fines recovery after $n$ washes: $R_n = 1 - \left( \dfrac{V_r}{V_0 + V_r} \right)^n$.
> ✅ **Elutriation** is a *continuous* technique using an *upward fluid stream*.
> ✅ The fluid velocity at equilibrium is the **cut velocity $u_c$**; the corresponding size is the **cut size $d_{p,\,cut}$**.
> ✅ **Multi-stage elutriators** exploit decreasing $u_f$ along the column to obtain multiple size cuts in one apparatus.
> ✅ **Stokes' Law** (laminar, $Re_p < 0.2$): $u_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$.
> ✅ **Newton's Law** (turbulent, $Re_p > 1000$): $u_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\rho_f}}$.
> ✅ **Cut-size inversion:** $d_{p,\,cut} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu u_f}{(\rho_p - \rho_f) g}}$.
> ✅ Always **verify the regime** by computing $Re_p = d_p u_t \rho_f / \mu$ after using any settling formula.
> ✅ Higher $\mu$ or $u_f$ → larger cut size; higher $g$ or $(\rho_p - \rho_f)$ → smaller cut size.
> ✅ Industrial relevance: thickeners, clarifiers, hydraulic classifiers, cement-dust elutriators, hydrocyclone principles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification: Sorting, Sink and Float, and Differential Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification: Sorting, Sink and Float, and Differential Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Classification" in Particle Technology?

In the context of **Particle Technology (PCCHT302)**, the term **Classification** refers to the physical separation of a heterogeneous mixture of solid particles into two or more size fractions, or density fractions, based on their response to an external physical field (gravitational, centrifugal, inertial, or through a porous barrier).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, classification is broadly divided into three primary unit operations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition (Module 1 Highlight):**
> Classification is the separation of a particulate material into two or more size (or density) classes using the resistance offered by a fluid medium (liquid or gas), the gravitational/centrifugal force acting on the particle, or a screening surface. It is fundamentally different from "comminution" (size reduction) and is a **size-enrichment** process.

The three pillars of classification studied in this module are:

| S.No. | Technique | Governing Property | Physical Basis |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Sorting (Screening)** | Particle **Size / Geometry** | Aperture geometry of a sieve |
| 2 | **Sink and Float (Heavy Media Separation)** | Particle **Density** | Buoyancy in a dense fluid |
| 3 | **Differential Classification** | Particle **Settling Velocity** | Fluid drag + Gravity/Centrifuge |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition 🍳

To make these three concepts instantly unforgettable, consider a **breakfast scene in a Kerala tea stall**:

### 🍚 Analogy 1 — Sorting (Screening)
Imagine you pour a bucket of **murukku pieces and broken bits** onto a traditional **kitchen sieve (ചലനി)**. The small crumbs fall through the holes into a bowl below, while the big intact pieces stay on top. **This is "Sorting" or "Screening"** — the sieve acts as a *geometric gatekeeper* allowing only particles smaller than the aperture to pass.

### 🪙 Analogy 2 — Sink and Float
Now take a **glass of salt water (brine)**. Drop a **fresh coconut shell piece** and a **river stone** into it. The stone (denser) sinks to the bottom. The coconut shell (lighter) floats. **This is "Sink-and-Float" classification** — separation based on density relative to the medium, completely independent of size.

### 🌪️ Analogy 3 — Differential Classification
Imagine pouring a mixture of **rice, dal, and mustard seeds** into a tall glass of water and stirring. The mustard seeds (smallest, lightest) take the longest to settle. The dal settles slowly. The rice settles fastest. After a few seconds, you get *layered strata* in the glass. **This is "Differential Classification"** — separation based on terminal settling velocity (which depends on both size and density).

---

## 1.3 The Core Physical Metrics Used

Every classification technique relies on measurable physical quantities. The **bold constants** below are fundamental:

* **ρₚ** = Density of particle **[kg/m³]**
* **ρ_f** = Density of fluid medium **[kg/m³]**
* **μ** = Viscosity of fluid **[Pa·s]**
* **g** = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
* **d_p** = Diameter of particle **[m]**
* **v_t** = Terminal settling velocity **[m/s]**
* **Re_p** = Particle Reynolds number = **d_p · v_t · ρ_f / μ**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Pointer:** Questions in **University Exams (ESE)** frequently ask students to *contrast sorting, sink-and-float, and differential classification* in a single 3-mark definition question. Memorize the *governing property* of each method.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SORTING (SCREENING) — Geometric Separation

### 2.1.1 Definition and Mechanism
**Sorting** is the mechanical separation of particles according to their **size alone**, by passing the feed through a surface containing apertures of a definite dimension. The particles that pass through are called **undersize (or fines)**, and the ones retained are **oversize (or tails)**.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Feed Introduction** — The heterogeneous particulate feed is delivered uniformly onto the screen surface (also called the **deck**).
2. **Stratification** — Vibratory or oscillatory motion causes small particles to migrate downward through the bed, while larger ones rise to the top.
3. **Probability of Passage** — A particle passes an aperture if its **minor dimension** is smaller than the aperture diameter. This is governed by probability theory.
4. **Separation Achieved** — Undersize particles fall through; oversize particles travel to the discharge end.

### 2.1.3 Key Performance Metrics

* **Screen Efficiency (E)** — A measure of how effectively the screen removes undersize material.

$$E = \frac{\text{Actual mass of undersize recovered in overflow}}{\text{Total mass of undersize in the feed}}$$

* **Capacity (Q)** — Mass throughput per unit time.
* **Effectiveness (η)** — Product of recovery and sharpness of cut.

### 2.1.4 Industrial Equipment
* **Grizzlies** — Coarse, parallel bars (typical aperture > 50 mm).
* **Trommels** — Rotating cylindrical screens (used in fertilizer and ore industries).
* **Vibrating Screens** — High-frequency oscillation for fine powders.
* **Reel Screens** — Rotating cylindrical with internal flights.

---

## 2.2 SINK-AND-FLOAT (HEAVY MEDIA SEPARATION)

### 2.2.1 Definition and Mechanism
**Sink-and-Float** is a **density-based** separation process. The feed is introduced into a fluid medium whose density lies *between* the densities of the two desired fractions.

* **Heavier particles (ρₚ > ρ_f)** → **Sink** to the bottom.
* **Lighter particles (ρₚ < ρ_f)** → **Float** at the surface.

### 2.2.2 The Choice of Dense Media

In industrial practice, the fluid is rarely a pure liquid (too expensive at high density). Engineers use **suspensions of finely ground dense solids in water** (called *heavy media* or *dense media*):

| Dense Medium | Density Range (kg/m³) | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Magnetite (Fe₃O₄) | 2200 – 2500 | Coal cleaning |
| Ferrosilicon (Fe-Si) | 2700 – 3200 | Iron ore, diamond |
| Galena (PbS) | 3500 – 4200 | Pre-concentration |

### 2.2.3 Critical Operational Steps

1. **Preparation of Medium** — Magnetite or ferrosilicon is suspended in water to give a slurry of desired density.
2. **Feed Immersion** — The ore/coal feed is plunged into the bath.
3. **Separation** — Two products are skimmed off: **Floats** (scooped from the surface) and **Sinks** (scraped from the bottom).
4. **Medium Recovery** — The dense medium is washed off, magnetically recovered (for magnetite), and recycled.

### 2.2.4 Industrial Applications
* **Coal cleaning** (remove shale and pyrite from clean coal).
* **Diamond ore processing**.
* **Garbage sorting** (separate organic from inorganic waste).
* **Pre-concentration of metallic ores** (e.g., lead-zinc ores).

### 2.2.5 Advantages over Sink-and-Float
* Highly accurate density cut.
* Independent of particle size (works on any size range).
* No fines lost as dust (closed wet system).

---

## 2.3 DIFFERENTIAL CLASSIFICATION — Velocity-Based Separation

### 2.3.1 Definition and Mechanism
**Differential Classification** separates particles according to their **terminal settling velocity** (also called *terminal falling velocity* or *sedimentation velocity*) in a fluid medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Differential classification is the *most heavily tested* topic in Module 1. Students must master Stoke's Law, Newton's Law, and the Intermediate Regime equations, along with the concept of **Equal Settling Particles (ESP)**.

The principle:
* A particle falling through a fluid accelerates until the **net downward force = drag force**.
* At this equilibrium, the particle falls at a constant **terminal velocity v_t**.
* Different particles have different v_t; therefore, they settle into different layers — enabling separation.

### 2.3.2 The Force Balance on a Falling Particle

Three forces act on a particle settling in a fluid:

1. **Gravitational Force (Downward):** $F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p \, g$
2. **Buoyant Force (Upward):** $F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_f \, g$
3. **Drag Force (Upward, opposing motion):** $F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_f v^2}{2}$

At terminal velocity: **F_g − F_b = F_D**

This yields the master equation (derived in Section 3):

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$$

### 2.3.3 The Three Flow Regimes (Critical for KTU)

The drag coefficient **C_D** is a function of the **Particle Reynolds number Re_p**:

| Regime | Re_p Range | C_D Formula | Governing Law |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stokes' Law (Laminar)** | Re_p < **0.1** | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p}$ | Viscous forces dominate |
| **Intermediate (Transition)** | 0.1 < Re_p < **1000** | $C_D = \dfrac{18.5}{Re_p^{0.6}}$ | Both forces comparable |
| **Newton's Law (Turbulent)** | 1000 < Re_p < 2×10⁵ | $C_D = 0.44$ (constant) | Inertia dominates |

### 2.3.4 Free Settling vs. Hindered Settling

* **Free Settling:** Each particle settles independently in an infinite fluid — no particle-particle interaction. This is the **theoretical ideal**.
* **Hindered Settling:** Particles are crowded (high solids concentration > 1% by volume). The effective fluid density and viscosity increase, and the wake of one particle affects others. The terminal velocity is **reduced**.

### 2.3.5 Industrial Equipment for Differential Classification

| Equipment | Operating Principle | Typical Cut Size |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Spitzkasten** | Conical settling tank | 100 – 1000 µm |
| **Dorr Classifier (Rake)** | Settling + raked sediment removal | 100 – 850 µm |
| **Hydrocyclone** | Centrifugal acceleration | 5 – 200 µm |
| **Sedimentation Centrifuge** | High-g centrifugation | < 10 µm |

## 2.5 🧮 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question paper on this module.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $v_t = \dfrac{g \, d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$ | **Stokes' Law** (laminar, Re_p < 0.1) |
| 3 | $v_t = \dfrac{0.153 \, g^{0.71} \, d_p^{1.14} (\rho_p - \rho_f)^{0.71}}{\rho_f^{0.29} \, \mu^{0.43}}$ | **Intermediate Regime** (Allen / Fair's correlation) |
| 4 | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.33 \, g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ | **Newton's Law** (turbulent, Re_p > 1000) |
| 5 | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_f}}$ | General terminal velocity (any regime) |
| 6 | $F_D = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \dfrac{\rho_f v^2}{2}$ | Drag force on a sphere |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{\text{Undersize recovered}}{\text{Total undersize in feed}}$ | Screen efficiency (Sorting) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{d_1}{d_2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{p2} - \rho_f}{\rho_{p1} - \rho_f}}$ | **Equal Settling Condition (differential classification)** |

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: General Terminal Velocity Equation (Newton's Master Equation)

We consider a single spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_p$ falling under gravity through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$. The particle accelerates until the net force equals zero (terminal condition).

### Step 1: Identify Forces Acting
Three forces act on the sphere during its fall:

* **Weight (downward):** $W = \rho_p \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot g$
* **Buoyancy (upward):** $F_b = \rho_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot g$
* **Drag (upward, opposing fall):** $F_D = C_D \cdot A_p \cdot \dfrac{\rho_f v^2}{2}$, where $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_p^2$ is the projected area.

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law
The net downward force on the particle equals mass times acceleration.

$$\rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = W - F_b - F_D$$

Substituting all force expressions:

$$\rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g - C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_f v^2}{2}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Terminal Velocity Condition
At terminal velocity, acceleration $dv/dt = 0$. The drag exactly balances the net gravitational force:

$$0 = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g - C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_f v_t^2}{2}$$

### Step 4: Solve for v_t
Rearrange to isolate $v_t^2$:

$$C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_f v_t^2}{2} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$

Cancel $\pi$ from both sides and rearrange:

$$\frac{C_D \, d_p^2 \, \rho_f \, v_t^2}{8} = \frac{d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{6}$$

Multiply both sides by 8:

$$C_D \, d_p^2 \, \rho_f \, v_t^2 = \frac{8 \, d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{6} = \frac{4}{3} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$

Divide by $C_D \, d_p^2 \, \rho_f$:

$$v_t^2 = \frac{4 \, g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_f}$$

### Step 5: Final General Terminal Velocity Equation

$$\boxed{v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_f}}}$$

This is the **master equation** of differential classification. The drag coefficient $C_D$ is a function of $Re_p$, which gives rise to the three regime-specific equations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stokes' Law (Laminar Regime)

### Step 1: Substitute C_D into the Master Equation
In the **Stokes regime** ($Re_p < 0.1$), the drag coefficient is:

$$C_D = \frac{24}{Re_p} = \frac{24 \, \mu}{d_p \, v_t \, \rho_f}$$

### Step 2: Plug C_D into the Terminal Velocity Equation

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \cdot \dfrac{24 \, \mu}{d_p \, v_t \, \rho_f} \cdot \rho_f}}$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Denominator
Notice $\rho_f$ cancels:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, g \, d_p^2 \, v_t \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \cdot 24 \, \mu}}$$

### Step 4: Square Both Sides to Eliminate the Square Root

$$v_t^2 = \frac{4 \, g \, d_p^2 \, v_t \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{72 \, \mu} = \frac{g \, d_p^2 \, v_t \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$$

### Step 5: Divide Both Sides by v_t (which is non-zero)

$$\boxed{v_t = \frac{g \, d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** In the Stokes regime, $v_t$ is proportional to $d_p^2$ — a small increase in particle diameter causes a *squared* increase in settling velocity. This is why fine dust particles take so long to settle in still air.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Newton's Law (Turbulent Regime)

### Step 1: Substitute C_D into the Master Equation
In the **Newton regime** ($Re_p > 1000$), $C_D$ becomes a constant equal to **0.44**.

### Step 2: Plug into the Terminal Velocity Equation

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \cdot 0.44 \cdot \rho_f}}$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Denominator
$3 \cdot 0.44 = 1.32$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{1.32 \, \rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.03 \, g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$$

### Step 4: Final Newton's Law Equation

$$\boxed{v_t = \sqrt{\frac{3.33 \, g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** In the Newton regime, $v_t$ is proportional to $\sqrt{d_p}$ — doubling the diameter only increases the velocity by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$. This regime applies to large, heavy particles falling fast.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Equal Settling Particles (ESP) — Critical KTU Concept

### Background
In differential classification, two particles of *different* size and *different* density may settle at the *same* velocity. This is exploited industrially to **separate mixtures where density varies but size is similar**.

### Step 1: Set Terminal Velocities Equal
Let particle 1 have diameter $d_1$ and density $\rho_{p1}$. Let particle 2 have diameter $d_2$ and density $\rho_{p2}$. Assuming both fall under **Stokes' regime** for simplicity:

$$v_{t1} = \frac{g \, d_1^2 (\rho_{p1} - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$$

$$v_{t2} = \frac{g \, d_2^2 (\rho_{p2} - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$$

### Step 2: Equate the Two Velocities

$$\frac{g \, d_1^2 (\rho_{p1} - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu} = \frac{g \, d_2^2 (\rho_{p2} - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$$

Cancel common terms $g / (18\mu)$:

$$d_1^2 (\rho_{p1} - \rho_f) = d_2^2 (\rho_{p2} - \rho_f)$$

### Step 3: Solve for the Diameter Ratio

$$\frac{d_1^2}{d_2^2} = \frac{\rho_{p2} - \rho_f}{\rho_{p1} - \rho_f}$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$\boxed{\frac{d_1}{d_2} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{p2} - \rho_f}{\rho_{p1} - \rho_f}}}$$

### Interpretation
* If $\rho_{p2} > \rho_{p1}$ (particle 2 is denser), then $d_1 > d_2$ (particle 1 must be larger to settle at the same rate).
* **Industrial Use:** A dense, small particle can settle at the same rate as a large, light particle — this is the basis of **hydraulic classification** in mineral processing.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Demonstration: Typical KTU Numerical Problem

> **Problem (Sample):** A spherical particle of diameter 100 µm and density 2500 kg/m³ falls through water at 30 °C. Find the terminal settling velocity and the regime.
>
> **Given:** $d_p = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, $\rho_f = 996$ kg/m³ (water at 30°C), $\mu = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

### Step 1: Assume Stokes' Law and Check
$$v_t = \frac{(9.81)(100 \times 10^{-6})^2 (2500 - 996)}{18 \times (0.8 \times 10^{-3})}$$

Numerator:
$$(9.81)(10^{-8})(1504) = 1.475 \times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator:
$$18 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3} = 1.44 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$v_t = \frac{1.475 \times 10^{-4}}{1.44 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.01024 \, \text{m/s} = 1.024 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m/s}$$

### Step 2: Verify the Regime
$$Re_p = \frac{d_p \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\mu} = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-6})(1.024 \times 10^{-2})(996)}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{1.02 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.275$$

Since $Re_p = 1.275 > 0.1$, **Stokes' law is NOT valid**. Use the **Intermediate Regime**.

### Step 3: Apply the Intermediate Regime Equation

$$v_t = \frac{0.153 \, g^{0.71} \, d_p^{1.14} (\rho_p - \rho_f)^{0.71}}{\rho_f^{0.29} \, \mu^{0.43}}$$

Plugging values (calculated step-by-step):
$$v_t \approx 0.0052 \, \text{m/s} = 5.2 \, \text{mm/s}$$

### Step 4: Re-verify Regime
$$Re_p = \frac{(10^{-4})(5.2 \times 10^{-3})(996)}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.647$$

This lies in the intermediate regime (0.1 to 1000), so the solution is **self-consistent**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Always verify the regime AFTER calculating $v_t$. If $Re_p$ falls outside the assumed regime, iterate the calculation using the correct regime equation.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Differential Classifier (Hydrocyclone)

A **hydrocyclone** is the most widely used differential classifier in chemical and mineral industries. The diagram below shows the functional flow architecture of separation inside a hydrocyclone.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feed["FEED SLURRY"]:::feed --> inlet["TANGENTIAL INLET"]:::inlet
    inlet --> vortex1["PRIMARY SPIRAL VORTEX OUTWARD"]:::vortex
    vortex1 --> apex["APEX UNDERFLOW DENSE COARSE"]:::under
    apex --> product1["COARSE FRACTION PRODUCT"]:::prod

    inlet --> vortex2["SECONDARY VORTEX UPWARD"]:::vortex
    vortex2 --> vortex3["CENTRAL LOW PRESSURE CORE"]:::core
    vortex3 --> overflow["VORTEX FINDER OVERFLOW"]:::over
    overflow --> product2["FINE FRACTION PRODUCT"]:::prod

    classDef feed fill:#fef3c7,color:#78350f,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef inlet fill:#dbeafe,color:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af
    classDef vortex fill:#e0e7ff,color:#3730a3,stroke:#4338ca
    classDef core fill:#fce7f3,color:#831843,stroke:#be185d
    classDef over fill:#d1fae5,color:#065f46,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:2px
    classDef under fill:#fed7aa,color:#7c2d12,stroke:#ea580c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef prod fill:#e5e7eb,color:#111827,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:**
* The feed enters **tangentially**, creating a **spiral vortex** (centrifugal acceleration 100 to 1000 × g).
* **Coarse, dense particles** move outward and downward — collected through the **apex** as **underflow**.
* **Fine, light particles** are carried upward through the central low-pressure core — collected as **overflow**.

---

## 4.3 Sink-and-Float Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["RAW ORE FEED"]:::feed --> P1["CRUSHING AND SCREENING"]:::stage
    P1 --> P2["WETTING TANK"]:::stage
    P2 --> P3["HEAVY MEDIA BATH SEPARATOR"]:::stage
    P3 --> P4["FLOATS PRODUCT LIGHT DENSITY"]:::float
    P3 --> P5["SINKS PRODUCT HIGH DENSITY"]:::sink
    P4 --> P6["DENSE MEDIUM RECOVERY WASH SCREEN"]:::recover
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7["MAGNETIC SEPARATOR FOR MAGNETITE"]:::recover
    P7 --> P8["RECYCLED DENSE MEDIUM TO BATH"]:::recover

    classDef feed fill:#fef3c7,color:#78350f,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px

    classDef recover fill:#e0e7ff,color:#3730a3,stroke:#4338ca
```

**Interpretation:** The ore is pre-crushed and screened to remove fines (which cannot be separated cleanly in a bath), wetted to remove air pockets, then plunged into the dense media bath. The two density fractions are recovered, and the expensive dense medium (magnetite) is washed off and magnetically recycled — making the process economically viable.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between "Sorting" and "Classification" in particle technology.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks | CO1, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Sorting** is the separation of particles according to their **size alone** by passing them through a surface containing apertures of definite dimensions (e.g., screens, sieves). The governing property is the **geometric dimension** of the particle.
>
> **Classification** (in the broader KTU sense) is the separation of particles according to their **settling velocity** in a fluid medium, which depends on **both size and density**. The governing property is the **terminal falling velocity**.
>
> **Key Difference:** Sorting is purely geometric (uses a sieve), while differential classification uses fluid drag and gravity. Sink-and-float, by contrast, separates purely by **density** independent of size.

---

### Q2. What is meant by "Terminal Settling Velocity"? Mention its significance in differential classification.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 1 | 3 Marks | CO1, Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Terminal settling velocity (v_t)** is the constant velocity reached by a particle falling through a fluid when the **net downward gravitational force** equals the **upward drag force**. Beyond this point, the particle no longer accelerates — it falls at a steady rate.
>
> **Significance in Differential Classification:**
> * It is the **governing parameter** used to distinguish one particle from another in a fluid medium.
> * Two particles with the **same v_t** (regardless of size or density) end up in the **same classification zone** — this is the basis of *equal settling particles* concept.
> * The magnitude of $v_t$ depends on the **flow regime**: Stokes, Intermediate, or Newton.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Differential Classification & Drag Regimes

> **(a)** Derive the general expression for the **terminal settling velocity** of a spherical particle falling under gravity through a viscous fluid. Clearly state all assumptions.
> **(b)** Using the derived expression, obtain the **Stokes' Law** and **Newton's Law** equations. Specify the **Re_p range** of validity for each.
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 1 | 14 Marks | CO1, CO2, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze | Internal Choice Option A]**

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a): Derivation of General Terminal Velocity

**Step 1 — List the forces acting on a spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ falling through fluid:**

* **Weight (downward):** $W = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p g$
* **Buoyant force (upward):** $F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_f g$
* **Drag force (upward, opposing motion):** $F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_f v^2}{2}$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law:**

$$\rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g - C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_f v^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply terminal velocity condition ($dv/dt = 0$):**

$$0 = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g - C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_f v_t^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange and isolate $v_t$:**

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$$

**Assumptions:**
* Particle is **rigid and spherical**.
* Particle falls in an **infinite, undisturbed fluid** (free settling).
* Fluid properties ($\rho_f, \mu$) are **constant**.
* **Steady-state** terminal condition is reached.

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b): Stokes and Newton Equations

**Stokes' Law derivation:**

Substitute $C_D = 24/Re_p = 24 \mu / (d_p v_t \rho_f)$ into the master equation. After simplification (shown in Section 3.2):

$$v_t = \frac{g d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}, \quad \text{valid for } Re_p < 0.1$$

**Newton's Law derivation:**

Substitute $C_D = 0.44$ (constant) into the master equation. After simplification (shown in Section 3.3):

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{3.33 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}, \quad \text{valid for } 1000 < Re_p < 2 \times 10^5$$

**Validity summary:**

| Regime | Re_p Range | Nature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stokes' | $< 0.1$ | Laminar (viscous dominant) |
| Intermediate | $0.1 - 1000$ | Transition |
| Newton's | $1000 - 2 \times 10^5$ | Turbulent (inertia dominant) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Sink and Float & Screen Efficiency

> **(a)** Explain the **principle, operation, and industrial applications** of **Sink-and-Float separation**. List the commonly used dense media and their density ranges.
> **(b)** A vibrating screen analyses a feed of 1000 kg/h containing 40% undersize material. The overflow contains 600 kg/h with 10% undersize. Calculate the **screen efficiency**.
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | Module 1 | 14 Marks | CO2, CO3, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze | Internal Choice Option B]**

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a): Sink-and-Float Separation

**Principle:**
The separation is based on **density difference** between particles and the suspending fluid medium. A particle **sinks** if $\rho_p > \rho_f$, and **floats** if $\rho_p < \rho_f$.

**Operation:**
1. **Preparation of dense medium** — A slurry of magnetite (or ferrosilicon) in water is prepared to achieve a chosen density (typically 1300 – 3000 kg/m³).
2. **Feed introduction** — Crushed ore is plunged into the bath.
3. **Separation** — Light particles float and are skimmed off; dense particles sink and are scraped off the bottom.
4. **Medium recovery** — The dense medium is drained, washed, and **magnetically recovered** for reuse.

**Common dense media and density ranges:**

| Dense Medium | Density (kg/m³) | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Magnetite (Fe₃O₄) | 2200 – 2500 | Coal cleaning |
| Ferrosilicon (Fe-Si) | 2700 – 3200 | Iron ore |
| Galena (PbS) | 3500 – 4200 | Pre-concentration |

**Industrial Applications:**
* Coal cleaning (removing ash and shale from clean coal).
* Diamond ore processing.
* Lead-zinc ore pre-concentration.
* Municipal solid waste separation.

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b): Screen Efficiency Calculation

**Given data:**
* Feed rate $F = 1000$ kg/h
* Undersize in feed = 40 % → Mass of true undersize in feed = $0.40 \times 1000 = 400$ kg/h
* Overflow rate $O = 600$ kg/h
* Undersize in overflow = 10 % → Mass of undersize recovered with overflow = $0.10 \times 600 = 60$ kg/h

**Step 1: Mass of undersize in feed (true undersize)**
$$M_{undersize,feed} = 400 \, \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2: Screen Efficiency formula**
$$E = \frac{\text{Undersize actually recovered in overflow}}{\text{Total undersize in feed}} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**
$$E = \frac{60}{400} \times 100 = 15\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> * Many students confuse "undersize recovered in overflow" with "undersize in the feed" — they are *not* the same.
> * Always identify the **direction of flow** correctly: in some screen configurations, the **oversize** is the **overflow** and undersize is the **underflow**. Read the problem carefully.
> * **Unit consistency** is mandatory — keep all mass flow rates in the same unit (kg/h in this case).
> * In a 7-mark sub-question, you must show **all three computational steps** — partial marking is given even if the final answer is wrong, provided the methodology is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this on your wall before the exam.**

* ✅ **Sorting (Screening)** separates by **size** alone using a sieve surface; produces **oversize** and **undersize**.
* ✅ **Sink-and-Float** separates by **density** alone using a dense medium; produces **floats** and **sinks**.
* ✅ **Differential Classification** separates by **terminal settling velocity**, which depends on **both size and density**.
* ✅ **Three Drag Regimes** based on $Re_p$:
    * Stokes (laminar, $Re_p < 0.1$) → $v_t \propto d_p^2$
    * Intermediate ($0.1 < Re_p < 1000$) → $v_t \propto d_p^{1.14}$
    * Newton (turbulent, $Re_p > 1000$) → $v_t \propto \sqrt{d_p}$
* ✅ **Master terminal velocity equation** (always valid): $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$
* ✅ **Stokes' Law:** $v_t = \dfrac{g d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$
* ✅ **Newton's Law:** $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.33 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$
* ✅ **Equal Settling Particles:** $\dfrac{d_1}{d_2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{p2} - \rho_f}{\rho_{p1} - \rho_f}}$ — heavily tested in KTU ESE.
* ✅ **Free settling** = single particle, undisturbed fluid; **Hindered settling** = particle swarm, fluid properties change.
* ✅ **Always verify the regime** by computing $Re_p$ after finding $v_t$; if it falls outside the assumed regime, **re-iterate** with the correct equation.
* ✅ **Hydrocyclones** are the most common industrial differential classifier — they amplify gravitational acceleration by a factor of 100–1000×.
* ✅ **Dense media** for Sink-and-Float: Magnetite (cheapest, magnetic recovery), Ferrosilicon (higher density), Galena (highest density, toxic, rare).
* ✅ **Screen Efficiency:** $E = \dfrac{\text{Recovered undersize}}{\text{True undersize in feed}} \times 100\%$

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Hack:** In every numerical, **begin by computing $Re_p$** (after assuming Stokes) and check if it is in the right regime. Most students lose marks because they use Stokes' Law blindly even when $Re_p > 0.1$. Always verify, always iterate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equal Settling Particles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equal Settling Particles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equal Settling Particles (Equal Settling Concept):** Two particles of *different size* and *different density* are said to be **equal settling** when they attain the *same terminal (free-settling) velocity* while settling independently through the same quiescent fluid under identical gravitational and fluid conditions.

Mathematically, if particle **1** has diameter $d_1$ and density $\rho_{p1}$, and particle **2** has diameter $d_2$ and density $\rho_{p2}$, the condition of equal settling is:

$$v_{t1} \;=\; v_{t2}$$

where $v_{t1}$ and $v_{t2}$ are their respective terminal velocities in the same fluid of density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** Equal settling particles **cannot be separated** by simple gravity sedimentation, because no matter how long they are allowed to settle, they remain mixed together. This single fact controls the design of every classifier, thickener, and hydrocyclone in chemical engineering.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine two friends walking down a corridor at the **exact same speed**:

- Friend A is **tall but wearing thin slippers** (large particle, low density).
- Friend B is **short but wearing heavy boots** (small particle, high density).

Even though they are physically very different, both reach the bus stop together. In sedimentation, a **large, light particle** (e.g., coarse quartz) can have the **same terminal velocity** as a **small, dense particle** (e.g., fine galena). They are *equal-settling particles*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

The following constants appear repeatedly and must be memorised for KTU board examinations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Water, 20 °C) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Density of water | $\rho_{H_2O}$ | **998** | kg/m³ |
| Dynamic viscosity of water | $\mu_{H_2O}$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³** | Pa·s |
| Kinematic viscosity of water | $\nu$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶** | m²/s |
| Density of air (STP) | $\rho_{air}$ | **1.20** | kg/m³ |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU numerical problems, if not stated otherwise, assume $\rho_{\text{fluid}} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$, and $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

### 1.4 Why Equal Settling Matters in Engineering

- **Mineral Beneficiation:** Separation of *galena* (PbS, $\rho_p = 7500 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) from *quartz* (SiO₂, $\rho_p = 2650 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) by sedimentation fails if the particles are equal-settling.
- **Sewage Treatment:** Grit chambers rely on *unequal* settling to remove sand but keep organic matter suspended.
- **Cyclone/Classifier Design:** The **cut-size $d_{50}$** is defined using the equal-settling principle — the particle that has equal probability of reporting to overflow or underflow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Settling Regimes (Foundation Concept)

A settling sphere experiences three forces: **gravity** (down), **buoyancy** (up), and **drag** (opposing motion). At *terminal velocity*, the net force is zero.

The flow regime is classified by the **Reynolds Number** for the particle:

$$Re_p \;=\; \frac{d \, v_t \, \rho}{\mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always identify the regime first using $Re_p$, then choose the appropriate drag-coefficient correlation. Marks are awarded *only* for correct regime identification.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

1. **Step 1 — Force balance on a single particle at terminal velocity:**

   $$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \, \rho_p \, g \;-\; \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \, \rho \, g \;=\; C_D \, \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \, \frac{\rho \, v_t^2}{2}$$

   The LHS is the **effective weight** (gravity − buoyancy); RHS is the **drag force**.

2. **Step 2 — Solve the generic equation for $v_t$:**

   $$v_t \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4 \, g \, d \, (\rho_p - \rho)}{3 \, \rho \, C_D}}$$

3. **Step 3 — Substitute the correct $C_D$ expression for each regime.**

4. **Step 4 — Apply equal-settling condition $v_{t1} = v_{t2}$ between two particles of different $(d, \rho_p)$.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — All Key Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for Module 1 problems. Memorise every row.

| # | Regime | Reynolds Range | Drag Coeff. $C_D$ | Terminal Velocity $v_t$ | Equal-Settling Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stokes (Laminar)** | $Re_p < 0.2$ | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p} = \dfrac{24\mu}{d v_t \rho}$ | $v_t = \dfrac{g \, d^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \, \mu}$ | $\dfrac{d_1}{d_2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{p2} - \rho}{\rho_{p1} - \rho}}$ |
| 2 | **Intermediate (Transition)** | $0.2 < Re_p < 1000$ | $C_D = \dfrac{18.5}{Re_p^{0.6}}$ | $v_t = \dfrac{0.153 \, d^{1.14} (\rho_p - \rho)^{0.71} \, g^{0.71}}{\rho^{0.29} \, \mu^{0.43}}$ | Iterative / graphical |
| 3 | **Newton (Turbulent)** | $Re_p > 1000$ | $C_D = 0.44$ | $v_t = 1.73 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{g \, d (\rho_p - \rho)}{\rho}}$ | $\dfrac{d_1}{d_2} = \dfrac{(\rho_{p2} - \rho)}{(\rho_{p1} - \rho)}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Absolute value / magnitude convention:** Whenever the formula requires a positive quantity inside a radical, the convention used in this sheet is $\vert \rho_p - \rho \vert$ written explicitly as $(\rho_p - \rho)$ because we always have $\rho_p > \rho$ for settling particles.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Classifier Design:** Determines the **separation size** (cut size) between coarse dense and fine light fractions.
- **Hydrocyclone Selection:** The geometric *d₅₀* cut-point is derived directly from equal-settling theory.
- **Thickener Underflow Design:** Equal-settling particles dictate the minimum residence time needed for a desired grade of separation.
- **Air Classifiers in Cement Industry:** Separate cement (fine) from coarse rejects using equal-settling principles in air as the medium.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stokes' Law (Foundation for Equal Settling in Laminar Regime)

**Step 1.** Write the force balance at terminal velocity on a spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$ in a fluid of density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$.

$$\text{Net Force} \;=\; 0$$

$$\underbrace{\frac{\pi}{6} d^{3} \, \rho_{p} \, g}_{\text{Gravity}} \;-\; \underbrace{\frac{\pi}{6} d^{3} \, \rho \, g}_{\text{Buoyancy}} \;=\; \underbrace{C_{D} \, \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \, \frac{\rho \, v_{t}^{2}}{2}}_{\text{Drag}}$$

**Step 2.** Simplify the gravity − buoyancy combination (this is the *effective weight*).

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^{3} \, (\rho_{p} - \rho) \, g \;=\; C_{D} \, \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \, \frac{\rho \, v_{t}^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 3.** Cancel the common factor $d^{2}$ from both sides (this is what makes the equation solvable for $v_t$).

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d \, (\rho_{p} - \rho) \, g \;=\; C_{D} \, \frac{\pi}{4} \, \frac{\rho \, v_{t}^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 4.** In the **laminar regime**, Stokes proved experimentally that $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p} = \dfrac{24 \, \mu}{d \, v_t \, \rho}$. Substitute.

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d \, (\rho_{p} - \rho) \, g \;=\; \left( \frac{24 \, \mu}{d \, v_t \, \rho} \right) \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \, \frac{\rho \, v_t^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5.** Notice that one factor of $v_t$ cancels with $v_t^{2}$, and $\rho$ cancels.

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d \, (\rho_{p} - \rho) \, g \;=\; \frac{24 \, \mu}{d} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{v_t}{2}$$

**Step 6.** Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{d}{1}$ and isolate $v_t$.

$$v_t \;=\; \frac{d^{2} (\rho_{p} - \rho) \, g}{18 \, \mu}$$

$$\boxed{\,v_t \;=\; \frac{g \, d^{2} (\rho_{p} - \rho)}{18 \, \mu} \quad \text{(Stokes' Law)}\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equal-Settling Condition in Stokes Regime

**Step 1.** Two particles (1) and (2) settle with the same terminal velocity.

$$v_{t1} \;=\; v_{t2}$$

**Step 2.** Apply Stokes' law to both particles.

$$\frac{g \, d_{1}^{2} (\rho_{p1} - \rho)}{18 \, \mu} \;=\; \frac{g \, d_{2}^{2} (\rho_{p2} - \rho)}{18 \, \mu}$$
