**Step 3.** The constants $g$, $18$, and $\mu$ cancel on both sides.

$$d_{1}^{2} \, (\rho_{p1} - \rho) \;=\; d_{2}^{2} \, (\rho_{p2} - \rho)$$

**Step 4.** Rearrange to obtain the *equal-settling diameter ratio*.

$$\frac{d_{1}}{d_{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{p2} - \rho}{\rho_{p1} - \rho}}$$

$$\boxed{\,\frac{d_{1}}{d_{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{p2} - \rho}{\rho_{p1} - \rho}} \quad \text{(Equal-Settling Ratio, Stokes Regime)}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning:** A *denser* particle (larger $\rho_p$) can have a *smaller* diameter and still match the settling velocity of a larger, lighter particle. This is why classifiers require a *density correction factor* for accurate cut-point estimation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equal-Settling Condition in Newton's Regime

**Step 1.** Write Newton's regime terminal velocity (with $C_D = 0.44$).

$$v_t \;=\; 1.73 \, \sqrt{\frac{g \, d \, (\rho_{p} - \rho)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 2.** Equate $v_{t1} = v_{t2}$ for two equal-settling particles.

$$1.73 \, \sqrt{\frac{g \, d_{1} (\rho_{p1} - \rho)}{\rho}} \;=\; 1.73 \, \sqrt{\frac{g \, d_{2} (\rho_{p2} - \rho)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 3.** Square both sides to remove the square roots; constants cancel.

$$d_{1} \, (\rho_{p1} - \rho) \;=\; d_{2} \, (\rho_{p2} - \rho)$$

**Step 4.** Rearrange to express the diameter ratio.

$$\boxed{\,\frac{d_{1}}{d_{2}} \;=\; \frac{\rho_{p2} - \rho}{\rho_{p1} - \rho} \quad \text{(Equal-Settling Ratio, Newton Regime)}\,}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A mixture of *quartz* ($\rho_{p1} = 2650 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) and *galena* ($\rho_{p2} = 7500 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) is settling in water at 20 °C ($\rho = 998 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$). A quartz particle has diameter $d_1 = 200 \, \mu\text{m}$. Determine the diameter of the *equal-settling* galena particle.

**Solution (Stokes regime assumed — verify at end):**

**Step 1.** Use the equal-settling ratio for Stokes regime.

$$\frac{d_{1}}{d_{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{p2} - \rho}{\rho_{p1} - \rho}}$$

**Step 2.** Plug in values.

$$\frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{d_{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{7500 - 998}{2650 - 998}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{6502}{1652}} \;=\; \sqrt{3.935}$$

**Step 3.** Compute the square root.

$$\sqrt{3.935} \;\approx\; 1.984$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $d_2$.

$$d_{2} \;=\; \frac{200 \, \mu\text{m}}{1.984} \;\approx\; 100.8 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 5.** Verify $Re_p < 0.2$ to confirm Stokes regime is valid.

First compute $v_{t1}$:

$$v_{t1} \;=\; \frac{9.81 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times (2650 - 998)}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 1.71 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s}$$

Then:

$$Re_{p1} \;=\; \frac{d_1 \, v_{t1} \, \rho}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.71 \times 10^{-3} \times 998}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 0.34$$

> [!WARNING]
> Since $Re_{p1} = 0.34$ is **slightly above the Stokes limit (0.2)**, the *intermediate regime* correlation would give a slightly more accurate answer. KTU board examiners usually accept the Stokes approximation if the deviation is explicitly noted.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Regime Selection Decision Table

| Given Information | Compute First | Choose Regime | Use Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small particle, viscous fluid | $Re_p$ | Stokes if $< 0.2$ | $v_t = \dfrac{g d^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18\mu}$ |
| Medium particle, $Re_p \approx 10$–500 | $C_D$ | Intermediate | Iterative on $C_D = \dfrac{18.5}{Re_p^{0.6}}$ |
| Large particle, $Re_p > 1000$ | $C_D = 0.44$ | Newton | $v_t = 1.73 \sqrt{\dfrac{g d (\rho_p - \rho)}{\rho}}$ |
| Two particles asked to be equal-settling | $v_{t1} = v_{t2}$ | Depends on $Re_p$ | $d_1/d_2 = \sqrt{(\rho_{p2}-\rho)/(\rho_{p1}-\rho)}$ (Stokes) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — Force-Body Diagram of a Settling Particle

The following block-level architecture illustrates the *free-body force balance* used in all Stokes/Newton derivations. It also shows how the **equal-settling condition** is constructed from two parallel particle analyses.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Particle 1: diameter d1, density rhoP1] --> F1A[Gravity Fg1 = pi d1 cubed rhoP1 g over 6]
    P1 --> F1B[Buoyancy Fb1 = pi d1 cubed rho g over 6]
    P1 --> F1C[Drag Fd1 = Cd times pi d1 squared rho vt1 squared over 8]
    F1A --> BAL1[Net Force Equals Zero at Terminal Velocity]
    F1B --> BAL1
    F1C --> BAL1
    BAL1 --> VT1[Terminal Velocity vt1 equals Stokes or Newton expression]

    P2[Particle 2: diameter d2, density rhoP2] --> F2A[Gravity Fg2 = pi d2 cubed rhoP2 g over 6]
    P2 --> F2B[Buoyancy Fb2 = pi d2 cubed rho g over 6]
    P2 --> F2C[Drag Fd2 = Cd times pi d2 squared rho vt2 squared over 8]
    F2A --> BAL2[Net Force Equals Zero at Terminal Velocity]
    F2B --> BAL2
    F2C --> BAL2
    BAL2 --> VT2[Terminal Velocity vt2 equals Stokes or Newton expression]

    VT1 --> EQ[Equal Settling Condition vt1 equals vt2]
    VT2 --> EQ
    EQ --> RES[Result: d1 to d2 ratio from equal settling equation]
```

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Regime-Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Particle Settling in Fluid] --> B[Compute Re equals d times vt times rho over mu]
    B --> C{Re less than 0.2}
    C -- Yes --> D[Stokes Regime: Cd equals 24 over Re]
    C -- No --> E{Re between 0.2 and 1000}
    E -- Yes --> F[Intermediate Regime: Cd equals 18.5 over Re to the 0.6]
    E -- No --> G{Re greater than 1000}
    G -- Yes --> H[Newton Regime: Cd equals 0.44]
    D --> I[Apply Equal Settling vt1 equals vt2]
    F --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Solve for d1 to d2 diameter ratio]
    J --> K[Report Cut Size or Separation Diameter]
```

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Concept Map: Equal Settling & Classification

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Mixture of Different Size and Density Particles] --> B[Feed to Classifier or Settling Tank]
    B --> C{Equal Settling Velocity?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Particles Report to Same Stream: Cannot Separate]
    C -- No --> E[Denser or Larger Particle Sinks Faster]
    E --> F[Underflow: Coarse Dense Fraction]
    D --> G[Overflow: Fine Light Fraction Plus Equal Settling Fraction]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Cut Size d50 from Equal Settling Calculation]
```

### 4.4 Figure 4 — Free vs. Hindered Settling Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Settling of Particles] --> S1[Dilute Suspension]
    S --> S2[Dense Suspension]

    S1 --> FS[Free Settling]
    FS --> FS1[Each Particle Settles Independently]
    FS1 --> FS2[Stokes Law or Newton Law Apply Directly]
    FS2 --> FS3[Equal Settling Concept Valid Here]

    S2 --> HS[Hindered Settling]
    HS --> HS1[Particles Interact; Upward Fluid Flow Created]
    HS1 --> HS2[Effective Density and Viscosity Modified]
    HS2 --> HS3[Use Richardson Zaki Equation or Empirical Correlations]
    HS3 --> HS4[Equal Settling Concept Modified for Hindered Case]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
Define **equal settling particles**. Derive the equal-settling condition for two particles settling under Stokes' law regime.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Equal settling particles** are particles of different size and density that attain the *same terminal velocity* in the same fluid under gravity.

Equating Stokes' law for two particles:

$$\frac{g \, d_1^2 (\rho_{p1} - \rho)}{18 \, \mu} \;=\; \frac{g \, d_2^2 (\rho_{p2} - \rho)}{18 \, \mu}$$

Simplifying:

$$\frac{d_1}{d_2} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{p2} - \rho}{\rho_{p1} - \rho}}$$

This relation is used to determine the *cut size* in classifiers. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
State the **significance of equal settling particles** in the design of sedimentation equipment.

**Model Answer:**

1. Determines the **separation size (cut size, $d_{50}$)** in hydraulic and mechanical classifiers.
2. Helps in selecting the correct **underflow and overflow streams** for grade-recovery optimisation.
3. Provides the theoretical basis for **hydrocyclone** and **thickener** design, where particles with equal settling velocity form the boundary between product streams.
4. Indicates the *limiting case* — beyond this size ratio, separation by sedimentation alone is impossible. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, every Part B question has a module-internal choice: students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B, but not both. Each sub-part is **7 marks**.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) Derive the equal-settling condition for two particles settling under Stokes' law regime. Clearly state the assumptions used. (7 marks)**

**Step 1.** Assumptions of Stokes regime:
- Spherical, smooth, rigid particles.
- Particles settle *freely* without interference.
- Reynolds number $Re_p < 0.2$ (creeping flow).
- Fluid is Newtonian and incompressible.
- Quiescent fluid (no turbulence).

**Step 2.** Force balance at terminal velocity:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^{3} (\rho_p - \rho) g \;=\; C_D \, \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \frac{\rho v_t^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $C_D = \dfrac{24\mu}{d v_t \rho}$ for Stokes regime and simplify to obtain:

$$v_t \;=\; \frac{g \, d^{2} (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \, \mu}$$

**Step 4.** For two equal-settling particles $v_{t1} = v_{t2}$:

$$\frac{d_{1}}{d_{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{p2} - \rho}{\rho_{p1} - \rho}}$$

**Marks distribution (as per KTU valuation key):**
- Assumptions — **2 marks**
- Force balance — **2 marks**
- Stokes' law derivation — **2 marks**
- Final equal-settling ratio — **1 mark**

**(b) A mixture of coal ($\rho_p = 1400 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) and sand ($\rho_p = 2600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) is to be separated by sedimentation in water. A coal particle has diameter $d_1 = 1 \, \text{mm}$. Find the diameter of the equal-settling sand particle. Assume Stokes regime is valid. (7 marks)**

**Step 1.** Write the equal-settling ratio (Stokes regime):

$$\frac{d_1}{d_2} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{p2} - \rho}{\rho_{p1} - \rho}}$$

Here $\rho_{p1}$ = coal = $1400 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{p2}$ = sand = $2600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step 2.** Substitute the values:

$$\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{d_2} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2600 - 1000}{1400 - 1000}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1600}{400}} \;=\; \sqrt{4} \;=\; 2$$

**Step 3.** Solve:

$$d_2 \;=\; \frac{1 \, \text{mm}}{2} \;=\; 0.5 \, \text{mm} \;=\; 500 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 4.** Verification of Stokes regime (1 mark):

Compute $v_t$ of the 1 mm coal particle using Stokes' law:

$$v_t \;=\; \frac{9.81 \times (10^{-3})^{2} \times (1400 - 1000)}{18 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 2.18 \times 10^{-1} \, \text{m/s}$$

Then:

$$Re_p \;=\; \frac{d \, v_t \, \rho}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{10^{-3} \times 0.218 \times 1000}{10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 218$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$Re_p \approx 218$ is FAR above 0.2, so Stokes regime is *not* valid here.** A correct answer should use the intermediate regime or note that Stokes' law over-predicts $v_t$. Full marks require this critical observation.

**Marks distribution:**
- Formula — **2 marks**
- Substitution — **2 marks**
- Final $d_2$ — **1 mark**
- Regime verification & critical comment — **2 marks**

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]

**(a) With neat sketches and relevant equations, explain the three settling regimes of a particle in a fluid. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A settling sphere is classified into three regimes based on the **particle Reynolds number** $Re_p = \dfrac{d v_t \rho}{\mu}$.

**Regime 1 — Stokes (Laminar) Regime:** $Re_p < 0.2$

Drag is dominated by viscous shear:

$$C_D \;=\; \frac{24}{Re_p}$$

$$v_t \;=\; \frac{g \, d^{2} (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \, \mu}$$

**Regime 2 — Intermediate (Transition) Regime:** $0.2 < Re_p < 1000$

Both viscous and inertial effects matter:

$$C_D \;=\; \frac{18.5}{Re_p^{0.6}}$$

$$v_t \;=\; \frac{0.153 \, d^{1.14} (\rho_p - \rho)^{0.71} g^{0.71}}{\rho^{0.29} \, \mu^{0.43}}$$

**Regime 3 — Newton (Turbulent) Regime:** $Re_p > 1000$

Drag is dominated by form/inertial effects:

$$C_D \;=\; 0.44$$

$$v_t \;=\; 1.73 \, \sqrt{\frac{g \, d (\rho_p - \rho)}{\rho}}$$

**Marks distribution:**
- Three regime identification with $Re_p$ ranges — **3 marks**
- $C_D$ for each — **2 marks**
- Velocity equations — **2 marks**

**(b) Two particles A and B have diameters $0.5 \, \text{mm}$ and $0.2 \, \text{mm}$ and densities $2500 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and $7500 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ respectively. Verify whether they are equal-settling in water at Stokes regime. If not, calculate the diameter of particle A that would make them equal-settling with particle B. (7 marks)**

**Step 1.** Compute equal-settling ratio assuming Stokes regime:

$$\frac{d_A}{d_B} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{pB} - \rho}{\rho_{pA} - \rho}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{7500 - 1000}{2500 - 1000}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{6500}{1500}} \;=\; \sqrt{4.333} \;\approx\; 2.08$$

**Step 2.** Required ratio for equal settling:

$$\frac{d_A}{d_B} \;=\; 2.08 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d_A}{0.2} \;=\; 2.08 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_A \;=\; 0.416 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3.** Compare with given $d_A = 0.5 \, \text{mm}$. Since $0.5 \neq 0.416$, the particles are **NOT equal-settling**.

**Step 4.** Required $d_A$ for equal settling with the given $d_B = 0.2 \, \text{mm}$: $d_A = 0.416 \, \text{mm}$.

**Marks distribution:**
- Equal-settling formula — **2 marks**
- Numerical computation — **2 marks**
- Verification step — **1 mark**
- Required new diameter — **2 marks**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Failing to verify the regime.** Always compute $Re_p$ before applying Stokes' law. If $Re_p > 0.2$, full marks may be *withheld* unless you explicitly state the regime is borderline.
> 2. **Forgetting to square/cancel.** Many students write $d_1 (\rho_{p1} - \rho) = d_2 (\rho_{p2} - \rho)$ (Newton's regime relation) when the question clearly specifies Stokes regime.
> 3. **Ignoring fluid density.** Some derivations drop $\rho$ from $(\rho_p - \rho)$, costing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency.** Mixing mm with m. Always convert diameters to **metres** before substituting.
> 5. **Using density of water as 1000 in numerical problems without stating it.** KTU valuation keys award the unit-conversion step explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Equal Settling Particles**

- **Definition:** Two particles with different $(d, \rho_p)$ having the **same terminal velocity** in the same fluid.
- **Stokes Regime Equal-Settling Ratio:** $\dfrac{d_1}{d_2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{p2} - \rho}{\rho_{p1} - \rho}}$
- **Newton Regime Equal-Settling Ratio:** $\dfrac{d_1}{d_2} = \dfrac{\rho_{p2} - \rho}{\rho_{p1} - \rho}$
- **Always verify regime** using $Re_p = \dfrac{d \, v_t \, \rho}{\mu}$.
- **Stokes limit:** $Re_p < 0.2$. **Newton limit:** $Re_p > 1000$.
- **Equal settling particles CANNOT be separated** by simple gravity sedimentation.
- **Cut size $d_{50}$** in classifiers is defined using equal-settling principles.
- **Free settling** assumes isolated particles; **hindered settling** uses Richardson–Zaki correction.
- **Physical meaning:** A denser particle can be *smaller* and still settle as fast as a lighter, larger one.
- **Standard values:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $\rho_{H_2O} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu_{H_2O} = 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$.
- **Engineering applications:** hydraulic classifiers, hydrocyclones, thickeners, air classifiers in cement industry.
- **Master formula sheet (Table 2.3)** is the single most important reference for KTU board problems on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical and Non-Mechanical Classifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanical and Non-Mechanical Classifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Classifier?

A **classifier** is a mechanical or non-mechanical device used in **particle technology** to separate a polydisperse suspension of solid particles in a fluid (typically water or air) into two or more fractions based on **size, density, or terminal settling velocity**. The oversize fraction is called the **coarse product (sands/underflow)** and the undersize fraction is called the **fine product (overflow/slimes)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A classifier is a device that separates a mixture of particles of different sizes into two or more portions on the basis of their **hydraulic (settling) characteristics** in a fluid medium.

## 1.2 Why Are Classifiers Required?

In the mineral, chemical, ceramic, and pharmaceutical industries, raw material streams (crushed ore, cement slurry, ceramic slip, pigments) contain a wide range of particle sizes. Most downstream unit operations — grinding, flotation, leaching, filtration, drying — perform optimally only with a **narrow size distribution**. Classifiers therefore act as **size-enforcement gates** placed either *before* (to reject already-fine material from a mill — closed-circuit grinding) or *after* a size-reduction step.

## 1.3 The Master Classification: Mechanical vs. Non-Mechanical

Classifiers are broadly divided based on **how the separating fluid–particle motion is achieved**:

- **Mechanical Classifiers:** Use a **physical, moving mechanical element** (rakes, spirals, bowls, buckets) to transport settled coarse solids up an inclined surface and out of the separating tank. Fluid drag is aided by gravity and mechanical conveying.
- **Non-Mechanical Classifiers:** Rely **solely on fluid dynamics and gravity** (or centrifugal force), without any moving solid parts conveying the coarse product. Examples: Spitzkasten, Dorr thickener, hydrocyclone, sedimentation tank.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Paddy & Mud Analogy**
> Imagine a farmer washing rice in a wide, shallow tub:
> 1. Mud (fine particles) stays suspended and washes out with the overflow water (non-mechanical, gravity-driven elutriation).
> 2. Paddy grains (coarse, dense particles) settle to the bottom.
> 3. The farmer uses a **wooden rake** to scoop out the settled paddy (this is the *mechanical conveying* action).
>
> If the rake is removed, separation still occurs, but someone has to manually bail out the settled paddy — that is essentially a **non-mechanical classifier**. If a **spiral screw** continuously lifts the grains out, it is a **spiral classifier (mechanical)**.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics in Classification

The following metrics are used throughout KTU problems and must be memorized:

- **Cut Size (d₅₀)** — particle size at which 50 % of particles report to the underflow and 50 % to the overflow.
- **Diameter of Separation (d_sep)** — the actual separating size reported by the classifier.
- **Correctness (η_c)** — fraction of particles correctly classified into their proper stream (dimensionless, **0–1**).
- **Imperfections (η_i)** — deviation from ideal separation.
- **Reduced Efficiency (η_r)** — corrected efficiency removing bypass (fines contamination of coarse stream).

---

## 1.6 Headline Distinction Table

| Property | Mechanical Classifier | Non-Mechanical Classifier |
|---|---|---|
| Moving parts | Yes (rakes/spirals/buckets) | No (only fluid flows) |
| Energy input | Mechanical drive + pump | Only pump / feed pressure |
| Maintenance | High (bearings, drives) | Very low |
| Typical cut size | 100 µm – 2 mm | 5 µm – 500 µm |
| Capacity | Very high | Moderate to high |
| Examples | Rake, Spiral, Bowl, Cone | Spitzkasten, Thickener, Hydrocyclone |
| Wear-prone areas | Rakes, flights, drags | Liners (cyclone) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of All Classifiers

Regardless of type, every classifier exploits the **balance between two forces** on a settling particle:

1. **Downward force:** Gravity minus buoyancy (effective gravitational pull).
2. **Upward force:** Fluid drag (resistance).

The particle reaches a **terminal settling velocity** when these balance.

$$F_g - F_b = F_d$$

The result, **Stokes' Law** (laminar, Re < 0.2), is the foundation of classifier design:

$$v_t = \frac{g \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$$

where $\rho_s$ is solid density, $\rho_f$ is fluid density, $d_p$ is particle diameter, $\mu$ is fluid viscosity, and **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

> [!NOTE]
> For non-laminar conditions (Re > 0.2), the **Newton**, **Allen**, and **transition** regime correlations apply; KTU problems typically restrict to the **laminar (Stokes) regime** unless specified otherwise.

## 2.2 Classification by Tracer Concept — The Plitt Approach

A classifier can be modelled as a **mixer**, where:
- Coarse stream (underflow, **S**) contains fraction $S_x$ of size $x$.
- Fine stream (overflow, **L**) contains the rest.

The **partition (correctness) curve** $f(x)$ plots the fraction of particles of size $x$ reporting to the **coarse product**. For an ideal classifier, $f(x)$ is a step function; for real classifiers, it is a sigmoidal curve centred at $d_{50}$.

## 2.3 Mechanical Classifiers — How Each Works

### 2.3.1 Rake Classifier
- A rectangular tank inclined at **15°–25°**.
- A reciprocating **rake (or flight)** moves up the inclined trough, dragging settled coarse solids up the slope.
- Settling occurs in the liquid pool at the bottom.
- **Cut size:** typically **0.1 – 0.8 mm**.

### 2.3.2 Spiral Classifier
- A **continuous helical screw** rotates at low speed (2–6 rpm) inside an inclined trough.
- The screw conveys settled solids up the slope while simultaneously mixing the slurry.
- Two variants: **low weir** (more fines overflow) and **high weir** (coarser overflow).
- **Cut size:** **0.1 – 1.5 mm**.

### 2.3.3 Bowl / Hardinge Classifier
- A circular bowl with a rotating rake at the bottom and a central overflow launder.
- Achieves **finer separations** than rake/spiral classifiers.
- **Cut size:** as low as **75 µm**.

### 2.3.4 Cone Classifier
- An inverted cone with a stirrer at the apex.
- Spigot at the bottom discharges coarse solids.
- Used for **desliming** (washing off ultra-fines).

### 2.3.5 Bucket (Dredge) Classifier
- A chain of buckets lifts settled solids up an inclined trough.
- Useful for very coarse separations (> 1 mm).

## 2.4 Non-Mechanical Classifiers — How Each Works

### 2.4.1 Spitzkasten
- A series of **inverted pyramidal or conical tanks** of increasing cross-section.
- As area increases, **upward velocity decreases**, so progressively finer particles overflow each stage.
- **Cut size per stage:** calculated from residence time and settling velocity.

### 2.4.2 Dorr Thickener (Sedimentation Tank)
- A large circular tank with a slowly rotating **central feed well** and a peripheral **overflow launder**.
- Solids settle radially; settled slurry is scraped to a central underflow by slowly rotating rakes (only *rakes* here act as scrapers, not as conveying elements — hence non-mechanical in classification sense).
- **Function:** mainly **clarification and thickening**, with classification as a side-effect.

### 2.4.3 Elutriator
- An **upward-flow column** where fluid velocity equals the terminal velocity of the desired cut size.
- Particles settling faster than $v_t$ settle down (coarse); particles settling slower are carried up (fine).
- **Vertical-current elutriator** (multiple pipes) and **scatter-plate elutriator** are common.

### 2.4.4 Hydrocyclone
- Tangential feed creates a **spiral downward–upward vortex**.
- Coarse solids exit at the apex (underflow); fines exit at the top overflow.
- Operates on **centrifugal** rather than gravitational acceleration (effective $g \to 10$–$100 \times g$).
- **Cut size:** **5 µm – 300 µm** — much finer than gravity classifiers.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_t = \dfrac{g \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$ | Stokes' terminal velocity (laminar, Re < 0.2) | m/s |
| 2 | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_f \, v_t \, d_p}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number (check regime) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, g \, d_p \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_f}}$ | General terminal velocity (Newton regime) | m/s |
| 4 | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \, \mu \, v_o}{g(\rho_s - \rho_f)}}$ | Cut size from overflow velocity $v_o$ | m |
| 5 | $v_o = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Overflow velocity = volumetric flow / tank area | m/s |
| 6 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{\sum \vert f(x) - \phi(x) \vert \Delta x}{2}$ | Correctness from partition curve | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_r = \dfrac{(\text{Actual coarse − Fines in coarse})}{(\text{Total feed fines})}$ | Reduced efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $v_o \le v_t$ for separation | Elutriation criterion | m/s |
| 9 | $Q \cdot C = U \cdot U_c + O \cdot O_c$ | Overall material balance | t/h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When solving numericals, always **first verify the flow regime** using $Re_p = \rho_f v_t d_p / \mu$. KTU examiners specifically check whether you blindly applied Stokes' Law in the wrong regime.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mineral Beneficiation:** Hydraulic classifiers (Spitzkasten, hydrocyclone) are the first step before froth flotation to **deslime** kaolin, phosphate, iron ore, and bauxite.
- **Cement Industry:** Closed-circuit ball mills use **spiral or rake classifiers** to recycle oversize; **air classifiers** finish the cement.
- **Chemical Industry:** Hydrocyclones separate catalyst fines, recover salt crystals, and clarify brine.
- **Pharma/Food:** Air classifiers and elutriators size dry powders without contamination.
- **Wastewater Treatment:** Dorr thickeners concentrate activated sludge; grit classifiers remove sand.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stokes' Law (Terminal Settling Velocity)

We derive from first principles, step-by-step, because it is the single most important equation in the entire Module 1.

### Step 1 — Identify forces on a spherical particle settling in still fluid

A spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ falls under gravity in a viscous fluid. Three forces act on it:

1. **Gravity (weight):** downward, $W = m \cdot g = \rho_s \cdot V \cdot g$.
2. **Buoyancy (upthrust):** upward, $F_b = \rho_f \cdot V \cdot g$.
3. **Drag force:** opposing motion, $F_d$.

In the **laminar regime** (Re < 0.2), the drag force follows **Stokes' drag law**:

$$F_d = 3 \pi \mu \, d_p \, v$$

where $v$ is the instantaneous particle velocity.

### Step 2 — Express volumes in terms of diameter

For a sphere: $V = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6}$

$$W = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, \rho_s \, g$$

$$F_b = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, \rho_f \, g$$

### Step 3 — Apply the equilibrium condition at terminal velocity

At terminal velocity, **net force = 0**, hence $W = F_b + F_d$:

$$\frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, \rho_s \, g = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, \rho_f \, g + 3 \pi \mu \, d_p \, v_t$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange

Move buoyancy to LHS:

$$\frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, (\rho_s - \rho_f) \, g = 3 \pi \mu \, d_p \, v_t$$

### Step 5 — Cancel $\pi$ from both sides and solve for $v_t$

$$v_t = \frac{\pi d_p^3 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{6 \cdot 3 \pi \mu \, d_p}$$

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 \, \mu}$$

This is **Stokes' Law** for terminal settling velocity of a sphere in laminar flow. Verified by dimensional analysis: $\dfrac{m^2 \cdot (kg/m^3) \cdot (m/s^2)}{kg/(m\cdot s)} = \dfrac{kg/(m \cdot s^2) \cdot m^3 / m^2}{kg/(m \cdot s)} = m/s$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Cut Size in an Upward-Flow Classifier (Elutriator)

**Problem setup:** A vertical column of cross-section $A$ has an upward fluid velocity $v_o = Q/A$. A particle will report to the **overflow (fine)** only if its terminal velocity is **less than or equal to** the upward fluid velocity; otherwise it falls to the underflow.

### Step 1 — Write elutriation criterion

For a particle at the boundary (cut size $d_{cut}$):

$$v_t(d_{cut}) = v_o$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Stokes' terminal velocity

$$\frac{g \, d_{cut}^2 \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu} = v_o$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the cut size $d_{cut}$

$$d_{cut}^2 = \frac{18 \, \mu \, v_o}{g \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{cut} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, v_o}{g \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental design equation** for an elutriator and an overflow Spitzkasten stage.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Question:** A quartz particle ($\rho_s$ = 2650 kg/m³) is settling in water at 20 °C ($\rho_f$ = 1000 kg/m³, $\mu$ = 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s). The upward fluid velocity in an elutriator is $v_o = 0.005$ m/s. Find the cut size $d_{cut}$.

**Solution:**

$$d_{cut} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times (1.002 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.005}{9.81 \times (2650 - 1000)}}$$

**Numerator:** $18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.005 = 9.018 \times 10^{-5}$

**Denominator:** $9.81 \times 1650 = 16,186.5$

**Inside the square root:** $\dfrac{9.018 \times 10^{-5}}{1.61865 \times 10^{4}} = 5.572 \times 10^{-9}$ m²

**Square root:** $d_{cut} = \sqrt{5.572 \times 10^{-9}} = 7.466 \times 10^{-5}$ m = **74.66 µm**

**Verification of regime:** $v_t = 0.005$ m/s, $d_p = 7.466 \times 10^{-5}$ m

$$Re_p = \frac{\rho_f v_t d_p}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.005 \times 7.466 \times 10^{-5}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{3.733 \times 10^{-4}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.372$$

Since $Re_p > 0.2$, the regime is **not strictly laminar**. For KTU answer purposes, note that Stokes' law overestimates the size slightly; a more accurate correlation from the Allen (transition) regime should ideally be used. However, KTU marks the answer as **74.7 µm (≈ 75 µm)** based on Stokes' law.

---

## 3.4 Spitzkasten Design — Cumulative Mass Balance

For an N-stage Spitzkasten operating in series, the design equation for the cross-sectional area of the $i$-th stage is:

$$A_i = \frac{Q}{v_{t,i}}$$

where $v_{t,i}$ is the terminal velocity corresponding to the desired cut of stage $i$. Since cut size decreases stage by stage, $A_i$ **increases** stage by stage — explaining why each tank is **larger** than the previous one (the hallmark geometry of a Spitzkasten bank).

For 3 stages with cut sizes $d_1 > d_2 > d_3$:

$$A_1 < A_2 < A_3$$

Cumulative overflow concentration (mass of fines in overflow of stage $i$):

$$C_{i} = C_{i-1} - \frac{\Delta C_i \cdot A_i}{A_i + A_{i+1}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mechanical vs. Non-Mechanical Decision Tree

| Decision Parameter | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| Cut size > 1 mm | Mechanical (rake/bucket) |
| Cut size 100 µm – 1 mm | Spiral or bowl (mechanical) |
| Cut size 50 – 150 µm | Cone or hydrocyclone |
| Cut size < 50 µm | Hydrocyclone, elutriator, centrifuge |
| Feed is dilute liquid | Spitzkasten, Dorr |
| Feed is dry powder | Air classifier |
| Need high underflow density | Mechanical (rake/spiral) |
| Minimum maintenance | Non-mechanical (hydrocyclone) |
| Fines overflow (large throughput) | Dorr thickener |
| Crystalline or fragile solids | Elutriator / hydrocyclone |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Master Taxonomy — Classifiers

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CLASSIFIERS] --> B[Mechanical]
    A --> C[Non-Mechanical]
    B --> B1[Rake Classifier]
    B --> B2[Spiral Classifier]
    B --> B3[Bowl Hardinge Classifier]
    B --> B4[Cone Classifier]
    B --> B5[Bucket Classifier]
    C --> C1[Spitzkasten]
    C --> C2[Dorr Thickener]
    C --> C3[Elutriator]
    C --> C4[Hydrocyclone]
    C --> C5[Sedimentation Tank]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Operational Flow of a Spiral Classifier (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    F[Feed Slurry] --> T[Inclined Trough]
    T --> S[Settling Zone]
    S -- coarse particles settle --> R[Rake and Spiral Drive]
    R -- lifts coarse --> U[Underflow Coarse Product]
    S -- fine particles overflow --> OF[Overflow Fine Product]
    OF --> LA[Overflow Launder]
    U --> ST[Stream Discharge]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Hydrocyclone Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Tangential Feed Inlet] --> VORT[Vortex Finder Region]
    VORT -- fine particles go up --> OF2[Overflow Outlet Top]
    VORT -- coarse particles go down --> APEX[Apex Nozzle]
    APEX --> UF2[Underflow Coarse Outlet]
    IN -- centripetal force --> FF2[Forces Coarse Outward and Downward]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Spitzkasten Multi-Stage Cascade

```mermaid
graph LR
    F2[Feed Slurry] --> ST1[Stage 1 Smallest Tank Coarse Overflow]
    ST1 --> ST2[Stage 2 Medium Tank]
    ST2 --> ST3[Stage 3 Largest Tank Fines Overflow]
    ST1 -- settled coarse --> DISP1[Coarse Discharge Stage 1]
    ST2 -- settled coarse --> DISP2[Coarse Discharge Stage 2]
    ST3 -- settled coarse --> DISP3[Coarse Discharge Stage 3]
    ST3 -- final fines --> FINOF[Final Overflow Clarified]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid — Cut-Size Decision Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Choose Classifier Based on Cut Size] --> Q1{What is d50?}
    Q1 -- greater than 1000 microns --> M1[Use Mechanical Bucket or Rake]
    Q1 -- between 100 and 1000 microns --> M2[Use Spiral or Bowl Mechanical]
    Q1 -- between 50 and 150 microns --> M3[Use Cone or Hydrocyclone]
    Q1 -- less than 50 microns --> M4[Use Elutriator or Centrifuge]
    M1 --> END[Selected]
    M2 --> END
    M3 --> END
    M4 --> END
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Generic Classifier Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subIN[Slurry Feed Pump] --> INLET[Inlet Feed Zone]
    INLET --> POOL[Settling Pool Under Gravity or Centrifugal]
    POOL --> COARSE[Coarse Stream Underflow]
    POOL --> FINE[Fine Stream Overflow]
    COARSE --> VALVE[Discharge Valve or Conveyor]
    FINE --> OVERFLO_LAUNDER[Overflow Launder to Downstream Process]
    POOL -- fluid dynamics controls separation --> CONTROL[C_d Drag and v_t control]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Series I]**

> Differentiate between a **mechanical classifier** and a **non-mechanical classifier** with at least two distinct points. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Definition:** A mechanical classifier uses a moving mechanical conveying element (rake, spiral, bucket) to remove settled coarse solids from the separating zone, whereas a non-mechanical classifier relies solely on **gravity or centrifugal force** acting on the particle without any moving conveying element. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Example:** Mechanical — *Rake Classifier / Spiral Classifier / Bowl Classifier*. Non-Mechanical — *Spitzkasten / Dorr Thickener / Hydrocyclone / Elutriator*. **(1 mark)**

3. **Operating cost:** Mechanical classifiers have higher power consumption and wear due to moving parts; non-mechanical classifiers are simpler to maintain. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Series II]**

> State **Stokes' Law** for terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle. Under what conditions is it valid?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$v_t = \frac{g \, d_p^2 \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$$

**Conditions for validity: (any two, 1 mark each)**

1. The flow regime around the particle must be **laminar**, i.e., particle Reynolds number $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_f v_t d_p}{\mu} < 0.2$.
2. The particle is a **smooth, rigid sphere**.
3. The fluid is **incompressible**, **Newtonian**, and the flow is **steady**.
4. There is **no interference** from other particles (dilute suspension).

**(1 mark for correct equation; 2 marks for conditions.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 1]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> Describe the working principle and construction of a **Spiral Classifier** with a neat sketch. Mention its advantages and limitations.

#### Model Solution (Part a)

**Working Principle: (3 marks)**

A spiral classifier consists of an **inclined semi-cylindrical trough** tilted at an angle of 12°–18° from the horizontal. A helical screw (or multiple screws) rotates slowly inside this trough. The slurry is fed at the lower end of the trough. The coarser, denser particles settle to the bottom due to gravity; the fine particles remain in suspension and overflow the weir at the lower end.

The rotating spiral **mechanically lifts** the settled coarse solids up the inclined trough and discharges them at the upper end as the **underflow product**. The spiral simultaneously *mixes and agitates* the slurry, which improves settling of the fine particles.

**Construction: (2 marks)**

- Inclined steel trough with **feed inlet (lower end)** and **discharge spout (upper end)**.
- **Helical screw** mounted on a hollow shaft, driven through a gear-reducer.
- **Pulp lift** mechanism for high-weir variants.
- **Submerged bearing** at lower end; **oversize discharge** at upper end.
- **Two-spiral type** used for very high capacities.

**Advantages: (1 mark)**

1. Continuous operation, high capacity.
2. Adjustable overflow weir for cut-size variation.
3. Relatively low power consumption.
4. Easy maintenance.

**Limitations: (1 mark)**

1. Heavy wear of spiral flights in abrasive slurries.
2. Cannot classify ultra-fines (< 100 µm) effectively.
3. Operating angle is fixed by design; difficult to adjust on the run.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> A quartz–water slurry ($\rho_s = 2650$ kg/m³) flows through an **elutriator column** of cross-sectional area $A = 0.05$ m² at a volumetric flow rate $Q = 0.0025$ m³/s. Find:
> (i) the upward fluid velocity in the column,
> (ii) the cut size (assume Stokes' regime; $\rho_w$ = 1000 kg/m³, $\mu_w$ = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, g = 9.81 m/s²).

#### Model Solution (Part b)

**Step 1 — Upward fluid velocity (2 marks)**

$$v_o = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.0025}{0.05} = 0.05 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Cut size calculation (5 marks)**

Using the elutriation criterion $v_t = v_o$ at cut size:

$$d_{cut} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \, \mu \, v_o}{g(\rho_s - \rho_f)}}$$

**Substituting values:**

$$d_{cut} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.05}{9.81 \times (2650 - 1000)}}$$

**Numerator:** $18 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05 = 9.0 \times 10^{-4}$

**Denominator:** $9.81 \times 1650 = 16,186.5$

**Inside the root:** $\dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.61865 \times 10^{4}} = 5.5608 \times 10^{-8}$ m²

$$d_{cut} = \sqrt{5.5608 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.358 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx \mathbf{236 \;\mu m}$$

**Final Answer (1 mark for unit conversion and significant figures):**

> $v_o$ = **0.05 m/s**; $d_{cut}$ = **2.36 × 10⁻⁴ m (≈ 236 µm)**

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 1, Alternate Set]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a **hydrocyclone** as a non-mechanical classifier. List its industrial applications.

#### Model Solution (Part a)

**Working Principle: (3 marks)**

A hydrocyclone is a cylindrical–conical vessel in which **centrifugal acceleration** (replacing gravity in a conventional classifier) separates particles. The feed is introduced **tangentially** through an inlet at the top under pressure (typically 30–200 kPa). The tangential entry creates a **vortex** inside the cylinder; this vortex produces a centrifugal force many times greater than gravity.

Particles denser than the fluid are flung **outward and downward** along the conical wall and exit through the **apex (spigot)** as the coarse underflow. The clarified (or fine-particle-rich) fluid reverses direction near the apex and spirals **upward** along the axis through the **vortex finder**, exiting through the **overflow pipe** at the top.

**Construction: (2 marks)**

- **Cylindrical section** with tangential feed inlet.
- **Conical section** terminating in the **apex nozzle** (underflow).
- **Vortex finder** (cylindrical tube) projecting into the top to extract overflow.
- **Two outlets:** overflow (top) and underflow (bottom apex).
- Liners of ceramic, rubber, or polyurethane to resist abrasion.

**Industrial Applications: (2 marks — list any 4)**

1. **Mineral processing:** desliming of iron ore, kaolin, bauxite before flotation.
2. **Cement industry:** closed-circuit grinding, separator for raw mill and cement mill.
3. **Chemical industry:** salt crystallization, catalyst recovery, brine clarification.
4. **Wastewater treatment:** grit removal, sludge thickening.
5. **Oil industry:** drilling mud solids control.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> A Dorr thickener receives **250 t/h of slurry** at 12 % solids by weight. The underflow is withdrawn at 65 % solids. Determine the mass flow rate of the underflow and the overflow. Take $\rho_{solids} = 2500$ kg/m³ and $\rho_{slurry\_liquid}$ = 1000 kg/m³.

#### Model Solution (Part b)

**Given:** Feed = 250 t/h at 12 wt % solids.

**Mass of solids in feed:** (2 marks)

$$S_F = 250 \times 0.12 = 30 \text{ t/h}$$

**Mass of liquid in feed:**

$$L_F = 250 - 30 = 220 \text{ t/h}$$

**Underflow:** 65 wt % solids

**Solids balance across the unit:** (2 marks)

Since all solids must leave via the underflow (overflow is clarified liquid):

$$S_U = S_F = 30 \text{ t/h}$$

**Underflow total mass flow:** (2 marks)

$$S_U = 0.65 \times M_U \implies M_U = \frac{30}{0.65} = 46.15 \text{ t/h}$$

**Overflow liquid mass flow:** (1 mark)

$$L_{overflow} = L_F - L_U = 220 - (46.15 - 30) = 220 - 16.15 = 203.85 \text{ t/h}$$

**Total overflow mass flow** (essentially liquid with trace fines):

$$M_O \approx L_{overflow} = \mathbf{203.85 \text{ t/h}}$$

**Final Answer (1 mark for clarity):**

> - **Underflow mass flow rate:** $M_U$ = **46.15 t/h** at 65 wt % solids.
> - **Overflow mass flow rate:** $M_O$ = **203.85 t/h** (≈ clear liquid).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the flow regime** before applying Stokes' Law. If $Re_p > 0.2$, full credit is **not** given for a Stokes-only solution. Always compute $Re_p$ and mention the regime.
> 2. **Wrong sign in $(\rho_s - \rho_f)$:** Density difference is a positive quantity; using it negatively yields imaginary velocities and an automatic zero. KTU examiners frequently test this.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing mm with m³/s or kg/L with kg/m³. Always convert to SI at the start.
> 4. **Missing the word "spherical"** in Stokes' Law answer — the law applies only to spheres; non-spherical particles require a **shape factor correction** (K = d_n / d_v).
> 5. **Confusing partition curve with grade-efficiency curve** in efficiency problems. Partition curve is for individual particle sizes; grade-efficiency is corrected for bypass.
> 6. **Spiral classifier answer:** Not mentioning **"low-weir vs high-weir"** distinction leads to a half-mark cut.
> 7. **Hydrocyclone answer:** Forgetting to state the **pressure drop** in operating parameters loses the marks tagged to "operating variables."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanical & Non-Mechanical Classifiers**

### Must-Memorize Definitions
- Classifier: device for separating particles by settling (hydraulic) characteristics.
- Cut size $d_{50}$: size at which 50 % reports to coarse and 50 % to fine stream.
- Terminal velocity $v_t$: velocity at which drag = net gravitational force.

### Must-Memorize Equations
- **Stokes' Law:** $v_t = \dfrac{g \, d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$ — valid only when **Re < 0.2**.
- **Cut-size from overflow velocity:** $d_{cut} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \mu \, v_o}{g(\rho_s - \rho_f)}}$.
- **Overflow velocity:** $v_o = Q / A$.
- **Re check:** $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_f \, v_t \, d_p}{\mu}$.

### Must-Memorize Equipment
| Equipment | Type | Typical Cut Size |
|---|---|---|
| Rake | Mechanical | 100 – 800 µm |
| Spiral | Mechanical | 100 – 1500 µm |
| Bowl | Mechanical | 75 – 250 µm |
| Cone | Mechanical | 100 – 300 µm |
| Bucket | Mechanical | > 1 mm |
| Spitzkasten | Non-Mechanical | 50 – 500 µm |
| Elutriator | Non-Mechanical | 5 – 200 µm |
| Dorr Thickener | Non-Mechanical | clarification |
| Hydrocyclone | Non-Mechanical | 5 – 300 µm |

### Must-Remember Concepts
- Mechanical classifiers **use a moving element to lift** settled coarse solids; non-mechanical classifiers **rely only on fluid drag and gravity (or centrifugal force)**.
- **Stokes' Law regime** must be verified using $Re_p < 0.2$.
- **Spitzkasten:** successive stages have **increasing cross-sectional area** because finer cut sizes need slower upward velocity.
- **Hydrocyclone:** uses **centrifugal force** (10–100 g) — much finer cut sizes achievable than gravity classifiers.
- **Dorr Thickener:** primarily a clarification/thickening device; classification is a secondary function.
- **Elutriator:** uses **upward fluid velocity equal to $v_t$ of desired cut size** — basic principle for several industrial classifiers.
- **Material balance:** always perform an overall mass balance and component balance in numerical problems.

### Typical 14-Mark Numerical Structure in KTU ESE
1. Identification of regime (1 mark)
2. Use of appropriate law / formula (2 marks)
3. Substitution (2 marks)
4. Calculation (2 marks)
5. Final answer with units (1 mark) — total 8 marks for computation part + 6 marks for descriptive part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Mineral Beneficiation Methods: Jigging, Wilfley Table, and Froth Flotation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Mineral Beneficiation Methods: Jigging, Wilfley Table, and Froth Flotation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — PRINCIPLES OF MINERAL BENEFICIATION METHODS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 What is Mineral Beneficiation?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Mineral Beneficiation** (also called **Ore Dressing** or **Mineral Processing**) is the branch of particle technology that deals with the physical and physico-chemical separation of valuable mineral particles (*valuables*) from the worthless gangue (*matrix*), exploiting differences in properties such as **density, size, shape, magnetic susceptibility, surface wettability, and electrical conductivity**.

The **objective** is to produce a **concentrate** (high-grade product) with minimum **tailings** (reject), while maximizing **recovery**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Focus**
> The three methods — **Jigging, Wilfley Shaking Table, and Froth Flotation** — collectively cover *gravity separation* (jigging & tabling) and *surface-chemistry separation* (flotation). Together, they account for the concentration of >90% of metallic and non-metallic ores globally.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine panning for **gold** in a river. The swirling water washes away the lighter sand particles, but the heavy gold flakes sink to the bottom and stay in your pan. This is **gravity concentration** — the same principle used in Jigging and the Wilfley Table.

Now imagine you have a mixture of two powders that have the **same density but different surface chemistry**. You cannot wash them apart with water alone — but you can make one powder *hydrophobic* (water-repelling) using a chemical called a **collector**, blow air bubbles through the slurry, and the bubbles carry only the hydrophobic particles to the top. This is **froth flotation**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary**
> *Jigging* = "Stratify by pulsing water so heavies go down, lights go up."
> *Wilfley Table* = "Flow water across an inclined shaking riffled table so heavies move opposite to the wash."
> *Froth Flotation* = "Coat wanted particles with oil, attach them to air bubbles, and skim the froth."

### 1.1.3 The Driving Property in Each Method

| Method | Property Exploited | Range of Particle Size | Density Range (SG) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Jigging** | Density difference under pulsating fluid | **0.5 – 50 mm** | 1.3 – 8.0 |
| **Wilfley Table** | Density + Size in a flowing film | **50 µm – 2 mm** | 1.5 – 19.0 |
| **Froth Flotation** | Surface wettability (contact angle) | **10 – 200 µm** | Independent of density |

---

## 1.2 Why Beneficiation is Required

> [!NOTE]
> *Run-of-Mine (ROM)* ore typically contains only **0.5 – 5 %** of the valuable metal as a sulfide/oxide/elemental mineral. Direct smelting of such dilute ore is economically and environmentally catastrophic. Beneficiation upgrades the feed to **20 – 70 %** metal content before pyrometallurgical or hydrometallurgical extraction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Common Theoretical Foundation: Settling of Particles

All three methods ultimately rely on **relative motion between particles and a fluid** (water or air). The single most important equation is the **terminal (free-settling) velocity** of a sphere.

For a spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$ settling in a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$, force balance at terminal velocity yields:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4\, g\, d\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3\, C_D\, \rho_f}}$$

where $C_D$ is the **drag coefficient**, a function of the **Reynolds Number**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho_f\, v_t\, d}{\mu}$$

### 2.1.1 The Three Flow Regimes (Drag Coefficient Correlations)

| Regime | Reynolds Range | Drag Coefficient $C_D$ | Terminal Velocity $v_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stokes' Law** (laminar) | $Re < 0.2$ | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re}$ | $v_t = \dfrac{g\,d^{2}(\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18\,\mu}$ |
| **Intermediate (Allen)** | $0.2 < Re < 1000$ | $C_D = \dfrac{18.5}{Re^{0.6}}$ | $v_t = 0.27\sqrt{\dfrac{g\,d(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,Re^{0.6}}{\rho_f}}$ |
| **Newton's Law** (turbulent) | $1000 < Re < 2 \times 10^{5}$ | $C_D \approx 0.44$ | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.03\,g\,d(\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Settling Law vs Particle Size — KTU Favourite Question**
> *Stokes' law* applies to **fine particles** (typically < 50 µm in water). For coarse ore particles in a jig, Newton's law applies. You must **always verify the Reynolds number** before applying a formula — a common KTU valuation trap!

## 2.2 Method 1 — JIGGING

### 2.2.1 Principle

Jigging separates particles of **different densities** in a fluid (usually water) subjected to a **pulsating vertical flow**. During the **pulsion stroke** (upward flow), the bed dilates and particles stratify; during the **suction stroke** (downward flow), the bed compacts. **Heavier particles** (valuables) settle to the **bottom** (hindered settling); **lighter particles** (gangue) remain near the top and are carried off as overflow.

### 2.2.2 Concentration Criterion (CC) — The Decisive Parameter

$$CC = \frac{\rho_h - \rho_f}{\rho_l - \rho_f}$$

where $\rho_h$ = density of heavy mineral, $\rho_l$ = density of light mineral, $\rho_f$ = density of medium (water).

| CC Value | Separability by Gravity |
|---|---|
| $> 2.5$ | Easy — single-stage jig sufficient |
| $1.75 < CC < 2.5$ | Possible with multi-stage jig |
| $1.25 < CC < 1.75$ | Difficult, needs heavy-medium separation |
| $< 1.25$ | Not feasible by gravity |

### 2.2.3 Jig Types Studied in KTU

1. **Harz Jig** — piston-actuated, used for coarse coal and metallic ores.
2. **Baum Jig** — uses air pulsation under water; standard for coal washing (10–50 mm).
3. **Hancock Jig** — travelling screen jig for fine coal (0.5–10 mm).
4. **Denver Mineral Jig** — used for fine metallics (down to 75 µm).

### 2.2.4 Key Operating Variables

* **Stroke length** — typically 5–25 mm (longer for coarser particles).
* **Stroke frequency** — 50–300 strokes/min.
* **Water addition** — control water distribution at top.
* **Feed rate** and **bed depth** — critical for hindered-settling conditions.

## 2.3 Method 2 — WILFLEY SHAKING TABLE

### 2.3.1 Principle

The Wilfley Table is an **inclined, riffled, oscillating surface** over which a thin film of water flows perpendicular to the direction of table motion. Particles are subjected to **two competing forces**:

1. **Differential acceleration along the riffles** — imparted by the asymmetric forward-and-back shake (stroke length ≈ 10–25 mm; frequency ≈ 250–350 rpm).
2. **Hydraulic wash by flowing film water** — flowing down the slope.

The **heavy particles** move *along* the riffles (slow, small displacement) toward the **concentrate end**, while **light particles** are swept by water across the riffles toward the **tailing end**. **Middlings** exit between the two.

### 2.3.2 The Three Product Streams

| Stream | Composition | Location on Table |
|---|---|---|
| **Concentrate** | High-grade heavies | Upper end of riffles, near corner |
| **Middling** | Mixed | Diagonal centre band — recirculated |
| **Tailings** | Light gangue | Lower end, washed off the edge |

### 2.3.3 Important Operating Variables

* **Tilt angle** of deck: 0.5° – 6° (typically 2°–5°).
* **Stroke length** and **riffle height** decrease from feed end to concentrate end.
* **Feed pulp density** ≈ 15–25 % solids by weight.
* **Wash water flow**: 5–15 L/min per metre of table width.

### 2.3.4 Typical Applications

* Tungsten (Wolframite, Scheelite), Tin (Cassiterite), Chromite, Iron ore, Coal fines.
* Removes particles differing in density by **as little as 0.1–0.5 SG units**.

## 2.4 Method 3 — FROTH FLOTATION

### 2.4.1 Principle

Froth flotation exploits **differences in surface wettability**. The ore is ground to a fine slurry (~75 µm), conditioned with **reagents**, and aerated in a cell. **Hydrophobic** (water-repelling) particles attach to rising air bubbles and are carried into the froth at the top, which is skimmed off. **Hydrophilic** (water-loving) particles remain in the slurry and are removed as tailings.

### 2.4.2 Contact Angle — The Master Variable

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}}$$

This is **Young's Equation**, where $\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LV}$ are the **interfacial tensions** at solid–vapour, solid–liquid, and liquid–vapour interfaces.

The **Work of Adhesion** of the bubble to the particle is:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}\,(1 + \cos\theta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *A high contact angle $\theta$ (closer to 90°) means a higher work of adhesion and a more strongly floatable particle. Collectors like potassium amyl xanthate (KAX) make sulfide mineral surfaces hydrophobic by raising $\theta$ from ~0° (fully hydrophilic) to ~70°–90°.*

### 2.4.3 The Three Classes of Flotation Reagents (KTU Favourite Topic)

| Class | Function | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Collectors** | Adsorb on mineral surface, render it hydrophobic | Xanthates, dithiophosphates, fatty acids |
| **Frothers** | Stabilise the air bubble froth | Pine oil, MIBC, alcohols |
| **Modifiers** | Activate or depress specific minerals | Lime (CaO), NaCN, CuSO₄, Na₂S |

> [!TIP]
> **Easy Memory Aid:** *"Collectors bring particles UP; Frothers keep the bubbles UP; Modifiers decide WHO goes up."*

### 2.4.4 Probability & Kinetics of Flotation

The classical first-order rate equation for flotation is:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k\,C \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C(t) = C_0\, e^{-k t}$$

where $C$ is the concentration of floatable particles in the cell, $k$ is the **flotation rate constant**, and the **recovery after time $\tau$** is:

$$R(\tau) = 1 - e^{-k \tau}$$

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT-SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise these — they appear in **almost every** KTU Module-2 numerical or derivation problem.

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_t = \dfrac{g d^{2}(\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$ | Stokes terminal settling | $Re < 0.2$ |
| 2 | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.03\, g\, d\, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ | Newton terminal settling | $Re > 1000$ |
| 3 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_f v_t d}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless Reynolds number | Always |
| 4 | $CC = \dfrac{\rho_h - \rho_f}{\rho_l - \rho_f}$ | Concentration criterion for gravity sep. | Always |
| 5 | $v_h \, / \, v_l = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_h - \rho_f}{\rho_l - \rho_f}}$ | Settling-velocity ratio (Newton regime) | Re > 1000 |
| 6 | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | Young's contact-angle equation | Thermodynamic |
| 7 | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Work of adhesion in flotation | Thermodynamic |
| 8 | $R(\tau) = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ | First-order flotation recovery | Kinetic model |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Jigging** dominates the **global coal preparation industry** (India's coal washeries).
* **Wilfley Table** is the workhorse for **tin, tungsten and chromite** gravity plants (e.g., Kerala Minerals & Metals — Chavara).
* **Froth Flotation** is the **backbone of copper, zinc, lead, nickel and sulfide-ore processing** worldwide (e.g., Hindustan Zinc, Hindalco, Vedanta).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stokes' Law Terminal Velocity

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a smooth, rigid, spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$ settling under gravity through a Newtonian fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$.

**Forces acting on the particle:**

1. Gravitational (downward): $F_g = m g = \rho_p \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{6} \cdot g$
2. Buoyancy (upward): $F_b = \rho_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{6} \cdot g$
3. Viscous drag (opposing motion): $F_D = 3 \pi \mu d v$ (Stokes' drag, valid for $Re < 0.2$)

### 3.1.2 Equation of Motion

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_g - F_b - F_D$$

At **terminal velocity**, $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$\rho_p \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6} g - \rho_f \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6} g - 3 \pi \mu d\, v_t = 0$$

### 3.1.3 Solving Step-by-Step

$$\frac{\pi d^{3}}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 3 \pi \mu d\, v_t$$

Divide both sides by $\pi d$:

$$\frac{d^{2}}{6}(\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 3 \mu\, v_t$$

Divide both sides by $3 \mu$:

$$\frac{d^{2}(\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu} = v_t$$

### 3.1.4 Final Result

$$\boxed{\,v_t = \frac{g\, d^{2}\, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18\, \mu}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensional Check:** $\dfrac{[L/T^{2}][L^{2}][M/L^{3}]}{[M/(L \cdot T)]} = \dfrac{[M/(L \cdot T)]}{[M/(L \cdot T)]} = [L/T]$ — units of velocity. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Newton's Law Terminal Velocity

### 3.2.1 Setup (Turbulent Regime, $Re > 1000$)

The drag is now dominated by **inertial (form) resistance**, not viscous resistance. The drag force is:

$$F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_f v^{2}}{2}$$

For Newton's regime, $C_D \approx 0.44$ (constant, independent of $Re$).

### 3.2.2 Force Balance at Terminal Velocity

$$\rho_p \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6} g - \rho_f \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6} g - C_D \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_f v_t^{2}}{2} = 0$$

Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{12}{\pi d^{3}}$:

$$2(\rho_p - \rho_f) g = \frac{3 C_D \rho_f v_t^{2}}{d}$$

### 3.2.3 Solving for $v_t$

$$v_t^{2} = \frac{2 g d (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{C_D \rho_f}$$

Substituting $C_D = 0.44$:

$$v_t^{2} = \frac{2 g d (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{0.44 \,\rho_f}$$

### 3.2.4 Final Result

$$\boxed{\,v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4.545\, g\, d\, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}\;\approx\;\sqrt{\frac{3.03\, g\, d\, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Work of Adhesion from Interfacial Energies

Consider a solid particle attached to a bubble at the solid–liquid–vapour three-phase contact line of contact angle $\theta$.

The **Young–Dupré equation** states that the work of adhesion equals the surface free-energy gained when the solid–liquid and liquid–vapour interfaces are replaced by the solid–vapour interface:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

By **Young's equation**:

$$\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

Substituting:

### 3.3.1 Final Result

$$\boxed{\,W_a = \gamma_{LV} (1 + \cos\theta)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *If $\theta = 0$ (perfectly hydrophilic, no flotation):* $W_a = 2\gamma_{LV}$ — but the bubble does not adhere to a fully wetted surface (this value represents work against cohesion of the liquid).
> *If $\theta = 90°$ (intermediate):* $W_a = \gamma_{LV}$ — moderately floatable.
> *If $\theta = 180°$ (perfectly hydrophobic):* $W_a = 0$ — maximum floatability.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Stokes + Newton's Selection)

> [!TIP]
> *Exemplar problem — same style as KTU Part B numericals.*

**Problem:** Separate galena (PbS, $\rho_p = 7500 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$) from silica ($\text{SiO}_2$, $\rho_p = 2650 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$) in water at 20 °C ($\rho_f = 1000 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{Pa \cdot s}$). Particle diameter $d = 100 \;\mu\text{m} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}$. Calculate terminal velocities. Which law applies?

### Step 1 — Assume Stokes' law and compute $v_t$ for galena

$$v_t^{\text{galena}} = \frac{(9.81)(1 \times 10^{-4})^{2}(7500 - 1000)}{18 (1 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$= \frac{(9.81)(10^{-8})(6500)}{(18)(10^{-3})}$$

$$= \frac{6.3765 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$= 3.543 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{m/s} \;(\text{approx } 0.035 \;\text{m/s})$$

### Step 2 — Verify Reynolds Number

$$Re_{\text{galena}} = \frac{\rho_f v_t d}{\mu} = \frac{(1000)(3.543 \times 10^{-2})(1 \times 10^{-4})}{10^{-3}}$$

$$= \frac{3.543 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}} = 3.54$$

Since $Re = 3.54 > 0.2$, **Stokes' law is INVALID**. We must use the intermediate regime.

### Step 3 — Intermediate Regime Trial

Use the iterative form. Start with the estimate $Re_0 = 3.54$. Compute:

$$v_t = 0.27 \sqrt{\frac{g d(\rho_p - \rho_f)\, Re^{0.6}}{\rho_f}}$$

$$v_t = 0.27 \sqrt{\frac{(9.81)(10^{-4})(6500)(3.54)^{0.6}}{1000}}$$

$$(3.54)^{0.6} = 2.131$$

$$v_t = 0.27 \sqrt{\frac{(9.81)(10^{-4})(6500)(2.131)}{1000}}$$

$$= 0.27 \sqrt{1.358 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$= 0.27 \times 0.1166 = 0.0315 \;\text{m/s}$$

### Step 4 — Re-verify Re

$$Re = \frac{(1000)(0.0315)(10^{-4})}{10^{-3}} = 3.15$$

Close to assumed 3.54. **Iteration converges to $v_t \approx 0.031$ m/s for galena.**

### Step 5 — Velocity Ratio (Selectivity)

For silica, perform the same calculation. After convergence: $v_t^{\text{silica}} \approx 0.022$ m/s.

**Settling velocity ratio:**

$$\frac{v_t^{\text{galena}}}{v_t^{\text{silica}}} = \frac{0.031}{0.022} \approx 1.41$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Galena settles only ~1.4× faster than silica in this size range. This is *poor selectivity* by gravity alone — flotation is preferred for fine PbS–SiO₂ separation.

## 3.5 Comparative Selection Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Criterion | Jigging | Wilfley Table | Froth Flotation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Particle size** | 0.5 – 50 mm | 50 µm – 2 mm | 10 – 200 µm |
| **Density difference** | High (CC > 1.75) | Moderate (CC > 1.25) | Independent |
| **Throughput** | Very high | Moderate | Moderate–High |
| **Capital cost** | Medium | Low | High |
| **Operating cost** | Low | Low | High (reagents) |
| **Selectivity** | Moderate | High | Very high |
| **Typical application** | Coal, iron ore | Tin, tungsten, chromite | Sulfide ores of Cu, Pb, Zn |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Overall Beneficiation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROM["ROM ORE (Run of Mine)"]
    CRUSHER["CRUSHING STAGE Jaw, Cone, Gyratory"]
    SCREEN["SIZING / SCREENING Vibrating Screens"]
    GRIND["GRINDING (Ball Mill / Rod Mill) 75 to 200 microns"]
    CLASS["CLASSIFICATION (Hydrocyclone / Spiral)"]
    JIG1["JIGGING (Coarse Fraction)"]
    TABLE1["WILFLEY TABLE (Mid Fraction)"]
    FLOT1["FROTH FLOTATION (Fine Fraction)"]
    CONC["CONCENTRATE (Smelter Feed)"]
    MID["MIDDLING (Recirculated)"]
    TAIL["TAILINGS (Rejects / Tailing Dam)"]

    ROM --> CRUSHER --> SCREEN
    SCREEN --> JIG1
    SCREEN --> GRIND --> CLASS
    CLASS --> TABLE1
    CLASS --> FLOT1
    JIG1 --> CONC
    TABLE1 --> CONC
    FLOT1 --> CONC
    JIG1 --> MID
    TABLE1 --> MID
    FLOT1 --> MID
    MID --> GRIND
    JIG1 --> TAIL
    TABLE1 --> TAIL
    FLOT1 --> TAIL
```

## 4.2 Jig Operation — Pulse Cycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEED["FEED SLURRY on screen"]
    PULSE["PULSATION CYCLE"]
    PUS["PULSION STROKE (Up flow) Bed expands, particles stratify"]
    SUC["SUCTION STROKE (Down flow) Bed compacts, heavies consolidate"]
    HGATE["HEAVIES SINK to bottom through hutch"]
    LGATE["LIGHTS RISE to top, overflow"]
    HPROD["HEAVY PRODUCT Discharge through bed"]
    LPROD["LIGHT PRODUCT Overflow launder"]

    FEED --> PULSE
    PULSE --> PUS --> SUC
    SUC --> HGATE --> HPROD
    SUC --> LGATE --> LPROD
```

## 4.3 Wilfley Table — Particle Trajectory Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FEEDIN["FEED (Top Corner)"]
    SLOPE["INCLINED DECK with RIFFLES"]
    MOTION["ASYMMETRIC SHAKE Stroke along long axis"]
    WATER["WASH WATER (Along slope)"]
    HEAVY["HEAVY PARTICLES Travel SLOW along riffles to concentrate end"]
    LIGHT["LIGHT PARTICLES WASHED DOWN slope to tailings edge"]
    CONC_OUT["CONCENTRATE Discharge end"]
    MID_OUT["MIDDLING Diagonal side discharge"]
    TAIL_OUT["TAILINGS Discharge edge"]

    FEEDIN --> SLOPE
    MOTION --> SLOPE
    WATER --> SLOPE
    SLOPE --> HEAVY --> CONC_OUT
    SLOPE --> MID_OUT
    SLOPE --> LIGHT --> TAIL_OUT
```

## 4.4 Froth Flotation Cell — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PULP["CONDITIONED PULP Feed at top with reagents"]
    MIXTANK["CONDITIONING TANK Collector + Frother + Modifiers"]
    CELL["FLOTATION CELL Aerated Impeller"]
    AIR["AIR BLOWER Low pressure air"]
    BUB["BUBBLES Generated by impeller"]
    ATTACH["HYDROPHOBIC PARTICLES Attach to BUBBLES"]
    FROTH["STABLE FROTH forms at top"]
    SKIM["SKIMMED FROTH Concentrate Launder"]
    HYDRO["HYDROPHILIC PARTICLES Stay in slurry"]
    TAIL_OUT2["TAILINGS Discharge at bottom"]

    PULP --> MIXTANK --> CELL
    AIR --> CELL
    CELL --> BUB --> ATTACH --> FROTH --> SKIM
    CELL --> HYDRO --> TAIL_OUT2
```

## 4.5 Reagent-Selection Logic for Flotation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["DESIRED MINERAL"]
    Q1{"Sulfide or oxide?"}
    SULFIDE["SULFIDE --> Potassium Amyl Xanthate KAX Collector"]
    OXIDE["OXIDE --> Fatty Acid or Amine Collector"]
    Q2{"Gangue type?"}
    SILIC["Silica or silicate --> Depress with Sodium Silicate Na2SiO3"]
    CARB["Carbonate --> Depress with Na2CO3 or starch"]
    PYR["Pyrite present --> Activate with CuSO4 or depress with lime CaO"]

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- YES --> SULFIDE
    Q1 -- NO  --> OXIDE
    SULFIDE --> Q2
    OXIDE --> Q2
    Q2 --> SILIC
    Q2 --> CARB
    Q2 --> PYR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in KTU answers:** Draw **labelled rectangles** for unit operations, **arrows for material flow**, and **annotations** for forces (gravity, drag, surface tension) at the relevant points. Examiners reward clear labelled diagrams for **full 14-mark questions**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

**Q:** Define **Mineral Beneficiation**. List any **two physical methods** of ore concentration and state the property each exploits.

**Model Answer:**

> **Mineral Beneficiation** is the sequence of physical and physico-chemical operations applied to a run-of-mine ore to separate the *valuable mineral* from the associated *gangue*, producing a higher-grade **concentrate** suitable for downstream metallurgical extraction.
>
> **Two physical methods:**
> 1. **Jigging** — exploits **difference in density** between particles in a pulsating water/空气 current.
> 2. **Froth Flotation** — exploits **difference in surface wettability** (hydrophobicity) of mineral particles.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q:** State **Stokes' law** for the terminal settling velocity of a fine particle. Mention the regime of validity.

**Model Answer:**

> Stokes' law:
> $$v_t = \frac{g\,d^{2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18\,\mu}$$
> where $d$ is the particle diameter, $\rho_p$ and $\rho_f$ are particle and fluid densities, $\mu$ is the fluid viscosity, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration.
>
> **Regime of validity:** **Laminar flow** around the particle, characterised by **Reynolds Number $Re < 0.2$**, i.e. typically for fine particles (< 50 µm in water).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> *KTU ESE pattern: Answer any **one** of the following full Module-2 questions. Each has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – Sample Module-2 Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **principle, construction, and operation of a Wilfley Shaking Table**. Discuss its industrial applications and limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A binary mixture of **hematite ($\rho = 5200 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$)** and **quartz ($\rho = 2650 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$)** is to be separated in a Wilfley table using water ($\rho_f = 1000 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 10^{-3} \;\text{Pa \cdot s}$). The particle diameter is $d = 200 \;\mu\text{m}$. Calculate the terminal settling velocity of each species. **State and justify** which settling law (Stokes or Newton) is valid.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

* **Construction:** A slightly inclined rectangular deck (~5 m × 1.8 m) covered with linoleum/rubber. The deck surface carries **riffles** (parallel strips) running along its length. Height of riffles decreases from the feed end to the concentrate end.
* **Motion:** An eccentric drive imparts an **asymmetric reciprocating motion** along the long axis — slow forward push, sharp return — typical stroke length 10–25 mm, frequency 250–350 rpm.
* **Operating principle:** Particles on the deck experience (i) **asymmetric acceleration** pushing heavies forward along riffles, and (ii) **transverse wash water** flowing down the slope. Heavies report at the upper corner (concentrate), lights at the lower edge (tailings), and mixed particles along the diagonal (middling).
* **Applications:** Tin (cassiterite), tungsten (wolframite), chromite, beach sands, coal fines, iron ore.
* **Limitations:** Limited to < 2 mm feed; sensitive to feed rate and dilution; low capacity per unit floor area.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $v_t$ for hematite using Stokes' law (assumption)**

$$v_t^{H} = \frac{(9.81)(2 \times 10^{-4})^{2}(5200 - 1000)}{18 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$= \frac{(9.81)(4 \times 10^{-8})(4200)}{1.8 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$= \frac{1.648 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8 \times 10^{-2}} = 9.16 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Verify $Re$**

$$Re_H = \frac{(1000)(0.0916)(2 \times 10^{-4})}{10^{-3}} = 18.3$$

Since $18.3 > 0.2$ but $< 1000$, Stokes' law is **invalid**; we are in the **intermediate regime**.

**Step 3 — Intermediate regime iterative solution**

Using the Allen correlation $v_t = 0.27 \sqrt{\frac{g d (\rho_p - \rho_f) Re^{0.6}}{\rho_f}}$:

Iterate starting from $Re = 18.3$; $(18.3)^{0.6} = 5.79$:

$$v_t = 0.27 \sqrt{\frac{(9.81)(2 \times 10^{-4})(4200)(5.79)}{1000}}$$

$$= 0.27 \sqrt{4.77 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.27 \times 0.2184 = 0.059 \;\text{m/s}$$

Re-check: $Re = (1000)(0.059)(2\times 10^{-4})/10^{-3} = 11.8$. Iterating once more converges to $v_t \approx 0.045$ m/s for hematite.

**Step 4 — Repeat for quartz**

$$v_t^{Q} \approx 0.029 \;\text{m/s (after iteration)}$$

**Step 5 — Selectivity**

$$\frac{v_t^{H}}{v_t^{Q}} \approx \frac{0.045}{0.029} \approx 1.55$$

> [!TIP]
> *This moderate ratio is sufficient because the Wilfley table **also** uses differential transport along the riffles, amplifying the density-based separation beyond what terminal-velocity ratio alone suggests.*

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Sample Module-2 Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **principle of Froth Flotation** with a neat diagram. Discuss the **role of collectors, frothers, and modifiers** with two specific examples for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a flotation cell, the **contact angle of a mineral particle** with the conditioned water surface is **$\theta = 70°$**. The **liquid–vapour interfacial tension** is $\gamma_{LV} = 35 \;\text{mN/m}$. Calculate the **Work of Adhesion** of the bubble to the particle. **Comment** on the floatability of this mineral.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

* **Principle:** Surface-chemistry-based separation. After fine grinding (~75 µm), the ore pulp is **conditioned** with reagents in an agitation tank. The slurry enters the flotation cell where an impeller draws in air and disperses it as fine bubbles. **Hydrophobic** particles (rendered water-repellent by collectors) attach to bubble surfaces and rise into the froth, which is skimmed as the concentrate. **Hydrophilic** gangue particles remain in the pulp and exit as tailings.
* **Collectors:** Adsorb selectively on target mineral surfaces, rendering them hydrophobic. *Examples:* (1) **Potassium amyl xanthate (KAX)** — for sulfide minerals like chalcopyrite, galena, sphalerite. (2) **Oleic acid** — for non-sulfides like fluorite, barite, phosphate.
* **Frothers:** Reduce surface tension and stabilise the froth. *Examples:* (1) **Pine oil** — traditional frother. (2) **Methyl isobutyl carbinol (MIBC)** — modern, widely used synthetic frother.
* **Modifiers:** Activate or depress specific minerals; control pH. *Examples:* (1) **Lime (CaO)** — depresses pyrite while activating chalcopyrite at pH ~10–11. (2) **Sodium cyanide (NaCN)** — depresses sphalerite and pyrite in Cu–Pb–Zn differential flotation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Apply the Work of Adhesion formula**

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$W_a = 35 \times (1 + \cos 70°)$$

$$\cos 70° = 0.342$$

$$W_a = 35 \times (1 + 0.342) = 35 \times 1.342$$

### Step 3 — Result

$$\boxed{\,W_a = 46.97 \;\text{mN/m}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Floatability Comment**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $W_a > 0$ (and is significantly large compared to the surface tension of water alone, ~72 mN/m), the thermodynamic driving force for bubble–particle attachment is **positive and substantial**. A contact angle of 70° indicates **good hydrophobicity**, characteristic of sulfide minerals conditioned with xanthate collectors. **Flotation is thermodynamically favourable** and is expected to give high recovery under proper aeration and froth conditions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make on This Topic — and How to Avoid Them**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $Re$ before applying Stokes' law.** The number-1 valuation trap. Always compute $Re$ and state the regime explicitly. If $Re > 0.2$, *do not* use Stokes' formula.
> 2. **Confusing $C_D$ and $Re$.** In the table-look-up, $C_D$ decreases from 24/Re at low $Re$ to a constant 0.44 at high $Re$. Many students write $C_D = 24 Re$ — instant zero.
> 3. **Mixing up $\gamma_{SL}$ and $\gamma_{SV}$.** In Young's equation, the term is $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$. Swapping them flips the sign of $\cos\theta$ — the entire flotation theory breaks down.
> 4. **Omitting units in numericals.** Always state SI units. Mark-cutters fail students for missing the final unit conversion (e.g., µm → m).
> 5. **Drawing a jig without labelling the pulsion and suction strokes.** A neat diagram with the **two-stroke cycle** clearly marked carries an extra 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin these to your revision wall — the items below are the minimum required for full marks on Module-2.**

* [x] **Mineral Beneficiation** = physical/physico-chemical separation of valuables from gangue.
* [x] **Three methods studied:** Jigging (gravity, coarse), Wilfley Table (gravity + flowing film, mid-size), Froth Flotation (surface chemistry, fine).
* [x] **Terminal settling velocity:**
    * Stokes (laminar, $Re < 0.2$): $v_t = \dfrac{g d^{2}(\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$
    * Newton (turbulent, $Re > 1000$): $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.03\, g\, d (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$
    * Intermediate ($0.2 < Re < 1000$): use Allen correlation.
* [x] **Concentration Criterion:** $CC = \dfrac{\rho_h - \rho_f}{\rho_l - \rho_f}$ — must exceed **2.5** for easy gravity separation.
* [x] **Jig types:** Harz, Baum (coal), Hancock, Denver Mineral.
* [x] **Wilfley Table products:** Concentrate (corner), Middling (diagonal), Tailings (edge).
* [x] **Flotation reagents:** Collectors (xanthates, fatty acids), Frothers (pine oil, MIBC), Modifiers (lime, NaCN, CuSO₄).
* [x] **Young's Equation:** $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}}$.
* [x] **Work of Adhesion:** $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ — higher $\theta \Rightarrow$ more floatable.
* [x] **Flotation recovery:** $R(\tau) = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ (first-order kinetics).
* [x] **Always verify $Re$** before applying any settling law.
* [x] **Typical industrial feeds:** Coal → Jig; Tin/Tungsten/Chromite → Wilfley; Cu/Pb/Zn sulfides → Froth Flotation.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Aid:** *"Stokes for slimes, Newton for nuts, Flotation for fines."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles and Additives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles and Additives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Principles of Size Reduction (Comminution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Size Reduction (Comminution)** is the unit operation in which the average particle size of a solid material is reduced by mechanical means — through the application of **compression**, **impact**, **attrition**, **shear**, or **cutting** forces — without changing the chemical state of the substance.

**Principles (Mechanisms) of Size Reduction:**

1. **Compression** — Slow squeezing of particles between two surfaces (e.g., jaw crusher, roll crusher). Best for coarse, hard, brittle solids.
2. **Impact** — Sudden collision of a particle with a moving body (e.g., hammer mill, ball mill). Best for hard, brittle materials.
3. **Attrition** — Rubbing/scraping action between particles (e.g., attrition mill, ball mill at low speeds). Best for softer materials and producing fine powders.
4. **Shear** — Combination of cutting and compression, where forces are applied tangentially (e.g., cutter mill, fluid energy mill).
5. **Cutting** — Sharp blades slicing through fibrous or tough materials (e.g., rotary cutter mill for plastics, biomass, fibrous drugs).

### 1.2 Additives (Grinding Aids)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
