Since $38.58^{\circ} > 21^{\circ}$, the standard design angle of **21°** is *safe* for this material.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Capacity of a Jaw Crusher

The capacity of a jaw crusher is governed by the **volume of material discharged per cycle** multiplied by the **number of cycles per minute**.

#### Volume Discharged Per Stroke

Imagine a thin *slab* of crushed material exiting the discharge during one open stroke. The slab has:
- Width $= D$ (discharge set)
- Length $= L$ (crusher length)
- Thickness $= S$ (stroke = how far the jaw retracts)

$$
\text{Volume per stroke} \;=\; D \times L \times S
$$

#### Cycles Per Minute

The eccentric shaft rotates at $N$ rpm → $N$ strokes per minute.

#### Mass Throughput

Multiplying volume by bulk density $\rho_b$:

$$
Q_{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; N \cdot D \cdot L \cdot S \cdot \rho_b
$$

#### Applying Empirical Correction Factor

In practice, material does *not* exit as a perfect slab — voids, irregular shapes, and inter-particle friction reduce throughput by roughly **40 %**. Taggart's empirical correction gives:

$$
\boxed{\;Q \;=\; 0.6 \cdot N \cdot L \cdot S \cdot D \cdot \rho_b \;\; \text{(tons/hr when D, L, S in m and } \rho_b \text{ in tons/m}^3\text{)}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Trap**: If $D, L, S$ are in **centimetres**, the formula simplifies to $Q = 0.6 \, B \, L \, S \, N$ in **tons/hr** (with $\rho_b \approx 1.6$ t/m³ for typical rock).

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Optimum Speed of Eccentric Shaft

A jaw crusher has an **upper** and a **lower** limit on operating speed:
- **Too slow** → low throughput.
- **Too fast** → particles cannot fall fast enough by gravity between strokes; throughput drops (paradoxically).

#### Empirical Relation (Brown & Mitchell)

$$
\boxed{\;N_{\text{opt}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{L}}\;}
$$

where $L$ is the length of the swing jaw in **metres**, and $N_{\text{opt}}$ is in **rpm**.

**Why the inverse-square-root?** Energy dissipation scales with $L^2$ (volume of jaw), while gravitational pull per cycle scales linearly with $L$ → balance gives $\sqrt{L}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation | Governing Physics | Final Equation | Critical Assumption |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Angle of Nip** | Friction vs. normal force | $\alpha \leq 2\phi$ | Particle is rigid sphere; smooth jaws |
| **Capacity** | Volumetric displacement per stroke | $Q = 0.6 \, N L S D \rho_b$ | Steady-state; no choking |
| **Optimum Speed** | Balance of inertial & gravitational | $N = 400 / \sqrt{L}$ | Empirical (rock-like feed) |
| **Reduction Ratio** | Geometric ratio of feed & product | $RR = D_{80,\text{feed}} / D_{80,\text{product}}$ | Single pass, no recirculation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Jaw Crusher Architecture & Material Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Raw Ore from Mine] --> step2[Vibrating Grizzly Feeder]
    step2 --> step3[Feed Hopper and Chute]
    step3 --> step4[Feed Opening Top Gape]
    step4 --> step5[Crushing Chamber Nip Zone]
    stepA[Fixed Jaw Plate Stationary] --> step5
    stepB[Swing Jaw Plate Oscillating] --> step5
    step5 --> step6[Discharge Opening Set]
    step6 --> step7[Discharge Chute]
    step7 --> step8[Conveyor Belt to Secondary Crusher]
    stepM[Drive Motor via V Belt Pulley] --> stepE
    stepE[Eccentric Shaft Rotating] --> stepP
    stepP[Pitman Toggle Beam Vertical Oscillation] --> stepT
    stepT[Toggle Plates Linkage and Safety Fuse] --> stepB
    stepF[Heavy Steel Frame and Foundation Bed] --> step5
    step5 -. Heat and Dust Generation .-> stepV[Dust Suppression Spray System]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip**: Trace the **solid arrows** to follow the *material* path from ROM ore to crushed product. Trace the **dashed arrows** to follow the *power transmission* chain from motor → eccentric shaft → pitman → toggle → swing jaw.

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Block-Level Architecture of Power Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inP[Power Input 415 V AC Motor] --> inP1[V Belt Drive and Pulley]
    inP1 --> inP2[Eccentric Shaft with Offset Crank]
    inP2 --> inP3[Pitman Vertical Reciprocation]
    inP3 --> inP4[Toggle Plate Force Amplification]
    inP4 --> inP5[Swing Jaw Closing Stroke]
    inP5 --> outQ[Compressive Force on Feed Particle]
    outQ --> outR[Particle Fracture and Discharge]
    inP2 -. return stroke .-> inP3
    inP4 -. safety shear .-> inP5
```

### 4.3 Force Diagram Description — Angle of Nip Analysis

For visualisation in your answer script, draw the following free-body diagram (textual sketch):

```
                  ↑ N sin(α/2)
                  │
            N  ↖   │
              ╲    │   Friction μN cos(α/2)
   ────────────●───↓────────────
              ╱│  
   Fixed Jaw╱  │ 
          ╱    │  
         ╱     α/2   
   ←────╱──────│────────────→  
   μN sin(α/2) │ N cos(α/2)
                │
              Particle
              (diameter d)
```

- The two thick lines represent the converging jaw surfaces meeting at total angle $\alpha$.
- The particle sits exactly at the apex (nip point).
- $N$ acts perpendicular to each jaw surface.
- Friction $\mu N$ acts along each jaw surface, directed **downward into the crusher**.

### 4.4 Application Mapping — Where Jaw Crushers Are Used

| Industry | Feed Material | Typical Gape | Capacity |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Cement | Limestone | 1.0 – 1.5 m | 200 – 1000 tph |
| Mining (Iron Ore) | Hematite | 1.2 – 1.8 m | 500 – 2500 tph |
| Power Plants | Coal | 0.5 – 1.0 m | 50 – 500 tph |
| Aggregate / Quarry | Granite / Basalt | 0.8 – 1.2 m | 100 – 800 tph |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term *Angle of Nip*. Why is it considered the most critical design parameter of a jaw crusher?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **angle of nip** ($\alpha$) is defined as the *included angle between the two jaw surfaces* at the point where a feed particle is first gripped, i.e., the angle subtended by the particle at the nip point.

It is critical because:
1. **It governs the maximum feed size** that can enter the crushing chamber. A larger $\alpha$ restricts feed size.
2. **It sets the upper limit for the friction coefficient** of the rock: $\alpha \leq 2\phi$ where $\phi = \tan^{-1}\mu$. If $\alpha > 2\phi$, the rock **slips upward** without being crushed.
3. **It determines the throughput geometry** and influences product size distribution.

Standard practice keeps $\alpha \approx 21^{\circ}$ for safe operation with most rock types.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Blake crusher and a Dodge crusher. (Any 3 points)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Blake Crusher | Dodge Crusher |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Pivot at the **top** | Pivot at the **bottom** |
| 2 | **High capacity**; used as primary crusher in industry | **Low capacity**; used in laboratories |
| 3 | Can accept **large feed lumps** (up to 1.5 m) | Accepts only **small feed** (typically < 50 mm) |
| 4 | Discharge opening has uniform width; choking risk low | Discharge opening remains nearly constant → **chokes easily** |
| 5 | Stroke amplitude **decreases** from top to bottom | Stroke is **uniform** along the jaw |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Blake jaw crusher**. List **two advantages** and **two disadvantages**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A Blake jaw crusher consists of:
- A **fixed jaw** mounted vertically on the front frame.
- A **swing jaw** pivoted at the top on the frame.
- An **eccentric shaft** mounted at the bottom of the pitman, driven by a motor through V-belts.
- A **pitman** (heavy cast-steel beam) carrying the eccentric, which moves up-and-down as the shaft rotates.
- **Two toggle plates** connecting the bottom of the pitman to the back of the swing jaw — these act as safety fuses.
- Heavy **flywheels** to store rotational energy during the idle (opening) stroke.

**Working:**
1. Motor rotates the eccentric shaft.
2. The eccentric motion lifts the pitman upward → push the toggle plates → swing jaw moves **toward** the fixed jaw (closing stroke).
3. Rock at the nip point is **compressed and fractured**.
4. As the eccentric continues to rotate, the pitman descends → toggle plates pull the swing jaw **away** from the fixed jaw (opening stroke).
5. Crushed fragments fall by gravity to the discharge opening; the cycle repeats.

**Advantages:**
1. High capacity; suitable for large ROM ore.
2. Simple, rugged construction; easy maintenance.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Produces a non-uniform product (more fines than roll crusher).
2. Subject to high vibration and mechanical shock.

---

**(b)** A jaw crusher has a feed opening of **1200 mm × 900 mm** (width × length) and a closed discharge setting of **75 mm**. The stroke of the swing jaw is **25 mm**. Calculate:
**(i)** The angle of nip.
**(ii)** The theoretical capacity in tons/hr (assume rock bulk density = 1.6 t/m³).
**(iii)** The reduction ratio. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $B = 1200 \text{ mm} = 1.2 \text{ m}$ (feed width)
- $L = 900 \text{ mm} = 0.9 \text{ m}$ (jaw length)
- $D = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$ (discharge set)
- $S = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$ (stroke)
- $\rho_b = 1.6 \text{ t/m}^3$

**Step (i): Angle of Nip**

For a Blake crusher, the angle of nip can be approximated using:

$$
\alpha \;\approx\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{B - D}{2L}\right)
$$

Wait — for this geometry, use the standard feed–discharge relation:

$$
\tan\alpha \;\approx\; \dfrac{B - D}{L} \quad (\text{approximation for small } \alpha)
$$

$$
\tan\alpha \;\approx\; \dfrac{1.2 - 0.075}{0.9} \;=\; \dfrac{1.125}{0.9} \;=\; 1.25
$$

$$
\alpha \;\approx\; 51.34^{\circ}
$$

This exceeds the safe limit ($\sim 21^{\circ}$), which means **the design is not feasible** as a single Blake crusher — the operator must either pre-crush the feed or use a gyratory crusher. **(This is the examiner's favourite trick!)**

*Correction note*: In practice, the *effective* angle of nip is measured at the nip point only, not across the entire jaw. For typical jaw operation:

$$
\alpha_{\text{effective}} \;\approx\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{L}\right) \;\approx\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.025}{0.9}\right) \approx 1.6^{\circ}
$$

This is the **operating nip angle**, which is well within safe limits. The earlier large value represents the *cone angle* of the jaw envelope, not the actual nip angle.

**Step (ii): Capacity**

$$
Q \;=\; 0.6 \cdot B \cdot L \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \rho_b
$$

Assume $N_{\text{opt}} = 400 / \sqrt{L} = 400 / \sqrt{0.9} \approx 421$ rpm. Take $N = 400$ rpm.

$$
Q \;=\; 0.6 \times 1.2 \times 0.9 \times 0.025 \times 400 \times 1.6
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 0.6 \times 1.2 \times 0.9 \times 0.025 \times 640
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q \;\approx\; 10.37 \text{ tons/hr}\;}
$$

**Step (iii): Reduction Ratio**

$$
RR \;=\; \dfrac{B}{D} \;=\; \dfrac{1200 \text{ mm}}{75 \text{ mm}} \;=\; 16
$$

A reduction ratio of 16 is **too high for a single jaw crusher** (typical RR is 4–8). This confirms that the configuration is industrially impractical without secondary crushing.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a Dodge jaw crusher** with a labelled diagram. Compare its performance with the Blake type. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A Dodge crusher has the **swing jaw pivoted at the bottom** rather than the top. The eccentric shaft drives a pitman that moves the top of the swing jaw.

**Working:**
- The top of the swing jaw describes the **largest arc**; the bottom (discharge end) describes the **smallest arc**.
- This means the **discharge opening has nearly constant width**, while the feed opening oscillates significantly.
- Particles at the top are repeatedly nipped; only those small enough to fit the discharge set escape.

**Comparison with Blake:**

| Aspect | Blake | Dodge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Capacity | High | Low |
| Product size uniformity | Moderate | **Uniform** (constant set) |
| Tendency to choke | Low | **High** |
| Feed size accepted | **Large** | Small |
| Industrial application | **Primary crushing** | **Lab / sample prep** |

**Conclusion:** The Dodge crusher is rarely used industrially due to choking and low throughput. The Blake crusher dominates primary crushing.

---

**(b)** A jaw crusher with a **length of 1.5 m** is to crush **limestone** (bulk density 1.5 t/m³) at a target capacity of **120 tons/hr**. The closed set is **50 mm** and stroke is **20 mm**.
Calculate: **(i)** Optimum speed of the eccentric shaft.
**(ii)** Width of the feed opening required. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_b = 1.5 \text{ t/m}^3$
- $Q = 120 \text{ t/hr}$
- $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $S = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$

**Step (i): Optimum Speed**

$$
N_{\text{opt}} \;=\; \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{L}} \;=\; \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{1.5}} \;=\; \dfrac{400}{1.2247}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_{\text{opt}} \;\approx\; 326.6 \;\text{rpm (round to 325 rpm)}\;}
$$

**Step (ii): Feed Width**

From $Q = 0.6 \, B \, L \, S \, N \, \rho_b$:

$$
B \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{0.6 \cdot L \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \rho_b}
$$

$$
B \;=\; \dfrac{120}{0.6 \times 1.5 \times 0.02 \times 326.6 \times 1.5}
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
0.6 \times 1.5 \times 0.02 \times 326.6 \times 1.5 \;=\; 8.82
$$

$$
\boxed{\;B \;\approx\; 13.6 \text{ m}\;}
$$

This is impractically large — indicating that the **target capacity is unattainable** with this stroke and length. The student must **increase the stroke** or **use a larger crusher**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap**: Forgetting to convert mm → m in $B$, $L$, $S$, $D$ leads to capacity answers that are off by a factor of **1000 or more**. Always convert first.
> 2. **Confusing $\alpha$ (full angle) with $\alpha/2$ (half-angle)**: Many students write $\alpha \leq \phi$ instead of $\alpha \leq 2\phi$. This halves the safe angle and loses 2 full marks.
> 3. **Using gross feed opening instead of effective gape** for the reduction ratio. $RR = B/D$ gives a *geometric* ratio, while $RR = D_{80,\text{feed}}/D_{80,\text{product}}$ is the *correct* process ratio. Examiners expect the latter.
> 4. **Forgetting the 0.6 empirical factor** in the capacity equation. Writing $Q = B L S N \rho_b$ gives **40 % extra** capacity — a guaranteed zero.
> 5. **Not commenting on feasibility**: When your calculated $\alpha$ or $B$ comes out absurdly large, *explicitly state* that the design is not feasible. Examiners reward engineering judgement.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Jaw Crushers**

- [ ] **Definition**: Compression-type primary crusher with one fixed + one swinging jaw.
- [ ] **Angle of Nip** $\alpha$: included angle between jaws at the nip point.
- [ ] **Master Equation**: $\alpha \leq 2\phi$ where $\tan\phi = \mu$.
- [ ] For typical rock $\mu \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ → $\alpha_{\text{crit}} \approx 22^{\circ}$–$44^{\circ}$; design uses $\alpha \approx 21^{\circ}$.
- [ ] **Blake**: pivot at **top** → high capacity, primary use.
- [ ] **Dodge**: pivot at **bottom** → uniform product but chokes easily.
- [ ] **Capacity**: $Q = 0.6 \, N \, B \, L \, S \, \rho_b$ (Taggart, with empirical 0.6 factor).
- [ ] **Optimum Speed**: $N = 400 / \sqrt{L}$ where $L$ is in metres, $N$ in rpm.
- [ ] **Reduction Ratio**: Typically **4:1 to 8:1**; > 10 indicates multi-stage crushing.
- [ ] **Power**: $P \approx 0.7 \, N \, L \, (B + D)$ in kW.
- [ ] **Safety Fuse**: Toggle plates — break under uncrushable object (tramp metal), protecting the pitman.
- [ ] **Stroke** $S$ = peak-to-peak displacement of swing jaw at the discharge.
- [ ] **Bulk Density $\rho_b$** of typical rock ≈ 1.5 – 1.8 t/m³.
- [ ] **Choking** occurs when discharge rate < feed rate — Dodge crusher most prone.
- [ ] **Curved Jaw Plates** ensure progressive nip — reduce $\alpha$ along the chamber.
- [ ] **Flywheels** store energy during opening stroke, release it during closing stroke (smoother load on motor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics**
> - **"A**ngle of **N**ip **N**ot **M**ore than **T**wice **F**riction" → $\alpha \leq 2\phi$.
> - **"B**ig **L**ittle **S**peed **Q**uickly" → $Q = 0.6 \cdot B \cdot L \cdot S \cdot N$.
> - **"B**lake **T**op, **D**odge **D**own" → Pivot positions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gyratory Crushers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gyratory Crushers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gyratory Crusher (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *Gyratory Crusher* is a primary size-reduction device operating on the principle of **gyratory compression**, in which the crushing action is imparted by a conical, gyrating mantle oscillating eccentrically within a fixed concave bowl. The material is repeatedly compressed and fractured as it descends through the narrowing annular gap until the particle size becomes smaller than the discharge setting (Open Side Setting, OSS).

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes it under **Coarse Crushers** (Primary Crushers) with a reduction ratio (RR) typically between **3:1 and 10:1**, designed to handle ROM (Run-of-Mine) ore feed sizes ranging from **150 mm to 1500 mm**.

**Symmetry of Operation:** The mantle performs a continuous closed-path wobble — a *planar ellipse-like trace* — driven by an eccentric shaft. This is fundamentally different from the linear, one-direction stroke of a jaw crusher.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifiers for Board Examinations:**
> - Primary crusher for large ROM feed.
> - Reduction Ratio (RR) = Feed Opening / Discharge Opening.
> - Operates continuously, not in a reciprocating (start-stop) cycle.
> - Higher throughput per unit footprint than Jaw Crushers of equivalent capacity.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a kitchen trying to crush a hard candy. Instead of pressing it once with the flat of your palm (like a **Jaw Crusher** does), you place the candy inside a hollow, heavy mortar and slowly rotate a pestle around the inside wall in a **circular, pressing motion**. The candy gets continuously pinched from every angle as the pestle wobbles, and it slowly tumbles downward as it breaks into smaller pieces.

This "rotating pinch" is exactly what a Gyratory Crusher does — only with steel components, hydraulic pressures exceeding **200 MPa**, and capacities measured in **thousands of tonnes per hour** in industrial installations.

**Real-World Scale-Up Analogy:**
- A **jaw crusher** = a single, repetitive hand-press.
- A **gyratory crusher** = a continuous, rotary hand-motion that never stops pinching.

| Feature | Jaw Crusher | Gyratory Crusher |
|---|---|---|
| Motion | Reciprocating (Linear) | Continuous Gyratory Wobble |
| Capacity | Lower (up to ~500 TPH) | Higher (up to ~5000 TPH) |
| Feed Size | Up to ~1 m | Up to ~1.5 m |
| Best Application | Smaller scale, intermittent | Large scale, continuous bulk |
| Wear Pattern | Unilateral | Circumferential (more uniform) |
| Cost per Tonne Crushed | Higher | Lower |

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The KTU 2024 evaluation scheme specifically expects the following parameters:

- **Open Side Setting (OSS):** Minimum discharge opening during the upward mantle stroke.
- **Closed Side Setting (CSS):** Maximum discharge opening during the downward mantle stroke.
- **Throw / Stroke (S):** The vertical displacement of the mantle apex during one cycle (typically **15 mm to 50 mm** for industrial units).
- **Gyratory Speed (n):** Typically **100 – 200 RPM**, controlled by the eccentric bushing design.
- **Bulk Density (ρ_b):** Density of the feed material when loosely packed (used in capacity calculations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme, students must be able to *derive* the capacity formula and *apply* Bond's crushing law to compute the power requirement. Memorization of constants alone will **not** earn full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Gyratory Crusher operates through the following sequence during **every single revolution** of the eccentric shaft:

1. **Feed Entry:** ROM ore enters the top of the crusher through the receiving hopper (Feed Opening).
2. **Compression Phase (Top Half-Cycle):** As the mantle rotates on its eccentric axis, it moves **toward** the stationary concave. Particles caught in the narrowing gap experience compressive stress.
3. **Expansion Phase (Bottom Half-Cycle):** As the mantle continues its rotation, it **retreats** from the concave. Fractured fragments fall gravitationally into the wider gap below.
4. **Material Migration:** Each new piece of feed sits on top of the previously broken mass — a phenomenon known as **"Inter-Particle Compression."**
5. **Discharge:** Particles smaller than the CSS exit the bottom of the crusher onto a discharge conveyor.

**The "Why" Behind Continuous Operation:**
Because the mantle is **never** lifting completely off the feed bed (unlike a jaw crusher opening every stroke), there is no empty period in the crushing chamber. Material is therefore *always* under some degree of load, which directly explains why gyratory crushers have **2× to 5×** the throughput of equivalent jaw crushers.

---

### 2.2 Mechanism of Gyratory Compression

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core "How" — Gyratory Motion Anatomy:**
> A rotating eccentric sleeve produces a circular wobble at the mantle's lowest point. The motion can be decomposed into two orthogonal harmonic components: a radial (compressive) and a tangential (shearing) component. The **resultant vector** describes the closed elliptical path.

- The eccentric radius controls the **throw (S)**.
- The eccentric rotational speed governs the **number of compression cycles per unit time (n)**.
- Both parameters directly dictate **capacity** and **power consumption**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarises **every formula** you must know for the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) for Gyratory Crushers. Use `\\vert` for absolute value notation in mathematical expressions to keep the table syntactically intact.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reduction Ratio (RR)** | $RR = \dfrac{D_f}{D_p}$ | $D_f$ = Feed size (m), $D_p$ = Product size (m) | Dimensionless; ideal range: 5 – 8 |
| 2 | **Crusher Capacity (Tonnes/hr)** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \, D_g \, L \, n \, \rho_b \, S}{3600}$ | $D_g$ = Gape diameter (m), $L$ = Length (m), $n$ = Speed (RPM), $\rho_b$ = Bulk density (kg/m³), $S$ = Stroke/Throw (m) | Simplified form (per kg/s basis); multiply by 3600 for TPH |
| 3 | **Alternative Capacity Formula** | $Q = 0.785 \, W \, D \, S \, n \, \rho_b$ | $W$ = Discharge width (m), $D$ = Gape (m) | Frequently used in textbook derivations |
| 4 | **Motor Power — Bond's Crushing Law** | $P = \dfrac{W_i \, Q}{1000} \left[\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right]$ | $W_i$ = Work index (kWh/tonne), $Q$ = Throughput (tonne/hr), $F_{80}$ = 80% passing feed size (μm), $P_{80}$ = 80% passing product size (μm) | The most-examined formula in KTU 2024 ESE |
| 5 | **Power via Kick's Law** | $E_k = K_k \, f_c \, \ln\left(\dfrac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$ | $K_k$ = Kick's constant, $f_c$ = Crushing strength factor, $D_f$, $D_p$ = Feed/Product sizes | Applicable to single-event breakage |
| 6 | **Rittinger's Number (Surface Energy)** | $E_R = K_R \, (D_f^{-1} - D_p^{-1})$ | $K_R$ = Rittinger's constant | Best for fine crushing |
| 7 | **Gyratory Speed Limit** | $n_{max} = \dfrac{420 \, \sqrt{S}}{\sqrt{D_g}}$ | $S$ = Stroke (cm), $D_g$ = Gape (cm) | Empirical formula — **must NOT be exceeded** to avoid choking |
| 8 | **Gyrating Length of Opening** | $L = \pi \, D_m \, \theta$ | $D_m$ = Mean mantle diameter, $\theta$ = Active crushing angle (radians) | Derived from geometric consideration |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always quote units explicitly in the final answer. KTU evaluators routinely deduct **½ to 1 mark** simply because a student wrote "Power = 14" without "kW" attached.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gyratory Crushers are the **backbone of the mining and mineral processing industry**. Their application spectrum is vast:

- **Copper & Iron Ore Mining:** Primary reduction of ROM ore before SAG (Semi-Autogenous Grinding) mills.
- **Aggregate & Quarrying:** Limestone and granite processing for the construction sector.
- **Cement Industry:** Limestone size reduction prior to raw mill feed.
- **Coal Handling Plants (CHP):** Used in thermal power stations for the primary crushing of coal lumps.

In Kerala's mineral-rich zones (e.g., the laterite and granite quarries in Pathanamthitta, Kollam, and Malappuram districts), gyratory-style cone crushers are increasingly preferred over jaw crushers for high-throughput granite aggregate production.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation #1 — Capacity of a Gyratory Crusher

This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU 2024 Board Examination. The derivation begins with a volumetric flow assumption.

**Assumptions (to be explicitly stated in your answer script):**

1. The crushing chamber is approximated as a frustum (truncated cone) with mean diameter $D_m$.
2. Material flows as a continuous, packed bulk layer with bulk density $\rho_b$.
3. The mantle completes $n$ revolutions per minute (RPM).
4. The throw (S) is the radial displacement per revolution.

**Step 1 — Volume discharged per revolution:**
A layer of thickness equal to the throw $S$ is discharged during every revolution of the eccentric. The volumetric discharge per revolution equals the cross-sectional area of the discharge opening multiplied by the throw.

$$V_{rev} = A_{discharge} \times S$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of discharge:**
For a gyratory crusher, the discharge area is taken as the product of the gape diameter and the active length of the crushing zone:

$$A_{discharge} = \pi \cdot D_g \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the length of the crushing opening (the perimeter segment that actively produces fines).

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow per minute:**
Multiplying the per-revolution volume by the speed in RPM:

$$V_{min} = V_{rev} \times n = \pi \cdot D_g \cdot L \cdot S \cdot n$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to mass flow rate:**
Mass flow rate per minute is volume multiplied by bulk density:

$$\dot{m}_{min} = \pi \cdot D_g \cdot L \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho_b \quad \text{[kg/min]}$$

**Step 5 — Conversion to Tonnes per Hour:**
Dividing by 1000 (to convert kg to tonnes) and multiplying by 60 (minutes per hour):

$$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot D_g \cdot L \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho_b \times 60}{1000 \times 1000}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \, D_g \, L \, S \, n \, \rho_b}{16{,}667} \;\; \text{[Tonnes/hr]}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalently**, the KTU-accepted form often quoted in textbooks is the per-second basis:
> $$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot D_g \cdot L \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho_b}{60} \;\; \text{[kg/s]}$$
> Always check the question's specified unit basis before plugging in numerical values.

---

### 3.2 Derivation #2 — Bond's Law Applied to Power Calculation

**Bond's General Theory:**
The total energy required for size reduction is proportional to the **new surface area generated** by the breakage. Bond postulated that the net energy consumed per tonne of feed is:

$$E = W_i \left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right]$$

where $W_i$ is the **Bond Work Index** — an empirical material property (in kWh/tonne) measured by a standardized laboratory test.

**Step 1 — Energy per tonne:**
By definition of $W_i$:

$$E = W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right) \;\; \text{[kWh/tonne]}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by throughput to get total power:**
Total power = Energy per tonne × Feed rate (tonne/hr):

$$P_{kW} = W_i \cdot Q \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Scaling for 80% passing sizes in micrometers:**
Since $F_{80}$ and $P_{80}$ are conventionally reported in micrometers (μm), the standard KTU textbook form is:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{W_i \cdot Q}{1000}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right] \;\; \text{[kW]}}$$

The factor $\frac{1}{1000}$ emerges from the unit reconciliation between kWh/tonne and kW capacity.

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Numerical (KTU-style):**
> A gyratory crusher has a gape diameter of **1.2 m**, an active crushing length of **0.9 m**, runs at **180 RPM**, has a stroke of **2.5 cm**, and processes a limestone ore of bulk density **1600 kg/m³**. Calculate the throughput in **Tonnes/hr (TPH)**.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $D_g = 1.2$ m
- $L = 0.9$ m
- $n = 180$ RPM
- $S = 2.5$ cm $= 0.025$ m
- $\rho_b = 1600$ kg/m³

Applying the capacity formula on a per-second basis:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot D_g \cdot L \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho_b}{60}$$

Step-wise substitution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{3.14159 \times 1.2 \times 0.9 \times 0.025 \times 180 \times 1600}{60} \\[6pt]
Q &= \frac{3.14159 \times 1.2 \times 0.9 \times 0.025 \times 180 \times 1600}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.14159 \times 1.08 \times 0.025 \times 180 \times 1600}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.14159 \times 0.027 \times 180 \times 1600}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.14159 \times 0.027 \times 288000}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{24{,}429.5}{60} \\[6pt]
Q &\approx 407.16 \;\; \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to **Tonnes per Hour:**

$$Q_{TPH} = 407.16 \times \frac{3600}{1000} = 407.16 \times 3.6 \approx 1465.8 \;\; \text{TPH}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 1466 \;\; \text{TPH}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bond vs. Kick vs. Rittinger

The following matrix is a high-yield KTU cheat sheet for the comparison questions that frequently appear in Part B.

| Parameter | Kick's Law | Rittinger's Law | Bond's Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underlying Assumption** | Energy ∝ Volume reduction | Energy ∝ New surface area | Energy ∝ Length of crack propagation |
| **Mathematical Form** | $E \propto \ln(D_f/D_p)$ | $E \propto (1/D_p - 1/D_f)$ | $E \propto (1/\sqrt{D_p} - 1/\sqrt{D_f})$ |
| **Best Applicable Regime** | Coarse crushing, single-event breakage | Fine grinding (large new surface area) | Intermediate crushing (industrial standard) |
| **Constant Name** | Kick's constant ($K_k$) | Rittinger's constant ($K_R$) | Work Index ($W_i$) |
| **Use in Gyratory Crushers** | Limited (rough estimate) | Less preferred | **Most preferred** |
| **Order of Magnitude of $W_i$** | — | — | Limestone ≈ 11 – 14 kWh/t; Granite ≈ 15 – 18 kWh/t |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Gyratory Crusher (Block-Level Mermaid Diagram)

Below is a high-resolution block diagram mapping the **functional flow** of materials, force, and motion in a gyratory crusher. The diagram adheres strictly to the mermaid compilation safeguards: alphanumeric node identifiers, no reserved keywords as node names, and clean uppercase labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[ROM ORE FEED] --> B1[FEED HOPPER]
    B1 --> C1[UPPER CONCAVE BOWL SECTION]
    C1 --> D1[CRUSHING CHAMBER FRUSTUM ZONE]
    D1 --> E1[ECCENTRIC SHAFT DRIVE UNIT]
    E1 --> F1[GYRATING MANTLE WITH ECCENTRIC SLEEVE]
    F1 --> G1[COMPRESSION AND SHEAR STRESS APPLICATION]
    G1 --> H1[PARTICLE FRACTURE AND BED COMPACTION]
    H1 --> I1[DISCHARGE OPENING CSS OSS GAP]
    I1 --> J1[PRODUCT CONVEYOR BELT]
    J1 --> K1[SECONDARY CRUSHER CIRCUIT]
    E1 --> L1[POWER TRANSMISSION GEARBOX]
    L1 --> M1[ELECTRIC MOTOR DRIVE]
    M1 --> N1[CONTROL PLC PANEL]
    N1 --> O1[CURRENT LOAD FEEDBACK]
    O1 -. Feedback .-> F1
    H1 --> P1[FINE FRACTIONS DUST EXTRACTION]
    P1 --> Q1[BAG FILTER DEDUSTING UNIT]
```

**Diagram Interpretation Walkthrough:**

- **A1 → B1 → C1:** Material entry pathway; gravity-fed from a haul truck into the hopper and into the bowl.
- **D1:** The conical frustum is the actual crushing zone — particles experience repeated stress here.
- **E1 → F1 → G1:** The drive mechanism. The eccentric rotates → mantle gyrates → compressive/shear force applied.
- **H1 → I1 → J1 → K1:** Fractured particles exit and are conveyed for further reduction (secondary crushing).
- **L1 → M1 → N1:** Power flow and modern automation; PLC feedback adjusts the CSS in real time based on current load (O1).

---

### 4.2 Physical Layout — Cross-Sectional Schematic Description

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Mermaid Block Diagram Instead of a Physical Drawing?**
> Pure cross-sectional physical drawings of industrial gyratory crushers contain curved, non-orthogonal shapes (the conical mantle, the bowl liner, the spider arm) that exceed the precision of vector Mermaid syntax. The block-level functional flow above captures **all the essential engineering interactions** — material, force, motion, and control — without loss of conceptual fidelity.

If the student wishes to manually sketch the physical cross-section in the answer script, the **labelled components** are:

1. **Drive Shaft (Top)** — vertical, driven by a bevel gear.
2. **Eccentric Bushing (Just below the spider arm)** — converts rotation into circular motion of the mantle.
3. **Mantle (Inner, Conical)** — wears out faster; rotated by the eccentric.
4. **Concave/Bowl Liner (Outer, Conical)** — stationary; worn by abrasion.
5. **Adjustment Ring (Lower Outer Ring)** — used to set the OSS/CSS.
6. **Dust Seal Assembly** — prevents fines from entering the eccentric bearing.
7. **Discharge Opening (Bottom)** — the minimum gap between mantle and concave.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Gyratory Crusher. Mention its reduction ratio range.**

**Model Answer:**
A gyratory crusher is a primary size-reduction device operating on the principle of continuous **gyratory compression**. The crushing action is provided by a conical mantle rotating eccentrically within a fixed concave bowl, imparting repeated compressive and shear stresses on the feed material as it descends.

**Reduction Ratio (RR):** Typically between **3:1 and 10:1**, with the **ideal industrial range being 5:1 to 8:1** for efficient primary crushing without excessive fines generation or choking.

*Self-Assessment Keywords for 3 marks:* Definition (1 mark) + Principle (1 mark) + RR range (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State Bond's Crushing Law. What does the term Work Index signify?**

**Model Answer:**
Bond's Crushing Law states that the **total work required to reduce a feed material from size $F_{80}$ to a product size $P_{80}$** is proportional to the square root of the reciprocal of the product size minus the square root of the reciprocal of the feed size:

$$W = W_i \left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right]$$

The **Work Index ($W_i$)** is the total energy input (in **kWh/tonne**) required to reduce a theoretically infinite feed size to **80% passing 100 μm** in a standard Bond grindability test apparatus.

*Self-Assessment Keywords for 3 marks:* Statement of Bond's Law (1.5 marks) + Definition of $W_i$ with units (1.5 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a gyratory crusher. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3.5 Marks):**
A gyratory crusher consists of the following principal components:
1. **Drive shaft and motor assembly** — mounted at the top.
2. **Spider arm** — holds the top of the mantle; suspended from the drive shaft.
3. **Eccentric bushing/sleeve** — converts the rotational motion of the drive shaft into a circular, wobbling motion of the mantle.
4. **Mantle** — the inner conical crushing surface that rotates eccentrically.
5. **Concave/Bowl liner** — the outer stationary conical surface.
6. **Adjustment ring** — at the bottom; used to raise or lower the concave to set CSS.
7. **Dust sealing arrangement** — protects the bearing from fine particles.
8. **Discharge opening** — at the bottom.

**Working (3.5 Marks):**
1. ROM ore is fed from the top hopper into the bowl-shaped crushing chamber.
2. The motor drives the eccentric sleeve through a gear train.
3. The eccentric causes the mantle to **gyrate** (oscillate in a circular path) within the concave.
4. As the mantle moves **toward** the concave, particles in the gap are compressed.
5. As the mantle moves **away**, the broken fragments fall further down by gravity into the new, wider gap region.
6. Particles that are smaller than the CSS exit the bottom of the crusher.
7. The process is **continuous** — there is no idle return stroke, unlike a jaw crusher.

---

**Q. (b)** A gyratory crusher has a gape diameter of **1.5 m**, an active crushing length of **1.0 m**, runs at **160 RPM**, with a stroke of **3.0 cm**. It is fed a copper ore of bulk density **2200 kg/m³**. Compute:
(i) The throughput in **TPH** (Tonnes per Hour).
(ii) The throughput in **kg/s**.

Take $\pi = 3.1416$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $D_g = 1.5$ m
- $L = 1.0$ m
- $n = 160$ RPM
- $S = 3.0$ cm $= 0.03$ m
- $\rho_b = 2200$ kg/m³

**Formula:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \, D_g \, L \, S \, n \, \rho_b}{60} \;\; \text{[kg/s]}$$

**Step-by-Step Calculation (3.5 Marks for (i), 3.5 Marks for (ii) + units):**

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{3.1416 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.03 \times 160 \times 2200}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.1416 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.03 \times 160 \times 2200}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.1416 \times 0.045 \times 160 \times 2200}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.1416 \times 0.045 \times 352000}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.1416 \times 15840}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{49{,}762.94}{60} \\[6pt]
Q &\approx 829.38 \;\; \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Converting to TPH:**

$$Q_{TPH} = 829.38 \times 3.6 = 2985.78 \;\; \text{TPH}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 829.4 \;\; \text{kg/s} \;\; \text{or} \;\; 2985.8 \;\; \text{TPH}}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**Q. (a)** Differentiate between **Jaw Crusher** and **Gyratory Crusher** based on at least **six** parameters. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Jaw Crusher | Gyratory Crusher |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle of Operation** | Reciprocating compression | Continuous gyratory compression |
| **Feed Size** | Up to ~1 m | Up to ~1.5 m |
| **Throughput** | Lower (≤ 500 TPH) | Higher (≥ 1000 TPH) |
| **Capacity for given Feed** | Lower | Higher (2× – 5× jaw crusher) |
| **Wear Pattern** | Unilateral — one jaw wears faster | Circumferential — even wear |
| **Footprint (per TPH)** | Larger | Smaller (more compact per unit capacity) |
| **Operating Cycle** | Stop-and-go (only compression on stroke) | Continuous (no idle period) |
| **Maintenance Cost** | Higher (frequent toggle plate replacement) | Lower |
| **Initial Capital Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Use of Reciprocating Parts** | Yes (toggle, pitman) | No (only rotating eccentric) |

**Jaw Crusher:**
- **Advantage:** Low capital cost; simpler design; suitable for sticky/wet material.
- **Disadvantage:** Lower throughput per unit time; high wear on fixed jaw; intermittent operation.

**Gyratory Crusher:**
- **Advantage:** Very high throughput; lower wear per tonne; handles very large ROM feed.
- **Disadvantage:** Higher initial investment; complex bearing assembly; requires skilled maintenance.

---

**Q. (b)** Using **Bond's Law**, calculate the power required by a gyratory crusher processing **250 TPH** of granite, given:
- Work index of granite, $W_i = 17$ kWh/tonne
- Feed size, $F_{80} = 120{,}000 \; \mu m$ (i.e., 12 cm)
- Product size, $P_{80} = 25{,}000 \; \mu m$ (i.e., 2.5 cm) **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Formula:**

$$P = \frac{W_i \cdot Q}{1000}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right]$$

**Step 1 — Compute the square roots:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{F_{80}} &= \sqrt{120{,}000} \approx 346.41 \;\; \mu m^{0.5} \\[6pt]
\sqrt{P_{80}} &= \sqrt{25{,}000} = 158.11 \;\; \mu m^{0.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bracket term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} &= \frac{1}{158.11} - \frac{1}{346.41} \\[6pt]
&= 0.006325 - 0.002887 \\[6pt]
&= 0.003438 \;\; \mu m^{-0.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $W_i$ and $Q$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{17 \times 250}{1000} \times 0.003438 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4250}{1000} \times 0.003438 \\[6pt]
&= 4.25 \times 0.003438 \\[6pt]
P &= 0.01461 \;\; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this yields an unrealistically small power. **This is the classic KTU pitfall!** Bond's formula uses **tonne/hr in the numerator when $W_i$ is given in kWh/tonne**, but the original form **does NOT have a 1/1000 factor** when $Q$ is in tonnes/hr. Let us correct:

The correct Bond equation (McCabe/Harriott/Perry standard) for power in kW is:

$$P = W_i \cdot Q \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right]$$

where $P$ is in **kW** when $Q$ is in **tonne/hr** and the bracket is in **m$^{-0.5}$** (not μm$^{-0.5}$).

**Re-doing with sizes in meters:**

- $F_{80} = 0.12$ m
- $P_{80} = 0.025$ m

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.025}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.12}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{0.1581} - \frac{1}{0.3464} \\[6pt]
&= 6.3246 - 2.8868 \\[6pt]
&= 3.4378 \;\; m^{-0.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Now compute Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 17 \times 250 \times 3.4378 \\[6pt]
&= 4250 \times 3.4378 \\[6pt]
P &\approx 14{,}611 \;\; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 14{,}611 \;\; \text{kW} \;\; (\text{or} \;\; 14.61 \;\; \text{MW})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch is the #1 cause of lost marks.** Always ensure $F_{80}$ and $P_{80}$ are in **consistent units** (either both in micrometers OR both in meters). Mixing them gives wildly incorrect answers.
> 2. The Bond's law expression does NOT carry a $1/1000$ factor when using SI units of metres and tonne/hr. The textbook simplification **does** include it for μm units — students often apply the wrong version.
> 3. Students frequently **omit the unit "kW"** in the final answer, losing ½ mark.
> 4. **Do not confuse** the power rating of the motor with the Bond's law output. The motor must be oversized by **15–25%** for safety factor.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must revise before ESE):**

- Gyratory crushers operate on **continuous compressive + shear stress** in a conical frustum chamber.
- **Reduction Ratio** = Feed size / Product size, ideally 5:1 – 8:1.
- **Capacity** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \, D_g \, L \, S \, n \, \rho_b}{60}$ (kg/s) or $\times \dfrac{3600}{1000}$ to convert to TPH.
- **Bond's Law** is the **standard** for industrial power estimation: $P = W_i \cdot Q \left[\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right]$.
- Always quote **units** (kg/s, TPH, kW, μm) explicitly.
- Gyratory crushers handle **larger ROM feed** than jaw crushers; **higher throughput**; **lower wear per tonne**.
- **Speed limit** $n_{max} \approx \dfrac{420 \sqrt{S}}{\sqrt{D_g}}$ — exceeding this causes choking.
- Mantle wears **conically and uniformly**; concave replacement is the main maintenance task.
- The eccentric sleeve's rotational speed and the throw (S) are the **two most critical design parameters**.
- Bond's Work Index ($W_i$) is **material-dependent**: granite ≈ 15–18; limestone ≈ 11–14; copper ore ≈ 13–15; iron ore ≈ 12–15 (all in kWh/tonne).
- **Inter-particle compression** is the dominant breakage mechanism in gyratory crushers — unlike jaw crushers where single-particle crushing dominates.
- For 7-mark derivation questions, **always begin with a diagram** and **list assumptions** before starting the algebra.
- For 7-mark numerical questions, **show every substitution step** in aligned equations — do not skip arithmetic.
- Modern gyratory crushers use **hydraulic adjustment** and **PLC-based CSS control** — frequently tested in 2024 scheme ESE under "modern trends."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crushing Rolls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crushing Rolls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Crushing Rolls — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Crushing Rolls (Roll Crusher):** A *comminution device* consisting of **two counter-rotating cylindrical rolls** (mounted horizontally on heavy shafts, with axes parallel) that draw the feed material into the gap between them, where it is **crushed by a combination of compression and shear (frictional drag)**. One roll is mounted on fixed bearings while the other is spring-loaded to permit the passage of uncrushable (tramp) material.

The classification under KTU 2024 syllabus places crushing rolls under the family of **intermediate and fine crushers** (compression-type crushers), distinct from **jaw crushers** (reciprocating compression) and **impact crushers** (dynamic impact). Crushing rolls typically achieve a **reduction ratio (RR) of 4 : 1 up to 10 : 1**, making them ideal for producing a comparatively **uniform, cubical product** with fewer fines than impact mills.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Crushing Rolls as a "Washing-Mangle for Rocks"** 🪨
> 
> Imagine two heavy steel cylinders rolling *toward each other* like a domestic wringer or pasta-roller. A piece of rock dropped between them is grabbed by friction, dragged downward, and **squeezed + sheared** until it shatters into smaller fragments that fall out the bottom. One cylinder is fixed like a wall; the other sits on a spring-loaded "shock absorber" so that if a metal bolt enters, the roll simply retreats momentarily instead of breaking the machine.

## 1.3 Classification of Crushing Rolls

| Type | Surface Profile | Typical Feed | Reduction Ratio |
|------|----------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Smooth Rolls** | Polished steel | Hard, abrasive materials (≤ 25 mm) | 4 : 1 |
| **Toothed Rolls** | Sharp protruding teeth | Soft, friable, moist materials (coal, limestone) | up to 10 : 1 |
| **Ribbed/Fluted Rolls** | Longitudinal grooves | Medium-hard materials | 5 : 1 to 7 : 1 |
| **Single-Roll Crusher** | One toothed roll + breaker plate | Coal, gypsum, soft ores | 4 : 1 to 6 : 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite:** Students frequently lose marks by failing to identify the *type* of roll used. The choice is governed by the **Mohs hardness** and **moisture content** of the feed.

## 1.4 Industrial Importance (Engineering Utility)

- **Mining & Mineral Processing**: Pre-reduction of iron ore, copper ore, and bauxite before ball milling.
- **Cement Industry**: Crushing of clinker, limestone, and slag.
- **Coal Preparation**: Toothed rolls are the workhorse for crushing ROM (Run-Of-Mine) coal.
- **Fertilizer Plants**: Size reduction of phosphate rock and potash.
- **Power Plants**: Pulverizer feed preparation.

The operating advantages include **lower power consumption per ton**, **lower fines generation**, **uniform product**, and **quiet, dust-free operation** compared to impact mills.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics of Nipping

A particle is "nipped" only when it is **dragged into the gap faster than it can escape upward**. The mechanics involve four simultaneous phenomena:

1. **Normal Compressive Stress** (from the roll surface reaction).
2. **Tangential Shear Stress** (from surface friction dragging the particle downward).
3. **Bending Failure** of the particle under the principal stress.
4. **Release Strain Energy** as the particle fractures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Threshold — The Angle of Nip (α):** The maximum angle (measured at the roll center) that the contact line can subtend before the particle is *ejected* rather than *crushed*. For **successful nipping**, α must be **less than the angle of friction (φ)** between the roll and the feed material. For steel on most minerals, φ ≈ **31°**, hence design rule: **α ≤ 16°** for smooth rolls, **α ≈ 24°** for toothed rolls.

## 2.2 Key Design & Operating Variables

| Variable | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Performance |
|----------|--------|---------------|----------------------|
| Roll radius | R | 0.15 – 0.60 m | Larger R → larger permissible feed |
| Set (gap between rolls) | 2s | 5 – 25 mm | Smaller s → smaller product, lower capacity |
| Roll speed | N | 50 – 300 rpm | Higher N → higher capacity, risk of slip |
| Roll length (width) | L | 0.3 – 1.5 m | Larger L → proportional capacity gain |
| Feed particle radius | r | d_f/2 | Larger r → smaller α |
| Angle of friction | φ | 16° – 32° | Sets upper limit on α |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — The "Must-Know" Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table and memorize every entry.** These seven equations constitute roughly **70 % of every numerical problem** on Crushing Rolls in KTU examinations.

### A. Geometric / Capacity Equations

$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{R + s}{R + r}
$$

$$
r_{\max} = R \,\frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) - \frac{s}{R}}{1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 60 \, \pi \, N \, L \, s \, D \, \rho \quad \text{[kg/hr basis when } \rho \text{ in kg/m³]}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{tons/hr}} \;=\; 0.6 \, N \, L \, D \, s \, \rho \quad (\rho \text{ in t/m³, dims in m})
$$

### B. Power / Energy Equations

$$
P_{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; T \, \omega \;=\; 2 \pi N T \quad [T = \text{torque in N·m}]
$$

$$
P_{\text{applied}} \;=\; \mu \, F_c \, v_{\text{peripheral}} \;=\; \mu \, F_c \, \pi D N
$$

$$
W_i \;=\; \frac{W}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}} \quad \text{[Bond's Work Index form]}
$$

### C. Reduction Ratio

$$
\text{RR} \;=\; \frac{D_{f,\text{mean}}}{D_{p,\text{mean}}} \;=\; \frac{F_{80}}{P_{80}}
$$

### D. Slip Condition (no-slip nipping)

$$
N_{\max} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{g}{2\pi^{2} D}} \cdot \frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)}{\left(1 + \cos\alpha\right)} \quad \text{[academic / derivation form]}
$$

---

### Formula Legend & Units (Anti-Hallucination Check)

| Symbol | Meaning | SI Units |
|--------|---------|----------|
| $\alpha$ | Angle of nip | rad or degrees |
| $R$ | Radius of rolls | m |
| $r$ | Radius of largest feed particle | m |
| $s$ | Half the set (gap ÷ 2) | m |
| $D$ | Diameter of rolls | m |
| $N$ | Roll rotational speed | rev/s or rpm |
| $L$ | Length of rolls (axial width) | m |
| $\rho$ | Bulk density of feed | kg/m³ or t/m³ |
| $Q$ | Volumetric / mass throughput | m³/hr or tons/hr |
| $F_c$ | Compressive crushing force | N |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction | dimensionless |
| $W_i$ | Bond Work Index | kWh/ton |
| $F_{80}, P_{80}$ | 80 %-passing size of feed/product | mm or μm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Unit Error:** Many students use $N$ in **rpm** but forget the factor $60$ when computing capacity in **per-hour** units. Always state the units explicitly to earn full marks.

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Plant Design**: Equations (1)–(3) are used in the **sizing** of new roll crushers before procurement.
- **Process Optimization**: Equation (4) enables operators to balance **throughput vs. product size** in real time.
- **Energy Auditing**: Equations (5)–(7) feed into the plant's overall **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** calculation — a key performance indicator (KPI) for ISO-50001 energy management systems.
- **Safety Interlocks**: The slip condition $N_{\max}$ prevents over-speeding, which would cause material to be thrown out centrifugally rather than crushed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — The Angle of Nip Formula

This is the *single most important derivation* in Crushing Rolls. Every KTU numerical problem depends on it.

### Step 1 — Establish the Geometric Setup

Consider two identical rolls of radius $R$, with their centers separated by a horizontal distance $2C$. The set (gap) between the rolls is $2s$, hence:

$$
C \;=\; R + s
$$

A feed particle (treated as a sphere) of radius $r$ is positioned at the angle of nip, tangent to **both** rolls. By symmetry, its center lies on the vertical line passing through the midpoint between the two roll centers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Insight:** The triangle formed by the right roll center, the left roll center, and the particle center is **isosceles** with the line of centers as its base.

### Step 2 — Apply the Tangency Condition

The distance from each roll center to the particle center must equal $R + r$ (because the particle is externally tangent to each roll). Using the right roll center at $(C, 0)$ and the particle center at $(0, y_p)$:

$$
\sqrt{C^{2} + y_{p}^{\,2}} \;=\; R + r
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
C^{2} + y_{p}^{\,2} \;=\; (R+r)^{2}
$$

Solving for $y_p$:

$$
y_{p}^{\,2} \;=\; (R+r)^{2} - C^{2}
$$

$$
y_{p}^{\,2} \;=\; (R+r)^{2} - (R+s)^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Express the Half-Angle of Nip

Let $\beta$ denote the half-angle of nip, measured at the right roll center between the line of centers (horizontal) and the line joining the roll center to the particle center. Then:

$$
\sin\beta \;=\; \frac{C}{R+r} \;=\; \frac{R+s}{R+r}
$$

$$
\cos\beta \;=\; \frac{y_{p}}{R+r}
$$

Therefore, by trigonometric identity:

$$
\cos\beta \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{(R+r)^{2} - (R+s)^{2}}}{R+r}
$$

### Step 4 — Obtain the Full Angle of Nip

The full angle of nip is $\alpha = 2\beta$ (since the geometry is symmetric). Applying the double-angle identity $\cos(2\beta) = 1 - 2\sin^{2}\beta$:

$$
\cos\alpha \;=\; 1 - 2\sin^{2}\beta
$$

$$
\cos\alpha \;=\; 1 - 2\left(\frac{R+s}{R+r}\right)^{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Alternative Compact Form (Using Half-Angle)

For practical engineering, the more useful form uses the **half-angle**:

$$
\boxed{\;\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) \;=\; \frac{R + s}{R + r}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This compact form is the **working formula** that examiners expect you to write down immediately when a numerical problem is posed.

### Step 6 — Derive the Maximum Feed Radius

Rearranging the boxed formula for $r$:

$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) (R + r) \;=\; R + s
$$

$$
R\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) + r\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) \;=\; R + s
$$

$$
r\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) \;=\; R + s - R\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
$$

$$
r \;=\; \frac{R + s - R\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)}{\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;r_{\max} \;=\; \frac{R(1 - \cos\!\frac{\alpha}{2}) + s}{\cos\!\frac{\alpha}{2}}\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Capacity (Throughput) of a Roll Crusher

### Step 1 — Visualize the Discharge Layer

At steady state, a thin layer of crushed material (thickness = set = $2s$) is discharged from each roll per revolution. The width of this layer along the roll circumference equals $L$ (the roll length).

### Step 2 — Volume of Material Discharged per Revolution (per roll)

For one roll, the discharge is approximately a slab of cross-section $L \times s$ and arc-length corresponding to one revolution ($2\pi R$). However, in practice, we use the **set-gap approximation** (assuming the material layer thickness equals $s$ on each side):

$$
V_{\text{per rev, per roll}} \;=\; \pi D \cdot L \cdot s
$$

### Step 3 — Account for Both Rolls

With two rolls discharging simultaneously:

$$
V_{\text{per rev, total}} \;=\; 2 \pi D L s
$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by Rotation Frequency

If the rolls rotate at $N$ revolutions per **minute**, the volumetric discharge rate is:

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; 2 \pi D L s N \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{min}]
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Per Hour and Multiply by Density

$$
Q_{\text{tons/hr}} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi D L s N \rho}{1000} \cdot 60 \quad [\rho \text{ in kg/m}^3]
$$

Simplifying (note $\pi \approx 3.1416$):

$$
\boxed{\;Q_{\text{tons/hr}} \;\approx\; 0.6 \, N \, D \, L \, s \, \rho \quad (\rho \text{ in t/m}^3,\; D, L, s \text{ in m},\; N \text{ in rpm})\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:** A smooth roll crusher is to crush limestone having a feed size of 8 cm. The rolls are 1.0 m in diameter and 0.4 m wide. The angle of nip is 31° 8′ and the coefficient of friction between the roll surface and limestone is 0.30. The roll speed is 100 rpm. The set (gap) is 1.5 cm. The bulk density of limestone is 1.6 t/m³.

**Required:** (a) Verify the rolls will successfully nip the feed. (b) Calculate the capacity in tons/hr. (c) Find the theoretical power required.

### Part (a) — Verify Nipping

Given:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{1.0}{2} \;=\; 0.5 \text{ m}, \quad r \;=\; \frac{0.08}{2} \;=\; 0.04 \text{ m}, \quad s \;=\; \frac{0.015}{2} \;=\; 0.0075 \text{ m}
$$

Compute $(R+s)/(R+r)$:

$$
\frac{R + s}{R + r} \;=\; \frac{0.5 + 0.0075}{0.5 + 0.04} \;=\; \frac{0.5075}{0.54} \;=\; 0.9398
$$

The required half-angle of nip:

$$
\frac{\alpha}{2} \;=\; \cos^{-1}(0.9398) \;=\; 20.0°
$$

So $\alpha = 40°$.

Angle of friction $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu) = \tan^{-1}(0.30) = 16.7°$.

**Comparison:**

$$
\alpha = 40° \quad \text{vs.} \quad \phi = 16.7°
$$

Since $\alpha \gg \phi$, the rolls will **NOT** successfully nip the feed — the particles will be ejected. **Action required:** Increase $R$, decrease $s$, or pre-crush the feed.

### Part (b) — Capacity

$$
Q \;=\; 0.6 \, N \, D \, L \, s \, \rho
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 0.6 \times 100 \times 1.0 \times 0.4 \times 0.015 \times 1.6
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 0.6 \times 100 \times 0.0096
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 0.576 \text{ tons/hr}
$$

### Part (c) — Theoretical Power (using Bond's Work Index)

For limestone, $W_i = 11 \text{ kWh/ton}$ (literature value).

Using Bond's equation (approximating $P_{80} \approx s = 1.5$ cm = 15 mm and $F_{80} = 80$ mm):

$$
W \;=\; W_i \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right) \cdot \frac{10}{\sqrt{1 \text{ mm unit}}}
$$

$$
W \;=\; 11 \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{15}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{80}}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1}}
$$

$$
W \;=\; 11 \left(0.2582 - 0.1118\right) \;=\; 11 \times 0.1464
$$

$$
W \;\approx\; 1.61 \text{ kWh/ton}
$$

Required power:

$$
P \;=\; W \times Q \;=\; 1.61 \times 0.576 \;\approx\; 0.93 \text{ kW}
$$

---

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of a Roll Crusher Assembly (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FEED HOPPER"] --> B["FEED CHUTE"]
    B --> C["FIXED ROLL ASSEMBLY"]
    B --> D["MOVABLE ROLL ASSEMBLY"]
    C --> E["MAIN SHAFT BEARINGS"]
    D --> F["SPRING TENSION SYSTEM"]
    F --> G["SAFETY RELIEF DEVICE"]
    D --> H["ADJUSTMENT SCREWS"]
    H --> I["SET GAP INDICATOR"]
    C --> J["DRIVE MOTOR VIA V-BELT"]
    D --> J
    J --> K["COUNTER-ROTATING GEARBOX"]
    K --> C
    K --> D
    C --> L["DISCHARGE CHUTE"]
    D --> L
    L --> M["PRODUCT CONVEYOR"]
    M --> N["SCREEN OR CLASSIFIER"]
    N --> O["OVERSIZE RECYCLE LOOP"]
    O --> B
    N --> P["FINAL PRODUCT STOCKPILE"]
```

### Reading the Architecture

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| A — Feed Hopper | Receives bulk raw material |
| B — Feed Chute | Directs material into the nip zone |
| C — Fixed Roll | Stationary crushing surface |
| D — Movable Roll | Spring-loaded to allow tramp material to pass |
| E — Main Shaft Bearings | Support rotating shafts |
| F — Spring System | Provides safety and maintains gap |
| G — Safety Relief | Mechanical overload protector |
| H — Adjustment Screws | Sets the desired gap (set) |
| I — Gap Indicator | Allows operator to monitor gap |
| J — Drive Motor | Prime mover (typically 15-300 kW) |
| K — Gearbox | Ensures counter-rotation |
| L — Discharge Chute | Collects crushed product |
| M — Conveyor | Transports crushed product |
| N — Screen | Separates oversize from undersize |
| O — Recycle Loop | Returns oversize for re-crushing |
| P — Stockpile | Final product storage |

## 4.2 Process Flow of Material Through the Rolls

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["STAGE 1: FEED ENTRY"] --> S2["STAGE 2: FRICTION GRAB"]
    S2 --> S3["STAGE 3: COMPRESSION + SHEAR"]
    S3 --> S4["STAGE 4: FRACTURE"]
    S4 --> S5["STAGE 5: DISCHARGE"]
    
    S1 -.->|angle of nip less than friction angle| S2
    S2 -.->|tangential drag exceeds gravity reaction| S3
    S3 -.->|stress exceeds material strength| S4
    S4 -.->|fragments smaller than gap| S5
```

## 4.3 Force-Velocity Diagram at the Nip Zone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["ROLL SURFACE"] -->|Normal Force N| P["FEED PARTICLE"]
    R2["ROLL SURFACE"] -->|Friction Force μN| P
    P -->|Gravity mg downward| G["GROUND REFERENCE"]
    P -->|Tangential Velocity v| V["DISCHARGE"]
    G -->|Centrifugal tendency at high N| P
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Aid:** The diagram above shows that the **friction force** $\mu N$ acts *downward* on the particle (drawing it into the rolls), while the **normal force** $N$ acts perpendicular to the roll surface. For the particle to be nipped, $\mu N \geq mg \sin\theta$ at every point in the nip region, where $\theta$ is measured from the vertical.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> **Q:** Define the term **"angle of nip"** as applied to roll crushers. Why must it be less than the angle of friction?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **angle of nip** is defined as the maximum angle (measured at the roll center, between the line joining the two roll centers and the line from the roll center to the point where the feed particle first contacts the roll surface) at which a feed particle can be successfully drawn into the crushing zone without being ejected.

It must be **less than the angle of friction** $\phi$ between the roll surface and the feed material. This is because the tangential frictional component $\mu N$ must exceed the outward normal component to drag the particle downward into the gap. If $\alpha > \phi$, the particle will ride on top of the rolls and be thrown outward, defeating the purpose of crushing. For typical mineral-steel contacts, $\phi \approx 31°$, so designs ensure $\alpha \le 16°$ for smooth rolls.

### Question A2 — Comparative

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Q:** Compare **smooth rolls** and **toothed rolls** based on feed material, reduction ratio, and product characteristics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Smooth Rolls | Toothed Rolls |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| Suitable feed | Hard, dry, abrasive (ores, rock, cement clinker) | Soft, moist, friable (coal, limestone, clay) |
| Feed size limit | ≤ 25 mm (strict) | Up to several hundred mm |
| Reduction ratio | 4 : 1 | Up to 10 : 1 |
| Mechanism | Pure compression + friction shear | Tearing + ripping + compression |
| Product shape | Cubical, uniform | Irregular, coarser |
| Power consumption | Lower | Higher (more impact loading) |
| Typical application | Cement, mining | Coal preparation, fertilizers |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — Design and Capacity Calculation

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Set A]**

> **Q(a) [7 marks]:** Derive the relationship between the angle of nip ($\alpha$), roll radius ($R$), radius of feed particle ($r$), and half the set ($s$) for a roll crusher. State the condition for successful crushing.

> **Q(b) [7 marks]:** A smooth roll crusher has rolls of 100 cm diameter $\times$ 50 cm wide. The rolls operate at 80 rpm with a set of 1.0 cm. Limestone (density 1.6 t/m³) is being crushed. Calculate:
> (i) The maximum permissible feed size for an angle of nip of 31°.
> (ii) The theoretical capacity in tons per hour.
> (iii) The reduction ratio if the product size is 1.0 cm.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry setup:**

Place the right roll center at $(R+s, 0)$ and the left at $(-R-s, 0)$. The set is $2s$. A feed particle of radius $r$ at the angle of nip lies on the vertical line of symmetry at $(0, y_p)$.

**Step 2 — Tangency condition:**

The distance from either roll center to the particle center equals $R + r$:

$$
\sqrt{(R+s)^{2} + y_{p}^{\,2}} = R + r
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $y_p$:**

$$
y_{p} = \sqrt{(R+r)^{2} - (R+s)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Trigonometric relation:**

The half-angle $\beta = \alpha/2$ satisfies:

$$
\sin\beta = \frac{R+s}{R+r}
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$
\boxed{\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{R + s}{R + r}}
$$

**Step 6 — Condition for successful crushing:**

For successful nipping, $\alpha \le 2\phi$ where $\phi$ is the angle of friction. Equivalently:

$$
\frac{R + s}{R + r} \le \cos\phi
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:**

$$
R = 0.50 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.50 \text{ m}, \quad N = 80 \text{ rpm}, \quad 2s = 0.01 \text{ m} \Rightarrow s = 0.005 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\rho = 1.6 \text{ t/m}^3, \quad \alpha = 31°, \quad D_p = 1.0 \text{ cm} = 0.01 \text{ m}
$$

**(i) Maximum feed size:**

$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{31°}{2}\right) = \cos(15.5°) = 0.9636
$$

From the formula:

$$
0.9636 = \frac{0.50 + 0.005}{0.50 + r}
$$

$$
0.50 + r = \frac{0.505}{0.9636} = 0.5241
$$

$$
r = 0.0241 \text{ m} \approx 2.41 \text{ cm}
$$

Maximum feed diameter:

$$
D_{f,\max} = 2r = 4.82 \text{ cm} \approx 48.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical capacity:**

$$
Q = 0.6 \, N \, D \, L \, s \, \rho
$$

$$
Q = 0.6 \times 80 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 0.005 \times 1.6
$$

$$
Q = 0.192 \text{ tons/hr}
$$

**(iii) Reduction ratio:**

$$
\text{RR} = \frac{D_f}{D_p} = \frac{4.82}{1.0} = 4.82 \approx 4.82 : 1
$$

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (Operating Principle and Power)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Set B]**

> **Q(a) [7 marks]:** Explain the working principle of a roll crusher with a neat sketch. Differentiate clearly between **single-roll** and **double-roll** crushers.

> **Q(b) [7 marks]:** A roll crusher is required to crush 9 t/hr of a mineral having a feed particle size of 20 mm and a desired product size of 2 mm. The rolls are 1.0 m in diameter and 0.5 m wide. The bulk density of the mineral is 2.0 t/m³. The rolls operate at 100 rpm. Calculate:
> (i) The set of the rolls.
> (ii) The angle of nip (assuming successful crushing).
> (iii) The peripheral velocity of the rolls.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Working Principle

A **roll crusher** consists of two heavy cylindrical rolls mounted on parallel horizontal shafts. The rolls rotate **toward each other** at the same speed (or slightly different speeds in some designs). Feed material entering from the top is gripped by friction between the particle and the roll surface, drawn downward into the narrowing gap, and crushed by a combination of **compression** and **shear (frictional drag)**. The crushed fragments discharge from the bottom.

**Differentiation:**

| Aspect | Single-Roll Crusher | Double-Roll Crusher |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Number of rolls | One toothed roll | Two rolls (smooth or toothed) |
| Other surface | Stationary breaker plate / anvil | Second roll rotates |
| Feed size | Larger (up to 200 mm) | Smaller (≤ 50 mm for smooth) |
| Mechanism | Tearing + impact against breaker | Compression + shear between rolls |
| Reduction ratio | 4:1 to 6:1 | 4:1 to 10:1 |
| Typical use | Coal crushing in power plants | Limestone, clinker, ores |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:**

$$
Q = 9 \text{ t/hr}, \quad D_f = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad D_p = 2 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D = 1.0 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.5 \text{ m}, \quad \rho = 2.0 \text{ t/m}^3, \quad N = 100 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(i) Set of the rolls:**

Using $Q = 0.6 \, N \, D \, L \, s \, \rho$:

$$
9 = 0.6 \times 100 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 \times s \times 2.0
$$

$$
9 = 60 \, s
$$

$$
s = 0.15 \text{ m} = 15 \text{ cm}
$$

Set $= 2s = 30$ cm. **Note:** This result indicates that the desired product size of 2 mm cannot be obtained at this throughput — a smaller set or reduced $N$ is required. We proceed assuming the geometry given.

**(ii) Angle of nip (using $D_p = 2s = 30$ mm, $D_f = 20$ mm — note feed is smaller than set, so nipping condition must be checked):**

For the largest feed particle ($r = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m, $s = 0.15$ m, $R = 0.5$ m):

$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{0.5 + 0.15}{0.5 + 0.01} = \frac{0.65}{0.51} = 1.275
$$

This is **physically impossible** ($\cos$ cannot exceed 1). Therefore the rolls **will not nip** under these conditions — a feed of 20 mm cannot be crushed when the gap is 30 cm in this configuration. This illustrates the importance of the **nipping constraint**.

Assuming instead a smaller set (say $2s = 20$ mm, $s = 0.01$ m):

$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{0.5 + 0.01}{0.5 + 0.01} = 1.000
$$

So $\alpha \approx 0°$, meaning nipping is marginal.

**(iii) Peripheral velocity:**

$$
v = \pi D N = \pi \times 1.0 \times \frac{100}{60} = 5.24 \text{ m/s}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Marks Are Lost**
> 1. **Confusing "set" with "half-set"**: The set is the *full* gap; half-set $s$ enters the formula. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion of $N$**: When $N$ is in rpm and capacity must be in per-hour, the factor of 60 must appear. Many students get $Q$ off by a factor of 60.
> 3. **Not verifying the nipping condition**: After computing $\alpha$, always check $\alpha \le 2\phi$. Ignoring this loses 2 marks in design problems.
> 4. **Using roll *diameter* instead of *radius*** in $\cos(\alpha/2) = (R+s)/(R+r)$: This is the most common algebraic mistake.
> 5. **Omitting the $\pi$ in peripheral velocity**: $v = \pi D N$, NOT $D N$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Crushing rolls** = compression + shear crusher; one fixed, one spring-mounted.
- ✅ **Primary formula (memorize):** $\cos(\alpha/2) = (R+s)/(R+r)$
- ✅ **Angle of nip rule:** $\alpha < 2\phi$ (for steel-mineral, $\phi \approx 31°$, so $\alpha \le 16°$ for smooth rolls).
- ✅ **Capacity formula:** $Q = 0.6 \, N \, D \, L \, s \, \rho$ (tons/hr when $\rho$ in t/m³, lengths in m, $N$ in rpm).
- ✅ **Maximum feed radius:** $r_{\max} = \frac{R(1-\cos(\alpha/2)) + s}{\cos(\alpha/2)}$
- ✅ **Reduction ratio range:** 4:1 (smooth) to 10:1 (toothed).
- ✅ **Peripheral velocity:** $v = \pi D N$; never exceed the slip limit.
- ✅ **Smooth rolls** → hard, dry, abrasive feeds; **Toothed rolls** → soft, moist, friable feeds.
- ✅ **Power** derived from Bond's Work Index: $W = W_i (1/\sqrt{P_{80}} - 1/\sqrt{F_{80}})$.
- ✅ **Tramp material protection** is provided by the **spring-loaded movable roll**.

### 🧠 Mnemonics for Memory

> **"C-R-O-L-L-S"** for the six operational variables:
> **C**apacity, **R**oll speed, **O**pening (set), **L**ength, **L**argest feed, **S**pring tension.

> **"Slip-Stick"** for the nipping rule:
> If $\alpha > \phi$ → particle **slips out** (failure).
> If $\alpha < \phi$ → particle **sticks and is drawn in** (success).

### 🎯 KTU Exam Strategy

1. Always start numerical problems by writing down the **given data** with units.
2. Draw a **quick sketch** showing $R$, $r$, $s$, and $\alpha/2$ — even a rough diagram earns 1-2 marks of "method" credit.
3. Derive or quote the **nipping formula** before substituting numbers.
4. **Always verify** $\alpha \le 2\phi$ before proceeding with capacity calculation.
5. Mention **at least one industrial application** in 2-mark definition questions — examiners award "breadth" marks liberally.
