> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Tumbling Mills # ⚙️ MODULE 2 — TUMBLING MILLS: COMPREHENSIVE TECHNICAL DOSSIER ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition** > **Tumbling Mills** are a class of comminution equipment in which size reduction of particulate solids is achieved by the action of a grinding medium (balls, rods, pebbles, or the ore itself) that is lifted and cascaded inside a rotating cylindrical shell. The grinding medium falls under the influence of gravity onto the feed material, producing **impact**, **attrition**, and **compression** forces that fracture the particles. In the KTU 2024 scheme, tumbling mills are classified as a sub-category of **Size Reduction Equipment (Mechanical Comminution)** operating on the principle of **free crushing** — where the grinding media is free to move inside the mill shell. ### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture) > [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy — "The Washing Machine of Rocks"** > Imagine a tilted, slowly rotating washing machine drum half-filled with steel balls and rocks. As the drum spins, the balls are carried up the rising side. At a certain height, gravity overcomes the centrifugal hold, and they tumble back down in a **cascading** or **cataracting** rain. Each falling ball is like a tiny hammer, and the cumulative hammer-blows pulverize the rocks inside. > - **Cascading** = balls rolling down (gentle abrasion) → like sandpaper on wood. > - **Cataracting** = balls falling in a parabolic projectile (heavy impact) → like a hammer hitting a nail. > - **Centrifuging** = balls stuck to the wall (no grinding) → like a stuck washing machine — useless! ### 📚 Classification Snapshot | Family | Grinding Medium | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Ball Mill** | Steel / ceramic balls | Fine & ultra-fine grinding | | **Rod Mill** | Steel rods (length > diameter) | Coarse grinding, prevents over-grinding | | **Pebble Mill** | Flint / porcelain pebbles | Non-contaminating grinding (paint, food) | | **Autogenous Mill (AG)** | The ore itself | Dry, competent ores | | **Semi-Autogenous Mill (SAG)** | Ore + small steel balls | Primary grinding in mineral processing | ### 🎯 Key Physical Constants (Highlighted) > [!IMPORTANT] **Standard Reference Constants** > - Standard acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** (or **981 cm/s²**) > - In KTU numericals, **g = 9.81 m/s²** is mandatory; non-use causes mark deduction. > - Centrifugal force factor: **R = D/2** (radius from mill center to ball center of mass) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Operational Regimes of a Tumbling Mill The motion of the grinding media inside the mill depends on the **rotational speed (N)** relative to the **critical speed (N_c)**. KTU examiners almost always frame a sub-question on this. #### (a) Cascading (N < 0.5 N_c) — Low Speed - Media **rolls** up the rising wall and **slips** back down the cascading slope. - Dominant force: **attrition** and **compression**. - Product is **coarse**, energy efficiency is **poor**. - Use case: Coarse mixing, preliminary blending. #### (b) Cataracting (0.5 N_c ≤ N ≤ 0.85 N_c) — Optimum Range - Media is **projected** into the mill interior in a parabolic arc. - Dominant force: **impact** at the toe of the charge. - This is the **designed operating window** for most industrial ball mills. - **KTU Highlight:** Operating speed is typically kept at **50 % to 80 % of N_c**. #### (c) Centrifuging (N > N_c) — Super-Critical - Centrifugal force > gravitational force → media **clings to the mill wall**. - No relative motion → **NO size reduction** occurs. - This is the failure condition and must be **strictly avoided**. > [!NOTE] **Mnemonic for Exams** > **"C-C-C"** → **C**ascading (**C**oarse), **C**ataracting (**C**rushing / Correct), **C**entrifuging (**C**ollapse / Catastrophe). ### 2.2 Geometry of the Mill Charge For analytical derivations (which KTU loves to test), we define: - **α** = Angle of repose of the grinding media - **β** = Angle of fall - **θ** = Angle of break - **R** = Internal radius of the mill (m) - **D** = Internal diameter of the mill (m) - **r** = Radius of the grinding ball (m) The relationship used in KTU derivations: $$\theta = 2\pi - 2\alpha \quad \text{...(i.e., the arc of cascading)}$$ ### 2.3 Derivation of Critical Speed (Detailed in Section 3) **Critical speed** is the rotational speed at which the centrifugal force on the outermost layer of grinding media just equals the gravitational force. Above this speed, centrifuging begins. ### 2.4 🔥 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Print This!) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Critical speed (rev/s) | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$ | rev/s | The "König's Equation" | | 2 | Critical speed (RPM) | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}} \times 60$ | rev/min | KTU most-asked form | | 3 | Critical speed (in terms of D in meters) | $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ | rev/min | Since $\sqrt{2g}/\pi \times 60/\sqrt{2} \approx 42.3$ | | 4 | Operating speed | $N_{op} = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.85) \times N_c$ | rev/min | Cataracting regime | | 5 | Centrifugal force on a ball | $F_c = m\,\omega^2 R$ | N | m = mass of one ball | | 6 | Condition for critical speed | $m\,\omega^2 R = m\,g$ | — | Equating forces | | 7 | Bond's Work Index (Energy) | $W = 10\,W_i\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)$ | kWh/ton | F, P in micrometres | | 8 | Power for ball mill (gross) | $P = 2.5\,D\,L$ | kW | Approximate (L in m) | | 9 | Power for rod mill (gross) | $P = 1.5\,D\,L$ | kW | Approximate | | 10 | Number of balls | $N_b = \dfrac{\pi\,D^2\,L\,(1 - \phi)}{4\,d_b^2}$ | — | $\phi$ = void fraction (≈ 0.4) | | 11 | Optimum ball size (Bond) | $d_b = \left(\dfrac{F}{K}\right)^{0.5} \left(\dfrac{\rho_s\,W_i}{C_s}\,\sqrt{D}\right)^{0.33}$ | m | K = 200 for ball mills | > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Valuation Note** > In tables, never write $N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$ with literal vertical bars in unit cells — the system collapses. The LaTeX-rendered table above is safe. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Tumbling mills are the **workhorses of the mineral processing industry**, handling: - 60-80 % of all comminution energy in a typical ore-to-metal plant. - Cement clinker grinding (ball mills) in the **cement industry** (KTU's neighbouring B.Tech branch focus). - Pharmaceutical intermediate size reduction (pebble mills) for **contamination-free** milling. - Ceramic glaze preparation in **industrial ceramics**. The **SAG mill** dominates modern mining (e.g., copper, gold, iron ore plants) because it eliminates the need for a secondary crushing stage, slashing capital and operating cost. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Critical Speed (N_c) > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Hot Topic — 7 Marks Guaranteed** > This derivation appears in nearly every KTU ESE and internal assessment paper. Master every line. **Assumption:** Consider the outermost layer of a single ball of mass $m$ at radius $R$ (measured from the center of the mill to the center of the ball), rotating with the mill at angular velocity $\omega$. **Step 1 — Centrifugal force on the ball** The ball undergoes uniform circular motion. The required centripetal force is supplied by the centripetal component of the contact force from the mill wall, and the corresponding outward "reactive" centrifugal force (in the rotating frame) is: $$F_c = m\,\omega^2 R$$ **Step 2 — Gravitational force on the ball** The weight (gravitational force) acting downward is: $$F_g = m\,g$$ **Step 3 — Condition for incipient centrifuging** Centrifuging begins when the centrifugal force exactly equals the gravitational force, i.e., the ball is on the verge of being thrown off the wall. Equating: $$m\,\omega^2 R = m\,g$$ **Step 4 — Cancel mass m** $$\omega^2 R = g$$ **Step 5 — Solve for angular velocity** $$\omega^2 = \dfrac{g}{R}$$ $$\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$$ **Step 6 — Convert angular velocity to revolutions per second** Since one revolution corresponds to $2\pi$ radians, the revolutions per second is: $$N_c = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$$ **Step 7 — Convert to revolutions per minute (RPM)** $$N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} \times 60 \quad \text{[rev/min]}$$ **Step 8 — Substitute R = D/2** Since the radius $R$ is half the internal diameter $D$ of the mill: $$N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{D/2}} \times 60 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}} \times 60$$ **Step 9 — Simplify numerically with g = 9.81 m/s²** $$N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.81}{D}} = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{19.62}{D}}$$ Evaluating the constants: $$\dfrac{30}{\pi} \times \sqrt{19.62} \approx \dfrac{30}{3.1416} \times 4.4294 \approx 42.3$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \quad \text{[rev/min, D in metres]}}$$ **Step 10 — Operating speed** Industrial mills are operated at a fraction of $N_c$: $$N_{op} = k \cdot N_c, \quad k = 0.5 \text{ to } 0.85$$ > [!NOTE] **KTU Shortcut Insight** > If the mill diameter is in **centimetres**, the formula becomes $N_c = \dfrac{423}{\sqrt{D}}$. This dual form is a favourite KTU trap in numericals. ### 3.2 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical **Problem:** A ball mill of internal diameter **1.2 m** is operated at **68 %** of its critical speed. Calculate: (a) The critical speed in RPM. (b) The operating speed in RPM. (c) The angular velocity at operating speed. **Solution:** **(a) Critical speed:** $$N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{1.2}}$$ $$\sqrt{1.2} = 1.0954$$ $$N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{1.0954} = 38.62 \text{ rev/min}$$ **(b) Operating speed (k = 0.68):** $$N_{op} = 0.68 \times 38.62 = 26.26 \text{ rev/min}$$ **(c) Angular velocity at operating speed:** First convert $N_{op}$ to rev/s: $$N_{op} = \dfrac{26.26}{60} = 0.4377 \text{ rev/s}$$ $$\omega = 2\pi N_{op} = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.4377 = 2.751 \text{ rad/s}$$ > [!WARNING] **Common Mistake in KTU Valuations** > Students often forget to convert RPM to rev/s before computing $\omega = 2\pi N$. The examiner deducts **1.5 marks** for this unit slip. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Tumbling Mill Variants | Feature | Ball Mill | Rod Mill | Pebble Mill | Autogenous (AG) | SAG Mill | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Grinding media** | Steel balls | Steel rods | Flint pebbles | Ore itself | Ore + 5-15 % balls | | **L/D ratio** | 1 to 1.5 | 1.4 to 1.9 | 1 to 1.5 | 0.3 to 0.5 | 0.3 to 0.5 | | **Feed size (max)** | 10-25 mm | 20-30 mm | 10-25 mm | 200-300 mm | 100-200 mm | | **Product size** | 45-150 µm | 500-2000 µm | 45-150 µm | 75-200 µm | 75-200 µm | | **Contamination** | High (Fe) | High (Fe) | Negligible | Negligible | Low | | **Slurry type** | Wet or dry | Wet preferred | Wet | Dry or wet | Wet | | **Capital cost** | Moderate | Moderate | Low | High | Highest | | **Over-grinding** | Yes | No (rod acts as classifier) | Yes | Minimal | Minimal | ### 3.4 Bond's Work Index Energy for Tumbling Mills (Extension) For a closed-circuit wet ball mill grinding ore from a feed $F$ (in µm) to a product $P$ (in µm), the specific energy requirement per ton is: $$W = 10\,W_i\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right) \quad \text{[kWh/ton]}$$ Where: - $W_i$ = Bond Work Index (typical values: 10-25 kWh/ton) - $F, P$ = 80 % passing sizes in micrometres **Total power** for throughput $T$ (ton/h): $$P_{total} = W \times T \quad \text{[kW]}$$ **Efficiency correction** (apply if P < 75 µm): $$W_{corrected} = W \times \left(\dfrac{10.5}{\sqrt{P}}\right)^{0.5} \quad \text{(for P < 75 µm)}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tumbling Mill ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FEED HOPPER
Raw Ore Feed F] --> B[FEED CHUTE
Controlled Entry] B --> C[MILL SHELL
Rotating Cylinder] C --> D[GRINDING MEDIA
Balls or Rods] D --> E[ZONE OF CASCADING
Attrition and Compression] D --> F[ZONE OF CATARACTING
Impact Fracture] D --> G[ZONE OF CENTRIFUGING
Failure Mode] E --> H[MILL DISCHARGE
Product P] F --> H H --> I[CLASSIFIER
Size Separation] I --> J[OVERSIZE
Recycle to Feed] I --> K[UNDERSIZE
Final Product] C --> L[DRIVE SYSTEM
Motor and Gearbox] L --> C C --> M[TRUNNION BEARINGS
Support and Rotation] ``` > [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram** > - The **green boxes** are the mill's physical regions. > - The **yellow boxes** are the three operational regimes (only cascading and cataracting produce size reduction). > - The **red box** is the failure mode (centrifuging) — must be prevented by operating below $N_c$. ### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Critical Speed Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[START
Given D and N] --> B[Compute Nc
42.3 / sqrt D] B --> C{Compare N with Nc} C -->|N less than 0.5 Nc| D[CASCADING
Attrition Dominant] C -->|N between 0.5 Nc and 0.85 Nc| E[CATARACTING
Impact Dominant
OPTIMUM ZONE] C -->|N between 0.85 Nc and Nc| F[TRANSITION
Inefficient] C -->|N greater than Nc| G[CENTRIFUGING
NO GRINDING
SHUT DOWN] E --> H[SIZE REDUCTION
Achieved] D --> I[COARSE PRODUCT
Energy Inefficient] G --> J[MILL DAMAGE RISK] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Constructional Anatomy of a Ball Mill ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SHELL[ROTATING MILL SHELL - Cylindrical Body] A1[Inlet Trunnion - Feed Entry] A2[Mill Lining - Rubber or Steel] A3[Grinding Balls - 25 to 75 mm] A4[Discharge Trunnion - Product Exit] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 subgraph SUPPORT[STATIONARY SUPPORT FRAME] B1[Trunnion Bearings] B2[Drive Pinion] B3[Gear and Motor Assembly] end A1 --- B1 A4 --- B1 B1 --- B2 --- B3 subgraph CONTROL[OPERATIONAL CONTROLS] C1[Speed Sensor - Tachometer] C2[Load Sensor - Weightometer] C3[Power Monitor - kW meter] end C1 -.->|feeds back| B3 C2 -.->|feeds back| A3 C3 -.->|feeds back| B3 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Diagram Note** > KTU examiners expect students to **label all parts** in a mill sketch and explain the **direction of slurry flow** (co-current for overflow, counter-current for grate discharge). The Mermaid block above maps all critical sub-systems; if a physical drawing is required in your exam, mirror this structure with proper arrows. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. Define critical speed of a ball mill. Why must a tumbling mill never be operated at or above its critical speed? [3 Marks] > **Model Answer:** > - **Definition:** Critical speed ($N_c$) is the rotational speed of the mill at which the centrifugal force acting on the outermost layer of grinding media just balances the gravitational force. At this speed, the grinding media begins to centrifuge (i.e., is pressed against the mill wall and rotates with it without falling). > - **Why avoid it:** Above $N_c$, the balls **do not fall back** onto the charge. The impact and attrition forces vanish, so **no size reduction occurs**. The mill consumes power but produces **no useful work**, leading to energy wastage and possible mechanical damage to the liners. > - **Formula (for writing):** $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}} \times 60$ rev/min. > > `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### Q2. Differentiate between an autogenous mill and a SAG mill. [3 Marks] > **Model Answer:** > - **Autogenous Mill (AG):** Uses **only the coarse ore itself** as the grinding media. No steel balls are added. Suitable for competent, harder ores. > - **SAG (Semi-Autogenous) Mill:** Uses a combination of **ore plus 5-15 % steel balls** (typically 100-125 mm diameter) to assist grinding. SAG mills are more common in modern plants. > - **Key Difference:** AG mills rely purely on the ore's self-grinding action; SAG mills augment this with steel ball impact, allowing them to handle softer or more variable ores and produce finer products. > > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` --- ### 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **[Choose ONE option]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive an expression for the critical speed of a ball mill in terms of the mill diameter. State clearly the assumptions made. [7 Marks] **(b)** A ball mill of internal diameter **1.8 m** is rotating at **28 rpm**. It is filled to **40 %** of its volume with steel balls of density $\rho_b = 7800 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The ore feed has a Bond Work Index $W_i = 12 \text{ kWh/ton}$ and the feed is at $F = 2000 \text{ µm}$. The product is desired at $P = 75 \text{ µm}$. If the mill handles **50 ton/h**, determine: (i) The critical speed and the **% of critical speed** at which it operates. [3 Marks] (ii) The specific energy consumption using Bond's equation. [2 Marks] (iii) The **total power** required. [2 Marks] > **Complete Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks — full credit):** - **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. The mill is horizontal (axis horizontal). 2. The grinding media behaves as rigid spheres. 3. At critical speed, the outermost layer just begins to centrifuge, i.e., centrifugal force = weight of the ball. 4. No-slip condition between balls and mill wall at the incipient point. - **Derivation (5 marks):** See Section 3.1 of these notes. Step 1 to Step 9 — equate $m\omega^2 R = mg$, solve for $\omega$, convert to RPM, substitute $R = D/2$, simplify to obtain: $$\boxed{N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \text{ rev/min, with } D \text{ in metres}}$$ **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Critical speed and % of N_c (3 marks):** $$N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{1.8}} = \dfrac{42.3}{1.3416} = 31.53 \text{ rev/min}$$ $$\% \text{ of } N_c = \dfrac{28}{31.53} \times 100 = 88.8 \text{ \%}$$ **Interpretation:** This is **slightly above** the recommended maximum (85 %). The mill is operating in the **transition zone**, which is inefficient. Recommend reducing speed to ≈ 75 % of $N_c$ → $N_{op} = 0.75 \times 31.53 \approx 23.6$ rev/min. **(ii) Specific energy by Bond's equation (2 marks):** $$W = 10 \cdot W_i \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)$$ $$W = 10 \times 12 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{75}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2000}}\right)$$ $$W = 120 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{8.660} - \dfrac{1}{44.721}\right)$$ $$W = 120 \times (0.1155 - 0.02236)$$ $$W = 120 \times 0.0931 = 11.17 \text{ kWh/ton}$$ **(iii) Total power (2 marks):** $$P_{total} = W \times T = 11.17 \times 50 = 558.5 \text{ kW}$$ > `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **ball mill** with a neat labelled sketch. Discuss the role of **mill liners**. [7 Marks] **(b)** A rod mill of internal diameter **2.4 m** and length **3.6 m** is wet-grinding an ore at **75 % critical speed**. The volumetric loading of rods is **45 %**. The rod charge consists of rods of density $\rho_r = 7600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and void fraction $\phi = 0.4$. Determine: (i) The critical speed and operating speed of the mill. [3 Marks] (ii) The mass of the rod charge inside the mill. [4 Marks] > **Complete Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working of a Ball Mill (7 marks):** - **Construction (3.5 marks):** - **Mill shell:** A hollow horizontal cylinder made of riveted or welded steel plates, supported on **trunnion bearings** at both ends. - **Mill liners:** Replaceable wear-resistant plates (rubber, manganese steel, or high-chrome cast iron) bolted to the inner shell to protect it from abrasion and to lift the grinding media. - **Drive system:** A motor, gearbox, and pinion gear engage with a girth gear on the mill shell. - **Feed and discharge:** Inlet trunnion (feed) and outlet trunnion (discharge), the latter fitted with a **grate** or **overflow** arrangement. - **Grinding media:** Steel balls, 25-75 mm diameter, occupying 30-50 % of the mill volume. - **Working (2.5 marks):** - The mill rotates at 50-80 % of $N_c$. - As the mill rotates, the balls are lifted up to a height (cascading) and then projected (cataracting). - Impact at the toe of the charge shatters particles; cascading produces fine attrition. - The slurry flows through the mill from feed end to discharge end in a continuous process. - **Role of Liners (1 mark):** - Protect the mill shell from impact wear. - Lift the grinding media to the desired cascading/cataracting height (the **lifter bar profile** controls this). - Reduce slip between balls and shell. **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Critical and operating speed (3 marks):** $$N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{2.4}} = \dfrac{42.3}{1.5492} = 27.31 \text{ rev/min}$$ $$N_{op} = 0.75 \times 27.31 = 20.48 \text{ rev/min}$$ **(ii) Mass of rod charge (4 marks):** - **Total mill volume:** $$V_{mill} = \pi \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^2 L = \pi \times (1.2)^2 \times 3.6 = \pi \times 1.44 \times 3.6 = 16.286 \text{ m}^3$$ - **Volume occupied by rod charge (45 % loading):** $$V_{rods} = 0.45 \times 16.286 = 7.329 \text{ m}^3$$ - **Volume of rods considering void fraction:** $$V_{rods, solid} = V_{rods} \times (1 - \phi) = 7.329 \times 0.6 = 4.397 \text{ m}^3$$ - **Mass of rods:** $$m_{rods} = V_{rods, solid} \times \rho_r = 4.397 \times 7600 = 33{,}420 \text{ kg} \approx 33.42 \text{ tonnes}$$ > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Tumbling Mills** > 1. **Unit mismatch in critical speed (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert rev/min to rev/s before $\omega = 2\pi N$. The radius $R$ in the formula must be in **metres** for SI consistency. > 2. **Wrong numerical constant (–1 mark):** Some students memorize $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$ but forget to multiply by 60. KTU expects **RPM**, not rev/s, unless stated otherwise. > 3. **Confusing R and r (–1 mark):** The $R$ in the critical speed formula is the **mill's internal radius** (or D/2), NOT the radius of the grinding ball. > 4. **Bond's equation sign error (–2 marks):** Bond's equation is $W = 10W_i(1/\sqrt{P} - 1/\sqrt{F})$, NOT $(1/\sqrt{F} - 1/\sqrt{P})$. Since $F > P$, the difference is positive. Reversing the terms gives a negative energy — a guaranteed zero in the valuation. > 5. **Operating speed expressed as 0.5 to 0.85 × N_c (–0.5 mark if range not stated):** KTU looks for the **range**, not just one number. > 6. **Missing assumption in derivation (–1 mark):** The "no slip at incipient centrifuging" assumption is mandatory. Skipping it costs marks. > 7. **No mention of "void fraction" in mass calculation (–1 mark):** In rod/ball mass problems, applying the 45-50 % loading without subtracting the void fraction is the most common error. ### 5.4 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — "Things to Remember" > 📌 **Tumbling Mills — Last-Minute Recall List** > - **Definition:** Grinding media + rotating shell + gravity-driven cascading/cataracting action. > - **Three regimes:** Cascading (N < 0.5 N_c), Cataracting (0.5-0.85 N_c), Centrifuging (N > N_c). > - **Critical speed formula:** $N_c = 42.3/\sqrt{D}$ in rev/min (D in metres). > - **In terms of R:** $N_c = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{g/R} \times 60$ rev/min. > - **Operating speed:** Always **between 0.5 N_c and 0.85 N_c** for efficient impact grinding. > - **Assumption:** $m\omega^2 R = mg$ at the critical condition. > - **L/D ratio:** Ball mill 1-1.5, Rod mill 1.4-1.9, AG/SAG 0.3-0.5. > - **Bond's equation:** $W = 10 W_i (1/\sqrt{P} - 1/\sqrt{F})$ kWh/ton. > - **Over-grinding control:** Rod mill outperforms ball mill. > - **Contamination control:** Pebble mill (no metal contact). > - **Mill liners:** Lift balls; protect shell; use lifter bars. > - **Classification at discharge:** Grate discharge (faster) vs. overflow discharge (finer). > - **g = 9.81 m/s²** — ALWAYS use SI units in derivations. > - **Major mills:** Ball, Rod, Pebble, AG, SAG. > - **Most common KTU trap:** Applying the loading % without subtracting void fraction. > [!NOTE] **End of Topic — Module 2: Tumbling Mills** > This concludes the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 dossier for the topic. Master the critical speed derivation and Bond's equation, and you will secure the full marks allotted to this topic in any KTU 2024 evaluation cycle.

topics.title: Hammer Mill
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hammer Mill

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Hammer Mill – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hammer Mill** is a high-speed impact-type size reduction equipment consisting of a series of freely swinging or fixed hammers (beaters) mounted on a rapidly rotating shaft enclosed within a perforated metal screen or cage. It reduces the particle size of a solid feed material primarily by the mechanism of **impact** (high-velocity collision of hammers against particles) and secondarily by **shear and attrition** (rubbing action between particles and against the screen).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note (PCCHT302 – Module 2):** Hammer Mill is grouped under the category of *Crushers / Size Reduction Equipment* and is studied alongside Ball Mill, Jaw Crusher, Gyratory Crusher, and Roll Crusher. The board routinely asks construction, working, and power consumption derivations from this topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **rotating ceiling fan armed with small metal blades**, but instead of air, it hits a stream of falling pebbles. Each blade (hammer) whacks the pebble with tremendous kinetic energy, shattering it into smaller pieces. The smaller fragments then squeeze through the holes in a surrounding wire mesh (screen) — anything too big stays inside to be hit again. This is exactly how a hammer mill works.

- A **kitchen mixer-grinder** (mixie) is a miniature hammer mill — the blades at high RPM impact the spices to pulverize them.
- A **whack-a-mole game** is a visual metaphor — a hammer (mallet) impacts stationary objects (moles) to push them down through a hole (screen).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following quantitative metrics are associated with hammer mill operation and are standard across KTU board exam questions:

- **Rotor Tip Speed** (peripheral speed of hammer tip): typically **50 – 80 m/s** for industrial mills.
- **Hammer Tip Speed (v_t)** is given by: $v_t = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$, where D = hammer tip diameter (m), N = rotor speed (rev/s).
- **Standard Capacity Range:** **0.1 – 30 tonnes/hour** depending on feed size, screen aperture, and rotor diameter.
- **Specific Energy Consumption:** approximately **15 – 70 kWh/tonne** of feed for size reduction.
- **Size Reduction Ratio (R):** typically lies in the range of **20:1 to 40:1** for hammer mills.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote the unit along with the numerical value. Writing *"v = 60"* loses marks; writing *"v = 60 m/s"* is the board-exam way.

## 1.4 Classification of Hammer Mill

| Type | Distinguishing Feature | Typical Use |
|------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Gravity Discharge Mill** | Feed enters top, hammers impact, product discharges by gravity through bottom screen. | Dry, free-flowing materials. |
| **Pneumatic Discharge Mill** | Air suction pulls product through screen into cyclone. | Fine, lightweight, dusty materials. |
| **Full-Circle Screen Mill** | Screen covers 360° around the rotor. | Very fine grinding, fibrous materials. |
| **Vertical Shaft Hammer Mill** | Rotor is vertical; used for sticky materials. | Wet or tacky biomass. |
| **Integrated Hammer Mill** | Combined with air separator / classifier. | Pharmaceutical and food industries. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details of Hammer Mill (Sectional View Components)

A hammer mill consists of the following sub-systems, listed in operational sequence:

1. **Feed Hopper (Inlet Chute):** A wide-mouthed funnel that receives the feed material and channels it by gravity into the milling chamber. Often equipped with a vibratory feeder or rotary valve for controlled feed rate.
2. **Milling Chamber (Casing):** A heavy-duty cylindrical or rectangular housing made of cast iron / fabricated steel. Internally lined with replaceable wear-resistant liners (e.g., manganese steel).
3. **Rotor (Shaft + Discs):** A heavy central shaft mounted on bearings, carrying a series of peripheral discs. Typical shaft diameter: **50–200 mm**; rotor diameter: **300–1500 mm**.
4. **Hammers (Beaters / Blades):** Rectangular or L-shaped bars of hardened steel, swing-mounted on the rotor discs via pins. Hammer length: **50–300 mm**; mass: **1–10 kg per hammer**.
5. **Breaker Plates (Upper & Side Wall Plates):** Stationary liners opposite the hammer tips that create the second striking surface, intensifying impact breakage.
6. **Perforated Screen (Grating):** A replaceable meshed screen at the bottom/sides of the chamber with apertures ranging from **0.5 mm to 25 mm**. Determines the maximum product size.
7. **Discharge Outlet:** Either gravity-based bottom discharge or air-suction pneumatic discharge.
8. **Drive Assembly:** A high-power electric motor (typically **5 kW to 500 kW**) coupled to the rotor via a V-belt drive or direct coupling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to Remember Construction:** *"Hammer Hits Rotted Material, Screen Stops it, Discharge Drains" → **H**opper, **H**ammers, **R**otor, **M**illing Chamber, **S**creen, **D**ischarge.*

## 2.2 Working Principle – Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The operation is governed by a clear sequence of physical events:

1. **Feeding:** The material is fed into the hopper and dropped into the milling chamber where it encounters the rotating hammers.
2. **Primary Impact Breakage:** As each hammer rotates, it strikes particles at the tip velocity (v_t). The kinetic energy of the hammer is transferred to the particle, generating internal stress. When this stress exceeds the particle's **compressive or tensile strength**, fracture occurs.
3. **Secondary Breakage (Shear and Attrition):** Particles not directly hit experience:
   - **Shear** between the high-velocity hammers and the stationary breaker plates.
   - **Attrition** due to inter-particle collisions and rubbing against the screen.
4. **Classification by Screen:** Particles smaller than the screen aperture pass through; oversize particles are retained for further hammering (closed-loop internal classification).
5. **Discharge:** The product exits the mill through the screen — either by gravity (sliding out) or by pneumatic suction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the screen the most critical component?**
> The screen controls the **maximum product size (d_p)**, the **capacity** (larger aperture → higher throughput), and indirectly the **specific energy** (finer screen → more recirculation → higher kWh/tonne). Replacing the screen with a finer mesh is the standard way to shift the mill's operating point without modifying rotor speed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam relevant**. The most frequently tested ones are **(1), (5), and (7)**.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | $\large v_t = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | Tip speed of hammer, D = tip diameter, N = rotational frequency | m/s |
| 2 | $\large KE_{hammer} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_t^{2}$ | Kinetic energy per hammer strike | J |
| 3 | $\large R_r = \dfrac{D_f}{D_p}$ | Reduction ratio (D_f = feed dia, D_p = product dia) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\large n_{opt} \propto \dfrac{v_t}{\pi D}$ | Optimal rotor speed | rev/s |
| 5 | $\large E \,=\, K \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} \,-\, \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$ | **Bond's Law** — Work Index E (kWh/tonne), D (μm) | kWh/tonne |
| 6 | $\large P \,=\, \dot{m} \cdot E_{Bond}$ | Required mill power (mass flow × specific energy) | kW |
| 7 | $\large E_{Rittinger} \,=\, K_R \, (D_{f}^{-1} - D_{p}^{-1})$ | Rittinger's Law (new surface per unit mass) | kWh/tonne |
| 8 | $\large E_{Kick} \,=\, K_K \cdot \ln(D_f / D_p)$ | Kick's Law (volumetric deformation) | kWh/tonne |
| 9 | $\large \eta_{mill} \,=\, \dfrac{P_{useful}}{P_{input}}$ | Milling efficiency (usually 0.5 – 0.7) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\large \dot{m} \,=\, C_d \cdot A_{screen} \cdot \sqrt{2 g \Delta h}$ | Discharge rate through screen (gravity flow) | kg/s |

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert$ only when needed inside formulas. In the table above, plain parentheses and squared roots were used to keep markdown table syntax intact.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hammer mills are ubiquitous in:

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Pulverization of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) and excipients.
- **Food Industry:** Grinding of spices, sugar, salt, grain, and food by-products.
- **Cement Industry:** Pre-crushing of limestone and gypsum.
- **Mineral Processing:** Liberation of valuable minerals from ore.
- **Biomass Processing:** Size reduction of agricultural residues (coconut shell, rice husk, sawdust) for pellet/briquette production.
- **Chemical Industry:** Powdering of dyes, pigments, and catalysts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Power Required by a Hammer Mill (Bond's Law Approach)

**Statement (Bond's Law):** The total work useful in size reduction is inversely proportional to the square root of the product size.

**Step 1: Write Bond's Differential Form**

For an infinitesimal size reduction from D to (D - dD), the energy E is given by:

$$\large dE \,=\, K \cdot \left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \cdot D^{-3/2} \, dD$$

where K is a constant and D is the particle diameter.

**Step 2: Integrate Between the Initial and Final Sizes**

Integrate from feed diameter $D_f$ to product diameter $D_p$:

$$\large \int_{0}^{E} dE \,=\, -\dfrac{K}{2} \int_{D_f}^{D_p} D^{-3/2} \, dD$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the Integral**

The antiderivative of $D^{-3/2}$ is $\dfrac{D^{-1/2}}{-1/2} = -2 D^{-1/2}$.

Substituting the limits:

$$\large E \,=\, -\dfrac{K}{2} \cdot \left[\, -2 \, D^{-1/2} \,\right]_{D_f}^{D_p}$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

$$\large E \,=\, K \cdot \left[\, D^{-1/2} \,\right]_{D_f}^{D_p} \,=\, K \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} \,-\, \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the standard form of **Bond's Law** used in KTU board problems. The constant K is empirically determined and is called the **Bond Work Index (W_i)** for the material.

Rewriting with the standard textbook notation:

$$\large E \,=\, W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} \,-\, \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right) \quad \text{[kWh/tonne]}$$

where $W_i$ is the Bond Work Index in kWh/tonne and D is in micrometres (μm).

**Step 5: Calculate the Power Requirement**

The required mill power is the product of the mass feed rate and the specific energy consumption:

$$\large P \,=\, \dot{m} \cdot E \,=\, \dot{m} \cdot W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} \,-\, \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate in tonnes/hour and P is the mill power in kW.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Hammer Tip Speed and Kinetic Energy

**Step 1: Hammer Tip Speed**

If a hammer of mass m is mounted at a radial distance R from the axis of a rotor rotating at N revolutions per second, the tip traverses a circular path. The linear speed of the hammer tip is:

$$\large v_t \,=\, 2 \pi R N$$

Using the diameter D = 2R:

$$\large v_t \,=\, \pi D N \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

**Step 2: Kinetic Energy of One Hammer**

Applying the kinetic energy formula $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$:

$$\large KE_{single} \,=\, \dfrac{1}{2} m \left(\pi D N\right)^{2} \,=\, \dfrac{\pi^{2} m D^{2} N^{2}}{2} \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

**Step 3: Total Kinetic Energy for n Hammers**

If the rotor carries n hammers, the total kinetic energy available per revolution is:

$$\large KE_{total} \,=\, \dfrac{n \pi^{2} m D^{2} N^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 4: Theoretical Power Required**

The theoretical power is the total kinetic energy delivered per unit time. Multiplying by N impacts per second:

$$\large P_{theory} \,=\, KE_{total} \cdot N \,=\, \dfrac{n \pi^{2} m D^{2} N^{3}}{2} \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 5: Actual Power After Efficiency Losses**

$$\large P_{actual} \,=\, \dfrac{P_{theory}}{\eta_{mill}} \quad \text{where } \eta_{mill} \approx 0.5 - 0.7$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Reduction Ratio and Mass-Energy Balance

**Step 1: Define Reduction Ratio**

The size reduction ratio is defined as the ratio of average feed size to average product size:

$$\large R_r \,=\, \dfrac{D_f}{D_p}$$

For hammer mills, $R_r$ typically lies in the range **20:1 to 40:1** for coarse-to-medium size reduction.

**Step 2: Mass Balance (Steady-State)**

In a continuous hammer mill, the mass flow rate in equals the mass flow rate out plus the mass held inside the mill (steady state):

$$\large \dot{m}_{in} \,=\, \dot{m}_{out} \,+\, \dot{m}_{accumulation}$$

Under steady-state, accumulation is zero:

$$\large \dot{m}_{in} \,=\, \dot{m}_{out} \,=\, \dot{m} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$

**Step 3: Energy Balance**

The net mechanical energy delivered to the product is:

$$\large P_{useful} \,=\, \dot{m} \cdot E_{Bond} \,=\, \dot{m} \cdot W_i \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$$

The remainder of the input power is dissipated as:

- **Heat** (≈ 60 – 80 % of input) due to inelastic collisions.
- **Sound and vibration** (≈ 5 %).
- **Friction** at bearings and seals (≈ 5 – 10 %).

---

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A hammer mill operating at a rotor speed of 1500 rpm has a tip diameter of 0.6 m. Each hammer has a mass of 2.5 kg and the rotor carries 24 hammers. Calculate:

(a) The tip speed of the hammers.
(b) The total kinetic energy of all hammers.
(c) The theoretical power required, assuming all kinetic energy is dissipated per second.

**Given:** N = 1500 rpm = 25 rev/s, D = 0.6 m, m = 2.5 kg, n = 24.

**Solution:**

**(a) Tip Speed:**

$$\large v_t \,=\, \pi D N \,=\, \pi \times 0.6 \times 25$$

$$\large v_t \,=\, 15 \pi \,=\, 47.12 \text{ m/s}$$

**(b) Total Kinetic Energy:**

$$\large KE_{single} \,=\, \dfrac{1}{2} m v_t^{2} \,=\, \dfrac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times (47.12)^{2}$$

$$\large KE_{single} \,=\, 1.25 \times 2220.3 \,=\, 2775.4 \text{ J}$$

For 24 hammers:

$$\large KE_{total} \,=\, 24 \times 2775.4 \,=\, 66,609.6 \text{ J} \approx 66.6 \text{ kJ}$$

**(c) Theoretical Power:**

Theoretical power is the energy dissipated per unit time. Since the rotor makes 25 revolutions per second, the hammers make 25 impacts per second on the material stream:

$$\large P_{theory} \,=\, KE_{total} \times N \,=\, 66,609.6 \times 25$$

$$\large P_{theory} \,=\, 1,665,240 \text{ W} \approx 1.665 \text{ MW}$$

If the mill efficiency is 60 % (η = 0.6):

$$\large P_{actual} \,=\, \dfrac{1.665}{0.6} \approx 2.776 \text{ MW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Hint:** Always show the substitution of numerical values with units to earn full method marks, even if the final answer is slightly off.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Hammer Mill

The Mermaid diagram below maps the functional interactions between components of a hammer mill. (Adapted for clarity since a true 3D sectional drawing is outside Mermaid's scope.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Feed Hopper] --> nodeB[Rotary Feeder / Vibratory Tray]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Milling Chamber Casing]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Rotor Assembly with Discs and Shaft]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Hammers Beaters Mounted on Pins]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Breaker Plates Stationary Lining]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Perforated Screen Cylindrical Grating]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Discharge Port Gravity or Pneumatic]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Drive Motor 5 kW to 500 kW]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[V Belt Drive or Direct Coupling]
    nodeJ --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeK[Product Collection Bin or Cyclone Separator]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Fines and Dust Extractor Bag Filter]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Final Powdered Product Storage Silo]
```

## 4.2 Operational Sequence (Sequential Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Start Motor] --> stepB[Accelerate Rotor to Rated RPM]
    stepB --> stepC[Open Feed Inlet Valve]
    stepC --> stepD[Feed Enters Milling Chamber]
    stepD --> stepE[Impact Shear and Attrition on Particles]
    stepE --> stepF{Smaller than Screen Aperture?}
    stepF --> stepG[Yes - Pass Through Screen] --> stepH[Discharge to Collection]
    stepF --> stepI[No - Retained for Further Hammering] --> stepE
    stepH --> stepJ[Stop Feed and Allow Emptying]
    stepJ --> stepK[Decelerate Rotor and Shut Down]
```

## 4.3 Energy Flow Diagram (Bond's Law Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1[Electrical Power Input kW] --> conv[Drive System V Belt Loss 5 to 10 percent]
    conv --> mill[Milling Chamber Impact and Attrition]
    mill --> useful[Useful Size Reduction Work]
    mill --> heat[Inelastic Heat Loss 60 to 80 percent]
    mill --> sound[Sound and Vibration Loss 5 percent]
    mill --> friction[Bearing and Seal Friction 5 to 10 percent]
    useful --> output[Reduced Particle Product]
    heat --> env[Lost to Cooling Water or Ambient Air]
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Table

| Component | Interacts With | Energy / Mass Transfer Type |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------------|
| Rotor | Hammers, Drive | Mechanical (rotational) |
| Hammers | Particles, Breaker Plates | Kinetic (impact + shear) |
| Screen | Particles, Discharge | Mechanical (size classification) |
| Breaker Plates | Particles, Hammers | Impact reflection |
| Drive Motor | Rotor | Electrical → Mechanical |
| Discharge | Screen, Cyclone | Mass (gravity or pneumatic) |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a Hammer Mill and list any two of its industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A Hammer Mill is a high-speed impact-type size reduction equipment in which a rotor carrying swinging hammers rotates inside a perforated screen, reducing the particle size of feed material by repeated impact, shear, and attrition.

**Two Industrial Applications:**

1. **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Pulverization of crystalline drugs and excipients to fine powders before tabletting.
2. **Food Industry:** Grinding of spices, sugar, salt, and grain into flour or powder form.

> [!NOTE]
> A third valid application may include cement pre-crushing, biomass pelletization, or mineral processing.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Mention the main parts of a Hammer Mill and state the role of the screen.**

**Model Answer:**

**Main Parts:** Feed hopper, rotor assembly with hammers, breaker plates, milling chamber (casing), perforated screen, discharge outlet, and drive motor.

**Role of the Screen:**
The perforated screen acts as an internal classifier. It allows particles smaller than the screen aperture to pass through into the discharge, while retaining larger particles inside the milling chamber for further impact breakage. Thus, the screen controls:

- The **maximum product size** (equal to the screen aperture).
- The **circulation load** (finer screen → more circulation).
- Indirectly, the **specific energy consumption** of the mill.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) Describe the construction and working of a Hammer Mill with a neat sketch. State its advantages and disadvantages.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A Hammer Mill consists of a feed hopper, milling chamber, rotor shaft carrying peripheral discs, swing-mounted hammers, breaker plates, perforated screen, and a discharge outlet. The rotor is driven by an electric motor through a V-belt drive. The casing is lined with manganese steel wear plates. Hammers are usually rectangular bars of hardened steel, pivoted on the rotor discs.

**Working:**
Feed material enters the hopper and falls into the milling chamber. The high-speed rotation of the rotor causes the hammers to swing outward due to centrifugal force. Each hammer strikes the feed particles, transferring kinetic energy and causing fracture. Larger fragments continue to be hit; particles smaller than the screen aperture pass through into the discharge. The cycle continues until the product meets the size specification.

**Advantages:**

1. Simple construction and easy maintenance.
2. Can handle a wide range of feed sizes, both dry and moderately wet.
3. High reduction ratio (up to 40:1).
4. Produces uniform particle size in a single pass.
5. Quick screen replacement allows flexible product size adjustment.

**Disadvantages:**

1. High wear rate of hammers and screens; frequent replacement needed.
2. Generates considerable heat and noise; not suitable for heat-sensitive materials.
3. High specific energy consumption compared to compression crushers.
4. Cannot handle very hard or abrasive materials efficiently.
5. Product size distribution can be wide if not combined with classifier.

---

**(b) A hammer mill has a rotor of diameter 0.8 m operating at 1200 rpm. Each hammer weighs 3 kg and the rotor carries 20 hammers. Calculate (i) the tip speed of the hammers, (ii) the kinetic energy of one hammer, and (iii) the theoretical power developed if the rotor makes one impact per hammer per revolution.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** D = 0.8 m, N = 1200 rpm = 20 rev/s, m = 3 kg, n = 20.

**(i) Tip Speed:**

$$\large v_t \,=\, \pi D N \,=\, \pi \times 0.8 \times 20 \,=\, 16 \pi \,=\, 50.27 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Kinetic Energy of One Hammer:**

$$\large KE \,=\, \dfrac{1}{2} m v_t^{2} \,=\, \dfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times (50.27)^{2}$$

$$\large KE \,=\, 1.5 \times 2527.0 \,=\, 3790.5 \text{ J} \approx 3.79 \text{ kJ}$$

**(iii) Theoretical Power:**

For 20 hammers delivering impacts 20 times per second:

$$\large P_{theory} \,=\, n \cdot KE \cdot N \,=\, 20 \times 3790.5 \times 20$$

$$\large P_{theory} \,=\, 1{,}516{,}200 \text{ W} \approx 1.516 \text{ MW}$$

If the mill efficiency is 60 %:

$$\large P_{actual} \,=\, \dfrac{1.516}{0.6} \,=\, 2.527 \text{ MW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks. One mark is reserved for units and final correct conclusion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**(a) Explain the laws of size reduction (Kick's, Rittinger's, and Bond's) and identify the law best suited for hammer mill operation.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Kick's Law (1885):**
Energy required for size reduction is proportional to the logarithmic ratio of feed size to product size.

$$\large E \,=\, K_K \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$$

Applicable to **coarse crushing** where particle deformation is volumetric (e.g., jaw crusher).

**2. Rittinger's Law (1867):**
Energy required is proportional to the new surface area produced.

$$\large E \,=\, K_R \cdot \left(D_f^{-1} - D_p^{-1}\right)$$

Applicable to **fine grinding** where new surface generation dominates (e.g., ball mill for very fine products).

**3. Bond's Law (1952):**
Energy required is inversely proportional to the square root of particle size.

$$\large E \,=\, W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$$

This is an **intermediate** law that works well for the operational range of hammer mills (coarse-to-medium size reduction with $D_p$ between 100 μm and 10 mm).

**Law Best Suited for Hammer Mill: Bond's Law.**

Hammer mills operate predominantly by impact, producing both fracture and new surface, but the energy is dominated by the structural breakdown of the particle rather than mere new surface generation. Bond's empirical law was specifically calibrated for impact-type grinding and is the standard KTU board-exam answer.

---

**(b) A feed of 8 mm size is to be reduced to 0.5 mm using a hammer mill. The feed rate is 12 tonnes/hour. The Bond Work Index of the material is 12 kWh/tonne. Estimate the power required by the mill.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $D_f$ = 8 mm = 8000 μm, $D_p$ = 0.5 mm = 500 μm, $\dot{m}$ = 12 tonnes/hour, $W_i$ = 12 kWh/tonne.

**Step 1: Apply Bond's Law to Find Specific Energy:**

$$\large E \,=\, W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$$

$$\large E \,=\, 12 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{500}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{8000}}\right)$$

$$\large \sqrt{500} \,=\, 22.36, \quad \sqrt{8000} \,=\, 89.44$$

$$\large E \,=\, 12 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{22.36} - \dfrac{1}{89.44}\right) \,=\, 12 \cdot (0.0447 - 0.0112)$$

$$\large E \,=\, 12 \times 0.0335 \,=\, 0.402 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Power from Mass Flow Rate:**

$$\large P \,=\, \dot{m} \times E \,=\, 12 \text{ tonnes/hr} \times 0.402 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

$$\large P \,=\, 4.824 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer: ≈ 4.82 kW** (rounded to two decimal places).

> [!NOTE]
> A mill efficiency factor of 0.6 to 0.7 may be applied to obtain the **actual motor rating**: $P_{motor} = 4.82 / 0.6 \approx 8.03$ kW.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Always convert mm to μm before using Bond's Law because the empirical constant $W_i$ is calibrated in μm. Writing 8 mm in the formula without conversion gives a wildly wrong answer and **loses 2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign Error:** Many students write Bond's Law as $W_i \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}}\right)$ — this is **reversed**. Since $D_f > D_p$, the correct form is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}$ to yield a positive specific energy.
> 3. **Forgetting the Mass Flow Term:** A common error is to stop after calculating E in kWh/tonne and forget to multiply by tonnes/hour to obtain kW. **Always close the loop with units.**
> 4. **Bond's Law vs. Rittinger's Law Confusion:** For hammer mills with product size in the **medium range (100 μm – 10 mm)**, Bond's Law is correct. Using Rittinger's Law here is acceptable for partial credit, but stating it as "the best law" loses the explicit mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A Hammer Mill is an **impact-type** size reduction equipment using high-speed swinging hammers.
- **Mechanism:** Primary mechanism is **impact**; secondary is **shear + attrition**.
- **Key Components:** Hopper, rotor, hammers, breaker plates, screen, casing, discharge, drive.
- **Screen Role:** Acts as **internal classifier** controlling maximum product size.
- **Hammer Tip Speed:** $v_t = \pi D N$, typically 50 – 80 m/s.
- **KE per Hammer:** $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_t^{2}$.
- **Theoretical Power:** $P_{theory} = \dfrac{n \pi^{2} m D^{2} N^{3}}{2}$.
- **Bond's Law:** $E = W_i \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$ — **best law for hammer mill.**
- **Rittinger's Law:** For fine grinding (very small $D_p$).
- **Kick's Law:** For coarse crushing (large $D_f$).
- **Reduction Ratio:** $R_r = D_f / D_p$, typically **20:1 to 40:1**.
- **Mill Efficiency:** 0.5 – 0.7 (only 50–70 % of input power is useful).
- **Energy Distribution:** ~70 % heat, ~5 % sound, ~5–10 % friction, ~15–25 % useful.
- **Advantages:** Simple, flexible, high R_r, quick screen change.
- **Disadvantages:** Hammer/screen wear, heat generation, noise, high specific energy.
- **Applications:** Pharma, food, cement, mineral, biomass.
- **Work Index ($W_i$):** Empirical material property in **kWh/tonne**.
- **Numerical Pitfalls:** Unit conversion (mm → μm), sign of Bond's equation, missing mass-flow multiplication.
- **Rule of Thumb:** Bond's Law for impact mills (Hammer Mill); Rittinger's for ball mill fine product; Kick's for jaw/gyratory crusher.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fluid Energy Mill** (also called a **Jet Mill** or **Fluidized Energy Mill**) is a *non-mechanical* size reduction device that utilizes a high-velocity stream of compressible fluid — typically **compressed air, inert gas, or superheated steam** — to accelerate feed particles, induce interparticle collisions, and thereby reduce particle size to the **micron (μm) and sub-micron range**. The mill possesses **no moving parts**, deriving its grinding energy entirely from the kinetic energy of the expanding fluid (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2, *Size Reduction Equipment*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fluid Energy Mill is classified under *Fine Grinders* and is a frequently tested topic in the **ESE (End Semester Examination) Module 2**, typically appearing as either a **3-mark direct question** or a **7-mark descriptive question** within a 14-mark Part B.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **whirlpool in a bathtub**: when you pull the plug, water spirals inwards carrying leaves and debris, smashing them against each other and against the walls in a continuous tornado. A Fluid Energy Mill works **identically** — except instead of water, we use **high-pressure gas/steam**, and instead of leaves, we use **solid particles**. The particles get caught in the vortex, repeatedly collide at tremendous speed, shatter, and the ultra-fine fragments are sucked out the top by the rising gas, while the heavier, coarser particles are flung outward by centrifugal force to be ground again.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Identity Statement:** *"Fluid Energy Mills grind not by squeezing or crushing, but by making particles hit each other at jet-aircraft speeds using only the energy of a flowing gas."*

### 1.3 Standard Operational Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Feed size | $< 2 \text{ mm}$ (often $< 500 \text{ μm}$) |
| Product size | $1 - 10 \text{ μm}$ (ultrafine range) |
| Operating fluid pressure | $700 \text{ kPa} - 7 \text{ MPa}$ |
| Nozzle exit velocity | $100 - 300 \text{ m/s}$ |
| Specific energy consumption | $200 - 1000 \text{ kWh/ton}$ |
| Classifier cut size | Adjustable, $1 - 50 \text{ μm}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **specific energy consumption** in Fluid Energy Mills is markedly higher than that of ball mills — this is a direct consequence of the thermodynamic inefficiency of converting pressure energy into useful particle fragmentation energy.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Principle

The operation of a Fluid Energy Mill is governed by six sequential fluid-mechanical and collision-dynamic steps:

1. **Feed Induction:** Feed particles are introduced into the **annular grinding chamber** (a flat, doughnut-shaped cavity) by a feed screw or venturi eductor. The feed rate is carefully metered to prevent overloading the gas stream.

2. **Tangential Nozzle Injection:** High-pressure fluid is injected through **two or more diametrically opposed nozzles** arranged *tangentially* into the chamber. The tangential entry creates a **high-velocity spiral vortex** (free-vortex flow pattern).

3. **Particle Acceleration & Vortex Entrainment:** Gas expansion from the nozzles accelerates the entrained particles. The fluid velocity typically reaches **2 - 3 times the velocity of sound** in the grinding zone (transonic to supersonic regime).

4. **Interparticle Collision (The Core Mechanism):** Particles in the vortex, traveling on intersecting spiral trajectories, undergo **head-on and glancing collisions**. The kinetic energy of these collisions is dissipated as fragmentation energy. The collision velocity $v_{rel}$ may exceed **$100 \text{ m/s}$**.

5. **Centrifugal Classification (Internal Classifier Effect):** The spiral gas flow establishes a centrifugal field. **Larger (heavier) particles** experience greater centrifugal force and migrate toward the *outer wall* of the chamber, where they continue to be ground. **Finer (lighter) particles** experience insufficient centrifugal force to overcome the radial gas drag and migrate *inwards and upwards*.

6. **Pneumatic Discharge:** Fine particles, now small enough, are entrained in the upward gas stream and carried out through the central outlet to an external cyclone/filter system. Coarse particles recirculate to the grinding zone.

### 2.2 Constructional Details

- **Annular Grinding Chamber:** A toroidal (doughnut-shaped) chamber typically $300 - 1500 \text{ mm}$ in diameter.
- **Tangential Nozzles:** Two or more nozzles (often three for spiral jet mills) fabricated from wear-resistant materials like tungsten carbide or polyurethane-lined steel.
- **Feed Inlet:** A venturi or screw feeder mounted on the chamber's periphery.
- **Discharge Outlet:** Central axial outlet connected to an external classifier.
- **External Classifier:** Cyclone separator, bag filter, or sometimes an internal dynamic classifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Tangential Injection:** Tangential injection converts the linear momentum of the gas into **angular momentum** (rotational kinetic energy), creating a vortex. This vortex does **two jobs simultaneously**:
> 1. It sustains particle recirculation (multiple collision opportunities).
> 2. It acts as a **built-in classifier** (centrifugal separation by size).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $v_{nozzle} = C_{v} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{f}}}$ | Nozzle exit velocity from pressure drop (Torricelli-type expansion) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| $KE_{particle} = \dfrac{1}{2} m_{p} v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy imparted to a particle | $\text{J}$ |
| $E_{specific} = \dfrac{P_{input}}{\dot{m}_{feed}}$ | Specific energy consumption | $\text{kWh/ton}$ |
| $F_{centrifugal} = \dfrac{m_{p} v_{t}^{2}}{r}$ | Centrifugal force on particle in vortex | $\text{N}$ |
| $F_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{D} \rho_{f} A_{p} (v_{g} - v_{p})^{2}$ | Aerodynamic drag force (resisting escape) | $\text{N}$ |
| $v_{p,terminal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 d_{p} (\rho_{p} - \rho_{f}) g}{C_{D} \rho_{f}}}$ | Particle terminal settling velocity (Stokes' regime) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| $d_{cut} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu Q}{2 \pi N^{2} \rho_{p} (r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2})}}$ | Classifier cut-size in spiral jet mill (semi-empirical) | $\text{m}$ |
| $P_{fluid} = \dot{V}_{f} \cdot \Delta P$ | Hydraulic power consumed by compressed fluid | $\text{W}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Glossary:** $C_v$ = velocity coefficient ($\approx 0.85 - 0.95$ for sharp-edged nozzles); $\rho_f$ = fluid density ($\text{kg/m}^3$); $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across nozzle ($\text{Pa}$); $\dot{m}_{feed}$ = feed mass rate ($\text{ton/h}$); $v_t$ = tangential velocity in vortex; $d_p, \rho_p$ = particle diameter and density; $C_D$ = drag coefficient; $\mu$ = fluid viscosity; $Q$ = volumetric flow rate; $N$ = number of nozzle jets; $r_o, r_i$ = outer and inner classifier radii.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fluid Energy Mills are the **workhorse of high-purity industries**:
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Micronization of APIs (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients) without thermal degradation.
- **Cosmetics & Pigments:** Production of sub-10 μm titanium dioxide, zinc oxide for sunscreen UV blockers.
- **Minerals & Ceramics:** Ultrafine grinding of talc, alumina, zircon, silicon carbide.
- **Chemicals & Battery Materials:** Graphite and cathode active material (CAM) micronization for Li-ion batteries.
- **Food Industry:** Spices, cocoa, sugar to micron-grade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Industrial Fact:** In the pharmaceutical industry, the *micronization* of poorly water-soluble drugs (BCS Class II) using Fluid Energy Mills can **increase dissolution rate by 10-100x** — directly improving drug bioavailability.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nozzle Exit Velocity (KTU Frequently Tested)

**Starting Premise:** Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between the upstream stagnation point (high-pressure reservoir) and the nozzle exit (atmospheric).

$$\dfrac{P_{1}}{\rho_{f}} + \dfrac{v_{1}^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{P_{2}}{\rho_{f}} + \dfrac{v_{2}^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 1 — Boundary Conditions:**
- Upstream (point 1, inside pressure reservoir): $P_1 = P_{inlet}$, $v_1 \approx 0$ (large reservoir, fluid is essentially stagnant).
- Downstream (point 2, nozzle exit): $P_2 = P_{atm}$, $v_2 = v_{nozzle}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into Bernoulli's Equation:**

$$\dfrac{P_{inlet}}{\rho_{f}} + 0 = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho_{f}} + \dfrac{v_{nozzle}^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the Nozzle Velocity:**

$$\dfrac{v_{nozzle}^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{P_{inlet} - P_{atm}}{\rho_{f}} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_{f}}$$

**Step 4 — Take the Square Root (Idealized Result):**

$$v_{nozzle,\,ideal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{f}}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Velocity Coefficient $C_v$** (to account for viscous losses, vena contracta effects, and non-isentropic expansion):

$$v_{nozzle} = C_{v} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{f}}}$$

**Step 6 — Sample Numerical Evaluation (KTU Style):**

Given: $\Delta P = 700 \text{ kPa}$, fluid = compressed air at $\rho_f = 7.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_v = 0.90$.

$$v_{nozzle} = 0.90 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 700{,}000}{7.0}}$$

$$v_{nozzle} = 0.90 \times \sqrt{200{,}000} = 0.90 \times 447.21 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{nozzle} = 402.5 \text{ m/s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Commentary:** This velocity ($> 400 \text{ m/s}$, i.e., faster than the speed of sound in air at STP, $\approx 343 \text{ m/s}$) confirms that fluid energy mills operate in the **transonic/supersonic regime**, which is the source of their extraordinary particle acceleration.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Classification Cut-Size from Force Balance (Internal Classifier Mechanism)

**Starting Premise:** A particle in the grinding chamber is in dynamic equilibrium between **centrifugal force** (pushing it outward) and **fluid drag force** (pulling it inward, toward the central outlet).

**Step 1 — Equate the Two Forces at the Classification Boundary:**

$$F_{centrifugal} = F_{drag}$$

$$\dfrac{m_{p} v_{t}^{2}}{r} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{D} \rho_{f} A_{p} (v_{g} - v_{p})^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express Particle Mass in Terms of Diameter (Spherical Assumption):**

$$m_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_{p}^{3} \rho_{p}, \quad A_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_{p}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$\dfrac{(\pi/6) d_{p}^{3} \rho_{p} v_{t}^{2}}{r} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{D} \rho_{f} (\pi/4) d_{p}^{2} (v_{g} - v_{p})^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $d_p^2$ from Both Sides:**

$$\dfrac{d_{p} \rho_{p} v_{t}^{2}}{6 r} = \dfrac{C_{D} \rho_{f} (v_{g} - v_{p})^{2}}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Cut-Size $d_p$:**

$$d_{p,\,cut} = \dfrac{3 r C_{D} \rho_{f} (v_{g} - v_{p})^{2}}{4 \rho_{p} v_{t}^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation (KTU Critical Insight):**

- $d_{p,\,cut} \uparrow$ when $r \uparrow$ (larger chamber $\Rightarrow$ coarser cut).
- $d_{p,\,cut} \uparrow$ when $\rho_f \uparrow$ (denser fluid carries fine particles outward more easily).
- $d_{p,\,cut} \downarrow$ when $v_t \uparrow$ (stronger vortex $\Rightarrow$ finer cut, as small particles are also flung outward).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of the Internal Classifier:** This force balance is the **mathematical reason** why the Fluid Energy Mill produces a *narrow size distribution* — only particles small enough such that $d_p < d_{p,cut}$ can escape, while larger ones are mechanically forced back into the grinding zone. **This is a self-regulating mechanism** that eliminates the need for an external classifier in many applications.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Energy Consumption

**Step 1 — Hydraulic Power Input:**

$$P_{input} = \dot{V}_{f} \times \Delta P$$

where $\dot{V}_f$ is the volumetric flow rate of the motive fluid at supply conditions.

**Step 2 — Mass Throughput of Feed:**

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = \rho_{bulk} \times \dot{V}_{feed}$$

**Step 3 — Define Specific Energy:**

$$E_{specific} = \dfrac{P_{input}}{\dot{m}_{feed}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{f} \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_{bulk} \cdot \dot{V}_{feed}}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Numerical Illustration (Pharmaceutical Micronization):**

Given: $\dot{V}_{air} = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $\Delta P = 600 \text{ kPa}$, $\dot{m}_{feed} = 0.5 \text{ ton/h} = 0.139 \text{ kg/s}$.

$$P_{input} = 0.05 \times 600{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ W} = 30 \text{ kW}$$

$$E_{specific} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{0.139} = 215{,}827 \text{ J/kg} = 60 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse the *fluid flow rate* $\dot{V}_f$ (in m³/s) with the *feed rate* $\dot{m}_{feed}$ (in ton/h). They are **two completely different streams** — the fluid is the grinding energy carrier, and the feed is the material being ground. The specific energy normalizes the power input against the **feed mass rate**, not the fluid flow rate.

### 3.4 Comparative Performance Matrix (Size Reduction by Equipment Type)

| **Parameter** | **Ball Mill** | **Hammer Mill** | **Fluid Energy Mill** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feed size | $< 25 \text{ mm}$ | $< 50 \text{ mm}$ | $< 2 \text{ mm}$ |
| Product size | $75 - 500 \text{ μm}$ | $100 - 1000 \text{ μm}$ | $1 - 10 \text{ μm}$ |
| Energy efficiency | High ($0.5 - 5 \text{ kWh/ton}$) | Moderate | **Low** ($200 - 1000 \text{ kWh/ton}$) |
| Contamination risk | High (media wear) | Moderate | **Negligible** (no media) |
| Heat-sensitive suitability | Poor | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Moving parts | Many (drum, media) | Many (rotor, hammers) | **None** |
| Capital cost | Moderate | Low | **High** |

### 4.3 Functional State Diagram of Particle Journey

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INJECTED: Feed Enters Chamber
    INJECTED --> ACCELERATED: Picked Up by Tangential Gas Jets
    ACCELERATED --> COLLIDING: Particle Particle Impact in Vortex
    COLLIDING --> FINE_ENOUGH: If Particle Size Below Cut Size
    COLLIDING --> STILL_COARSE: If Particle Size Above Cut Size
    FINE_ENOUGH --> DISCHARGED: Carried Out Through Central Outlet
    STILL_COARSE --> RECIRCULATING: Centrifugal Force Pushes It Outward
    RECIRCULATING --> COLLIDING: Re-Enters Collision Zone
    DISCHARGED --> COLLECTED: Captured in Cyclone or Bag Filter
    COLLECTED --> [*]: Final Ultrafine Product
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The state diagram visually reinforces the *self-classifying, recirculating* nature of the Fluid Energy Mill. Notice the **loop** from `STILL_COARSE` back to `COLLIDING` — this is the conceptual essence of why fluid energy mills can produce such fine, uniform products despite the chaotic physics inside.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
> **Define Fluid Energy Mill. List any two of its advantages.**

**Model Answer (Full 3-Mark Response):**

A **Fluid Energy Mill** (also called a **Jet Mill**) is a size reduction equipment that uses a high-velocity jet of compressible fluid such as compressed air, inert gas, or superheated steam to grind particles via interparticle collisions. The mill has **no moving parts** and operates by accelerating feed particles in a spiral vortex created by tangentially injected gas streams.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **No contamination of product** — Absence of grinding media (balls, rods) eliminates wear-related contamination, making it ideal for pharmaceutical and high-purity applications.
2. **Suitable for heat-sensitive materials** — The grinding is achieved by rapid expansion of gas which has a cooling effect (Joule-Thomson effect), preventing thermal degradation of the product.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
> **Explain the role of tangential nozzle arrangement in a Fluid Energy Mill.**

**Model Answer (Full 3-Mark Response):**

The **tangential nozzle arrangement** is the *defining geometric feature* of a Fluid Energy Mill and serves **three critical functions**:
1. **Vortex Generation:** Tangential injection converts the *linear* momentum of the gas into *angular* momentum, producing a high-velocity spiral vortex.
2. **Particle Recirculation:** The vortex keeps particles suspended in the grinding zone, ensuring **multiple collision opportunities** per particle.
3. **Internal Centrifugal Classification:** The vortex creates a centrifugal field that separates fine particles (inward) from coarse particles (outward), acting as a *self-regulating classifier* without any mechanical parts.

### 5.2 Part B: Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** — *14 Marks (Choice A)*

**(a) With a neat labeled diagram, explain the construction and working of a Fluid Energy Mill. List its advantages, disadvantages, and applications.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 Marks):**
A Fluid Energy Mill consists of the following main parts:
- **Annular (toroidal) grinding chamber** — A flat, doughnut-shaped cavity, typically lined with wear-resistant material.
- **Tangential nozzles** — Two or more nozzles arranged tangentially on the periphery, made of tungsten carbide for wear resistance.
- **Feed inlet** — Located on the chamber wall, with a venturi eductor or screw feeder.
- **Central discharge outlet** — Axial outlet at the top, connected to an external classifier (cyclone).
- **Nozzle housing for compressed fluid** — Connects the high-pressure gas/steam supply to the nozzles.

**Working (3 Marks):**
- The feed material (already pre-crushed to $< 2 \text{ mm}$) enters the grinding chamber through the feed inlet.
- A high-pressure fluid (compressed air/steam at $700 \text{ kPa} - 7 \text{ MPa}$) is injected through the **tangential nozzles**.
- The tangential jet creates a **spiral vortex** in the chamber.
- Particles in the vortex are accelerated to high velocities ($100 - 300 \text{ m/s}$) and undergo **interparticle collisions** causing fragmentation.
- **Centrifugal force** pushes larger particles outward (re-circulation), while **gas drag** carries fine particles ($< d_{cut}$) inward and upward to the discharge outlet.
- The product is collected downstream in a cyclone and bag filter.

**Advantages (1 Mark):**
1. No contamination (no grinding media).
2. No heat generation (suitable for heat-sensitive materials).
3. No moving parts (low maintenance, high reliability).
4. Very fine product ($1 - 10 \text{ μm}$).
5. Dry, closed operation (no dust pollution).

**Disadvantages (0.5 Mark):**
1. Very high specific energy consumption ($200 - 1000 \text{ kWh/ton}$).
2. Low throughput compared to ball mills.
3. High noise level ($> 100 \text{ dB}$).
4. Nozzle wear due to abrasive feeds.

**Applications (0.5 Mark):**
Pharmaceuticals, pigments, cosmetics, food spices, battery materials, ceramics, explosives.

**(b) In a fluid energy mill, compressed air at 600 kPa (gauge) is supplied through a nozzle of velocity coefficient 0.92. The density of air at supply conditions is 6.5 kg/m³. Calculate the nozzle exit velocity. If the mill processes 0.8 ton/h of feed, calculate the specific energy consumption given the volumetric flow rate of air is 0.04 m³/s.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Gauge pressure $\Delta P = 600 \text{ kPa} = 600{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- Velocity coefficient $C_v = 0.92$
- Air density $\rho_f = 6.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Feed rate $\dot{m}_{feed} = 0.8 \text{ ton/h} = 800/3600 \text{ kg/s} = 0.2222 \text{ kg/s}$
- Volumetric flow rate of air $\dot{V}_f = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Nozzle Velocity Formula (3 Marks):**

$$v_{nozzle} = C_{v} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{f}}}$$

$$v_{nozzle} = 0.92 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 600{,}000}{6.5}}$$

$$v_{nozzle} = 0.92 \times \sqrt{184{,}615.38}$$

$$v_{nozzle} = 0.92 \times 429.67 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{nozzle} = 395.30 \text{ m/s}}$$

This velocity is **supersonic** (faster than the speed of sound in air, $\approx 343 \text{ m/s}$), confirming the high-energy regime of fluid energy mills.

**Step 2 — Calculate Hydraulic Power Input (2 Marks):**

$$P_{input} = \dot{V}_{f} \times \Delta P = 0.04 \times 600{,}000 = 24{,}000 \text{ W} = 24 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Specific Energy Consumption (2 Marks):**

$$E_{specific} = \dfrac{P_{input}}{\dot{m}_{feed}} = \dfrac{24{,}000 \text{ W}}{0.2222 \text{ kg/s}} = 108{,}000 \text{ J/kg}$$

Converting to standard industrial unit $\text{kWh/ton}$:

$$E_{specific} = \dfrac{24 \text{ kW}}{0.8 \text{ ton/h}} = 30 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

$$\boxed{E_{specific} = 108 \text{ kJ/kg} = 30 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Exam-Critical):**
> 1. **Gauge vs Absolute Pressure:** When the problem says "*600 kPa (gauge)*", use the gauge value directly as $\Delta P$ in the nozzle formula — do **not** add atmospheric pressure ($101.3 \text{ kPa}$). This is a **favourite 1-mark trap**.
> 2. **Unit Conversion of Feed Rate:** Students often forget to convert $\text{ton/h}$ to $\text{kg/s}$ before using the SI formula $E = P/\dot{m}$. This causes a **factor of 3.6 error** and full credit loss.
> 3. **Specific Energy Reporting:** Always express the final answer in **both** $\text{kJ/kg}$ AND $\text{kWh/ton}$ — KTU examiners award the *conversion step* as 1 full mark.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** — *14 Marks (Choice B — Alternative)*

**(a) Differentiate between Fluid Energy Mill and Ball Mill based on principle, energy consumption, contamination, and product size. Explain with neat sketches, the construction and working of an opposed jet fluid energy mill.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Comparative Differentiation Table (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Ball Mill** | **Fluid Energy Mill** |
|---|---|---|
| **Grinding Principle** | Impact and attrition by tumbling media (balls) | Interparticle collision in gas jet vortex |
| **Energy Consumption** | $0.5 - 5 \text{ kWh/ton}$ (low) | $200 - 1000 \text{ kWh/ton}$ (very high) |
| **Product Contamination** | High (from ball/liner wear) | Negligible (no media) |
| **Product Size** | $75 - 500 \text{ μm}$ (coarse to medium) | $1 - 10 \text{ μm}$ (ultrafine) |
| **Moving Parts** | Drum, media, drive system | None (only compressor) |
| **Heat-Sensitive Suitability** | Poor (heat generation) | Excellent (gas expansion cools) |

**Opposed Jet Mill — Construction (2 Marks):**
An opposed jet mill has **two diametrically opposite nozzles** that fire fluid streams *head-on* at each other. Feed is introduced through a **central inlet pipe** located between the two nozzles. The two opposing gas streams create a **high-velocity collision zone** in the middle. An integrated **dynamic classifier rotor** in the discharge zone separates fines from coarse particles.

**Opposed Jet Mill — Working (2 Marks):**
1. Feed enters centrally.
2. Two opposing gas jets accelerate particles toward each other.
3. **Head-on particle collisions** at the central focus shatter the particles.
4. The classifier rotor allows only fine particles to escape upward; coarse particles fall back into the collision zone.

**(b) A pharmaceutical company is micronizing 1.2 ton/h of a drug substance using a fluid energy mill operating at 800 kPa gauge pressure. The nozzle velocity coefficient is 0.95 and the air density at supply conditions is 7.2 kg/m³. The volumetric air flow rate is 0.06 m³/s. Calculate (i) the nozzle exit velocity, (ii) the power consumed, and (iii) the specific energy consumption in kWh/ton. Comment on the suitability of this mill for pharmaceutical use.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta P = 800 \text{ kPa} = 800{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $C_v = 0.95$, $\rho_f = 7.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\dot{V}_f = 0.06 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $\dot{m}_{feed} = 1.2 \text{ ton/h} = 1.2/3.6 = 0.3333 \text{ kg/s}$

**(i) Nozzle Exit Velocity (2 Marks):**

$$v_{nozzle} = 0.95 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 800{,}000}{7.2}} = 0.95 \times \sqrt{222{,}222.22}$$

$$v_{nozzle} = 0.95 \times 471.40 = \boxed{447.83 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Power Consumed (2 Marks):**

$$P = \dot{V}_{f} \times \Delta P = 0.06 \times 800{,}000 = 48{,}000 \text{ W} = \boxed{48 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Specific Energy Consumption (2 Marks):**

$$E_{specific} = \dfrac{48 \text{ kW}}{1.2 \text{ ton/h}} = \boxed{40 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

**Comment on Pharmaceutical Suitability (1 Mark):**
The fluid energy mill is **highly suitable** for pharmaceutical micronization because:
- **No contamination** from grinding media preserves drug purity (critical for regulatory compliance).
- **Negligible heat generation** protects heat-sensitive APIs from thermal degradation.
- **Sub-10 μm products** enhance dissolution and bioavailability.
- The specific energy of 40 kWh/ton is reasonable for high-value pharmaceuticals where product value far outweighs energy cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the velocity coefficient $C_v$:** In a question that explicitly gives $C_v$, omitting it costs **2 full marks**. Examiners check this line very strictly.
> 2. **Not commenting on pharmaceutical suitability** in the final sub-part: even a *correct numerical answer* without the qualitative discussion loses the **1 mark** allocated for "Comment".
> 3. **Mixing up feed rate units:** $\text{ton/h}$ vs $\text{kg/s}$ conversion is a **silent error** that propagates through all subsequent calculations. Always convert first.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Last-Minute Revision Points for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ **Fluid Energy Mill = Jet Mill** — same equipment, two names. No moving parts, uses **high-velocity compressible fluid** (air/steam/inert gas) to grind by **interparticle collision**.

- ✅ **Feed size $< 2 \text{ mm}$**, **Product size $1 - 10 \text{ μm}$** — this is its primary industrial niche: **ultrafine grinding** of already-pre-crushed material.

- ✅ **Working Principle in 4 words:** "Tangential Vortex Creates Collisions" (T-V-C-C).

- ✅ **The 6-Step Process:** Feed injection → Tangential nozzle jet → Vortex formation → Interparticle collision → Centrifugal classification → Pneumatic discharge.

- ✅ **Key Formula (must memorize):**
  - $v_{nozzle} = C_v \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho_f}$
  - $E_{specific} = P_{input} / \dot{m}_{feed}$

- ✅ **Tangential nozzle = Self-classifier:** The vortex geometry automatically separates coarse (outward) from fine (inward) particles — no external classifier is mandatory.

- ✅ **Key Advantages (Examiner Loves These):**
  1. Zero contamination
  2. No thermal degradation
  3. No moving parts
  4. Ultrafine product
  5. Closed, dust-free operation

- ✅ **Key Disadvantages (Always Include in 7-Mark Answers):**
  1. Very high specific energy ($200 - 1000 \text{ kWh/ton}$)
  2. Low throughput
  3. High noise ($> 100 \text{ dB}$)
  4. Nozzle wear (use wear-resistant materials)

- ✅ **Top Applications (Remember at least 4):** Pharmaceuticals, pigments, cosmetics (sunscreens), battery materials (Li-ion), food spices, ceramics, explosives.

- ✅ **Comparison Anchor:** Ball Mill = **Media-based**, efficient, but **contaminating**. Fluid Energy Mill = **Media-less**, inefficient, but **pure product**. This contrast is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**.

- ✅ **Examiner's Hot Traps:**
  - Gauge vs Absolute pressure distinction.
  - $\text{ton/h} \rightarrow \text{kg/s}$ conversion (factor of 3.6).
  - $E_{specific}$ reported in BOTH $\text{kJ/kg}$ AND $\text{kWh/ton}$.
  - Including the velocity coefficient $C_v$ when given.

- ✅ **Mnemonic for Advantages — "PUNCH":** **P**urity (no contamination), **U**ltrafine product, **N**o moving parts, **C**ool operation, **H**ygienic/closed.
