| # | Formula | Use |
|---|---------|-----|
| 1 | $V^2 + 2 \frac{A R_m}{\alpha c} V = \frac{2 A^2 \Delta P \,\theta}{\mu \alpha c}$ | Ruth's equation (constant P) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\theta}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ | Linear form: $y = mx + c$ |
| 3 | $A = 2 n A_f$ | Plate & Frame area |
| 4 | $\theta_f = \psi / N$ | RDVF filtration time per rev |
| 5 | $L_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P \, \theta_f}{\mu \alpha \rho_s (1-\epsilon)}}$ | Cake thickness in RDVF |
| 6 | $V = A \, L_c \, \psi$ | Filtrate volume per rev (RDVF) |
| 7 | $\theta_{\text{total}} = \theta_f + \theta_w + \theta_d$ | Cycle time (RDVF) |
| 8 | Cake vol. = cake vol. (vol. balance) | $L_c \cdot A = c \cdot V / [(1-\epsilon)\rho_s]$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Ruth's Equation (Constant-Pressure Filtration)

We will derive the foundational equation that governs all three filters. The starting point is Darcy's law, applied to a filter cake of increasing thickness.

**Starting point: Darcy's Law**

$$u = \frac{1}{A} \frac{dV}{d\theta} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$

**Step 1 — Define Total Resistance**

The total resistance is the sum of the resistance of the cake (which grows) and the resistance of the filter medium (which is constant):

$$R_{total} = R_{cake} + R_m$$

**Step 2 — Relate Cake Resistance to Filtrate Volume**

For a cake of mass $w$ deposited per unit area, with specific cake resistance $\alpha$:

$$R_{cake} = \alpha \cdot w$$

If $c$ = mass of solids per unit volume of filtrate, then for filtrate volume $V$:

$$w = \frac{c \cdot V}{A}$$

Therefore:

$$R_{total} = \frac{\alpha \cdot c \cdot V}{A} + R_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Darcy's Law**

$$\frac{1}{A}\frac{dV}{d\theta} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \left(\frac{\alpha c V}{A} + R_m\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Separate Variables**

$$\left(\frac{\alpha c V}{A} + R_m\right) dV = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} d\theta$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from 0 to V and 0 to θ**

$$\int_0^V \left(\frac{\alpha c V}{A} + R_m\right) dV = \int_0^\theta \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} d\theta$$

$$\frac{\alpha c}{A}\cdot\frac{V^2}{2} + R_m \cdot V = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P \cdot \theta}{\mu}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by 2A / (αc)**

$$V^2 + \frac{2 A R_m}{\alpha c}\cdot V = \frac{2 A^2 \Delta P \cdot \theta}{\mu \alpha c}$$

This is **Ruth's Equation** (constant pressure). $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> The linear form $\dfrac{\theta}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ is obtained by dividing both sides of Ruth's equation by $V^2$ (after some algebra). It gives a straight line on a $\theta/V$ vs. $V$ plot, with **slope** $m = \mu \alpha c / (2 A^2 \Delta P)$ and **intercept** $b = \mu R_m / (A \Delta P)$. From these, $\alpha$ and $R_m$ can be determined experimentally.

## 3.2 Derivation of RDVF Cake Thickness Equation

**Starting assumption:** At steady state, drum rotates at speed $N$ rev/s. Submergence is $\psi$. The filtration time per revolution is:

$$\theta_f = \frac{\psi}{N}$$

**Cake mass per unit area** at end of filtration step:

$$w = c \cdot v_f$$

where $v_f$ = volume of filtrate per unit area during the filtration step = $V_f / A$.

**Cake thickness** (assuming cake porosity $\epsilon$ and solids density $\rho_s$):

$$L_c = \frac{w}{(1 - \epsilon)\rho_s} = \frac{c \cdot v_f}{(1 - \epsilon)\rho_s}$$

**Volume balance:** Volume of solids in cake = Volume of filtrate × concentration of solids / solid's actual density

$$L_c \cdot A = \frac{c \cdot V_f}{(1-\epsilon)\rho_s}$$

Using Darcy's law for a continuous process (steady state, $\theta_f$ fixed):

$$v_f^2 + 2\frac{R_m}{\alpha c} v_f = \frac{2 \Delta P \, \theta_f}{\mu \alpha c}$$

For a thick drum filter, $R_m$ is often small compared to cake resistance, so:

$$v_f^2 \approx \frac{2 \Delta P \, \theta_f}{\mu \alpha c} \Rightarrow v_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P \, \theta_f}{\mu \alpha c}}$$

**Substitute $\theta_f = \psi / N$:**

$$\boxed{L_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P \, \psi}{\mu \alpha \rho_s (1-\epsilon) N}}}$$

This is the standard KTU expression for RDVF cake thickness.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A plate-and-frame filter press has **20 frames**, each with filtering area **0.5 m² per face**. The slurry contains **10 wt% solids** of density **2200 kg/m³**, and the filtrate density is **1000 kg/m³**. Filter medium resistance is $R_m = 1.2 \times 10^{10}$ m⁻¹. Specific cake resistance $\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{11}$ m/kg. Filtration pressure $\Delta P = 4 \times 10^5$ Pa, filtrate viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate the **filtration time** to collect **8 m³** of filtrate.

**Step 1 — Total filtration area**

$$A = 2 \cdot n \cdot A_f = 2 \cdot 20 \cdot 0.5 = 20 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Mass of solids per unit filtrate volume, c**

For 1 kg slurry: 0.1 kg solids + 0.9 kg liquid.
Mass of liquid = 0.9 kg → volume of filtrate = 0.9/1000 = $9 \times 10^{-4}$ m³.
Mass of solids per m³ of filtrate:

$$c = \frac{0.1}{9 \times 10^{-4}} = 111.11 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ruth's equation**

$$V^2 + \frac{2 A R_m}{\alpha c} V = \frac{2 A^2 \Delta P \,\theta}{\mu \alpha c}$$

Compute the constant $\dfrac{2 A R_m}{\alpha c}$:

$$\frac{2 \cdot 20 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{10}}{1.5 \times 10^{11} \cdot 111.11} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{11}}{1.667 \times 10^{13}} = 0.0288 \text{ m}^3$$

Compute the coefficient $\dfrac{2 A^2 \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c}$:

$$\frac{2 \cdot 400 \cdot 4 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{11} \cdot 111.11} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{8}}{1.667 \times 10^{10}} = 0.0192 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute V = 8 m³ and solve for θ**

$$(8)^2 + 0.0288 \cdot 8 = 0.0192 \cdot \theta$$
$$64 + 0.2304 = 0.0192 \cdot \theta$$
$$64.2304 = 0.0192 \cdot \theta$$
$$\theta = 3345.3 \text{ s} \approx 55.8 \text{ minutes}$$

**Answer:** $\theta \approx 55.8$ minutes.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (3 × 3)

| Aspect | Plate & Frame | Rotary Drum | Leaf Filter |
|--------|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Filter equation (1)** | Ruth (constant P) | Ruth + $\theta_f = \psi/N$ | Ruth (constant P) |
| **Cycle step (2)** | Batch = 1 cycle = (F + W + C) | Continuous, infinite cycles | Batch = 1 cycle = (F + D) |
| **Discharge (3)** | Manual/auto dump | Scraper knife | Manual/auto blow |
| **Cake dry (4)** | Yes (press) | Partial | Partial |
| **Maintenance (5)** | Cloth wash/replace | Cloth wash (auto) | High (leaf removal) |
| **Capital (6)** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Pressure (7)** | Up to 15 bar | 0.5–0.85 bar | Up to 5 bar |
| **Solids % (8)** | Up to 30% | 10–20% | Up to 20% |
| **Particle size (9)** | Fine | Medium (10 µm +) | Fine |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plate and Frame Filter Press — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HydraulicClosure["Hydraulic Closure System"]
        HCS1[Manual or Hydraulic Ram]
        HCS2[End Plate Fixed]
        HCS3[End Plate Movable]
        HCS1 --> HCS3
    end

    subgraph StackAssembly["Plate-Frame Stack Assembly"]
        SF1[Frame 1 - Cake Chamber]
        SP1[Plate 1 - Filtered Surface]
        SC1[Filter Cloth on Plate 1]
        SF2[Frame 2 - Cake Chamber]
        SP2[Plate 2 - Filtered Surface]
        SC2[Filter Cloth on Plate 2]
        SP1 --> SC1
        SP2 --> SC2
        SP1 --> SF1
        SF1 --> SP2
        SP2 --> SF2
    end

    subgraph FeedSystem["Feed and Wash System"]
        F1[Slurry Feed Pump]
        F2[Slurry Inlet Manifold]
        F3[Wash Water Inlet]
        F4[Compressed Air for Cake Drying]
        F1 --> F2
        F3 --> F2
        F4 --> F2
    end

    subgraph Collection["Filtrate and Wash Collection"]
        C1[Filtrate Outlet Cocks]
        C2[Filtrate Manifold]
        C3[Filtrate Tank]
        C4[Wash Water Outlet]
        C1 --> C2
        C2 --> C3
        C4 --> C3
    end

    F2 --> SF1
    SC1 --> C1
    SC2 --> C1
    F2 --> SF2
```

## 4.2 Plate and Frame Filter Press — Construction Schematic (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fixed End Plate] --> B1[Filter Cloth]
    B1 --> C1[Plate 1 Grooved]
    C1 --> D1[Frame 1 Hollow]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Distinguish between a filter cake and filter medium. Why is the resistance of the filter medium considered negligible at long filtration times but important at the beginning?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Filter medium (1 mark):** The porous barrier (cloth, mesh) that initially retains solids; it has a constant resistance $R_m$ that does not change with time.
- **Filter cake (1 mark):** The accumulated layer of solids deposited on the medium; its resistance grows linearly with the mass deposited ($R_{cake} = \alpha w$).
- **Relative importance (1 mark):** At the start of filtration ($V \to 0$), the cake resistance is nearly zero, so $R_m$ dominates. As $V$ grows, $R_{cake} \propto V$ while $R_m$ stays constant, so $R_m$ becomes negligible at long times. The dimensionless ratio is $R_m A / (\alpha c V)$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"With a neat sketch, explain the working of a rotary drum vacuum filter. Define submergence."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Working (1.5 marks):** A hollow drum with sectors rotates slowly in a slurry trough. Each sector is connected alternately to vacuum and compressed air via a trunnion valve. Vacuum sucks filtrate out as the sector passes through the slurry, dewatering, and washing zones. Compressed air blows the cake off just before a scraper knife removes it.
- **Submergence (1 mark):** Fraction $\psi$ of the total drum surface area submerged in the slurry at any instant, typically **0.25 to 0.75**.
- **Key parameters (0.5 marks):** Drum speed $N$, drum area $A$, submergence $\psi$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes that cost 1–3 marks each in this topic:
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** when computing filtration area of a plate & frame press ($A = 2 n A_f$, not $n A_f$).
> 2. **Mixing constant-pressure and constant-rate** forms of Ruth's equation — KTU almost always asks **constant-pressure** form.
> 3. **Unit errors in $c$** — confusing kg/kg slurry with kg/m³ filtrate (you must base it on filtrate, not on slurry).
> 4. **Neglecting $\rho_s(1-\epsilon)$ vs. $c$** in RDVF cake thickness — these are different quantities; do not equate them.
> 5. **Skipping the linearised plot** when the question says "from the data, find $\alpha$ and $R_m$" — you must show $y = mV + c$ form explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, PCCHT302]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **construction and working of a plate and frame filter press**. Discuss both the **non-wash plate** and **wash plate** arrangements, and explain why the wash plate arrangement gives cleaner cake washing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plate-and-frame filter press has **15 frames**, each frame providing a filtering area of **0.6 m² on each face**. A slurry containing **8 wt% calcium carbonate** ($\rho_s = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$) is filtered at a constant pressure of $\Delta P = 3.5 \times 10^5$ Pa. Filtrate properties: $\mu = 1.1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³. Cake has porosity $\epsilon = 0.42$ and specific cake resistance $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{11}$ m/kg. Filter medium resistance $R_m = 8.5 \times 10^9$ m⁻¹. Determine: **(i)** total filtration area, **(ii)** mass of solids per m³ of filtrate, **(iii)** filtration time to collect **5 m³ of filtrate**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step (i) — Total area**
$$A = 2 n A_f = 2 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.6 = 18 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (ii) — Concentration c**
For 1 kg slurry: 0.08 kg solids, 0.92 kg liquid → 0.92/1000 = $9.2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³ filtrate.
$$c = \frac{0.08}{9.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 86.96 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step (iii) — Filtration time**
Ruth's equation:
$$V^2 + \frac{2 A R_m}{\alpha c} V = \frac{2 A^2 \Delta P \,\theta}{\mu \alpha c}$$

Compute coefficients:

$$\frac{2 A R_m}{\alpha c} = \frac{2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8.5 \times 10^9}{1.2 \times 10^{11} \cdot 86.96} = \frac{3.06 \times 10^{11}}{1.0435 \times 10^{13}} = 0.02932 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\frac{2 A^2 \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c} = \frac{2 \cdot 324 \cdot 3.5 \times 10^5}{1.1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{11} \cdot 86.96} = \frac{2.268 \times 10^8}{1.1479 \times 10^{10}} = 0.01976 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Substitute $V = 5$ m³:
$$(5)^2 + 0.02932 \cdot 5 = 0.01976 \cdot \theta$$
$$25 + 0.1466 = 0.01976 \cdot \theta$$
$$\theta = \frac{25.1466}{0.01976} = 1272.6 \text{ s} \approx 21.2 \text{ minutes}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $A = 18 \text{ m}^2$; (ii) $c = 86.96 \text{ kg/m}^3$; (iii) $\theta = 21.2$ min.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, PCCHT302]**

**(a)** Describe with a **neat sketch** the **construction and working of a rotary drum vacuum filter**. Clearly identify the **submerged, washing, dewatering, and discharge zones**, and explain the role of the **trunnion valve**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotary drum vacuum filter has **30% submergence** ($\psi = 0.3$) and rotates at **0.5 rpm** ($N = 0.5/60 = 1/120$ rev/s). The filtration area is **6 m²**. The filter operates under a vacuum of **0.7 bar** (ΔP = $7 \times 10^4$ Pa). For the slurry: $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\alpha = 5 \times 10^{10}$ m/kg, $\rho_s = 1500$ kg/m³, cake porosity $\epsilon = 0.45$. Determine: **(i)** filtration time per revolution, **(ii)** cake thickness $L_c$, **(iii)** filtrate volume per revolution, and **(iv)** filtration rate in m³/h. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step (i) — Filtration time per rev**
$$\theta_f = \frac{\psi}{N} = \frac{0.3}{1/120} = 0.3 \cdot 120 = 36 \text{ s}$$

**Step (ii) — Cake thickness**
$$L_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P \, \psi}{\mu \alpha \rho_s (1-\epsilon) N}}$$

Numerator: $2 \cdot 7 \times 10^4 \cdot 0.3 = 4.2 \times 10^4$
Denominator: $1.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{10} \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.55 \cdot (1/120)$
$$= 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{10} \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.55 \cdot 0.00833 = 3.438 \times 10^6$$

$$L_c = \sqrt{\frac{4.2 \times 10^4}{3.438 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{0.01222} = 0.1105 \text{ m} = 11.05 \text{ cm}$$

**Step (iii) — Filtrate volume per revolution**
$$V = A \cdot L_c \cdot \psi = 6 \cdot 0.1105 \cdot 0.3 = 0.199 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step (iv) — Filtration rate**
Revolutions per hour: $N \cdot 3600 = (1/120) \cdot 3600 = 30$ rev/h
$$Q = V \cdot N_{\text{per h}} = 0.199 \cdot 30 = 5.97 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $\theta_f = 36$ s; (ii) $L_c = 0.1105$ m; (iii) $V_{rev} = 0.199$ m³; (iv) $Q = 5.97$ m³/h.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Filter** = porous medium + cake; total resistance = $R_{cake} + R_m$.
- [x] **Ruth's equation** (constant P): $V^2 + 2 A R_m V / (\alpha c) = 2 A^2 \Delta P \theta / (\mu \alpha c)$.
- [x] **Linear form** (for $\alpha$ and $R_m$ from data): $\theta/V = (\mu \alpha c / 2 A^2 \Delta P) V + \mu R_m / (A \Delta P)$ — slope gives $\alpha$, intercept gives $R_m$.
- [x] **Plate & Frame** is **batch, pressure-driven**; $A = 2 n A_f$ (factor of 2 is mandatory).
- [x] **Wash plates** give clean washing because wash water passes through the *entire cake thickness* in one pass; non-wash plates have wash water following the same path as filtrate (less efficient).
- [x] **Rotary Drum** is **continuous, vacuum-driven**; operates by submergence $\psi$ and rotation $N$.
- [x] **Cake thickness in RDVF**: $L_c = \sqrt{2 \Delta P \psi / [\mu \alpha \rho_s (1-\epsilon) N]}$.
- [x] **Filtration time per rev in RDVF**: $\theta_f = \psi / N$.
- [x] **Leaf Filter** is **batch, pressure or vacuum**; large filter area in compact space; clean filtrate; labour-intensive cloth replacement.
- [x] **Comparative choice** — Use Plate & Frame for high-purity, dry cake, batch jobs; RDVF for large continuous throughput; Leaf filter for medium batches with high clarity needs.
- [x] **Trunnion valve** in RDVF = a rotary seal that connects each sector to vacuum (for filtration) or air (for cake discharge) at the correct zone in the cycle.
- [x] **Washing ratio** (Plate & Frame with wash plates) = volume of wash water / volume of filtrate, usually 0.1–0.3.
- [x] **Cake moisture** is lowest in Plate & Frame (due to compression) and highest in RDVF (thin cake, no compression).
- [x] **Incompressible vs. compressible cakes**: Ruth's equation assumes incompressible; for compressible cakes, $\alpha$ varies with ΔP, requiring correction factors.
- [x] **Cycle time** in RDVF = $1/N$; in Plate & Frame = filtration time + wash time + discharge time + cleaning time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Washing of Filter Cakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Washing of Filter Cakes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Washing of Filter Cakes** is a downstream solid–liquid separation operation in which a pure **wash liquid** (typically water or a solvent) is passed through a freshly formed filter cake to **displace, dilute, and/or dissolve the residual mother liquor** retained within the cake pores, thereby recovering dissolved solute and/or purifying the solid product.

The process is governed by the **Wash Ratio (W)**, defined as:

$$W = \frac{V_w}{V_L}$$

where:
- $V_w$ = volume of wash liquid passed through the cake (m³)
- $V_L$ = volume of mother liquor retained in the cake pores (m³)

The **Fractional Recovery (R)** of the solute is:

$$R = \frac{\text{Mass of solute recovered in effluent}}{\text{Mass of solute originally present in cake}}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge Under the Tap"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine squeezing a wet kitchen sponge** (the filter cake) that has soaked up a **salty broth** (the mother liquor containing solute). If you hold the sponge under a slow, steady stream of fresh water (wash liquid), the water physically pushes out most of the salty liquid in one go (this is **displacement washing**). However, some salt remains trapped deep inside the sponge fibres. Given enough **time**, that residual salt slowly diffuses out into the fresh water (this is **diffusion washing**). The longer you rinse and the more water you use, the cleaner (purer) the sponge becomes.

This simple analogy captures the two simultaneous physical mechanisms — bulk **displacement** and molecular **diffusion** — that govern industrial cake washing.

## 1.3 Purpose & Industrial Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why wash filter cakes?** Three driving motives exist in the chemical process industry:
> 1. **Solute Recovery** — to reclaim valuable dissolved product (e.g., sugar in beet refining, NaOH in alumina (Bayer) process, NaCl in table-salt manufacture, citric acid in fermentation).
> 2. **Solid Purification** — to remove impurities adhering to the crystals (e.g., pharmaceuticals, fine chemicals).
> 3. **Mother-Liquor Reuse** — the wash effluent is often recycled to upstream feed tanks, improving overall yield.

## 1.4 Wash Liquid Selection Criteria

A suitable wash liquid must satisfy the following engineering criteria:

- **Chemical inertness** toward the solid and solute
- **High solubility** for the target solute
- **Low viscosity** (to maintain high permeability)
- **Low corrosivity** toward filter media and equipment
- **Easy separability** from the product downstream
- **Economical availability**

The ratio of the **viscosity of wash liquid to that of mother liquor** is a critical design parameter, often appearing in the form of a **Hagen–Poiseuille** correction factor for flow through the cake pores.

## 1.5 Position of the Washing Unit Operation

> [!NOTE]
> In a typical **solid–liquid separation flowsheet**, washing always follows **filtration** and precedes **drying** or further processing. It is therefore the **second sub-operation** in the canonical sequence: **Filtration → Washing → Dewatering → Drying**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Dominant Mechanisms of Washing

### 2.1.1 Displacement Washing (Bulk / Piston-like)

- The wash liquid flows as a coherent **plug** through the cake pores.
- It physically pushes out the mother liquor ahead of it.
- **No solute appears in the effluent** until the wash front has traversed the entire cake thickness.
- Highly **efficient** if the cake is uniform, non-compressible, and crack-free.
- Governing factor: cake **permeability** and **hydraulic gradient**.

### 2.1.2 Diffusion Washing (Molecular)

- Even after bulk displacement is complete, **residual solute** remains in:
  - Stagnant pockets inside porous particles
  - Boundary layers around particles
  - Liquid films held by capillary forces
- This solute migrates by **molecular diffusion** into the bulk wash liquid.
- **Time-dependent**, follows **Fick's Second Law**.
- Governed by: solute **diffusivity (D)**, **residence time (t)**, **contact area**.

### 2.1.3 Real Washing Curves — Three Distinct Regions

A real industrial washing curve plotted as $C/C_0$ versus $W$ (or recovery $R$ versus $W$) shows **three regions**:

| Region | Mechanism | Slope Behaviour | Time-Scale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **I — Initial** | Bulk displacement | Very steep fall in $C/C_0$ | Short (seconds) |
| **II — Transition** | Combined displacement + diffusion | Moderate slope | Intermediate |
| **III — Tail** | Pure diffusion from stagnant zones | Very shallow asymptotic tail | Long (minutes–hours) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** A well-designed washing operation aims to maximise **Region I** (efficient displacement) and minimise **Region III** (avoid the uneconomical long diffusion tail) by selecting an **optimal wash ratio** of typically **W = 1.3 to 2.5** depending on cake compressibility and product value.

## 2.2 Idealised Mathematical Models

### Model A — Ideal Displacement (Plug Flow)

- Assumes no axial mixing between wash liquid and mother liquor.
- Recovery is **linear** until $W = 1$:

$$R = W \quad \text{for} \quad W \leq 1$$

$$R = 1 \quad \text{for} \quad W \geq 1$$

- The effluent concentration ratio:

$$\frac{C}{C_0} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad W < 1$$

- A **discontinuous step** at $W = 1$ (impractical in real systems but the theoretical benchmark).

### Model B — Perfect Mixing (Back-Mix / Well-Stirred)

- Assumes the wash liquid and the mother liquor are **instantaneously and perfectly mixed** within the cake.
- The differential mass balance yields an **exponential decay**:

$$\frac{C}{C_0} = \exp(-W)$$

$$R = 1 - \exp(-W)$$

- Taking the natural log:

$$\ln(1 - R) = -W$$

- A plot of $\ln(1-R)$ versus $W$ is a **straight line of slope $-1$** — this is the diagnostic plot used in laboratory washing tests to identify back-mix dominated regimes.

### Model C — Dispersion Model (Realistic)

- Incorporates **axial dispersion** via a Péclet-type number:

$$\text{Pe} = \frac{v \cdot L}{D_{ax}}$$

where $v$ = interstitial velocity, $L$ = cake thickness, $D_{ax}$ = axial dispersion coefficient.
- High Pe $\Rightarrow$ approaches **plug flow** (ideal displacement).
- Low Pe $\Rightarrow$ approaches **back-mix** (perfect mixing).
- Real cakes with **Pe between 1 and 100** show intermediate behaviour.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem on cake washing.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Wash Ratio | $W = V_w / V_L$ | Dimensionless; $V_L$ = pore volume in cake |
| 2 | Fractional Recovery (Perfect Mixing) | $R = 1 - \exp(-W)$ | Always $0 \leq R \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Effluent Concentration Ratio (Perfect Mixing) | $C / C_0 = \exp(-W)$ | $C_0$ = initial mother-liquor concentration |
| 4 | Linearised Mixing Equation | $\ln(1-R) = -W$ | Diagnostic straight-line plot |
| 5 | Recovery (Ideal Displacement) | $R = W$ for $W \leq 1$; $R = 1$ for $W \geq 1$ | Theoretical maximum efficiency |
| 6 | Pore (Liquid Hold-up) Volume | $V_L = \varepsilon \cdot V_c$ | $\varepsilon$ = cake porosity, $V_c$ = cake volume |
| 7 | Mass of Solute in Cake | $M_s = C_0 \cdot V_L$ | Used in material balance |
| 8 | Péclet Number | $\text{Pe} = v L / D_{ax}$ | High Pe $\Rightarrow$ plug flow; Low Pe $\Rightarrow$ mixing |
| 9 | Hagen–Poiseuille Flow through Cake | $Q = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P \cdot \varepsilon^3}{\mu \cdot L \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^2 \cdot k}$ | $k$ = Kozeny–Carman constant $\approx 5$ |
| 10 | Diffusion Time Scale | $t \sim L^2 / D_{eff}$ | $D_{eff}$ = effective diffusivity in cake |
| 11 | Wash Liquid Requirement (Mixing Model) | $V_w = -V_L \cdot \ln(1 - R)$ | Rearrangement of eqn. (4) |
| 12 | Overall Cake Porosity | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_s}$ | $\rho_c$ = bulk cake density, $\rho_s$ = true solid density |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Convention for KTU Scripts:** Always use $C_0$ for **initial** mother-liquor concentration and $C$ for **instantaneous effluent** concentration. The ratio $C/C_0$ is the dimensionless group examiners most commonly test.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

> [!TIP]
> Cake washing is **economically pivotal** in several flagship industrial processes:

- **Bayer Process (Alumina Production):** NaOH is recovered from the red mud cake by multi-stage counter-current washing, with wash ratios engineered to keep NaOH loss below 0.5 %.
- **Sugar Refining:** White sugar crystals are washed in centrifuges; the wash water removes molasses film and reduces ash content.
- **Pharmaceutical Crystallisation:** Crystals are washed on Nutsche filters to remove mother-liquor impurities critical to API purity.
- **Kaolin / TiO₂ Processing:** Clay cakes are washed to remove soluble salts that affect brightness and rheology.
- **Effluent Treatment:** Sludge cakes from filter presses are washed to recover heavy metals and reduce leachate toxicity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Perfect Mixing (Back-Mix) Model

> **Physical Premise:** At every instant, the wash liquid and the mother liquor inside the cake pores are **perfectly mixed**, so the effluent leaving the cake has the **same composition** as the liquid inside the cake.

**Step 1 — System Definition and Control Volume**

Consider a differential volume $dV$ of wash liquid that has just entered the cake. During this differential step, the following mass balance is written over the cake as the control volume:

$$\underbrace{(\text{Mass of solute entering})}_{= 0 \; (\text{pure wash liquid})} - \underbrace{(\text{Mass of solute leaving})}_{= C \cdot dV} = \underbrace{(\text{Accumulation of solute in cake})}_{= V_L \cdot dC}$$

**Step 2 — Writing the Differential Balance**

Rearranging the above:

$$0 - C \cdot dV = V_L \cdot dC$$

$$-C \cdot dV = V_L \cdot dC$$

**Step 3 — Variable Separation**

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{dV}{V_L}$$

**Step 4 — Integration with Initial Condition**

Integrate from $V = 0$ (where $C = C_0$) to a generic state $V = V_w$ (where $C = C$):

$$\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{1}{V_L} \int_{0}^{V_w} dV$$

$$\left[ \ln C \right]_{C_0}^{C} = -\frac{V_w}{V_L}$$

**Step 5 — Solving for the Concentration Ratio**

$$\ln \left( \frac{C}{C_0} \right) = -W \quad \text{where} \quad W = \frac{V_w}{V_L}$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\boxed{\frac{C}{C_0} = \exp(-W)}$$

**Step 6 — Deriving the Recovery Expression**

Fractional recovery of solute:

$$R = \frac{\text{Mass recovered}}{\text{Mass originally in cake}} = \frac{C_0 V_L - C V_L}{C_0 V_L} = 1 - \frac{C}{C_0}$$

Substituting the result from Step 5:

$$\boxed{R = 1 - \exp(-W)}$$

**Step 7 — Useful Inverse Form**

If a target recovery $R$ is specified, the required wash volume is:

$$1 - R = \exp(-W) \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = -\ln(1 - R)$$

$$\boxed{V_w = -V_L \cdot \ln(1 - R)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ideal Displacement (Plug Flow) Model

> **Physical Premise:** The wash liquid and the mother liquor do **not mix**; the wash pushes the mother liquor ahead of it as a sharp **piston front**.

**Step 1 — Volumetric Displacement Principle**

When a volume $V_w$ of wash has passed:

- The mother liquor displaced = $V_w$ (incompressible, no mixing).
- The mass of solute recovered = $C_0 \cdot V_w$ (since the displaced liquid is unchanged in concentration, equal to $C_0$).

**Step 2 — Recovery Definition**

$$R = \frac{\text{Mass recovered}}{C_0 \cdot V_L} = \frac{C_0 \cdot V_w}{C_0 \cdot V_L} = \frac{V_w}{V_L} = W$$

This holds **only as long as $V_w \leq V_L$** (i.e., $W \leq 1$).

**Step 3 — Saturation Beyond W = 1**

Once the entire mother-liquor volume has been displaced, no more solute can be recovered:

$$R = 1 \quad \text{for} \quad W \geq 1$$

**Step 4 — Effluent Concentration Profile**

The effluent leaving the cake is **pure mother liquor** of concentration $C_0$ until the wash front reaches the cake exit:

$$\frac{C}{C_0} = 1 \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \leq W \leq 1$$

Beyond $W = 1$, the effluent is pure wash liquid (no solute):

$$\frac{C}{C_0} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad W > 1$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form**

$$\boxed{R = \min(W, 1)}$$

---

## 3.3 Comparative Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style 7-mark problem):**
> A rotary drum filter produces a cake of thickness 8 mm with porosity $\varepsilon = 0.42$ and solids density $\rho_s = 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The mother liquor contains $C_0 = 80 \text{ kg/m}^3$ of dissolved solute. The cake is washed with pure water.
> **(a)** Calculate the wash ratio and fractional recovery when $V_w = 0.005 \text{ m}^3$ of water is used per m² of filter area, assuming (i) ideal displacement, (ii) perfect mixing.
> **(b)** What wash volume is required to achieve **98 % recovery** under each model? Comment on the practical implication.

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- Cake thickness: $L_c = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008 \text{ m}$
- Porosity: $\varepsilon = 0.42$
- $C_0 = 80 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $V_w = 0.005 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2$ of filter area

**Part (a) — Pore Volume and Wash Ratio**

Pore (mother-liquor) volume per m² of cake:

$$V_L = \varepsilon \cdot L_c = 0.42 \times 0.008 = 3.36 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2$$

Wash ratio:

$$W = \frac{V_w}{V_L} = \frac{0.005}{3.36 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.488$$

**Sub-part (a)(i) — Ideal Displacement Model**

$$R = \min(W, 1) = \min(1.488, 1) = 1.00 \text{ (i.e., 100 \% recovery)}$$

The excess water ($V_w - V_L = 0.00164 \text{ m}^3$) carries no further solute.

**Sub-part (a)(ii) — Perfect Mixing Model**

$$R = 1 - \exp(-W) = 1 - \exp(-1.488)$$

$$\exp(-1.488) = 0.2260$$

$$R = 1 - 0.2260 = 0.774 \quad \text{or} \quad 77.4 \%$$

**Part (b) — Required Wash Volume for 98 % Recovery**

**(i) Ideal Displacement:** Since $R = W$ up to unity, $R = 0.98$ is achieved at:

$$W = 0.98 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_w = 0.98 \times 3.36 \times 10^{-3} = 3.293 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Perfect Mixing:** $R = 1 - \exp(-W) = 0.98$:

$$\exp(-W) = 0.02 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = -\ln(0.02) = 3.912$$

$$V_w = 3.912 \times 3.36 \times 10^{-3} = 1.314 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2$$

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Model | Wash Ratio $W$ | Wash Volume (m³/m²) | Recovery |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Ideal Displacement | 0.98 | 3.29 × 10⁻³ | 98 % |
| Perfect Mixing | 3.91 | 1.31 × 10⁻² | 98 % |
| **Ratio (Mixing / Displacement)** | **3.99** | **3.99** | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Implication:** The perfect mixing model requires **~4× more wash water** than ideal displacement for the same 98 % recovery. Real industrial systems always lie between these two extremes, but are typically **closer to the perfect mixing model** for compressible, cracked, or fine-particle cakes. This drives the use of **multi-stage counter-current washing** to economise on wash-water consumption.

---

## 3.4 Multi-Stage Counter-Current Washing — Analytical Setup

In industrial practice, single-stage washing is wasteful. A **counter-current cascade** of $N$ stages with the same total wash ratio $W_{\text{total}}$ dramatically improves recovery.

For $N$ ideal mixing stages in series with equal wash distribution:

$$R_N = 1 - \left( \frac{1}{1 + W/N} \right)^N$$

As $N \to \infty$, $R_N \to 1$ for **any** finite $W$ — illustrating why **infinite-stage counter-current washing is the theoretical optimum**.

> **Worked Mini-Example:** For $W = 1.5$ and $N = 3$ stages:

$$R_3 = 1 - \left( \frac{1}{1 + 0.5} \right)^3 = 1 - (0.6667)^3 = 1 - 0.2963 = 0.7037 \; (70.4 \%)$$

Compare to single stage: $R_1 = 1 - \exp(-1.5) = 1 - 0.2231 = 0.7769 \; (77.7 \%)$ — but in a true single stage using ALL the wash at once. In **stagewise** operation, the $R_1$ formula uses the **stage wash ratio** $W/N = 0.5$, giving $R_1 = 1 - \exp(-0.5) = 0.3935$, and the cumulative $R_3$ from the cascade equation above gives the correct overall result.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Cake Washing on a Filter Medium

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Filter Cake Formed] --> B[Cake Contains Mother Liquor in Pores]
    B --> C{Choose Wash Strategy}
    C --> D[Displacement Washing]
    C --> E[Diffusion Washing]
    C --> F[Reslurry Washing]
    D --> G[Wash Liquid Sprayed Above Cake]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Cake Repulped in Wash Liquid]
    G --> I[Wash Passes Through Cake Pores]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Solute Transferred to Effluent]
    J --> K{Wash Ratio W Reached?}
    K -->|No| L[Continue Washing]
    L --> I
    K -->|Yes| M[Collect Wash Effluent]
    M --> N[Recovery Measured vs W]
    N --> O[Plot Washing Curve]
    O --> P{R Target Met?}
    P -->|No| Q[Add More Stages / Counter Current]
    P -->|Yes| R[Washed Cake to Dewatering]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — Two Idealised Models vs. Reality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLO[Ideal Displacement Plug Flow]
        P1[C Equals Zero for W Less Than 1]
        P2[R Equals W Up to Unity]
        P3[Step Function on Curve]
    end
    subgraph BAC[Perfect Mixing Back Mix]
        B1[C by C0 Equals Exp Minus W]
        B2[R Equals 1 Minus Exp Minus W]
        B3[Exponential Decay on Curve]
    end
    subgraph REA[Real Industrial Cake]
        R1[Region 1 Steep Displacement]
        R2[Region 2 Transition Zone]
        R3[Region 3 Long Diffusion Tail]
    end
    PLO --> REA
    BAC --> REA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mass-Transfer Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INP[Inputs]
        I1[Filter Cake with Mother Liquor]
        I2[Pure Wash Liquid Supply]
    end
    subgraph PRO[Process Unit]
        S1[Stage 1 Initial Bulk Displacement]
        S2[Stage 2 Transition Bulk Plus Diffusion]
        S3[Stage 3 Pure Diffusion Tail]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Outputs]
        O1[Recovered Solute in Effluent Stream]
        O2[Washed Cake Leaving Filter]
    end
    I1 --> S1
    I2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> O1
    S3 --> O2
```

## 4.4 Coordinate Schematic — The Washing Curve (C/C₀ vs. W)

The standard washing curve shows two idealised extremes bounding the real behaviour:

| Wash Ratio $W$ | Ideal Displacement $C/C_0$ | Perfect Mixing $C/C_0$ | Real Cake $C/C_0$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| 0.5 | 1.0 | 0.607 | 0.85 |
| 1.0 | 0.0 (step) | 0.368 | 0.45 |
| 1.5 | 0.0 | 0.223 | 0.20 |
| 2.0 | 0.0 | 0.135 | 0.10 |
| 3.0 | 0.0 | 0.050 | 0.04 |

> The real cake curve **starts** close to the displacement ideal, **bends** through the transition region, and **tails** off in the diffusion-controlled long-time region — never reaching zero practically, but mathematically approaching it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Define wash ratio. Explain how it governs the design of a cake-washing operation on a rotary drum filter.**

**Model Answer:**

The **wash ratio (W)** is defined as the ratio of the volume of wash liquid passed through the cake to the volume of mother liquor retained within the cake pores:

$$W = \frac{V_w}{V_L} = \frac{V_w}{\varepsilon \cdot V_c}$$

In rotary drum filter design, the wash ratio is the **primary lever** controlling the **fractional recovery** of the dissolved solute. For a given cake (fixed $\varepsilon$, $V_c$), increasing $W$ monotonically increases the recovery $R$ — but with **diminishing returns** beyond $W = 2$ to $3$, especially in the diffusion-controlled tail. Economical design therefore targets a wash ratio that achieves the desired product purity at the **minimum wash-water consumption**, often lying in the range $W = 1.3$ to $2.5$. The wash ratio also dictates the **residence time** of the cake under the wash spray nozzles, which in turn fixes the **drum speed** and the **arc length** of the washing zone.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Differentiate clearly between displacement washing and diffusion washing of filter cakes. Under what conditions does each dominate?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Displacement Washing | Diffusion Washing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mechanism** | Bulk hydraulic push of mother liquor by wash liquid | Molecular diffusion of solute from stagnant zones |
| **Time-scale** | Fast (seconds) | Slow (minutes to hours) |
| **Driving force** | Pressure gradient (hydraulic head) | Concentration gradient (Fick's law) |
| **Dominates when** | Cake is porous, non-compressible, no cracks; wash liquid is of low viscosity | Cake is fine, compressible, or wash liquid has been depleted; solute trapped in particle pores |
| **Efficiency indicator** | Approaches 100 % at $W = 1$ | Long exponential tail |
| **Mathematical idealisation** | Plug flow (ideal displacement) | Back-mix / Fickian diffusion |

In **Region I** of the washing curve, displacement dominates. In **Region III** (the long tail), diffusion dominates. The two mechanisms operate **simultaneously**, but their **relative contribution** shifts with time.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of neat sketches, derive the **expressions for fractional recovery (R)** under the (i) ideal displacement and (ii) perfect mixing models of cake washing. State clearly the assumptions of each model.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A filter cake of thickness 10 mm and porosity 0.45 is to be washed on a continuous belt filter. The mother liquor concentration is $C_0 = 60 \text{ kg/m}^3$. If the operation follows the perfect mixing model, determine:
> 1. The volume of wash water required per m² of belt area to achieve 95 % recovery.
> 2. The instantaneous effluent solute concentration when the wash ratio $W = 2$.

**Model Solution (a):**

The derivations are as shown in **Section 3.1** and **Section 3.2** above. Summary:

**(i) Ideal Displacement:** $R = \min(W, 1)$ — recovery grows linearly up to $W = 1$ and saturates. Assumes plug flow, no axial mixing, incompressible pores.

**(ii) Perfect Mixing:** $R = 1 - \exp(-W)$ — exponential approach to unity. Assumes instantaneous and complete mixing of wash and mother liquor within cake at all times.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Cake thickness $L_c = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$
- Porosity $\varepsilon = 0.45$
- $C_0 = 60 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Target recovery $R = 0.95$

**Step 1 — Pore Volume per m²:**

$$V_L = \varepsilon \cdot L_c = 0.45 \times 0.01 = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Required Wash Ratio for 95 % Recovery (Perfect Mixing):**

$$R = 1 - \exp(-W) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.95 = 1 - \exp(-W)$$

$$\exp(-W) = 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = -\ln(0.05) = 2.996$$

**Step 3 — Required Wash Volume:**

$$V_w = W \cdot V_L = 2.996 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.348 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2$$

$$\boxed{V_w \approx 0.0135 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 = 13.5 \text{ L/m}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Effluent Concentration at W = 2:**

$$\frac{C}{C_0} = \exp(-2) = 0.1353$$

$$C = 0.1353 \times 60 = 8.12 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain, with a clearly labelled washing curve, the **three characteristic regions** observed during a real industrial cake-washing operation. Discuss the physical phenomena responsible for each region and their relative importance in equipment design.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A rotary vacuum filter handles a slurry that yields a cake 6 mm thick with $\varepsilon = 0.38$ and solids density $\rho_s = 2200 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The mother liquor has a solute concentration of $C_0 = 120 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The cake is washed using **two equal stages of counter-current washing** with a total wash ratio $W_{\text{total}} = 2.0$. Assuming each stage behaves as a perfect mixer, calculate the **overall fractional recovery**.

**Model Solution (a):**

The three regions of the real washing curve (as plotted in Section 4.4) are:

**Region I — Bulk Displacement:**
- The wash liquid physically displaces mother liquor from the cake pores.
- The concentration $C$ in the effluent drops **sharply** from $C_0$ to very low values.
- Dominates in the **first 0.5 to 1.0 wash volumes**.
- This region is the **most economically efficient** — bulk recovery with minimum water.

**Region II — Transition:**
- The remaining mother liquor is trapped in **dead-end pores, particle interiors, and stagnant pockets**.
- Both displacement and diffusion contribute.
- The concentration decay becomes **smoother** and more gradual.

**Region III — Diffusion Tail:**
- The remaining solute migrates by **molecular diffusion** from particle interiors.
- The concentration decays **exponentially** with a long tail.
- Most **water-inefficient** region; small incremental recovery per unit wash water.

In **equipment design**, we maximise Region I efficiency by ensuring **uniform cake thickness, no cracking, low-viscosity wash liquid, and proper spray coverage**. We minimise Region III by using **counter-current multi-stage washing** to recover the small residual solutes economically.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $L_c = 6 \text{ mm} = 0.006 \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon = 0.38$
- $C_0 = 120 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $W_{\text{total}} = 2.0$, $N = 2$ equal stages

**Step 1 — Stage Wash Ratio:**

Each stage receives an equal share of the total wash:

$$W_{\text{stage}} = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{N} = \frac{2.0}{2} = 1.0$$

**Step 2 — Single-Stage Recovery (Perfect Mixing):**

For one stage with $W = 1.0$:

$$R_{\text{single}} = 1 - \exp(-1.0) = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321$$

**Step 3 — Two-Stage Counter-Current Recovery Formula:**

For $N$ equal perfect-mixing stages in series:

$$R_N = 1 - \left( \frac{1}{1 + W_{\text{stage}}} \right)^N = 1 - \left( \frac{1}{1 + 1} \right)^2 = 1 - (0.5)^2 = 1 - 0.25$$

$$\boxed{R_2 = 0.75 \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad 75 \% \text{ overall recovery}}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Single-Stage Same Total Wash:**

If the entire $W = 2$ were used in a single stage:

$$R_1 = 1 - \exp(-2) = 0.8647 \quad (86.5 \%)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive Result:** The single-stage operation with the **same total wash ratio** actually gives higher recovery (86.5 %) than two stages (75 %). This is because, in the **mixing model**, splitting the wash reduces the concentration driving force in each stage. The advantage of counter-current washing emerges in the **displacement-dominant regime** or when the total wash is much larger. This nuance is a **favourite KTU trick question**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Cake-Washing Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $V_L$ first** — Many students plug wash volumes directly into the recovery formula without converting to wash ratio $W$. **Always convert $V_w$ to $W$ before applying $R = 1 - \exp(-W)$.**
> 2. **Mixing the two models** — In a single numerical problem, the student is asked to compute recovery under BOTH the displacement and mixing models. Using the wrong formula in either sub-part loses full marks.
> 3. **Units of $L_c$** — Cake thickness is almost always in **mm** in industry, but the formula requires **metres**. A factor of 1000 error here cascades through the entire problem.
> 4. **Sign convention in $\ln$** — Using $W = \ln(1 - R)$ without the negative sign gives a meaningless negative wash ratio. Always write $W = -\ln(1 - R)$.
> 5. **Counter-current vs. co-current confusion** — The multi-stage formula $R_N = 1 - (1/(1+W/N))^N$ applies to **counter-current** cascades. For **co-current** equal stages, the formula is different and gives lower recovery.
> 6. **Ignoring the diffusion tail** — In real problems with "achieve X % recovery", remember that the **last 5 %** (e.g., 95 % → 100 %) requires disproportionately large wash water. Always compute and report the cost-benefit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Definition:** Cake washing = displacement + diffusion of mother liquor from filter cake using pure wash liquid.
- **Wash Ratio:** $W = V_w / V_L$ — dimensionless, primary design variable.
- **Pore Volume:** $V_L = \varepsilon \cdot V_c = \varepsilon \cdot A \cdot L_c$ — compute this first in every numerical.
- **Perfect Mixing Model:** $R = 1 - \exp(-W)$ and $C/C_0 = \exp(-W)$.
- **Ideal Displacement Model:** $R = \min(W, 1)$ — benchmark theoretical maximum.
- **Diagnostic Plot:** $\ln(1 - R)$ vs. $W$ — straight line of slope $-1$ indicates perfect mixing.
- **Péclet Number:** High $\Rightarrow$ plug flow; Low $\Rightarrow$ mixed flow.
- **Three Regions of Real Washing Curve:** Bulk displacement (steep) → Transition (moderate) → Diffusion tail (shallow asymptote).
- **Multi-Stage Counter-Current:** $R_N = 1 - (1/(1 + W/N))^N$ for equal perfect-mixing stages.
- **Practical Wash Ratios:** $W = 1.3$ to $2.5$ in industry for single-stage; lower per-stage ratios used in multi-stage.
- **Wash Liquid Properties Matter:** Viscosity ratio, density, surface tension, and solute diffusivity all affect performance.
- **Equipment-Specific Behaviour:** Rotary drum filters (spray under vacuum), filter presses (through-wash), centrifuges (reslurry wash), belt filters (multi-spray zones).
- **Industrial Examples:** Bayer process (NaOH), sugar refining, API crystallisation, kaolin washing.
- **Key Conversion:** Cake thickness is invariably given in **mm** — convert to **metres** before computing pore volume.
- **Master Two Equations:** $R = 1 - \exp(-W)$ and $W = -\ln(1 - R)$ — these two are the workhorses of every KTU numerical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Centrifugal Filtration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Centrifugal Filtration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# PRINCIPLES OF CENTRIFUGAL FILTRATION — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Centrifugal Filtration** is a unit operation in which a slurry is rotated at high angular velocity inside a perforated bowl (basket) so that the resulting **centrifugal force** acts as the driving potential for separating the liquid filtrate from the solid cake. The slurry is retained against the perforated wall, the filtrate passes radially inward (or more commonly, the cake is retained and the filtrate escapes through perforations) under the influence of the apparent body force, while the solids are retained as a porous cake on the filter medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor**
> In a *perforated-basket centrifuge*, the filtrate flow is driven by the centrifugal pressure head, which is an exact analogue of the applied pressure in a conventional filter press. The pressure analogue is termed the **Equivalent Centrifugal Pressure (P_e)**.

Mathematically, the driving force is expressed as:

$$\Delta P_{centrifuge} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^{2}\,\bigl(r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2}\bigr)$$

where **ρ** is the filtrate density, **ω** is the angular velocity in **rad·s⁻¹**, **r₂** is the outer radius of the cake (at the basket wall) and **r₁** is the inner radius of the cake (at the slurry-cake interface).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Spin-Dryer**
> Imagine a wet towel inside a washing machine's spin basket. As the drum spins, water is *thrown outward* through the holes in the drum. You do not need any pump or pressurized air — the rotation itself creates an effective "push." A basket centrifuge works on exactly the same idea, but it is engineered to capture the solid particles as a uniform cake while letting the liquid phase (filtrate) drain out.

**Intuitive breakdown for first-time learners:**

| Stage | What the Eye Sees | What is Physically Happening |
|---|---|---|
| Slurry loading | A thick, opaque liquid | Solid + liquid mixture enters the basket |
| Cake formation | A solid layer is deposited on the wall | Solids are pinned by the centrifugal pseudo-force |
| Filtration | Clear liquid drips out of the holes | Liquid is forced through the cake & screen |
| Cake washing (optional) | Wash liquid displaces mother liquor | Replacement of entrained mother liquor |

**Physical constants and standard metrics for KTU problems:**

- Standard filtrate density (water-like) — **ρ = 1000 kg·m⁻³**
- Standard cake density (typical CaCO₃) — **ρ_c ≈ 1500–2500 kg·m⁻³**
- Operating ω range — **100 to 600 rad·s⁻¹** (≈1000–6000 rpm)
- Typical cake thickness — **0.5 to 10 cm**

---

## 1.3 Where Centrifugal Filtration is Used (KTU Industrial Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Application Domains**
> 1. Dewatering of crystals (sugar, common salt, ammonium sulphate)
> 2. Dewatering of fibrous mass (paper pulp, textile fibres)
> 3. Recovery of solids from slurries in pharmaceutical & food industries
> 4. Pre-thickening step before drying (thermal energy saving)
> 5. Separation of immiscible liquid–liquid systems (when used as a settler)

The **three governing physical variables** that define the operation are: **(i) angular velocity ω, (ii) basket radius r₂, (iii) cake thickness (r₂ − r₁)**. Manipulating these gives the engineer direct control over throughput and cake dryness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The centrifugal filter operates in a **cyclic batch mode** consisting of the following stages:

> 1. **Slurry Charging** — A measured volume of slurry is fed into the rotating basket. The angular velocity is raised to operating speed.
> 2. **Cake Build-up** — Solids are thrown outward and deposited on the perforated cylindrical wall, building a porous cake of increasing thickness.
> 3. **Filtration Phase** — Liquid (filtrate) drains through the cake and out of the perforations under the equivalent centrifugal pressure.
> 4. **Cake Washing (Optional)** — Wash liquid is sprayed onto the cake to displace the entrained mother liquor.
> 5. **Cake Discharge** — The basket decelerates; a peeler blade shaves the cake out, or the cake is discharged hydraulically.

### The "Why" and "How" Behind the Driving Force

Consider an elemental annular ring of filtrate at radius **r** and thickness **dr** rotating with the basket. The body force per unit volume is **ρ·ω²·r** directed radially outward. Integrating this from the inner cake surface (r₁) to the perforated wall (r₂) gives the total pressure head pushing the filtrate through the cake. The result is **½·ρ·ω²·(r₂² − r₁²)** — this is the **Equivalent Centrifugal Pressure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Link to Pressure Filtration**
> The filtration equation for a *pressure filter* and a *centrifugal filter* are mathematically identical — only the driving ΔP differs. The substitution
> **ΔP → ½·ρ·ω²·(r₂² − r₁²)**
> is the central trick used in KTU derivations.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU 2024 examination-ready**. They have been checked for dimensional consistency and the symbols follow the textbook convention.

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal force per unit mass | $F/m \;=\; \omega^{2} r$ | m·s⁻² | Pseudo-acceleration |
| 2 | Equivalent pressure | $P_{e} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^{2}\,(r_{2}^{2}-r_{1}^{2})$ | Pa (N·m⁻²) | The centrifugal driving force |
| 3 | Angular velocity | $\omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad·s⁻¹ | N in revolutions per minute |
| 4 | Cake thickness | $\ell_{c} \;=\; r_{2}-r_{1}$ | m | Cylindrical geometry |
| 5 | Cake resistance | $R_{c} \;=\; \alpha\,c\,\dfrac{V}{A}$ | m⁻¹ | α = specific cake resistance (m·kg⁻¹) |
| 6 | Specific cake resistance (Kozeny–Carman) | $\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{K\,(1-\varepsilon_{s})}{\rho_{s}\,\varepsilon_{s}^{3}\,d_{p}^{2}}$ | m·kg⁻¹ | K = 5 (Kozeny constant) |
| 7 | Filtration rate (general) | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \dfrac{A^{2}\,P_{e}}{\mu\,( \alpha c V + R_{m}A )}$ | m³·s⁻¹ | c = mass solid per vol. filtrate |
| 8 | Filtration rate (R_m ≪ R_c) | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \dfrac{A^{3}\,P_{e}}{\mu\,\alpha\,c\,V}$ | m³·s⁻¹ | Simplified form for thick cake |
| 9 | Integrated form (R_m negligible) | $V^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,A^{3}\,P_{e}\,t}{\mu\,\alpha\,c}$ | m⁶ | Direct KTU result |
| 10 | Filter area (basket) | $A \;=\; 2\pi r_{2} h$ | m² | h = basket height |
| 11 | Mass of cake | $m_{c} \;=\; cV \;=\; \rho_{s}\,(1-\varepsilon_{s})\,\pi\,(r_{2}^{2}-r_{1}^{2})\,h$ | kg | Cake mass balance |
| 12 | Relation to pressure filter | $\dfrac{P_{e}}{\Delta P_{press}} \;=\; \dfrac{t_{press}}{t_{cent}}$ | dimensionless | When A, V, slurry identical |

> **Notation Key** — A = filtration area (m²), V = cumulative filtrate volume (m³), μ = filtrate viscosity (Pa·s), c = mass of solid per unit filtrate volume (kg·m⁻³), R_m = medium resistance (m⁻¹), α = specific cake resistance (m·kg⁻¹), ρ_s = solid particle density, ε_s = cake porosity, d_p = particle diameter.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Equations

> [!TIP]
> **Why the KTU examiner loves this topic**
> 1. It links **mechanical separation** (Module 2) with **pressure filtration** (Module 1) — a favourite integration question.
> 2. It involves a **transformation** of an existing formula — testing the student's ability to *substitute* intelligently, not just memorise.
> 3. The numerical problems are **solvable in 4–5 steps** but require careful unit conversion (rpm → rad·s⁻¹).

The equations enable engineers to:
- **Scale up** lab basket tests to industrial filters.
- **Predict filtrate volume** for a given operating time and rpm.
- **Compare** centrifugal and pressure filtration on an *equivalent pressure basis* — a fair engineering comparison.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Derivation Anchor**
> The following two derivations are the *only* derivations required at the KTU 2024 examination level for Module 3. They are presented in exhaustive form, leaving no intermediate step unjustified.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expression for the Equivalent Centrifugal Pressure

**Physical setup:** A cylindrical cake of inner radius **r₁** and outer radius **r₂** rotates with the basket at angular velocity **ω**. The filtrate is modelled as an incompressible Newtonian fluid of density **ρ**.

**Step 1 — Write the centrifugal pseudo-force per unit volume on a fluid element at radius r**

For a fluid element of volume dV located at radius r, the centrifugal force is:

$$dF_{c} \;=\; (\rho\,dV)\,(\omega^{2}\,r)$$

The body force per unit volume is therefore:

$$f_{c}(r) \;=\; \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r$$

**Step 2 — Relate body force to pressure gradient**

For a fluid in mechanical equilibrium under rotation, the radial pressure gradient balances the centrifugal body force:

$$\frac{dP}{dr} \;=\; \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r$$

> **Why this is true:** In a rotating reference frame, the apparent body force (centrifugal) is balanced by the radial pressure gradient at every point. This is a *steady-state* force balance on the fluid.

**Step 3 — Integrate from r₁ to r₂ to obtain the total pressure drop across the cake**

$$\int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} dP \;=\; \int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r\,dr$$

$$P_{2} - P_{1} \;=\; \rho\,\omega^{2}\,\Bigl[\tfrac{r^{2}}{2}\Bigr]_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Write the final result**

$$\boxed{\;P_{e} \;=\; P_{2} - P_{1} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^{2}\,\bigl(r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2}\bigr)\;}$$

> **Units check:** kg·m⁻³ × (rad·s⁻¹)² × m² = kg·m⁻¹·s⁻² = Pa ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Centrifugal Filtration Equation (Constant Pressure Analogue)

**Starting point — The general filtration equation (from Module 1):**

$$\frac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \frac{A\,\Delta P}{\mu\,(R_{c}+R_{m})}$$

For cake filtration, the cake resistance is given by:

$$R_{c} \;=\; \alpha\,c\,\frac{V}{A}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the cake-resistance expression into the filtration equation**

$$\frac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \frac{A\,\Delta P}{\mu\,\bigl(\alpha\,c\,V/A + R_{m}\bigr)}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by A:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\,\Delta P}{\mu\,\bigl(\alpha\,c\,V + R_{m}\,A\bigr)}$$

**Step 2 — Replace ΔP with the Equivalent Centrifugal Pressure P_e**

$$\frac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\,P_{e}}{\mu\,\bigl(\alpha\,c\,V + R_{m}\,A\bigr)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the simplifying assumption valid for basket centrifuges**

In most centrifugal filtration operations, the cake builds up rapidly and the medium resistance becomes negligible compared to the cake resistance. So we set **R_m ≈ 0**:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\,P_{e}}{\mu\,\alpha\,c\,V}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to a separable form**

$$V\,dV \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\,P_{e}}{\mu\,\alpha\,c}\,dt$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from 0 to V and 0 to t**

$$\int_{0}^{V} V\,dV \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\,P_{e}}{\mu\,\alpha\,c}\int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\frac{V^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\,P_{e}}{\mu\,\alpha\,c}\,t$$

**Step 6 — Final integrated form**

$$\boxed{\;V^{2} \;=\; \frac{2\,A^{2}\,P_{e}\,t}{\mu\,\alpha\,c}\;}$$

If we substitute P_e = ½·ρ·ω²·(r₂² − r₁²):

$$\boxed{\;V^{2} \;=\; \frac{A^{2}\,\rho\,\omega^{2}\,(r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2})\,t}{\mu\,\alpha\,c}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Centrifugal vs Pressure Filtration

> [!TIP]
> **This matrix is the highest-weightage analytical summary in Module 3.** Memorise it for the KTU 2024 examination.

| Attribute | Pressure Filter (Constant ΔP) | Centrifugal Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Pumping pressure ΔP | Centrifugal pseudo-force ½·ρ·ω²·(r₂² − r₁²) |
| Cake formation direction | Perpendicular to filter medium | On the cylindrical basket wall |
| Filtration area | Constant (for plate-frame) | Effectively constant for thin cake |
| V²–t relationship | $V^{2} + 2VV_{e} = \dfrac{2A^{2}\Delta P\,t}{\mu\alpha c}$ | $V^{2} = \dfrac{2A^{2}P_{e}\,t}{\mu\alpha c}$ |
| Power input | Pump | Motor (kinetic energy) |
| Typical operating ΔP | 1–10 bar (10⁵–10⁶ Pa) | 1–50 bar equivalent (10⁵–5×10⁶ Pa) |
| Cake dryness | Moderate | High (centrifugal dewatering continues) |
| Capital cost | Low–medium | Higher (rotating machinery) |
| Continuous operation | Easy (rotary drum) | Possible (conical or pusher) |
| Governing parameter | ΔP | ω (or N in rpm) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Equivalent Pressure Calculation

> **Given:** Basket diameter 0.50 m, cake thickness 0.02 m, N = 1500 rpm, ρ_filtrate = 1000 kg·m⁻³.

**Step 1 — Compute ω**

$$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} \;=\; 50\pi \;=\; 157.08\;\text{rad·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute radii**

$$r_{2} \;=\; \frac{0.50}{2} \;=\; 0.25\;\text{m} \qquad r_{1} \;=\; 0.25 - 0.02 \;=\; 0.23\;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the equivalent pressure formula**

$$P_{e} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^{2}\,(r_{2}^{2}-r_{1}^{2})$$

$$P_{e} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\times 1000\times (157.08)^{2}\times\bigl(0.25^{2}-0.23^{2}\bigr)$$

$$P_{e} \;=\; 500\times 24\,674\times(0.0625 - 0.0529)$$

$$P_{e} \;=\; 500\times 24\,674\times 0.0096$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{e} \;\approx\; 1.184\times 10^{5}\;\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 118.4\;\text{kPa}\;}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
