# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define centrifugal filtration. Write the expression for the equivalent centrifugal pressure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Centrifugal filtration is a solid–liquid separation process in which slurry is rotated in a perforated basket at high angular velocity. The centrifugal pseudo-force drives the liquid (filtrate) through the cake and the perforated wall, while the solids are retained as a cake on the basket wall.

**Equivalent pressure expression (1 mark):**

$$P_{e} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^{2}\,\bigl(r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2}\bigr)$$

where ρ = filtrate density (kg·m⁻³), ω = angular velocity (rad·s⁻¹), r₂ = outer cake radius (m), r₁ = inner cake radius (m).

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Distinguish between centrifugal filtration and pressure filtration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Pressure Filter | Centrifugal Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | External pressure ΔP from a pump | Centrifugal pseudo-force P_e = ½ρω²(r₂² − r₁²) |
| Equipment | Static plates / frames | Rotating perforated basket |
| Cake geometry | Planar | Cylindrical annular |
| Energy source | Hydraulic (pump) | Mechanical (motor + rotation) |

(Any 3 valid points × 1 mark each = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) Derive the equation for the equivalent centrifugal pressure for a basket centrifuge, starting from the basic force balance on a fluid element.** (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Statement of the physical model (1 mark):**
   Consider a cylindrical cake with inner radius r₁ and outer radius r₂, rotating at angular velocity ω. The filtrate flows radially outward through the cake.

2. **Force balance on an elemental volume (2 marks):**
   The centrifugal force per unit volume on a fluid element of density ρ at radius r is:
   $$f_{c}(r) \;=\; \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r$$
   At steady state, the radial pressure gradient balances this:
   $$\frac{dP}{dr} \;=\; \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r$$

3. **Integration across the cake (2 marks):**
   $$\int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} dP \;=\; \int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} \rho\,\omega^{2}\,r\,dr$$
   $$P_{2} - P_{1} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^{2}\,(r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2})$$

4. **Final result and units verification (2 marks):**
   $$P_{e} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\omega^{2}\,(r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2}) \quad \text{[Pa]}$$
   The driving force for centrifugal filtration is therefore directly proportional to ρ, ω² and the difference of squares of the outer and inner cake radii.

---

**(b) A basket centrifuge of diameter 50 cm and height 40 cm operates at 1500 rpm. The cake thickness is 2 cm. The filtrate density is 1000 kg·m⁻³. Calculate (i) the equivalent centrifugal pressure, and (ii) the filtration area. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given:** d = 0.50 m, h = 0.40 m, N = 1500 rpm, cake thickness ℓ_c = 0.02 m, ρ = 1000 kg·m⁻³.

**Step 1 — Compute ω (1 mark):**

$$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} \;=\; 50\pi \;=\; 157.08\;\text{rad·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute radii (1 mark):**

$$r_{2} \;=\; \frac{0.50}{2} \;=\; 0.25\;\text{m}$$

$$r_{1} \;=\; r_{2} - \ell_{c} \;=\; 0.25 - 0.02 \;=\; 0.23\;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute equivalent pressure (3 marks):**

$$P_{e} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\times 1000\times (157.08)^{2}\times\bigl(0.25^{2}-0.23^{2}\bigr)$$

$$P_{e} \;=\; 500\times 24\,674\times(0.0625 - 0.0529)$$

$$P_{e} \;=\; 500\times 24\,674\times 0.0096$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{e} \;\approx\; 1.184\times 10^{5}\;\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 118.4\;\text{kPa}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Compute filtration area (2 marks):**

$$A \;=\; 2\pi r_{2} h \;=\; 2\pi\times 0.25\times 0.40 \;=\; 0.6283\;\text{m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;A \;\approx\; 0.628\;\text{m}^{2}\;}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a) With a neat schematic, explain the principle of operation of a perforated-basket centrifuge. List the variables that control the equivalent pressure.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Diagram description (3 marks):** A vertical cylindrical basket with perforated walls rotates about its vertical axis. Slurry is fed from the top; the centrifugal force throws solids outward to form an annular cake on the perforated wall. Filtrate escapes through the perforations and is collected in the outer casing.

2. **Operating sequence (2 marks):** Charging → Cake formation → Filtration → Optional washing → Discharge (peeler blade).

3. **Variables controlling P_e (2 marks):**
   - Filtrate density ρ
   - Angular velocity ω (square dependence)
   - Outer cake radius r₂
   - Inner cake radius r₁

   The dependence is **square** on ω, so doubling rpm increases P_e by 4×.

---

**(b) The same slurry gives 10 L of filtrate in 5 min in a basket centrifuge, and 10 L of filtrate in 15 min in a plate-and-frame filter operated at a constant pressure of 50 kPa. Estimate the equivalent pressure in the centrifuge. Assume the filter area and cake resistance are the same in both cases.** (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given:** V = 10 L (same in both), t_c = 5 min = 300 s, t_p = 15 min = 900 s, ΔP_p = 50 kPa.

**Step 1 — State the integrated filtration law (1 mark):**

For constant pressure with negligible medium resistance:

$$V^{2} \;=\; \frac{2A^{2}\Delta P\,t}{\mu\alpha c}$$

So **V² ∝ ΔP · t** (with all other terms constant).

**Step 2 — Equate V² for the two cases (3 marks):**

$$V_{c}^{2} \;=\; K\,P_{e}\,t_{c} \quad\text{and}\quad V_{p}^{2} \;=\; K\,\Delta P_{p}\,t_{p}$$

Since V_c = V_p (same filtrate volume):

$$P_{e}\,t_{c} \;=\; \Delta P_{p}\,t_{p}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for P_e (2 marks):**

$$P_{e} \;=\; \Delta P_{p}\times\frac{t_{p}}{t_{c}} \;=\; 50\;\text{kPa}\times\frac{900}{300}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{e} \;=\; 150\;\text{kPa}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation (1 mark):** The centrifugal filter, although not applying any "pump pressure," is generating an effective pressure of 150 kPa — three times the pressure filter's ΔP. This is the key advantage of high-speed centrifuges.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm → rad·s⁻¹.** A large majority of students lose 1–2 marks by writing ω = 1500 instead of ω = 157.08. *Always* write $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
> 2. **Mixing up r₁ and r₂.** r₂ is the **outer** cake radius (at the basket wall) — the larger of the two. r₁ is the **inner** cake radius (slurry–cake interface).
> 3. **Dropping the factor ½.** The integral of r is r²/2; do not omit the half.
> 4. **Comparing P_e and ΔP_p incorrectly.** When the same slurry/area is used, $P_{e}/\Delta P_{p} = t_{p}/t_{c}$, **not** $t_{c}/t_{p}$.
> 5. **Unit mismatch in cake thickness.** Cake thickness is in **metres**, not centimetres. Convert before substitution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final-minute revision notes — 60-second scan**

- ✅ **Core equation to memorise:** $P_{e} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\omega^{2}(r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2})$
- ✅ **Filtration law:** $V^{2} = \dfrac{2A^{2}P_{e}t}{\mu\alpha c}$ (R_m negligible)
- ✅ **Filter area for basket:** $A = 2\pi r_{2} h$ (cylindrical, not end caps)
- ✅ **Angular velocity conversion:** $\omega = 2\pi N/60$
- ✅ **Centrifugal filtration is a *batch* operation** (unless using pusher / scroll types)
- ✅ **Cake builds radially inward** (from r₂ inward to r₁)
- ✅ **Filtrate density ρ** (not slurry density) is used in P_e.
- ✅ **Doubling ω quadruples P_e** (square-law dependence — most powerful lever).
- ✅ **Higher r₂** for a given ω gives higher P_e (centrifuges scale favourably with diameter).
- ✅ **Comparison rule:** $P_{e}/\Delta P_{p} = t_{p}/t_{c}$ when slurry, V, A are identical.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Centrifugal filtration is **not** suitable for very fine particles (they clog perforations) or for slurries with very low solid content.
- ✅ **Key application:** Dewatering of crystals (sugar, salt, fertilizer).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Separation of Solids from Gases: Cyclone Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Separation of Solids from Gases: Cyclone Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cyclone Separator** is a mechanical device that uses **centrifugal force**, generated by a **tangentially injected gas stream**, to separate solid or liquid particulate matter from a carrier gas without the use of filters. The device operates on the principle of **inertial separation**, where particles denser than the surrounding gas are forced radially outward against the cyclone wall, lose their kinetic energy through friction, and slide down into a collection hopper under gravity, while the cleaned gas spirals upward through a central vortex tube (vortex finder) and exits through the top.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The cyclone separator is the **most important** gas-solid separation device in the syllabus. Students must master: (1) constructional features, (2) working principle with flow pattern, (3) the **cut-size (d₅₀) equation**, and (4) the **overall collection efficiency (Grade Efficiency)**. Questions on this topic consistently appear for **14 marks** in KTU ESE.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Spinning Tea Cup Analogy"**
> 
> Imagine swirling a cup of tea leaves in water. The heavier tea leaves move **outward** to the walls of the cup, while the lighter water circulates in the middle. A cyclone does **exactly this** with air and dust:
> - The **dust-laden air** enters the cylindrical body **tangentially** (just like stirring the cup).
> - A **spinning vortex** is created inside — the air goes down on the outside, reaches the cone, reverses direction, and **exits through the central pipe** at the top (clean gas).
> - The **heavier dust particles**, unable to follow the tight gas streamlines, are flung to the wall by **centrifugal force** and fall down into the dust collection bin.
> - **In simple words:** Lighter = gas; Heavier = dust. The trick is making the air spin fast enough that the dust "feels" a much heavier weight and slams into the wall.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range (Industrial) |
|---|---|---|
| Inlet Velocity | $v_i$ | **15 m/s to 30 m/s** (optimum ≈ 15 m/s for efficiency) |
| Particle Density | $\rho_p$ | **1000 kg/m³ – 5000 kg/m³** |
| Gas Density | $\rho$ | **1.2 kg/m³** (air at NTP) |
| Gas Viscosity | $\mu$ | **1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** (air at NTP) |
| Number of Effective Turns | $N_e$ | **5 turns** (Stairmand), **5 turns** (Lapple) |
| Cyclone Body Diameter | $D$ | **0.1 m to 1.0 m** for single units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is inlet velocity bounded?** Below **15 m/s** → insufficient centrifugal force (poor collection). Above **30 m/s** → excessive turbulence re-entrains collected dust from the wall (back-mixing), **decreasing** efficiency. This is a classic KTU viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Features of a Standard Cyclone

A cyclone separator consists of **five** essential parts:

1. **Cylindrical Body (Barrel)** — Where primary separation occurs; tangential entry forces vortex.
2. **Conical Section (Cone)** — Enhances centrifugal action; particles slide down the narrowing walls.
3. **Inlet (Tangent Box / Volute)** — Rectangular duct delivering gas tangentially. Width = $B$, Height = $a$.
4. **Outlet Pipe (Vortex Finder)** — Central vertical tube extending into the cylinder for clean gas exit. Diameter = $S$.
5. **Dust Outlet (Solid Discharge / Hopper)** — At the bottom cone tip, connected to a collection bin. Diameter = $B'$.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1: Tangential Injection.** Dust-laden gas enters the cylindrical body through the tangential inlet. The geometry converts linear gas momentum into a **spiral (helical) flow pattern**.
- **Step 2: Double-Vortex Formation.** A **primary (downward) vortex** forms along the wall (carrying heavy dust), and a **secondary (upward) vortex** forms along the central axis (carrying cleaned gas). The two are separated by a **cylindrical surface of zero vertical velocity**.
- **Step 3: Centrifugal Throw-Out.** Each particle experiences a centrifugal force $F_c = m_p v^2/r$ acting radially outward, while aerodynamic drag pulls it inward. For particles above the **cut-size**, the centrifugal force dominates → they hit the wall.
- **Step 4: Wall Sliding and Collection.** Once at the wall, friction slows the particles; gravity pulls them down the cone into the dust hopper.
- **Step 5: Vortex Finder Exit.** Cleaned gas reverses direction at the cone tip and escapes upward through the vortex finder with minimal dust carryover.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Geometry:** The **cone shape** is not decorative — by reducing the radius as gas descends, the **tangential velocity increases** (by conservation of angular momentum), boosting centrifugal force on finer particles.

## 2.3 Standard Geometric Ratios (KTU Critical Data)

### **A. Stairmand High-Efficiency Cyclone** (Most Tested in KTU)

$$
a = \frac{D}{4}, \quad b = \frac{D}{4}, \quad S = \frac{D}{2}, \quad h_c = \frac{D}{2}, \quad H = 2D, \quad B' = \frac{D}{4}
$$

### **B. Lapple Standard Cyclone** (Common KTU Variant)

$$
a = \frac{D}{2}, \quad b = \frac{D}{4}, \quad S = \frac{D}{2}, \quad h_c = 2D, \quad H = 4D, \quad B' = \frac{D}{4}
$$

(Here $D$ = cyclone body diameter, $a$ = inlet height, $b$ = inlet width, $S$ = vortex finder diameter, $h_c$ = cylindrical height, $H$ = total height, $B'$ = dust outlet diameter.)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c = \dfrac{m_p \, v^2}{r}$ | Centrifugal force on particle | N |
| 2 | $F_d = 3\pi \mu d_p v_r$ | Stokes' drag (radial velocity $v_r$) | N |
| 3 | $d_{p50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9\mu \, B}{2\pi \, N_e \, v_i \, (\rho_p - \rho)}}$ | **CUT-SIZE** (50% collection diameter) — Lapple equation | m |
| 4 | $\eta_d = 1 - \exp\left[-2\,(d_p/d_{p50})\right]$ for **Lapple** | Fractional (grade) efficiency — small particles | — |
| 5 | $\eta_d = \left(\dfrac{d_p}{d_{p50}}\right)^2$ for **Stairmand** | Fractional (grade) efficiency — common KTU form | — |
| 6 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, v_i^2 \left(1 + 2\phi^2\right)\dfrac{H}{H-h_c}$ | Pressure drop (Stairmand form) | Pa |
| 7 | $\Delta P = \xi \,\dfrac{\rho\, v_i^2}{2}$ | Simplified pressure drop (ξ = friction factor, typically 4–8) | Pa |
| 8 | $\dfrac{v_r}{v_t} = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) v_t}{18 \mu r}$ | Radial-to-tangential velocity ratio (Stokes regime) | — |
| 9 | $N_e = 5$ | Effective number of turns (universal KTU default) | — |
| 10 | $Q = v_i \times B \times a = v_i \times \dfrac{D^2}{16}$ | Gas volumetric flow rate (Stairmand) | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for Grade Efficiency Equations:**
> - For $d_p > d_{p50}$ → $\eta_d = 1$ (100% collection, **cannot exceed 1**).
> - For $d_p < d_{p50}$ → use the appropriate correlation (Lapple or Stairmand) above.
> - Always **state clearly** which correlation (Lapple OR Stairmand) you are using — KTU examiners deduct marks otherwise.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cyclone separators are ubiquitous in:
- **Cement industry** — pre-heater tower dust recovery.
- **Power plants** — fly-ash collection from boiler flue gas.
- **Pulp & paper** — lime kiln dust recovery.
- **Food processing** — pneumatic conveying line receivers.
- **Spray dryers** — product recovery.
- **Environmental engineering** — first-stage particulate control before bag filters/ESP.

> They are **cheap**, have **no moving parts**, can handle **high temperatures** and **corrosive dusts**, but have **low efficiency for sub-10 µm particles** (typically 50–80% overall on industrial dusts).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cut-Size (d₅₀) Equation — Lapple/Stairmand Model

The derivation balances **centrifugal force** against **Stokes' drag force** on a particle tracing a **radial equilibrium orbit** at the cyclone wall.

### Step 1: Identify the Force Balance

A particle at radius $r$ in the cyclone experiences:
- **Outward:** Centrifugal force $F_c = \dfrac{m_p v_t^2}{r}$
- **Inward:** Stokes' drag force $F_d = 3\pi \mu d_p v_r$

where $v_t$ = tangential velocity of the gas, $v_r$ = radial drift velocity of the particle, $m_p = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3 \rho_p}{6}$.

### Step 2: State Equilibrium Condition

At the **cut-size**, the particle just reaches the wall (radius $r = D/2$) and is collected with **50% probability**. Equating forces:

$$
\frac{\pi d_p^3 \rho_p \, v_t^2}{6 \, r} = 3\pi \mu d_p v_r
$$

### Step 3: Solve for Radial Drift Velocity

$$
v_r = \frac{d_p^2 \rho_p \, v_t^2}{18 \mu \, r}
$$

### Step 4: Relate Radial Drift to Cyclone Geometry

The particle must travel across the inlet width $B$ to reach the wall. Over the residence time of the gas (which makes $N_e$ effective revolutions), the radial drift is:

$$
v_r = \frac{B}{T_{res}}
$$

The tangential gas velocity at the wall is approximated as $v_t \approx v_i$ (the inlet velocity), and the residence time relates to $N_e$ revolutions as:

$$
T_{res} = \frac{2\pi r \, N_e}{v_i} = \frac{\pi D N_e}{v_i}
$$

Therefore:

$$
v_r = \frac{B \, v_i}{\pi D N_e}
$$

### Step 5: Equate the Two Expressions for $v_r$

$$
\frac{d_p^2 \rho_p \, v_i^2}{18 \mu \, r} = \frac{B \, v_i}{\pi D N_e}
$$

Substitute $r = D/2$:

$$
\frac{d_p^2 \rho_p \, v_i^2}{18 \mu \cdot (D/2)} = \frac{B \, v_i}{\pi D N_e}
$$

$$
\frac{d_p^2 \rho_p \, v_i}{9 \mu} = \frac{B}{\pi N_e}
$$

### Step 6: Solve for the Cut-Size $d_{p50}$

$$
d_{p50}^2 = \frac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e v_i \rho_p}
$$

Including the **buoyancy correction** $(\rho_p - \rho)$ for rigorous flow:

$$
\boxed{\,d_{p50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu B}{2\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)}}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight from the equation:**
> - $d_{p50} \propto \sqrt{\mu}$ → higher viscosity → larger cut-size → **poorer separation**.
> - $d_{p50} \propto 1/\sqrt{v_i}$ → higher inlet velocity → **smaller cut-size** → better collection (until turbulence re-entrainment kicks in).
> - $d_{p50} \propto 1/\sqrt{\rho_p - \rho}$ → heavier particles → better separation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Pressure Drop (Simplified Energy Balance Form)

Apply **Bernoulli / mechanical energy balance** between inlet and outlet of the cyclone. The dominant loss is due to the vortex swirl (kinetic-energy dissipation).

**Energy balance per unit volume of gas:**

$$
\Delta P = \text{(kinetic energy of swirl)} + \text{(wall friction losses)}
$$

For an idealized free vortex, tangential velocity scales as $v_t \cdot r = $ constant. The integrated kinetic energy of rotation is:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_i^2 \left[1 + 2\phi^2 \right]
$$

where $\phi$ is an **inlet-shape factor**:
- $\phi = 0$ for ideal tangential inlet (theoretical minimum)
- $\phi \approx \pi/2$ for typical rectangular inlet
- $\phi \approx 0.5$ for volute scroll inlet

Multiplying by the height ratio $\dfrac{H}{H - h_c}$ accounts for exit loss recovery:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_i^2 \left(1 + 2\phi^2\right)\frac{H}{H-h_c}\,}
$$

For a simple **working KTU answer**, this is often written compactly as:

$$
\Delta P = \xi \, \frac{\rho v_i^2}{2}, \quad \xi \approx 4 \text{ to } 8
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** A Stairmand high-efficiency cyclone has body diameter $D = 0.6$ m. The inlet gas velocity is $v_i = 15$ m/s, gas viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, gas density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, particle density $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, inlet width $B = D/4 = 0.15$ m, and number of effective turns $N_e = 5$. Calculate:
- (a) The cut-size $d_{p50}$.
- (b) The grade efficiency for a particle of size $d_p = 5 \, \mu m$ using the Stairmand correlation.
- (c) The pressure drop across the cyclone (assume $\phi = \pi/2$, $H = 2D$, $h_c = D/2$).

### Solution (a): Cut-size

$$
d_{p50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu B}{2\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
d_{p50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \times (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.15}{2\pi \times 5 \times 15 \times (2500 - 1.2)}}
$$

$$
d_{p50} = \sqrt{\frac{2.43 \times 10^{-5}}{1.179 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{2.061 \times 10^{-11}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d_{p50} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 4.54 \, \mu m\,}
$$

### Solution (b): Grade Efficiency (Stairmand)

Since $d_p = 5 \, \mu m > d_{p50} = 4.54 \, \mu m$, the **Stairmand correlation** gives:

$$
\eta_d = \left(\frac{d_p}{d_{p50}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{5}{4.54}\right)^2 = (1.1013)^2 \approx 1.213
$$

**But efficiency cannot exceed 1!** So the **physical answer is**:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_d = 1.0 \;\;(\text{i.e., } 100\%)\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write the formula and forget the cap at $\eta_d = 1$. The Stairmand expression is **only valid for $d_p < d_{p50}$**. For $d_p \geq d_{p50}$, the answer is **exactly 1.0** — write this explicitly!

### Solution (c): Pressure Drop

$$
\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_i^2 \left(1 + 2\phi^2\right)\frac{H}{H-h_c}
$$

$$
\phi = \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \phi^2 = \frac{\pi^2}{4} \approx 2.467
$$

$$
1 + 2\phi^2 = 1 + 2(2.467) = 5.934
$$

$$
\frac{H}{H - h_c} = \frac{2D}{2D - D/2} = \frac{2D}{1.5D} = 1.333
$$

$$
\Delta P = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times (15)^2 \times 5.934 \times 1.333
$$

$$
\Delta P = 0.6 \times 225 \times 5.934 \times 1.333 \approx 1067.5 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta P \approx 1067.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.07 \text{ kPa}\,}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dust Laden Gas Inlet] --> B[Tangential Entry Into Cylinder]
    B --> C{Primary Vortex Forms Along Wall}
    C --> D[Particles Flung To Wall by Centrifugal Force]
    D --> E[Particles Slide Down Cone Wall]
    E --> F[Dust Hopper Collection]
    F --> G[Solids Discharge Outlet]
    C --> H[Secondary Vortex Reverses Direction at Cone Tip]
    H --> I[Cleaned Gas Rises Through Vortex Finder]
    I --> J[Clean Gas Outlet at Top]
    K[Inlet Width B] -.controls.-> C
    L[Inlet Velocity Vi] -.drives.-> C
    M[Cut Size d50] -.determines.-> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CycloneSeparator[CYCLONE SEPARATOR ASSEMBLY]
        direction TB
        A1[Tangential Inlet Duct Width B Height a] --> A2[Cylindrical Barrel Height hc]
        A2 --> A3[Conical Section Tapering to Dust Outlet]
        A3 --> A4[Dust Outlet Hopper Diameter B Prime]
        A2 --> A5[Central Vortex Finder Diameter S]
        A5 --> A6[Clean Gas Outlet Top]
    end
    P1[Gas Stream In] --> A1
    A6 --> P2[Clean Gas to Stack]
    A4 --> P3[Recovered Solids to Bin]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Force & Efficiency Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Inlet Velocity vi] --> Q2[Centrifugal Force Fc Increases]
    Q2 --> Q3[Cut Size d50 Decreases]
    Q3 --> Q4[Grade Efficiency Increases]
    R1[Particle Size dp] --> R2{Compare With d50}
    R2 -->|dp GREATER d50| R3[Eta d equals 1]
    R2 -->|dp LESS d50| R4[Apply Stairmand or Lapple Correlation]
    R4 --> R3
    S1[Gas Viscosity mu] --> S3[Cut Size d50 Increases]
    S3 --> S5[Grade Efficiency Decreases]
    S2[Particle Density rho p] --> S4[Cut Size d50 Decreases]
    S4 --> S6[Grade Efficiency Increases]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each arrow is a cause-and-effect chain used in design — increasing one variable (blue) shifts the cut-size and efficiency in a predictable way. KTU viva examiners commonly test these **trend questions**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a cyclone separator. State any two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A cyclone separator is a device used to separate solid or liquid particles from a gas stream by employing **centrifugal force** generated through **tangential gas entry** into a cylindrical-conical body. Particles are flung to the wall and collected in a hopper, while clean gas exits axially.

**Advantages (any two, 1 mark each):**
1. No moving parts → low maintenance, high reliability.
2. Can handle high-temperature and corrosive dusts.
3. Low capital cost compared to ESP / bag filters.

**Limitation (1 mark):**
Low efficiency for fine particles (below ~10 µm); typically used as a **pre-cleaner** before more efficient devices.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the "cut-size" (d₅₀) of a cyclone? Why is it called the "50% cut diameter"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **cut-size** ($d_{p50}$) is the diameter of a particle that has a **50% probability of being collected** by the cyclone. It is the most important single design parameter of a cyclone separator.
It is called the "50% cut diameter" because at this particle size, the **grade efficiency** $\eta_d$ equals exactly 0.5 — i.e., half of the particles of that size are collected, and half escape. Particles larger than $d_{p50}$ are collected with progressively higher efficiency (approaching 100%), while smaller ones are mostly lost.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working** of a cyclone separator. Describe the formation of the **double-vortex** flow pattern.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Lapple equation** for the cut-size ($d_{p50}$) of a cyclone separator, clearly stating all assumptions.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

- **Diagram (2 marks):** Draw and label all five parts — Inlet, Cylindrical body, Conical section, Vortex finder, Dust outlet.
- **Working description (3 marks):** Tangential entry → swirling motion → centrifugal throw-out of particles to the wall → particles slide down cone into hopper → clean gas reverses and exits through vortex finder.
- **Double vortex (2 marks):** Describe the **outer downward vortex** (carrying dust along the wall) and the **inner upward vortex** (clean gas through the vortex finder), separated by a **cylindrical surface of zero vertical velocity** (often called the "natural boundary").

**(b) Derivation of Lapple Equation (7 marks):**
1. State assumptions: Stokes' regime, equilibrium orbit at the wall, $N_e$ effective turns, $v_t \approx v_i$. (1 mark)
2. Write force balance: centrifugal = drag. (2 marks)
3. Express $v_r$ two ways (one from force balance, one from residence time across inlet width $B$). (2 marks)
4. Substitute and solve for $d_{p50}$. (1 mark)
5. Final boxed expression: $d_{p50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9\mu B}{2\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)}}$. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A Stairmand high-efficiency cyclone has a body diameter $D = 0.8$ m. The inlet gas velocity is $v_i = 20$ m/s, gas viscosity $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, gas density $\rho = 1.15$ kg/m³, particle density $\rho_p = 2700$ kg/m³, inlet width $B = 0.2$ m, and $N_e = 5$. Calculate:
1. The cut-size $d_{p50}$ in µm.
2. The grade efficiency for $d_p = 10 \, \mu m$ using the **Stairmand correlation**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Stairmand** and **Lapple** standard cyclone designs. State the pressure drop equation and discuss how pressure drop is influenced by **inlet velocity** and **inlet shape factor**.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Part 1 — Cut-size (4 marks):**

$$
d_{p50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu B}{2\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)}}
$$

$$
d_{p50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \times (1.85 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.2}{2\pi \times 5 \times 20 \times (2700 - 1.15)}}
$$

$$
d_{p50} = \sqrt{\frac{3.33 \times 10^{-5}}{1.696 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{1.963 \times 10^{-11}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d_{p50} \approx 4.43 \, \mu m\,}
$$

**Part 2 — Grade efficiency at $d_p = 10 \, \mu m$ (3 marks):**

Since $d_p = 10 \, \mu m > d_{p50} = 4.43 \, \mu m$, the Stairmand correlation gives:

$$
\eta_d = \left(\frac{d_p}{d_{p50}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{4.43}\right)^2 = (2.257)^2 \approx 5.09
$$

Since efficiency cannot exceed 1:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_d = 1.0 \;\; (\text{100\% collection})\,}
$$

**(b) Stairmand vs Lapple + Pressure Drop (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Stairmand (High Efficiency) | Lapple (Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| Inlet area | $a \times b = D/4 \times D/4$ | $a \times b = D/2 \times D/4$ |
| Vortex finder $S$ | $D/2$ | $D/2$ |
| Cylinder height $h_c$ | $D/2$ | $2D$ |
| Total height $H$ | $2D$ | $4D$ |
| Typical application | Fine dust | General purpose |

**Pressure drop equation (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_i^2 (1 + 2\phi^2) \frac{H}{H - h_c}
$$

- **Inlet velocity:** $\Delta P \propto v_i^2$ → doubling velocity quadruples pressure drop.
- **Inlet shape factor $\phi$:** Volute scroll ($\phi \approx 0.5$) gives lower $\Delta P$ than rectangular inlet ($\phi \approx \pi/2$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Cyclone Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cap at $\eta_d = 1$** when $d_p > d_{p50}$. — *Lose 1 mark.*
> 2. **Using wrong geometric ratios** — confusing Stairmand with Lapple dimensions. Always re-state which cyclone you are using. — *Lose 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Not mentioning $N_e = 5$** explicitly in derivations. — *Lose 1 mark.*
> 4. **Mixing up units** — cut-size must be reported in **µm**, not m, in the final answer. — *Lose 0.5 mark.*
> 5. **Pressure drop must include the height ratio** $\frac{H}{H - h_c}$, not just the swirl term. — *Lose 1 mark.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Principle:** Cyclone separation works on **centrifugal force** generated by **tangential entry** of dust-laden gas.
- [x] **Five parts:** Inlet, Cylindrical body, Cone, Vortex finder, Dust outlet.
- [x] **Two vortices:** Outer (downward, dust-laden) and inner (upward, clean gas).
- [x] **Optimum inlet velocity:** 15 m/s (industrial standard for efficiency vs pressure drop trade-off).
- [x] **Effective turns:** $N_e = 5$ (universally used KTU default).
- [x] **Master Formula (Cut-Size):** $d_{p50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{2\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho)}}$.
- [x] **Master Formula (Stairmand Grade Efficiency):** $\eta_d = \left(\dfrac{d_p}{d_{p50}}\right)^2$ for $d_p < d_{p50}$; $\eta_d = 1$ for $d_p \geq d_{p50}$.
- [x] **Master Formula (Lapple Grade Efficiency):** $\eta_d = 1 - \exp\left[-2(d_p/d_{p50})\right]$.
- [x] **Master Formula (Pressure Drop):** $\Delta P = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_i^2 (1 + 2\phi^2)\dfrac{H}{H-h_c}$.
- [x] **Stairmand dimensions:** $a=b=D/4, S=D/2, h_c=D/2, H=2D, B'=D/4$.
- [x] **Lapple dimensions:** $a=D/2, b=D/4, S=D/2, h_c=2D, H=4D, B'=D/4$.
- [x] **Effect of inlet velocity:** Higher $v_i$ → smaller $d_{p50}$ → better efficiency → higher $\Delta P$.
- [x] **Effect of particle density:** Higher $\rho_p$ → smaller $d_{p50}$ → better efficiency.
- [x] **Effect of gas viscosity:** Higher $\mu$ → larger $d_{p50}$ → poorer efficiency.
- [x] **Limitation:** Inefficient for sub-10 µm particles; hence used as a **pre-cleaner**.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Cement kilns, power-plant fly-ash, spray dryers, pneumatic conveying receivers, environmental dust control.
- [x] **Always** state the correlation (Stairmand vs Lapple) and **always** cap $\eta_d$ at 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrostatic Precipitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrostatic Precipitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electrostatic Precipitation (ESP)** is a dry, high-efficiency gas-cleaning unit operation in which suspended solid or liquid particulate matter is removed from a gas stream by imparting an electrical charge to the particles and then deflecting them onto collecting surfaces under the influence of a strong electrostatic field. It is classified as a *gas-solid separation* technique belonging to the family of **force-field separation** processes and is governed by Coulomb's law and the principles of corona discharge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** "Electrostatic precipitator is a particulate control device that uses electrical energy to charge particles (normally negative) for the purpose of separating them from a flowing gas stream by the application of an electric field."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Dustpan" Intuition

Imagine a flowing river of dusty air entering a long tunnel. Inside the tunnel, fine invisible "static electricity wires" act like a giant electrical comb, brushing every dust speck with a negative charge. The walls of the tunnel are positively charged, so every newly-charged dust speck is yanked sideways and stuck to the walls, exactly the way a statically-charged balloon sticks paper bits to itself. The cleaned air exits the other end, and a mechanical "rapping hammer" periodically knocks the accumulated dust loose into a hopper below.

- **Negatively charged particles** (electrons dominate) → drawn toward the **positive collecting plate**.
- **Gas molecules**, being neutral, **pass through unaffected**.
- The device removes **fine particulates (0.1 µm – 10 µm)** that are difficult to capture by cyclones or filters.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in ESP Design

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Voltage | V | 30 – 100 | kV (DC) |
| Migration (Drift) Velocity | w | 0.03 – 0.30 | m/s |
| Specific Collection Area (SCA) | A/Q | 30 – 150 | s/m |
| Corona Current | i | 0.1 – 1.0 | mA/m² |
| Gas Velocity | u | 0.6 – 2.5 | m/s |
| Particle Resistivity Limit | ρ | $10^{4}$ – $2\times10^{10}$ | Ω·cm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions:** ESPs in KTU problems are usually evaluated at **STP (25 °C, 1 atm)** unless explicitly stated otherwise. The **migration velocity (w)** is the single most important design parameter, often back-calculated from operating data using the **Deutsch–Anderson equation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Three Sequential Steps

ESP operation is fundamentally a three-stage process. Each stage must succeed for the overall collection efficiency to be high.

### Stage 1 — **Corona Discharge (Charging the Field)**
A high negative DC voltage (typically **30–100 kV**) is applied between a thin **discharge electrode (wire)** and grounded **collecting plates (electrodes)**. When the potential gradient exceeds the breakdown strength of air (≈ 30 kV/cm at wire surface), the air near the wire ionizes:

$$\text{N}_2 + e^- \longrightarrow \text{N}_2^+ + 2e^-$$

A purple-blue luminous glow (the **corona**) is observed. Negative ions (mostly O₂⁻, CO₃⁻) migrate outward and flood the inter-electrode space, forming a **unipolar negative ion cloud**.

### Stage 2 — **Particle Charging**
Dust particles passing through this ion cloud acquire charge by two mechanisms:

- **Field Charging (Pauthenier limit):** Dominant for particles **> 1 µm**. The external electric field distorts the ion trajectories, depositing charge on the particle up to a saturation value.

$$q_f = 12 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, \frac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r + 2} \, a^2 E_0$$

- **Diffusion Charging:** Dominant for particles **< 0.5 µm**. Random thermal motion of ions causes collisions and charge deposition. There is **no saturation limit**.

$$q_d = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \, a k T}{e} \ln\left(1 + \frac{a N_0 e^2 \bar{u}_i t}{4\varepsilon_0 k T}\right)$$

- **Combined (Fuchs) Charging:** Used for the full sub-micrometer range, simply the sum $q_{total} = q_f + q_d$.

### Stage 3 — **Migration and Collection (Drift Velocity)**
A charged particle in the electric field $E_c$ between the plates experiences a Coulomb force. Terminal migration velocity is reached when drag balances the electrical force:

$$w = \frac{q E_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p} \quad \text{(Stokes' drag regime)}$$

The particle drifts perpendicular to the gas flow and is deposited on the collection plate. Periodically, a **rapping mechanism** strikes the plate, dislodging the dust layer into a bottom hopper.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{w A}{Q}\right)$ | **Deutsch–Anderson efficiency equation** | dimensionless | Single-stage ideal ESP |
| 2 | $w = \dfrac{q E_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$ | Migration (drift) velocity | m/s | $Re_p < 0.1$ |
| 3 | $q_f = 12 \pi \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r+2} a^2 E_0$ | Pauthenier (field) charge | C | $d_p > 1\ \mu m$ |
| 4 | $E_0 = \dfrac{V}{r \ln(R/r)}$ | Field at wire surface | V/m | Wire-plate geometry |
| 5 | $SCA = \dfrac{A}{Q}$ | Specific Collection Area | s/m | Sizing parameter |
| 6 | $\eta_n = 1 - (1-\eta)^n$ | Efficiency of *n* identical fields in series | dimensionless | Cascade design |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d\eta}{dV} \propto \eta(1-\eta)$ | Sensitivity of efficiency to design changes | — | Optimization |
| 8 | $\rho_p < 10^4$ or $> 2\times 10^{10}$ | Practical **resistivity limits** | Ω·cm | Operating envelope |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical Bar Safety:** When writing $\vert V \vert$ in KTU answer scripts, use the absolute value in **plain English** ("magnitude of V") to avoid markdown/LaTeX rendering issues. In this sheet, $V$ refers to applied DC voltage magnitude.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Pollutant Targeted |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal Power Plants | Coal-fired boiler flue gas | Fly ash (PM₂.₅ / PM₁₀) |
| Cement Kilns | Pre-heater exhaust | Cement dust, alkalis |
| Steel Plants | Sinter plant and BOF gas | Iron oxide fume |
| Pulp & Paper | Recovery boiler | Na₂SO₄ smelt |
| Non-ferrous Smelters | Roaster off-gas | Sulfuric acid mist, metals |
| Municipal/Incineration | MSW flue gas | Dioxin-adsorbing particulates |

The **re-entrainment problem** at high gas velocities and the **back-corona** phenomenon at high particle resistivity are the two most important practical limitations addressed in modern ESPs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Deutsch–Anderson Equation (Collection Efficiency)

This is the **most derivation-favoured** topic in the KTU Module 4 question bank. We derive it from a mass-balance on a differential slice of the ESP.

### 3.1.1 Geometry Setup

Consider a **wire-plate ESP** of length $L$ (gas flow direction), plate height $H$, and plate-to-plate spacing $b$. Total plate area on both sides:

$$A = 2 H L$$

Gas flow rate $Q$ enters uniformly at the inlet. Particles entering at the bottom travel upward and simultaneously drift horizontally to the collection plate at velocity $w$.

### 3.1.2 Differential Mass Balance

Take an elemental slice of thickness $dx$ along the gas flow direction. Let $N(x)$ be the number concentration of suspended particles at position $x$.

Particles removed per unit time in slice $dx$ = drift flux times plate area on both sides:

$$\text{Removed} = N(x) \, w \, (2H)\, dx$$

The reduction in particle flux as gas passes through $dx$ is:

$$-Q \, dN = N(x) \cdot w \cdot 2H \, dx$$

### 3.1.3 Separation of Variables

Rearrange:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\frac{2 H w}{Q} dx$$

### 3.1.4 Integration

Integrate from inlet ($x=0$, $N = N_0$) to outlet ($x = L$, $N = N_L$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N_L} \frac{dN}{N} = -\frac{2 H w}{Q} \int_0^L dx$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_L}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{2 H L \, w}{Q}$$

### 3.1.5 Substitute $A = 2 H L$ and Solve for Efficiency

Since fractional collection efficiency is $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{N_L}{N_0}$:

$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{wA}{Q}\right)}$$

This is the **Deutsch–Anderson equation** — the cornerstone of all ESP sizing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Migration Velocity from Force Balance

### Step 1: Electrical (Coulomb) Force on a Charged Particle

$$F_e = q E_c$$

### Step 2: Viscous Drag (Stokes Regime, $Re_p \ll 1$)

For a sphere of diameter $d_p$ in a fluid of viscosity $\mu$:

$$F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p \, w$$

### Step 3: Force Balance at Terminal Velocity

At steady state, $F_e = F_d$:

$$q E_c = 3 \pi \mu d_p \, w$$

### Step 4: Solve for $w$

$$w = \frac{q E_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$$

### Step 5: Substitute Field-Charging Charge $q_f$

Plugging Pauthenier's saturation charge from §2.1:

$$w = \frac{12 \pi \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r+2} a^2 E_0 \cdot E_c}{3 \pi \mu (2a)} = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \, \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r+2} \, a \, E_0 E_c}{\mu}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Confusing $E_0$ (charging field, near the wire) with $E_c$ (collection field, between plates). They are **not** the same. $E_0$ is orders of magnitude larger because of the small wire radius.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem:** A wire-plate ESP is to treat $50{,}000\ m^3/h$ of flue gas at 150 °C. The effective drift velocity is $0.12\ m/s$. Each collecting plate is 8 m tall × 4 m long. The ESP has 12 plates in parallel. Determine:
(a) The specific collection area (SCA).
(b) The expected collection efficiency.
(c) The plate-to-plate spacing, if total horizontal cross-section is 6 m wide.

### Solution (a) — SCA and Area

Total plate area on both sides:

$$A = 2 \times (\text{plates}) \times H \times L = 2 \times 12 \times 8 \times 4 = 768\ m^2$$

Convert volumetric flow to SI:

$$Q = 50{,}000\ \frac{m^3}{h} = \frac{50{,}000}{3600}\ m^3/s = 13.89\ m^3/s$$

Specific Collection Area:

$$SCA = \frac{A}{Q} = \frac{768}{13.89} = 55.30\ s/m$$

### Solution (b) — Deutsch–Anderson Efficiency

$$\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{wA}{Q}\right) = 1 - \exp(-0.12 \times 55.30)$$

$$\eta = 1 - \exp(-6.636) = 1 - 0.00132$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 0.99868 \approx 99.87\%}$$

### Solution (c) — Plate Spacing

Cross-sectional flow area:

$$A_{cs} = (\text{plates} - 1) \times b \times H + 2 \times b \times H = (\text{plates} + 1) \times b \times H$$

Wait — for **wire-plate** with $n$ plates, there are $(n+1)$ gas channels of width $b$ and height $H$:

$$A_{cs} = (n+1) \times b \times H = 13 \times b \times 8 = 104 b$$

Gas velocity constraint — take typical $u = 1.5\ m/s$:

$$Q = A_{cs} \times u \Rightarrow 13.89 = 104 \, b \times 1.5$$

$$b = \frac{13.89}{156} = 0.0891\ m \approx 89\ mm$$

Thus plate-to-plate spacing is **≈ 89 mm**, which is within the standard 100–400 mm ESP design envelope. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Variables Influencing Efficiency

| Variable | Symbol | Effect on $\eta$ | Typical KTU-Asked Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Migration velocity | $w$ | $\uparrow w \Rightarrow \uparrow \eta$ | "If voltage increases, w increases" |
| Plate area | $A$ | $\uparrow A \Rightarrow \uparrow \eta$ | "Add another field in series" |
| Gas flow rate | $Q$ | $\uparrow Q \Rightarrow \downarrow \eta$ | "Boiler load increases" |
| Particle size | $d_p$ | Larger $d_p$ → larger $w$ → $\uparrow \eta$ | "Switch coal source, dust is finer" |
| Particle resistivity | $\rho_p$ | Both very high & very low $\rho_p$ hurt | "Use conditioning agent" |
| Gas viscosity | $\mu$ | $\uparrow \mu \Rightarrow \downarrow w$ | "Operating at higher temperature" |
| Number of fields | $n$ | Series fields multiply efficiency | "Convert 2-field to 3-field ESP" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — ESP Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flue Gas Inlet PM Loaded] --> B[Gas Distribution Plates]
    B --> C[Field 1 Charging Zone High Voltage Wires]
    C --> D[Field 1 Collection Zone Grounded Plates]
    D --> E[Field 2 Charging Zone]
    E --> F[Field 2 Collection Zone]
    F --> G[Field n Charging Zone]
    G --> H[Field n Collection Zone]
    H --> I[Outlet Gas Clean Stack]
    D --> J[Rapping Mechanism Periodic Impact]
    F --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K[Hopper Collection]
    K --> L[Ash Handling System]
    M[High Voltage T/R Set 30 to 100 kV DC] --> C
    M --> E
    M --> G
    N[Control Panel Voltage and Current] --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Particle Charging & Collection Micro-Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Neutral Particle Enters ESP] --> B[Corona Discharge Region]
    B --> C{Negative Ions Migrate Outward}
    C --> D[Field Charging Dominant d sub p greater than 1 micrometer]
    C --> E[Diffusion Charging Dominant d sub p less than 0.5 micrometer]
    D --> F[Particle Acquires Negative Charge q]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Collection Zone Electric Field E sub c]
    G --> H[Coulomb Force q times E sub c]
    H --> I[Migration Velocity w]
    I --> J[Particle Deposits on Positive Plate]
    J --> K[Rapping Releases Dust to Hopper]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — ESP Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrostatic Precipitators] --> B[Wire Plate Type]
    A --> C[Plate Plate Type]
    A --> D[Tubular Type]
    A --> E[Wet ESP]
    A --> F[Dry ESP]
    B --> G[Single Stage Most Industrial]
    C --> H[Two Stage Used for Gas Phase Cleaning]
    D --> I[High Efficiency for Mist and Submicron]
    E --> J[Used for Acid Mist and Tar]
    F --> K[Used for Fly Ash and Cement]
```

## 4.4 Component-Level Cross-Sectional Schematic (Textual)

A typical industrial ESP consists of the following vertically-stacked components in the gas flow path:

1. **Inlet Duct** with turning vanes and flow-smoothing screens.
2. **Gas Distribution Plates** — perforated plates ensuring uniform velocity profile across all fields.
3. **High-Voltage Discharge Electrodes** — thin (3–4 mm) wires or rigid frames suspended from insulators in the upper housing.
4. **Collecting Electrodes** — large flat plates (typically 0.5–1.5 mm thick, with shapes like C, Z, or wavy profile for stiffness).
5. **Rapping System** — electromagnetic or motor-driven hammers, with a top-rap (wires) and side-rap (plates) timing offset.
6. **Hoppers** — pyramidal bins with heating to prevent ash bridging and re-entrainment.
7. **Outlet Duct** with sampling ports for opacity / particulate monitors.
8. **High-Voltage Transformer-Rectifier (T/R) Set** — steps up grid AC and rectifies to pulsed DC.
9. **Control Cubicle** — automatic voltage controller (AVC) with spark-rate optimization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "C" or "Wavy" plate shape?** Sharp edges and points on flat plates would induce *back-discharge* at locations where resistivity builds up. Curved or embossed shapes (C, CS, OMEGA profiles) suppress back-corona, which is essential for high-resistivity fly ash.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Electrostatic Precipitator. State any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** is a particulate control device that uses an electrostatic field to charge and separate solid or liquid particles from a gas stream. The particles are given a negative charge by corona discharge and are then attracted to positively charged collecting plates.

**Two industrial applications:**

1. **Thermal power plants** — removal of **fly ash** from coal-fired flue gases before discharge to the stack.
2. **Cement industry** — recovery of **cement kiln dust** and pre-heater particulates as product, avoiding raw-material loss.

*(Acceptable alternatives: steel sinter plant fume, sulfuric acid mist in smelters, pulp-mill recovery boiler smelt.)* **(3 Marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is "corona discharge" and why is it essential in an ESP?**

**Model Answer:**

**Corona discharge** is a localized electrical breakdown of air occurring near a sharply curved electrode (the discharge wire) when the potential gradient exceeds the dielectric strength of air (≈ 30 kV/cm). It produces a continuous supply of **negative gas ions** that flood the inter-electrode space.

**Why it is essential:**

- It is the **source of unipolar ions** required to charge the dust particles.
- Without corona, the gas would remain a perfect insulator and **no particles could be charged or collected**.
- The corona current itself also sets up a small electric field that aids in transporting ions toward the collecting plates. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Deutsch–Anderson Efficiency + Sizing*

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **Deutsch–Anderson equation** for the collection efficiency of a wire-plate electrostatic precipitator, starting from a differential mass balance. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wire-plate ESP handles **$80{,}000\ m^3/h$** of gas at **150 °C and 1 atm**. The drift velocity is **$0.18\ m/s$**. Each plate is **10 m tall × 4 m long**, and the ESP has **16 collecting plates** in parallel. Calculate:
   (i) The total effective collection area.
   (ii) The specific collection area (SCA).
   (iii) The fractional collection efficiency.
   (iv) If the plant load increases gas flow to **$110{,}000\ m^3/h$** (same $w$), what is the new efficiency? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**

1. Steady-state, isothermal, plug-flow gas.
2. Particles are uniformly distributed at inlet across the cross-section.
3. Drift velocity $w$ is constant for all particles (monodisperse).
4. No re-entrainment, no back-corona.
5. The gas velocity profile is uniform.

**Derivation:**

Consider an elemental slice of thickness $dx$ in a wire-plate ESP of length $L$ and plate height $H$. Let $N(x)$ = particle number concentration at position $x$.

In time $dt$, gas volume $Q\,dt$ carries $N(x) Q\,dt$ particles into the slice. The number removed by collection on both plates (area $2H\,dx$ each side) is:

$$-dN \cdot Q\,dt = N(x) \cdot w \cdot 2H\,dx \cdot dt$$

Cancelling $dt$ and rearranging:

$$-Q\,dN = 2 H w N(x)\,dx$$

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\frac{2 H w}{Q}\,dx$$

Integrate from inlet to outlet:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N_{out}}{N_{in}}\right) = -\frac{2 H L w}{Q}$$

Substitute total area $A = 2 H L$ and use $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{N_{out}}{N_{in}}$:

$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{w A}{Q}\right)}$$

This is the **Deutsch–Anderson equation**. **(7 Marks — full step-by-step working gets full marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**(i) Total collection area:**

$$A = 2 \times n_{plates} \times H \times L = 2 \times 16 \times 10 \times 4 = 1280\ m^2$$

**(ii) Specific Collection Area:**

$$Q = \frac{80{,}000}{3600} = 22.22\ m^3/s$$

$$SCA = \frac{A}{Q} = \frac{1280}{22.22} = 57.60\ s/m$$

**(iii) Fractional Collection Efficiency:**

$$\eta = 1 - \exp(-w \cdot SCA) = 1 - \exp(-0.18 \times 57.60) = 1 - \exp(-10.37)$$

$$\eta = 1 - 3.12 \times 10^{-5} = 0.999969 \approx 99.997\%$$

**(iv) New efficiency at higher flow:**

$$Q_{new} = \frac{110{,}000}{3600} = 30.56\ m^3/s$$

$$SCA_{new} = \frac{1280}{30.56} = 41.88\ s/m$$

$$\eta_{new} = 1 - \exp(-0.18 \times 41.88) = 1 - \exp(-7.539) = 1 - 5.30 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{new} \approx 99.947\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** for area on both sides of each plate — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Mixing $wA/Q$ and $A/Q$** — examiners specifically check the numerical value of the dimensionless argument $wA/Q$.
> 3. **Not converting $m^3/h$ to $m^3/s$** — a classic **2-mark trap**.
> 4. **Showing calculator-only final answer** — always show the intermediate $wA/Q$ calculation.
> 5. **Assuming $\eta = wA/Q$** (linear approximation) — only valid when $wA/Q \ll 1$. For large values, the full exponential form is mandatory. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this misuse.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Migration Velocity + Particle Charging + ESP Components*

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanisms of **particle charging** in an ESP. Derive the expression for **migration velocity** under Stokes' law regime. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a wire-plate electrostatic precipitator**. List any **four factors affecting its efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Charging + Migration (7 Marks)

**Mechanisms of Particle Charging:**

When dust particles enter the corona-discharge region, they acquire charge by **two simultaneous mechanisms**:

**(i) Field (Pauthenier) Charging — dominant for $d_p > 1\ \mu m$:**

The external field $E_0$ distorts ion trajectories, depositing charge on the particle. Saturation charge is reached when the local field at the surface becomes zero:

$$q_f = 12 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r + 2} \cdot a^2 E_0$$

**(ii) Diffusion Charging — dominant for $d_p < 0.5\ \mu m$:**

Random thermal motion of ions causes bombardment; charge accumulates indefinitely (no saturation):

$$q_d = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 a k T}{e} \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{a N_0 e^2 \bar{u}_i t}{4 \varepsilon_0 k T}\right)$$

**Total charge (Fuchs interpolation):** $q = q_f + q_d$ — used in design codes.

---

**Derivation of Migration Velocity:**

Consider a particle of charge $q$ in collection field $E_c$. Forces on the particle:

- **Coulomb force:** $F_e = q E_c$
- **Viscous drag (Stokes regime, $Re_p \ll 1$):** $F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p \, w$

At terminal velocity, $F_e = F_d$:

$$q E_c = 3 \pi \mu d_p \, w$$

$$\boxed{w = \frac{q E_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}}$$

For field-charged particles, substituting $q_f$:

$$w = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \, \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{\varepsilon_r+2} \, a \, E_0 E_c}{\mu}$$

The migration velocity is **directly proportional** to particle size and applied fields, and **inversely proportional** to gas viscosity. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Construction & Working + Factors (7 Marks)

**Construction (refer to schematic in §4.4 above):**

The wire-plate ESP consists of:

1. **Shell / Housing** — steel casing, often with internal baffles for gas distribution.
2. **Discharge Electrodes** — thin tungsten or stainless-steel wires (≈ 3 mm dia), suspended from a frame at the top, vertically tensioned with weights.
3. **Collecting Electrodes** — large flat plates (C-, Z-, or wavy-profile) of mild steel, mounted vertically and grounded.
4. **Hoppers** — pyramidal ash collection bins at the bottom with level indicators and heaters.
5. **Rapping Gear** — motor/cam-driven hammers striking the top frame (for wires) and side frames (for plates) at programmed intervals.
6. **High-Voltage T/R Set** — 30–100 kV DC supply with automatic voltage controller.
7. **Insulators** — porcelain or composite bushings separating HV electrodes from the grounded casing.

**Working:**

Dirty gas enters → passes between charged wires and grounded plates → particles become negatively charged in the corona region → drift under electric field to plates → plates rapped periodically → dust falls into hoppers → cleaned gas exits via induced-draft fan to stack.

---

**Four Factors Affecting Collection Efficiency:**

1. **Particle Resistivity ($\rho_p$):** Ideal range $10^4$ to $2 \times 10^{10}\ \Omega\cdot cm$. Below $10^4$, particles re-entrain on rapping. Above $2 \times 10^{10}$, **back-corona** occurs, drastically reducing efficiency.
2. **Gas Velocity & Flow Distribution:** Non-uniform flow causes channeling and reduces residence time. Excess velocity re-entrains collected dust.
3. **Operating Voltage & Current:** Higher field strength increases both particle charge and migration velocity, but sparking must be limited.
4. **Particle Size Distribution:** Larger particles have higher $w$ and are collected more easily. Sub-micron particles require diffusion charging enhancement.

*(Acceptable: Gas temperature, humidity, fly-ash chemical composition, specific collection area, sparking rate.)* **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **The Deutsch–Anderson equation** $\eta = 1 - \exp(-wA/Q)$ is the single most important formula. Memorize it and its derivation.
- ✅ **Migration velocity ($w$)** is typically **0.03 – 0.30 m/s**; treat as constant in design.
- ✅ **SCA = A/Q** is the standard sizing index (units: s/m). KTU problems give $Q$ in $m^3/h$ — always convert to $m^3/s$.
- ✅ **Field charging** is dominant above 1 µm; **diffusion charging** below 0.5 µm.
- ✅ **Corona discharge** requires negative DC voltage; positive DC is rarely used industrially.
- ✅ **Back-corona** occurs at **high resistivity ($>2 \times 10^{10}\ \Omega\cdot cm$)** — fix with $\text{SO}_3$ conditioning or flue-gas humidification.
- ✅ **Re-entrainment** occurs at high gas velocity or when rapping is too vigorous — limit gas velocity to 1.0–1.5 m/s.
- ✅ **Two-stage / plate-plate ESPs** are used for gas-phase cleaning (e.g., ventilation air); **wire-plate** is for dust-laden industrial gas.
- ✅ **Number of fields in series:** $\eta_n = 1 - (1-\eta)^n$ — always apply this for cascade ESPs.
- ✅ **Conversion of flow rate is the #1 silly-mistake in KTU exam papers** — cross-check the units of $wA/Q$ before computing the exponential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Mantra:** *"Charge the dust, drift the dust, rap the dust."* Three steps, three marks each in any descriptive answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bag Filters and Venturi Scrubber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bag Filters and Venturi Scrubber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bag Filter (Fabric Filter / Baghouse)

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> A **Bag Filter** (also called a **fabric filter** or **baghouse**) is a dry, intermittent/particulate control device that separates particulate matter (dust) from a contaminated gas stream by forcing the gas through a porous fabric medium, on which the dust collects as a *filter cake*. The accumulated cake is periodically removed by a mechanical cleaning mechanism, restoring the filtration capacity.

**Classification of Bag Filters (by cleaning mechanism):**

| Type | Cleaning Method | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Shaker-type | Mechanical shaking of bags | Small, low-concentration dusts |
| Reverse-air type | Back-flushing with cleaned gas | Large baghouses, fibrous dusts |
| Pulse-jet type | Short bursts of high-pressure compressed air | High-temperature, fine dusts |

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuition Building

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of a *bag filter* like a *cricket player's thigh pad* with a *dusty road*.
> - As the dusty air passes through the fabric (the pad), the dust gets trapped in the tiny pores, much like how a *tea strainer* holds back tea leaves but lets water through.
> - Over time, the dust builds a thick layer (the *cake*). This cake is NOT a problem; it actually helps filter even better particles.
> - When the layer gets too thick (breathing becomes difficult for the bag), the system shakes the bag or blows air backward — like tapping your tea strainer to dislodge used leaves.

The **Air-to-Cloth Ratio (A/C ratio)** is the heart of bag filter design. It is the ratio of volumetric gas flow to total filter cloth area, measured in m³/min per m² of cloth (or m/min). Low A/C = cleaner operation but expensive; high A/C = compact but more cleaning cycles.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Air-to-Cloth ratio (A/C)**: 0.5 – 2.0 m/min (typical industrial range)
- **Filtration velocity (face velocity)**: 0.005 – 0.03 m/s
- **Collection efficiency**: **99.9 %** for particles > 1 μm
- **Pressure drop across filter**: 1000 – 2500 Pa (during operation)
- **Operating temperature**: Up to **260 °C** (with glass fabric); > 260 °C with specialty fibers

---

## 1.2 Venturi Scrubber

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Venturi Scrubber** is a high-efficiency *wet* particulate control device that uses a converging–throat–diverging duct to accelerate the gas stream to high velocity (typically 30–150 m/s). Liquid (usually water) is introduced at the throat, atomized into fine droplets by the high-velocity gas, and particles are captured by **inertial impaction** on these droplets. The droplet–particle agglomerates are then separated in a downstream mist eliminator.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuition Building

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine driving a car (gas stream) into a *narrow tunnel* (venturi throat) at high speed. The air squeezes faster. Now imagine *spraying water* from the roof of this tunnel directly into the fast-moving airstream. The water shatters into tiny droplets (atomization). Any dust particle in the air has too much inertia to follow the curving streamlines around the droplets — so it *crashes into* a droplet like a moth hitting a windshield. The droplet becomes a "mothership" carrying the dust particle, and later both are collected in a settling chamber.

**Why is the throat so important?**
- Higher throat velocity → smaller droplets → more droplet surface area → better impaction
- Higher velocity → more turbulence → better mixing → higher collection efficiency
- BUT higher velocity → higher pressure drop → higher operating cost (trade-off!)

### Standard Metrics

- **Throat velocity (vₜ)**: 30 – 150 m/s (commonly 60–100 m/s)
- **Liquid-to-Gas ratio (L/G)**: 0.5 – 1.5 L/m³ (volumetric)
- **Pressure drop (ΔP)**: 5 – 50 kPa typical
- **Collection efficiency**: **95 – 99.9 %** for particles > 1 μm (depending on design)
- **Cut diameter (d₅₀)**: 0.5 – 5 μm (very efficient for fine particles)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bag Filter — Operating Principle & Theory

### The Filtration Cycle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Dirty Gas Entry**: Particle-laden gas enters the baghouse through an inlet duct.
2. **Distribution**: Gas is distributed to multiple filter bags (cylindrical or envelope-shaped) suspended from a tubesheet.
3. **Filtration**: Gas flows *inward* (or outward) through the fabric. Particles larger than the pore size are captured on the upstream side.
4. **Cake Formation**: A dust cake builds up — paradoxically, the cake itself becomes the *primary filter medium* for very fine particles.
5. **Pressure Drop Buildup**: As cake thickens, ΔP rises from ~500 Pa to ~1500–2500 Pa.
6. **Cleaning Trigger**: When ΔP exceeds a threshold, the cleaning cycle activates (shaker / reverse air / pulse jet).
7. **Dust Discharge**: Dislodged cake falls into the hopper and is removed by a screw conveyor or rotary valve.

### Filter Media Selection (High-Yield Table)

| Media | Max Temp (°C) | Acid Resistance | Cost | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cotton | 80 | Poor | Low | General room-temp dust |
| Polyester | 130 | Good | Low–Medium | Most common industrial use |
| Nomex (Aramid) | 200 | Good | High | Asphalt, cement clinker |
| Fiberglass | 260 | Fair | Medium | High-temperature flue gas |
| PTFE (Teflon) | 260 | Excellent | Very High | Aggressive chemical/corrosive dust |
| P84 (Polyimide) | 260 | Excellent | High | Incinerators, metal refining |

### Why Pulse-Jet Dominates Modern Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why?** Pulse-jet bag filters offer:
> - Continuous filtration (no shutdown for cleaning)
> - Highest A/C ratio (1.0–2.0 m/min) → smaller footprint
> - Effective on sticky, fine, or high-temperature dusts
> - Cleaning by 100-µs compressed air pulse — non-contact, hence longer bag life

---

## 2.2 Venturi Scrubber — Operating Principle & Theory

### The Three Geometric Sections

```
[Gas+Particles] → [Converging] → [Throat] → [Diverging] → [Separator] → [Clean Gas]
                                    ↑
                            [Liquid Injection]
```

1. **Converging Section**: Gas accelerates; pressure drops (Bernoulli effect).
2. **Throat**: Maximum velocity (vₜ). Liquid is injected and atomized.
3. **Diverging Section**: Velocity decreases; pressure partially recovers.
4. **Separator / Entrainment Separator**: Cyclone or chevron mist eliminator collects droplets.

### The Physical Mechanism of Particle Capture

The dominant mechanism is **inertial impaction**. A particle with high inertia cannot follow the curved gas streamlines around a droplet and instead crashes into it.

The **Impaction Parameter (Nᵢ)** or **Stokes Number (Stk)** is the key dimensionless number:

$$
N_i = Stk = \frac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, v \, C_c}{18 \, \mu_g \, d_d}
$$

where:
- $\rho_p$ = particle density
- $d_p$ = particle diameter
- $v$ = relative velocity (≈ throat velocity)
- $C_c$ = Cunningham slip correction factor
- $\mu_g$ = gas viscosity
- $d_d$ = droplet diameter

**Higher Nᵢ → higher collection efficiency.**

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** you MUST memorize for KTU ESE. Every line is exam-tested.

### A. Bag Filter Formulas

| Formula | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air-to-Cloth ratio | $A/C = Q_{total} / A_{cloth}$ | m/min or m/s | Filter sizing |
| Filter area per bag | $A_{bag} = \pi \, D_b \, L_b$ | m² | For cylindrical bag |
| Number of bags | $N_b = A_{total} / A_{bag}$ | dimensionless | Round up |
| Total cloth area | $A_{total} = Q / (A/C)$ | m² | Basic design equation |
| Clean-filter pressure drop | $\Delta P_{clean} = K_0 \, \mu \, V_f$ | Pa | $K_0$ = fabric resistance |
| Total pressure drop | $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{clean} + \Delta P_{cake}$ | Pa | During filtration |
| Cake pressure drop | $\Delta P_{cake} = \alpha \, \mu \, V_f \, W$ | Pa | $W$ = cake mass/area; $\alpha$ = specific cake resistance |
| Filtration velocity | $V_f = Q / A_{cloth}$ | m/s | Same as face velocity |
| Collection efficiency | $\eta = 1 - (C_{out}/C_{in})$ | fraction or % | |

### B. Venturi Scrubber Formulas

| Formula | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Throat velocity | $v_t = Q_g / A_t = Q_g / (\pi D_t^2/4)$ | m/s | Critical design var. |
| L/G (mass) | $(L/G)_{mass} = (\rho_L/\rho_g) \times (L/G)_{vol}$ | kg/kg | Always convert |
| Pressure drop (Calvert) | $\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_g \, v_t^2 \, \bigl(1 - X^2 + 2 \, \rho_L/\rho_g \cdot L/G \cdot (1-X)\bigr)$ | Pa | Full Calvert; $X$ = slip ratio |
| Pressure drop (Simplified) | $\Delta P \approx \rho_L \, v_t^2 \, (L/G)_{mass} \, / \, 2$ | Pa | Theoretical, no slip |
| Pressure drop (Empirical) | $\Delta P \approx 0.85 \, \rho_L \, v_t^2 \, (L/G)_{mass} \, / \, 2$ | Pa | Calvert correlation |
| Impaction parameter | $N_i = C_c \, \rho_p \, d_p^2 \, v_t / (18 \, \mu_g \, d_d)$ | dimensionless | Capture driver |
| Cut diameter (50% eff.) | $d_{50} = \dfrac{9 \, \mu_g \, D_t}{C_c \, \rho_p \, v_t}$ | m | Important design param |
| Penetration | $P_t = \exp(-K_v \, \Delta P)$ | fraction | Calvert's shortcut |
| Overall efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \exp(-K_v \, \Delta P)$ | fraction | Substitute $K_v \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-3}$ for $\Delta P$ in Pa |
| Droplet diameter (Nukiyama–Tanasawa) | $d_d = \dfrac{585}{v_t - v_d} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_L}{\rho_L}} + 597 \left(\dfrac{\mu_L}{\sqrt{\sigma_L \, \rho_L}}\right)^{0.45} \left(\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_g}\right)^{1.5}$ | μm | Empirical correlation |

### Real-World Utility of These Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **A/C ratio** decides the capital cost of every industrial baghouse (cement plants, steel mills, pharma).
> - **Calvert's pressure drop equation** is the *bible* of wet scrubber design for acid-gas and particulate control.
> - **Cut diameter d₅₀** tells the engineer whether the scrubber will meet PM₂.₅ emission norms (CPCB India, USEPA).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bag Filter Cake Pressure Drop

### Starting Premise

We model the bag + accumulated cake as **two resistances in series** (analogous to electrical resistors).

Let:
- $R_m$ = resistance of clean filter medium (m⁻¹)
- $R_c$ = resistance of the accumulated cake (m⁻¹)
- $\mu$ = gas viscosity (Pa·s)
- $V_f$ = face velocity (m/s)

### Step 1 — Analogous to Darcy's Law

For flow through a porous medium, the volumetric flow rate per unit area (superficial velocity $V_f$) is proportional to the pressure drop divided by resistance:

$$
V_f = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{total}}
$$

where $R_{total} = R_m + R_c$ (resistances add for series flow).

### Step 2 — Express Total Pressure Drop

$$
\Delta P_{total} = \mu \, V_f \, (R_m + R_c) = \underbrace{\mu \, V_f \, R_m}_{\Delta P_{clean}} + \underbrace{\mu \, V_f \, R_c}_{\Delta P_{cake}}
$$

This explains why pressure drop increases linearly with cake resistance.

### Step 3 — Cake Resistance is Proportional to Cake Mass

The cake resistance depends on cake thickness (and hence mass per unit area, $W$):

$$
R_c = \alpha \, W
$$

where $\alpha$ = *specific cake resistance* (m/kg), a property of the dust.

### Step 4 — Final Working Equation

$$
\Delta P_{total} = \mu \, V_f \, R_m + \alpha \, \mu \, V_f \, W
$$

$$
\Delta P_{total} = \underbrace{K_0 \, \mu \, V_f}_{\text{Clean filter}} + \underbrace{\alpha \, \mu \, V_f \, W}_{\text{Filter cake}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** As filtration proceeds, $W$ increases linearly with time → ΔP rises linearly. Cleaning is triggered when ΔP crosses the upper threshold (typically 1500–2500 Pa).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Venturi Scrubber Pressure Drop (Calvert Approach)

### Step 1 — Identify the Control Volume

Take the venturi scrubber from inlet (point 1) to throat (point 2). Apply momentum conservation to the *gas + liquid* mixture.

### Step 2 — Write the Momentum Equation

For a horizontal venturi (no elevation change):

$$
P_1 A_1 + \dot{m}_g v_1 + \dot{m}_L (0) = P_2 A_2 + \dot{m}_g v_t + \dot{m}_L v_t
$$

The liquid enters with negligible axial velocity (injected perpendicular to flow).

### Step 3 — Simplify Using Continuity

For the gas at the throat: $v_t = Q_g / A_t$, so $\dot{m}_g = \rho_g \, A_t \, v_t$.

The liquid mass flow: $\dot{m}_L = (L/G)_{mass} \times \dot{m}_g = (L/G)_{mass} \, \rho_g \, A_t \, v_t$.

### Step 4 — Pressure Drop at the Throat

The pressure drop across the venturi:

$$
\Delta P = P_1 - P_2
$$

Combining the momentum terms (ignoring inlet kinetic energy as $v_1 \ll v_t$):

$$
\Delta P \, A_t \approx \dot{m}_g (v_t - v_1) + \dot{m}_L \, v_t
$$

Assuming $v_1 \ll v_t$:

$$
\Delta P \, A_t \approx \dot{m}_g \, v_t + \dot{m}_L \, v_t = (\dot{m}_g + \dot{m}_L) \, v_t
$$

Divide by $A_t$ and substitute $\dot{m}_g / A_t = \rho_g \, v_t$:

$$
\Delta P = \rho_g \, v_t^2 + \frac{\dot{m}_L}{A_t} \, v_t
$$

### Step 5 — Express Liquid Mass Flux in Terms of L/G

Since $\dot{m}_L / A_t = (L/G)_{mass} \cdot \rho_g \, v_t$:

$$
\Delta P = \rho_g \, v_t^2 + (L/G)_{mass} \, \rho_g \, v_t^2
$$

$$
\Delta P = \rho_g \, v_t^2 \left( 1 + (L/G)_{mass} \right)
$$

### Step 6 — Introducing the Slip Ratio (X)

In reality, liquid droplets do not reach gas velocity (slip exists). Define $X = v_d / v_t$ (slip ratio, typically 0.3–0.7).

The complete **Calvert correlation**:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{\rho_g \, v_t^2}{2} \left( 1 - X^2 \right) + \rho_L \, v_t^2 \left( \frac{L}{G} \right)_{mass} \left( 1 - X \right)
$$

### Step 7 — Simplified Theoretical Form (Pedagogical)

If $X \to 0$ (perfect momentum transfer, droplets fully accelerated) and liquid term dominates:

$$
\Delta P \approx \frac{1}{2} \, \rho_L \, v_t^2 \, \left( \frac{L}{G} \right)_{mass}
$$

This is the form most commonly used in textbook problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cut Diameter (d₅₀) in a Venturi Scrubber

### Physical Concept

The **cut diameter** is the particle size collected with **50 % efficiency**. Smaller particles escape; larger particles are collected.

### Setting Up the Force Balance

At the throat, a particle in a high-velocity gas stream approaches a stationary droplet. The particle's inertia carries it across streamlines.

The stopping distance $S$ of a particle (inertia-driven distance before stopping due to gas drag):

$$
S = \frac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, v_t \, C_c}{18 \, \mu_g}
$$

### Geometric Condition for 50% Collection

For 50% collection, the stopping distance equals half the throat width:

$$
S \approx \frac{D_t}{2}
$$

(where $D_t$ = throat diameter).

### Solving for d₅₀

$$
\frac{\rho_p \, d_{50}^2 \, v_t \, C_c}{18 \, \mu_g} = \frac{D_t}{2}
$$

$$
d_{50}^2 = \frac{9 \, \mu_g \, D_t}{\rho_p \, v_t \, C_c}
$$

$$
d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \, \mu_g \, D_t}{\rho_p \, v_t \, C_c}}
$$

A more conservative (and widely used) form simplifies to:

$$
d_{50} = \frac{9 \, \mu_g \, D_t}{C_c \, \rho_p \, v_t}
$$

**Insight:** Larger throat diameter → larger d₅₀ → poorer fine-particle capture. Higher throat velocity → smaller d₅₀ → better capture. This is why venturi scrubbers use *narrow throats* at *high velocities*.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Air-to-Cloth Ratio Design

### Problem

A baghouse handles **150 m³/min** of dust-laden air. Each cylindrical bag is **0.15 m** diameter and **4.0 m** long. Design **air-to-cloth ratio = 1.2 m/min**. Calculate the number of bags required.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Filter area per bag**

$$
A_{bag} = \pi \, D_b \, L_b = \pi \times 0.15 \times 4.0
$$

$$
A_{bag} = 1.885 \, m^2
$$

**Step 2 — Total cloth area required**

$$
A_{total} = \frac{Q}{A/C} = \frac{150}{1.2} = 125 \, m^2
$$

**Step 3 — Number of bags**

$$
N_{bags} = \frac{A_{total}}{A_{bag}} = \frac{125}{1.885} = 66.3
$$

**Step 4 — Round up to nearest integer**

$$
N_{bags} = 67 \, \text{bags}
$$

### Design Sanity Check

- A/C actually used = 150 / (67 × 1.885) = **1.187 m/min** ✓ (slightly below design)
- Reserve bags for maintenance: typically 10–20 % → keep ~10 spares

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bag Filter — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the functional architecture of an industrial **pulse-jet baghouse** (the most common modern configuration).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inletA[Dirty Gas Inlet] --> distrA[Inlet Plenum and Diffuser]
    distrA --> bagsA[Filter Bags Array 67 Bags]
    bagsA --> cakeA[Dust Cake Buildup on Bag Surface]
    cakeA --> dpaA[Pressure Drop Increases 500 to 2500 Pa]
    dpaA --> ctrlA{Differential Pressure Controller Setpoint 1500 Pa}
    ctrlA -- Below Setpoint -->> contA[Continue Filtration Mode]
    ctrlA -- Above Setpoint -->> pulseA[Trigger Pulse Jet Cleaning]
    pulseA --> compA[Compressed Air Reservoir 6 bar]
    compA --> diaphA[Diaphragm Valve Actuates]
    diaphA --> injA[Air Pulse Injected into Bag]
    injA --> flexA[Bag Snaps and Cake Dislodges]
    flexA --> hopA[Cake Falls to Hopper]
    hopA --> screwA[Screw Conveyor]
    screwA --> rotA[Rotary Air Lock Valve]
    rotA --> dustA[Collected Dust to Disposal]
    contA --> cleanA[Clean Gas Through Cage Support]
    cleanA --> outA[Clean Gas to Stack or Exhauster]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular block is a unit operation; arrows show mass (gas or dust) or signal flow. The dashed decision logic shows the **automatic cleaning cycle** triggered when ΔP exceeds 1500 Pa.

---

## 4.2 Venturi Scrubber — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inletG[Particulate Laden Gas Inlet] --> convA[Converging Section Gas Accelerates]
    convA --> throatA[Throat Section Velocity 60 to 100 m per s]
    liqA[Liquid Water or Reagent Inlet] --> nozA[Liquid Injection Nozzle]
    nozA --> throatA
    throatA --> atomA[Atomization Droplets Form 50 to 500 microns]
    atomA --> impactA[Inertial Impaction Particles Hit Droplets]
    impactA --> divA[Diverging Section Pressure Recovery]
    divA --> sepA[Entrainment Separator Cyclone or Chevron]
    sepA --> sludgeA[Slurry Out Let to Thickener]
    sepA --> outG[Clean Saturated Gas to Stack]
    divA -. Pressure Drop .-> dpmeterA[Differential Pressure Transmitter]
    dpmeterA --> ctrlB{Controller}
    ctrlB -- High Delta P -->> liqA
    ctrlB -- Low Delta P -->> contB[Maintain Flow]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The throat is the heart of the device — gas and liquid meet, atomization occurs, and particles impact droplets. The downstream separator (cyclone) is essential to remove the now-contaminated droplets from the gas stream.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Side-by-Side Functional View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BagFilterSys[Bag Filter System]
        BF1[Dirty Gas] --> BF2[Fabric Filtration]
        BF2 --> BF3[Clean Gas Out]
        BF2 -. Cake Removed .-> BF4[Dust Hopper]
    end
    subgraph VenturiSys[Venturi Scrubber System]
        VS1[Dirty Gas + Liquid] --> VS2[Throat Atomization]
        VS2 --> VS3[Droplet Particle Agglomerates]
        VS3 --> VS4[Separator Droplets Removed]
        VS4 --> VS5[Clean Saturated Gas]
        VS3 -. Slurry .-> VS6[Liquid Recycle]
    end
```

This side-by-side schematic emphasises the **key architectural difference**: the bag filter uses a **solid filtration medium** (dry process) while the venturi scrubber uses **liquid droplets** as the collection medium (wet process).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

> Define **Air-to-Cloth Ratio (A/C)** for a bag filter. State its typical range and explain how it influences filter design and operating cost.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Air-to-Cloth Ratio (A/C)** is defined as the volumetric flow rate of dirty gas handled per unit area of filter cloth:
> $$A/C = \frac{Q_{gas}}{A_{cloth}}$$
>
> **Typical industrial range:** **0.5 – 2.0 m/min** (8 – 33 mm/s).
>
> **Design implications:**
> - **Low A/C (0.5–0.8 m/min)** → Larger cloth area required → Higher capital cost, but longer bag life and lower pressure drop.
> - **High A/C (1.5–2.0 m/min)** → Compact baghouse → Lower capital cost, but frequent cleaning, higher ΔP, shorter bag life.
>
> For **pulse-jet filters**, A/C can be 1.0–2.0 m/min; for **shaker-type**, it is lower (0.5–0.8 m/min).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for range, 1 for design implication)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

> With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a Venturi Scrubber** in **3–4 sentences**.

**Model Answer:**

> A Venturi Scrubber is a wet scrubbing device consisting of a converging section, a throat, and a diverging section. Particle-laden gas is accelerated to a high velocity (typically 60–100 m/s) at the throat, where liquid (usually water) is injected.
