The high-velocity gas **atomizes** the liquid into fine droplets, and **inertial impaction** causes the dust particles to collide with and be captured by these droplets. The droplet–particle agglomerates are then separated in a downstream cyclone or mist eliminator, and clean saturated gas exits to the stack.

*(3 marks: 1 for components, 1 for atomization, 1 for impaction + separation)*

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Module-4 questions typically offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. The following Question A and Question B are designed as a true choice pair.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 4, Choice-1]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **construction and working of a bag filter** with the help of a labelled diagram. Explain the **three cleaning mechanisms** in detail. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A baghouse handles **250 m³/min** of gas from a cement kiln. Each bag is **0.20 m diameter** and **6.0 m long**. The design air-to-cloth ratio is **1.0 m/min**. Determine:
>     (i) Filter area per bag
>     (ii) Total cloth area required
>     (iii) Number of bags needed
>     (iv) Number of compartments (if each compartment holds 20 bags) (7 Marks)

### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Construction:**
- **Filter Housing (Shell)**: Made of mild steel (for hot gases) or stainless steel (corrosive). Divided into a *dirty-gas plenum* and a *clean-gas plenum* by a **tubesheet**.
- **Filter Bags**: Cylindrical (typical), made of polyester, Nomex, fiberglass, or PTFE. Mounted on **wire cages** (snap rings) to prevent collapse under suction.
- **Hopper**: Conical bottom for collected dust; has a rotary airlock or screw conveyor for discharge.
- **Cleaning System**: Shaker motor / reverse-air fan / pulse-jet manifold.

**Working Principle:**
Dirty gas enters the dirty plenum, flows through the bags (inward in most designs), and clean gas exits to the clean plenum. Particles deposit on the outer surface of the bag, forming a filter cake.

**Cleaning Mechanisms:**

1. **Mechanical Shaking**: Bags are shaken vertically by an eccentric motor at the top. The cake fractures and falls. Used in **shaker-type** baghouses; offline cleaning only.

2. **Reverse-Air Cleaning**: Clean gas is blown backward through the bags, dislodging the cake. Bags are usually divided into compartments; one compartment is offline while others filter. Used in **large baghouses** with delicate, fibrous dusts.

3. **Pulse-Jet Cleaning**: A short burst (60–100 ms) of high-pressure (5–7 bar) compressed air is injected through a venturi nozzle at the top of each bag. The bag snaps violently; cake is dislodged. **Online cleaning** (no shutdown) — most widely used.

### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Given:** $Q = 250 \, m^3/min$, $D_b = 0.20 \, m$, $L_b = 6.0 \, m$, $A/C = 1.0 \, m/min$

**(i) Filter area per bag:**

$$
A_{bag} = \pi \, D_b \, L_b = \pi \times 0.20 \times 6.0 = 3.770 \, m^2
$$

**(ii) Total cloth area:**

$$
A_{total} = \frac{Q}{A/C} = \frac{250}{1.0} = 250 \, m^2
$$

**(iii) Number of bags:**

$$
N_{bags} = \frac{A_{total}}{A_{bag}} = \frac{250}{3.770} = 66.31
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{bags} = 67 \, \text{bags (rounded up)}}
$$

**(iv) Number of compartments (20 bags each):**

$$
N_{comp} = \frac{67}{20} = 3.35 \implies \boxed{N_{comp} = 4 \, \text{compartments}}
$$

**Verification:** With 4 compartments of 20 + 1 compartment of 7 bags = 87 bags total (allows 30 % spare capacity for offline maintenance). Actual A/C = 250 / (87 × 3.77) = **0.76 m/min**, comfortably below design value.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Exam, Module 4, Choice-2]**

> **(a)** Explain the **construction and working principle of a Venturi Scrubber**. Derive the **Calvert pressure drop equation** for the venturi scrubber. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A venturi scrubber is used to remove fly ash from a flue gas stream. The operating data are:
>     - Throat velocity, $v_t = 80 \, m/s$
>     - Liquid-to-gas ratio (volumetric), $L/G = 1.0 \, L/m^3$
>     - Liquid density, $\rho_L = 1000 \, kg/m^3$
>     - Gas density, $\rho_g = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$
>
>     Calculate:
>     (i) Mass ratio $(L/G)_{mass}$
>     (ii) Theoretical pressure drop across the venturi
>     (iii) The **cut diameter** $d_{50}$ if throat diameter $D_t = 0.10 \, m$, gas viscosity $\mu_g = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, Pa \cdot s$, particle density $\rho_p = 2200 \, kg/m^3$, and $C_c \approx 1$ (assume Stokes regime). (7 Marks)

### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Construction:**
A venturi scrubber consists of:
1. **Converging Section** (angle 20°–25°): Gas enters here and accelerates.
2. **Throat** (length ≈ throat diameter): Maximum velocity region; liquid is injected here.
3. **Diverging Section** (angle 6°–7°): Gas decelerates; pressure partially recovers.
4. **Entrainment Separator**: Cyclone or chevron-type, removes liquid droplets from gas.

**Working:**
Liquid is injected at the throat either through a spray nozzle or as a flooded wall. The high-velocity gas atomizes the liquid into fine droplets (50–500 μm). Particle-laden gas undergoes **inertial impaction** on these droplets. The agglomerated droplet–particle mixture is separated downstream.

**Derivation of Calvert's Pressure Drop Equation:** (See SECTION 3.2 above for full derivation.)

Starting from the momentum equation and simplifying:

$$
\Delta P = \rho_g \, v_t^2 + (L/G)_{mass} \, \rho_g \, v_t^2
$$

Including the slip ratio $X = v_d / v_t$ (droplet velocity / gas velocity):

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{\rho_g \, v_t^2}{2}(1 - X^2) + \rho_L \, v_t^2 \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{mass}(1 - X)}
$$

For pedagogical approximation ($X \to 0$):

$$
\Delta P \approx \frac{1}{2} \rho_L \, v_t^2 \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{mass}
$$

### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Given:** $v_t = 80 \, m/s$, $(L/G)_{vol} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \, m^3/m^3$, $\rho_L = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $\rho_g = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$, $D_t = 0.10 \, m$, $\mu_g = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, Pa \cdot s$, $\rho_p = 2200 \, kg/m^3$, $C_c = 1$.

**(i) Mass ratio:**

$$
\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{mass} = \frac{\rho_L}{\rho_g} \times \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{vol}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{mass} = \frac{1000}{1.2} \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.8333 \, kg/kg
$$

**(ii) Theoretical pressure drop (simplified form):**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho_L \, v_t^2 \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{mass}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times (80)^2 \times 0.8333
$$

$$
\Delta P = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 6400 \times 0.8333
$$

$$
\Delta P = 2{,}666{,}667 \, Pa \approx \boxed{2.67 \, MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical note:** This is the *theoretical maximum* assuming perfect droplet–gas coupling. In practice, with $X \approx 0.4$–$0.6$ and the Calvert coefficient (0.85), actual ΔP is typically **5–50 kPa**.

**(iii) Cut diameter:**

$$
d_{50} = \frac{9 \, \mu_g \, D_t}{C_c \, \rho_p \, v_t}
$$

$$
d_{50} = \frac{9 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.10}{1 \times 2200 \times 80}
$$

$$
d_{50} = \frac{1.62 \times 10^{-5}}{1.76 \times 10^{5}}
$$

$$
d_{50} = 9.20 \times 10^{-11} \, m \approx \boxed{0.092 \, \mu m}
$$

This extremely small cut diameter indicates that the venturi scrubber can theoretically capture **submicron particles with 50 % efficiency** — confirming its high-efficiency reputation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert L/G from volumetric to mass** → Loses 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Using ρ_g instead of ρ_L** in the simplified ΔP formula → loses 1 mark; examiners specifically test if you know *which density* appears in which form.
> 3. **Squaring only one quantity** in $(v_t)^2 (L/G)_{mass}$ → ensure you square $v_t$ only.
> 4. **Forgetting units in $d_{50}$ calculation** → express answer in **μm** to make it physically meaningful.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This and Pin It to Your Wall!**

### Bag Filter Essentials

- ✅ **Three types of bag filter**: Shaker, Reverse-air, Pulse-jet (pulse-jet is industry standard today).
- ✅ **Air-to-Cloth ratio (A/C)**: typically 0.5–2.0 m/min; lower A/C → longer bag life, higher cost.
- ✅ **Filter area per cylindrical bag**: $A_{bag} = \pi D_b L_b$.
- ✅ **Number of bags**: $N_b = Q_{gas} / (A/C \cdot A_{bag})$.
- ✅ **Pressure drop model**: $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{clean} + \Delta P_{cake} = K_0 \mu V_f + \alpha \mu V_f W$.
- ✅ **Typical ΔP**: 1000–2500 Pa; cleaning triggered at ~1500 Pa.
- ✅ **Filter media**: Polyester (most common), Nomex (200 °C), Fiberglass (260 °C), PTFE (corrosive).
- ✅ **Collection efficiency**: **99.9 %** for particles > 1 μm.
- ✅ **Key advantage**: Highest dry collection efficiency; recovers product for recycle.

### Venturi Scrubber Essentials

- ✅ **Three sections**: Converging, Throat, Diverging + downstream Separator.
- ✅ **Throat velocity**: 60–100 m/s (very high — drives atomization).
- ✅ **L/G ratio**: 0.5–1.5 L/m³ (volumetric); convert to mass ratio for ΔP equation.
- ✅ **Calvert ΔP equation**: $\Delta P = \tfrac{\rho_g v_t^2}{2}(1-X^2) + \rho_L v_t^2 (L/G)_{mass}(1-X)$.
- ✅ **Simplified form**: $\Delta P \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_L v_t^2 (L/G)_{mass}$.
- ✅ **Cut diameter formula**: $d_{50} = \tfrac{9 \mu_g D_t}{C_c \rho_p v_t}$.
- ✅ **Impaction parameter**: $N_i = \tfrac{C_c \rho_p d_p^2 v_t}{18 \mu_g d_d}$.
- ✅ **Pressure drop**: 5–50 kPa (high — major operating cost).
- ✅ **Key advantage**: Excellent for fine particles; simultaneous gas absorption (SO₂, HCl).
- ✅ **Key disadvantage**: Creates wastewater; high pressure drop → high fan power.

### Comparative Engineering Judgement (Often Asked!)

| Feature | Bag Filter | Venturi Scrubber |
|---|---|---|
| Process type | Dry | Wet |
| Collection efficiency | 99.9 % | 95–99.9 % |
| Pressure drop | Low (1000–2500 Pa) | High (5–50 kPa) |
| Handles sticky dust | Poor (without conditioning) | Excellent |
| Recovers dry product | Yes | No (slurry) |
| Handles hot gas (>200 °C) | Yes (with right fabric) | Limited (water vapor issues) |
| Waste | Dry dust | Wastewater + sludge |
| Footprint | Medium | Small (compact) |

### Memorization Mnemonic — "**PARTICLES**"

For **P**articulate control devices, remember:
- **P**ressure drop drives cost
- **A**ir-to-cloth (bag) / L/G ratio (scrubber) drives design
- **R**eplace filters / Refill water
- **T**emperature limits the media (bag) or the cooling duty (scrubber)
- **I**mpaction is the capture mechanism (scrubber); interception + impaction (bag)
- **C**leaning is automatic (pulse-jet) — for bag filter
- **L**iquid is the collection medium (scrubber)
- **E**fficiency is 99 %+ for both — when well-designed
- **S**ize matters: cut diameter d₅₀ ≈ 1 μm for well-designed venturi

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Bag Filters and Venturi Scrubber.**
> *All formulas, derivations, and exam patterns strictly aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme assessment regulations and B.Tech (NEP 2020) learning outcomes.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixing of Granular Solids and Pastes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mixing of Granular Solids and Pastes # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Mixing** is defined as a unit operation that involves the *intermingling* of two or more particulate solids (powders, granules) or the blending of solids with liquids to form **homogeneous pastes, slurries, or doughs**, by means of mechanical agitation aimed at reducing compositional non-uniformity to an acceptable statistical limit. In particle technology, mixing is treated as a **stochastic (probabilistic) process** rather than a deterministic one. The "goodness" of mixing is therefore not measured by visual inspection alone but by a **statistical parameter** known as the *Index of Mixing* ($M$) or *Degree of Mixing*. **Two broad classifications under KTU 2024 scheme:** | Sub-Category | System State | Typical Industries | |---|---|---| | **Mixing of Granular Solids** | Free-flowing particulates (dry powders, crystals, granules) | Pharmaceuticals, detergents, fertilizers, food grains, plastics | | **Mixing of Pastes** | Viscous, cohesive blends of solids + liquids | Paints, adhesives, cement mortar, chocolate, cosmetics, rubber compounds | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-world analogy – "Chai Masala Blending":** Imagine preparing a cup of masala chai. The dry powders (cardamom, ginger, clove, cinnamon) must be blended so that *every pinch contains nearly the same proportion* of each spice. This is exactly what industrial mixing of granular solids achieves — statistical homogeneity of a multi-component powder bed. > > For pastes, think of **mixing cement, sand, and water with a trowel or a paddle**. The blade forces a viscous mass to fold onto itself, smearing, stretching, and recombining the components. This is the essence of paste mixing: overcoming high viscous resistance through **mechanical shearing and folding**. ## 1.3 Key Metrics (Mandatory Bold Callouts) - **Scale of Scrutiny (S):** The smallest distinguishable region whose composition is deemed acceptable. A pharmaceutical tablet weighing 500 mg has a much smaller S than a 50 kg fertilizer bag. - **Degree of Mixing (M):** A dimensionless number between **0** (completely segregated) and **1** (perfect mixing). - **Coefficient of Variation (CoV):** The standard deviation of composition expressed as a percentage of the mean. **A CoV below 5–10%** is generally accepted as a "well-mixed" state. - **Mixing Index (Mohr's Number, $\mathbf{M}$):** Used to compare mixers quantitatively. - **Standard reference batch size for industrial granular mixers:** **50 kg to 5000 kg**. ## 1.4 Why Mixing Matters in Particle Technology > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Pharmaceutical tablets:** Uniformity of Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API) is *legally mandated* by pharmacopeias. A poorly mixed blend causes dose variation — potentially fatal. > - **Cement clinker production:** Raw meal homogenization ensures constant kiln feed composition. > - **Detergents:** Color speckles, enzymes, and surfactants must be uniformly distributed. > - **Food powders:** Spices, milk powder, instant coffee — segregation in the bag ruins the product. --- # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Mechanisms of Mixing Every solid–solid mixing operation — regardless of equipment — relies on **three transport mechanisms** acting simultaneously: | # | Mechanism | Physical Description | Dominant In | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Convective Mixing** | Bulk transport of groups of particles from one location to another (like folding dough) | Tumbler mixers (V-type, double-cone) | | 2 | **Diffusive Mixing** | Individual particles move randomly across freshly exposed surfaces, similar to molecular diffusion | Ribbon, paddle, fluidized mixers | | 3 | **Shear Mixing** | Slip planes develop within the bulk; one layer slides over another, creating new interfaces | High-speed intensifier bars, paste mixers | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Any mixer description in the exam must mention *which mechanism predominates*. Tumbler mixers → convective; ribbon → diffusive; sigma blade → shear. ## 2.2 Classification of Industrial Mixers ### A. Mixers for Granular Solids (Dry Mixing) #### (i) Tumbler Mixers Rotate the entire vessel. Components cascade under gravity, mixing by **convection + diffusion**. - **Double-Cone Mixer** – Two cones joined base-to-base; rotates on a horizontal axis. Excellent for free-flowing powders and color blending. - **V-Type Mixer (Twin-Shell)** – Two cylindrical shells joined at an angle to form a "V"; splits and recombines the charge twice per revolution. - **Horizontal Drum Mixer** – Simple rotating cylinder, used at very large scale (fertilizer, cement). - **Cube Mixer** – A cube that tumbles end-over-end. - **Y-Cone / Octagonal / Bin Blender** – Modifications for specific segregation problems. #### (ii) Convective / Paddle / Ribbon Mixers A stationary trough containing rotating shafts with paddles or helical ribbons. - **Ribbon Blender** – Outer helical ribbon moves material in one direction, inner ribbon in the opposite direction. Standard for powders with low to moderate cohesion. - **Paddle Mixer** – Two or four paddles on a horizontal shaft; gentle, low-shear action. - **Vertical Screw Mixer (Nauta Mixer)** – An orbiting screw in a conical vessel; ideal for heat-sensitive and cohesive powders. - **Sigma Mixer (Z-blade)** – Used for stiff pastes and doughs. Two tangential blades in a trough. - **Planetary Mixer** – The mixing element orbits the vessel axis while spinning, scraping walls. - **High-Intensity / High-Speed Mixer (e.g., Lodige ploughshare + intensifier bar)** – Used for short cycle times; the intensifier bar chops agglomerates. ### B. Mixers for Pastes | Type | Description | Application | |---|---|---| | **Sigma Blade Mixer** | Two heavy S-shaped blades rotating at different speeds in a trough | Dough, putty, rubber, paint | | **Banbury Mixer** | Enclosed chamber; rotor + ram; intense shearing | Rubber compounding, PVC | | **Change-Can Mixer** | Removable can; intermeshing paddles | Adhesives, ink | | **Triple-Roll Mill** | Three rolls rotating at different speeds; very high shear | Pigments, cosmetics, chocolate refining | | **Helical (Screw Extruder)** | Continuous; one or two screws | Polymer compounding, ceramic pastes | ## 2.3 The Statistical Foundation – Index of Mixing > [!IMPORTANT] > Mixing is never 100% complete; it is a **statistical state**. The KTU syllabus (Module 4) treats mixing quality using **two parallel formalisms**: > 1. **Mohr's Mixing Index (M)** — based on the standard deviation of a tracer component. > 2. **Lacey's Mixing Index** — also based on variance, expressed differently. ### 2.3.1 Variance and Standard Deviation in a Powder Mixture Let the powder contain a **key (tracer) component** with mean concentration $c$ (mass fraction) in samples of size $S$. - Variance of completely segregated mixture: $\sigma_0^2 = c(1-c)$ - Variance of a randomly mixed mixture: $\sigma_R^2 = \dfrac{c(1-c)}{n}$, where $n$ is the number of particles in a sample. - Variance of the actual mixture at time $t$: $\sigma^2(t)$. ### 2.3.2 Mohr's Index of Mixing $$M = \frac{\sigma_0 - \sigma}{\sigma_0 - \sigma_R}$$ This is bounded: $M = 0$ at the start (segregated) and $M = 1$ at random mixing limit. However, $M$ can exceed 1 in real mixers before settling. ### 2.3.3 Lacey's Mixing Index $$M_L = \frac{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma^2}{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2}$$ Bounded strictly between 0 and 1. **KTU-preferred** form for numerical problems. ## 2.4 Rate of Mixing – First-Order Decay Model Mixing curves follow a typical pattern: rapid initial improvement followed by an asymptotic approach to an equilibrium mixing index. Empirically: $$\frac{d\sigma^2}{dt} = -K\left(\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2\right)$$ Integrating: $$\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2 = \left(\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2\right) e^{-K t}$$ - $K$ = mixing rate constant (units: $\text{time}^{-1}$) - $t$ = mixing time (s or min) In terms of Lacey's index: $$M_L = 1 - e^{-K t}$$ A semi-log plot of $(1 - M_L)$ vs. $t$ gives a straight line whose slope is $-K$. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_0^2 = c(1-c)$ | Variance of *fully segregated* mixture | dimensionless² | | 2 | $\sigma_R^2 = \dfrac{c(1-c)}{n}$ | Variance of *random (ideal)* mixture | dimensionless² | | 3 | $M_L = \dfrac{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma^2}{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2}$ | **Lacey's Mixing Index** | dimensionless, $0 \le M_L \le 1$ | | 4 | $M_{\text{Mohr}} = \dfrac{\sigma_0 - \sigma}{\sigma_0 - \sigma_R}$ | Mohr's Index | dimensionless (may exceed 1) | | 5 | $M_L = 1 - e^{-K t}$ | Time evolution of mixing | dimensionless | | 6 | $K = \dfrac{1}{t}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-M_L}\right)$ | Mixing rate constant | $\text{s}^{-1}$ or $\text{min}^{-1}$ | | 7 | $\text{CoV} = \dfrac{\sigma}{c}\times 100\%$ | Coefficient of Variation | % | | 8 | $P = P_0 + K_p\, \rho\, N^3 D^5$ | Power for paddle/impeller (proportional) | W or kW | | 9 | $N_{\text{Fr}} = \dfrac{N^2 D}{g}$ | Froude number (gravity vs. centrifugal) | dimensionless | | 10 | $N_{\text{Re,mix}} = \dfrac{\rho N D^2}{\mu}$ | Mixing Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 11 | $V_{\text{tip}} = \pi N D$ | Impeller tip speed | m/s | | 12 | $t_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | Optimum mixing time (rule of thumb) | s or min | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formula Hygiene Rule (KTU Board Examiners' Preference):** Always state the *type of index* (Lacey or Mohr) you are computing. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 1-mark penalty. ## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Mixing Theory - **Scale-up:** Use the *constant Froude number* principle — if a laboratory mixer works at $N_{\text{Fr}}=0.3$, scale up the impeller diameter keeping $N_{\text{Fr}}$ constant. - **Cycle-time optimization:** Over-mixing causes *re-segregation* (e.g., over-blended detergent separates color specks). Find the optimum $t$ from the $M_L$ vs. $t$ curve. - **Process Control:** Inline NIR (Near-Infrared) probes measure $M_L$ in real time and stop the mixer when the target index is reached. - **Regulatory Compliance:** FDA Process Validation guidelines and WHO GMP require documented evidence of blend uniformity (BU) for solid oral dosage forms. --- # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Lacey's Mixing Index ### 3.1.1 Setup Consider a binary mixture of two powders A and B in a mixer. Let: - Mass fraction of the **tracer (key) component** in the overall mixture = $c$ (e.g., 0.20) - A small sample of size $S$ drawn from the mixture has tracer concentration $x_i$ in the $i^{\text{th}}$ sample - Mean of $x_i$ across many samples: $\bar{x} = c$ - Sample variance: $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - c)^2$ ### 3.1.2 Two Limiting States **(a) Completely Segregated State (before mixing):** Each sample contains *either* pure A *or* pure B. The fraction of samples containing B is $c$. The variance of a Bernoulli process is: $$\sigma_0^2 = c(1-c)$$ **(b) Random (Ideal) Mixed State:** Each sample contains $n$ particles, of which the *expected* number of B-particles is $nc$. The probability of finding $k$ B-particles in any sample follows a hypergeometric distribution. For a large population: $$\sigma_R^2 = \frac{c(1-c)}{n}$$ ### 3.1.3 Lacey Normalization Lacey reasoned that the *fractional reduction* in variance from the segregated limit to the random limit measures the degree of mixing. He defined: $$M_L = \frac{\text{Actual reduction in variance}}{\text{Total possible reduction in variance}}$$ $$M_L = \frac{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma^2}{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2}$$ ### 3.1.4 Limiting Behaviour $$\text{If } \sigma^2 = \sigma_0^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_L = 0 \quad \text{(segregated)}$$ $$\text{If } \sigma^2 = \sigma_R^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_L = 1 \quad \text{(random / ideal)}$$ $$\text{If } \sigma^2 < \sigma_R^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_L > 1 \quad \text{(ordered mixing — possible with cohesive powders)}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Mixing Rate Equation (First-Order Decay) ### 3.2.1 Phenomenological Basis Mixing occurs by particle movement across concentration gradients. The rate of *homogenization* is proportional to the *remaining* difference between the actual variance and the random-mix variance: $$\frac{d(\sigma^2)}{dt} = -K\left(\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2\right)$$ ### 3.2.2 Integration $$\int_{\sigma_0^2}^{\sigma^2}\frac{d(\sigma^2)}{\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2} = -K\int_0^t dt$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2}{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2}\right) = -Kt$$ $$\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2 = \left(\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2\right) e^{-Kt}$$ ### 3.2.3 Expressing in Lacey's Index Divide both numerator and denominator by $(\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2)$: $$\frac{\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2}{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2} = 1 - M_L = e^{-Kt}$$ $$\boxed{M_L(t) = 1 - e^{-Kt}}$$ ### 3.2.4 Practical Implication To find $K$ experimentally, take samples at multiple time points, compute $M_L$, and fit: $$K = \frac{1}{t}\ln\left(\frac{1}{1 - M_L}\right)$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem:** A binary mixture of powders A and B contains 20% by mass of B. A V-type mixer is charged. After **5 minutes** of mixing, sampling yields a standard deviation of the B-concentration equal to $\sigma = 0.025$. A perfectly random mixture of the same composition with $n = 100$ particles per sample is taken as the ideal reference. > > **(a)** Compute Lacey's mixing index after 5 minutes. > **(b)** Determine the mixing rate constant $K$. > **(c)** What is the *optimum mixing time* to reach $M_L = 0.95$? ### 3.3.1 Given Data - $c = 0.20$, hence $1 - c = 0.80$ - $\sigma^2 = (0.025)^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$ - $n = 100$ - $t = 5$ min ### 3.3.2 Step 1 — Compute $\sigma_0^2$ $$\sigma_0^2 = c(1-c) = 0.20 \times 0.80 = 0.16$$ ### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Compute $\sigma_R^2$ $$\sigma_R^2 = \frac{c(1-c)}{n} = \frac{0.16}{100} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0016$$ ### 3.3.4 Step 3 — Apply Lacey's Formula $$M_L = \frac{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma^2}{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2}$$ Substituting: $$M_L = \frac{0.16 - 0.000625}{0.16 - 0.0016}$$ $$M_L = \frac{0.159375}{0.1584}$$ $$M_L = 1.00615 \approx 1.006$$ > [!NOTE] > $M_L > 1$ indicates **ordered mixing**, common in fine cohesive powders. The V-mixer's gentle action produced *better than random* uniformity. ### 3.3.5 Step 4 — Find $K$ $$M_L = 1 - e^{-Kt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-Kt} = 1 - M_L$$ $$e^{-K(5)} = 1 - 1.006 = -0.006$$ This is mathematically problematic (negative inside log). **Interpretation:** The experimental variance is *below* the random-mix variance, so the first-order decay model with $\sigma_R^2$ as asymptote breaks down. Practically, we treat the system as having *reached* a steady ordered-mix state. The **rate constant is undefined** in this classical sense, but we can fit a modified model with $\sigma_\infty^2$ in place of $\sigma_R^2$. **Worked fix — modified model:** $$\sigma^2 - \sigma_\infty^2 = \left(\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_\infty^2\right) e^{-Kt}$$ Assuming $\sigma_\infty^2 \approx \sigma^2$ (already at asymptote), $K$ is very large (i.e., $t_{\text{opt}} \ll 5$ min). ### 3.3.6 Step 5 — Optimum Time for $M_L = 0.95$ (revised problem, $M_L = 0.95$ at $t = 5$ min) $$0.95 = 1 - e^{-K(5)}$$ $$e^{-5K} = 0.05$$ $$-5K = \ln(0.05) = -2.996$$ $$K = 0.5992 \text{ min}^{-1}$$ For $M_L = 0.95$ specifically: $$t_{0.95} = \frac{-\ln(1-0.95)}{K} = \frac{\ln 20}{0.5992} = \frac{2.996}{0.5992} \approx 5.0 \text{ min}$$ This serves as a self-consistency check: $t_{\text{opt}} \approx 5$ min. > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Insight:** The number $K$ being very small or very large is *not* an error — it is a **physical observation** about the mixer's speed. A 5-minute blending cycle is a *typical industry standard* for a V-type mixer with 20% minor component. ## 3.4 Power Requirement for an Impeller (Paddle / Ribbon) ### 3.4.1 Empirical Correlation For a paddle/impeller in a Newtonian or granular bed: $$P = P_0 \cdot N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$ - $P$ = power consumed (W) - $N_p$ = power number (depends on impeller geometry, $N_{\text{Re}}$ and $N_{\text{Fr}}$) - $\rho$ = bulk density of material ($\text{kg/m}^3$) - $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s) - $D$ = impeller diameter (m) ### 3.4.2 In the *Turbulent* regime (typical for granular mixers): $$P \propto \rho \, N^3 D^5 \quad \text{(independent of viscosity)}$$ ### 3.4.3 In the *Laminar* regime (typical for pastes): $$P \propto \mu \, N^2 D^3$$ ### 3.4.4 Scale-up at Constant Power per Unit Volume If $P/V$ is held constant on scale-up, then: $$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{2/3}$$ --- # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Master Functional Architecture of a Mixing Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Material A
Granular Solid] --> C[Feed Hopper] B[Raw Material B
Granular Solid or Liquid] --> C C --> D[Weighing and
Proportioning Station] D --> E[Mixer Selection
Tumbler or Paddle or Paste] E --> F{Is it a
dry powder?} F -- Yes --> G[Tumbler / Ribbon Mixer
Convective + Diffusive] F -- No --> H[Paste / Sigma Mixer
Shear Dominant] G --> I[In-process Sampling
NIR or Thief Probe] H --> I I --> J{Index of Mixing
M sub L greater than
target 0.95?} J -- No --> K[Continue Mixing
Recompute M sub L] J -- Yes --> L[Discharge to
Downstream Unit] L --> M[Final Blend Uniformity
Test on 10 Samples] M --> N[Packaging and Storage] ``` ## 4.2 Equipment Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start: Material State] --> S1{Physical State} S1 -- Free-flowing powder --> T1[Choose Tumbler or Ribbon] S1 -- Cohesive powder --> T2[Choose High-Intensity Mixer] S1 -- Paste or Dough --> T3[Choose Sigma or Banbury] T1 --> C1{Batch Size} C1 -- less than 500 kg --> C2[V-type or Double-Cone] C1 -- greater than 500 kg --> C3[Horizontal Drum or Ribbon] T2 --> H1[Ploughshare with Intensifier Bar] T3 --> P1[Sigma Blade or Planetary] ``` ## 4.3 Detailed V-Type Mixer Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Inlet Limb 1]:::vessel L2[Inlet Limb 2]:::vessel V((V-type Shell)):::vessel SH[Shaft]:::rotor M[Motor and Gearbox]:::rotor D[Discharge Valve]:::rotor S1[Sample Port 1]:::vessel S2[Sample Port 2]:::vessel L1 --> V L2 --> V V --- SH SH --- M V --> D V --> S1 V --> S2 classDef vessel fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rotor fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Sigma Blade Mixer Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR J1[Charging Hopper]:::in T1[Trough Body]:::vessel SA[Sigma Blade A
Slow Speed]:::rotor SB[Sigma Blade B
Fast Speed]:::rotor TT[Tilt Mechanism]:::rotor DJ[Discharge Chute]:::out J1 --> T1 T1 --- SA T1 --- SB T1 --> TT TT --> DJ classDef in fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef vessel fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rotor fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Mixing Index Evolution Chart (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR P0["t = 0
M sub L = 0
Fully Segregated"]:::s0 P1["t small
Convective Burst
M rises rapidly"]:::s1 P2["t intermediate
Diffusive Stage
M approaches plateau"]:::s2 P3["t large
M greater than 0.95
Target Achieved"]:::s3 P4["t very large
Over-mixing
Segregation returns"]:::s4 P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 classDef s0 fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef s1 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef s2 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef s3 fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef s4 fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the term "Index of Mixing" as proposed by Lacey. How is it different from Mohr's index?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > Lacey's index of mixing $M_L$ is a **dimensionless statistical measure** of the degree of homogeneity of a particulate mixture, defined as: > > $$M_L = \frac{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma^2}{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2}$$ > > where $\sigma_0^2$, $\sigma^2$, $\sigma_R^2$ are the variances in the segregated, actual, and random mixed states. > > **Difference from Mohr's index:** > 1. Lacey uses *variance* ($\sigma^2$); Mohr uses *standard deviation* ($\sigma$). > 2. $M_L$ is **bounded between 0 and 1**; Mohr's index can exceed 1 (especially during ordered mixing). > 3. Mohr's index has a **physical interpretation** in terms of particle movement, while $M_L$ is purely statistical. > 4. For numerical problems, **KTU examiners prefer $M_L$** because of its bounded nature. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any three mechanisms by which mixing of granular solids occurs and identify the type of mixer in which each is dominant.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The three mechanisms are: > > 1. **Convective Mixing:** Bulk transport of particle groups; dominant in **tumbler mixers** such as V-type, double-cone, and rotating drum. > 2. **Diffusive Mixing:** Random movement of individual particles across fresh surfaces; dominant in **ribbon blenders, paddle mixers, and fluidized-bed mixers**. > 3. **Shear Mixing:** Slip-plane formation between layers; dominant in **high-intensity ploughshare mixers and paste mixers (sigma blade, Banbury)**. > > (3 marks: 1 mark per mechanism with its dominant equipment.) --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Mixers and Mixing Index* **(a)** Classify the various types of industrial mixers used for the mixing of granular solids. Discuss the working principle, construction, advantages, and limitations of any **two tumbler mixers** in detail. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Two powders are blended in a V-type mixer. The mean mass fraction of the key component is $c = 0.30$. Samples of $n = 50$ particles are withdrawn and analyzed, giving a measured standard deviation of $\sigma = 0.045$. Compute: - (i) Lacey's mixing index $M_L$ **[2 marks]** - (ii) Mohr's mixing index $M_{\text{Mohr}}$ **[2 marks]** - (iii) State whether the mixture is in a "segregated", "random", or "ordered" state, and justify. **[3 marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Classification of Mixers for Granular Solids:** Mixers for granular solids are broadly classified into: 1. **Tumbler mixers** (V-type, double-cone, drum, cube) 2. **Convective/ribbon/paddle mixers** (ribbon blender, paddle, Nauta) 3. **High-intensity mixers** (ploughshare with intensifier bar, fluidized mixers) **Detailed discussion of two tumbler mixers:** **(i) Double-Cone Mixer:** - *Construction:* Two conical sections joined at their bases, mounted on a horizontal shaft that passes through the central axis. Internal baffles or intensifier bars are optional. - *Working:* The mixer rotates at a low speed (5–15 rpm). The charge is split into the two cones, cascades, and recombines at the base. Material also diffuses at the interface. - *Mechanism:* Predominantly **convective** with some **diffusive** mixing. - *Advantages:* Gentle action, low power, no dead spots, easy to clean, suitable for free-flowing powders and color blending, low segregation during discharge. - *Limitations:* Long mixing times (15–30 min), not suitable for cohesive or pasty materials, breakage of fragile crystals possible. **(ii) V-Type Mixer (Twin-Shell):** - *Construction:* Two cylindrical shells welded at an angle to form a "V" shape, mounted on a horizontal shaft through the apex of the V. - *Working:* Rotates at 5–20 rpm. The charge divides into two streams at the top of the V and merges at the bottom twice per revolution, giving high convective mixing efficiency. - *Mechanism:* Convective (dominant) + diffusive. - *Advantages:* Faster mixing than double-cone (typically 8–15 min), excellent for blending two or more dry powders of similar density, good for color and minor-component dispersion, low attrition. - *Limitations:* Not suitable for wet masses or pastes; not efficient for very cohesive or sticky materials; requires significant headroom for discharge. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $c = 0.30$, $1 - c = 0.70$, $n = 50$, $\sigma = 0.045$. **Step 1:** Compute variances. $$\sigma_0^2 = c(1-c) = 0.30 \times 0.70 = 0.21$$ $$\sigma_R^2 = \frac{c(1-c)}{n} = \frac{0.21}{50} = 0.0042$$ $$\sigma^2 = (0.045)^2 = 0.002025$$ **(i) Lacey's index:** $$M_L = \frac{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma^2}{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2} = \frac{0.21 - 0.002025}{0.21 - 0.0042}$$ $$M_L = \frac{0.207975}{0.2058} = 1.0106$$ **(ii) Mohr's index:** $$M_{\text{Mohr}} = \frac{\sigma_0 - \sigma}{\sigma_0 - \sigma_R} = \frac{\sqrt{0.21} - 0.045}{\sqrt{0.21} - \sqrt{0.0042}}$$ $$M_{\text{Mohr}} = \frac{0.4583 - 0.045}{0.4583 - 0.0648} = \frac{0.4133}{0.3935} = 1.0504$$ **(iii) State of Mixture:** Since both $M_L = 1.011 > 1$ and $M_{\text{Mohr}} = 1.050 > 1$, the mixture is in an **ordered mixing state**. This occurs when the minor component adheres to the surface of the larger carrier particles due to cohesive (van der Waals, electrostatic) forces, producing *better-than-random* uniformity. This is common in fine cohesive pharmaceutical blends. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Mixing Kinetics and Paste Mixers* **(a)** Derive the first-order rate equation for the mixing of particulate solids and obtain the expression $M_L = 1 - e^{-Kt}$. Clearly state the assumptions. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain the construction, working principle, and applications of the **sigma blade mixer** and the **Banbury mixer** for paste mixing. Compare their relative merits. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation of Mixing Rate Equation:** **Assumptions:** 1. Mixing proceeds by a *first-order decay* of variance. 2. The rate of homogenization is proportional to the *remaining* difference between the actual variance and the random-mix variance. 3. The mixer operates isothermally and at constant power input. 4. The particle size and shape distributions remain unchanged. 5. There is no chemical reaction or agglomeration during mixing. **Derivation:** The driving force for homogenization is the gradient between the current variance and the random-mix variance. The decay rate is: $$\frac{d\sigma^2}{dt} = -K\left(\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2\right)$$ **Step 1:** Separate variables. $$\frac{d\sigma^2}{\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2} = -K \, dt$$ **Step 2:** Integrate from $\sigma_0^2$ (at $t=0$) to $\sigma^2$ (at time $t$). $$\int_{\sigma_0^2}^{\sigma^2}\frac{d\sigma^2}{\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2} = -K\int_0^t dt$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the integral. $$\Big[\ln\left(\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2\right)\Big]_{\sigma_0^2}^{\sigma^2} = -Kt$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2}{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2}\right) = -Kt$$ **Step 4:** Exponentiate. $$\frac{\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2}{\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2} = e^{-Kt}$$ $$\sigma^2 - \sigma_R^2 = \left(\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2\right) e^{-Kt}$$ **Step 5:** Rearrange. $$\sigma^2 = \sigma_R^2 + \left(\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2\right) e^{-Kt}$$ **Step 6:** Subtract $\sigma_R^2$ and divide by $(\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2)$ to get $1 - M_L$. $$1 - M_L = e^{-Kt}$$ $$\boxed{M_L = 1 - e^{-Kt}}$$ This shows mixing is *asymptotic*: it never quite reaches 1, but approaches it exponentially with time constant $1/K$. **Part (b) — Paste Mixers:** **(i) Sigma Blade Mixer:** - *Construction:* A horizontal trough (usually jacketed for heating/cooling) fitted with two heavy blades of "S" or "sigma" cross-section, rotating towards each other (one slow at ~20 rpm, one fast at ~40 rpm). The blade tips clear the trough wall by a few millimetres; the blades also intermesh slightly. - *Working:* The two blades shear the paste between them and against the trough wall, producing **folding + shearing + stretching** of the material. Material is repeatedly pulled and folded. - *Mechanism:* Predominantly **shear mixing**. - *Applications:* Dough, putty, rubber compounds, pharmaceutical ointments, adhesives, ceramic pastes, cosmetic creams. - *Capacity:* Batch sizes from 5 L (lab) to 2000 L (production). **(ii) Banbury Mixer:** - *Construction:* A completely enclosed chamber (the "body") with a pair of rotors rotating in opposite directions at slightly different speeds (one at ~30 rpm, the other at ~40 rpm). A pneumatically operated ram closes the top. The body is jacketed for temperature control. Discharge is through a sliding door at the bottom. - *Working:* The rotors are cored for water circulation and have a *wing-shaped* profile that creates intense shear zones. The ram pressure (typically 4–6 bar) forces material into the high-shear region between rotor and chamber wall. - *Mechanism:* **High-intensity shear** + compression. - *Applications:* Rubber compounding (carbon-black dispersion in natural/synthetic rubber), PVC compounding, mastication of raw elastomers, hot-melt adhesives. - *Capacity:* 1.5 L lab to 500 L production. **Comparison:** | Parameter | Sigma Blade Mixer | Banbury Mixer | |---|---|---| | Shear intensity | Moderate | Very high | | Batch time | 15–45 min | 1–5 min | | Material state | Medium-viscosity pastes | Highly viscous, sometimes dry rubber | | Heating/cooling | Jacket | Jacket + cored rotors | | Sealed operation | Optional (open or closed) | Yes (sealed) | | Capital cost | Low | High | | Best for | Pharmaceuticals, food, cosmetics | Rubber, PVC, high-viscosity polymers | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often *omit the assumption list* in the derivation. Listing the assumptions carries 1 mark — skipping them loses 1 mark. > > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Confusion between **Mohr's index and Lacey's index** in numericals. Always *announce which index you are computing* before plugging in values. Interchanging formulas is a 1–2 mark deduction. > > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** In paste-mixer questions, students frequently describe sigma and Banbury mixers as "the same thing." They are *distinct* — sigma blades are open-top and gentle; Banbury is enclosed and ultra-high-shear. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Recall Points for KTU Module 4 — Mixing of Granular Solids and Pastes:** > > 1. **Mixing is a stochastic process** — its quality is measured statistically, not visually. > 2. **Three mechanisms:** Convective, Diffusive, Shear. > 3. **Tumbler mixers** = convective-dominant. **Ribbon/Paddle** = diffusive. **Sigma/Banbury** = shear. > 4. **Lacey's index** $M_L = (\sigma_0^2 - \sigma^2)/(\sigma_0^2 - \sigma_R^2)$ is **bounded 0–1**; $M_L > 1$ → ordered mixing. > 5. **Mohr's index** uses standard deviation, may exceed 1. > 6. **Variance limits:** $\sigma_0^2 = c(1-c)$ (segregated), $\sigma_R^2 = c(1-c)/n$ (random). > 7. **Rate equation:** $M_L = 1 - e^{-Kt}$; time constant $= 1/K$. > 8. **Mixing rate constant** $K$ is found from a semi-log plot of $(1 - M_L)$ vs. $t$. > 9. **Coefficient of Variation** < 5–10% indicates an acceptable blend (pharma: < 6% is gold standard). > 10. **Scale-up** at constant Froude number $N_{\text{Fr}} = N^2 D / g$. > 11. **Power for impeller** in turbulent regime: $P \propto \rho N^3 D^5$. > 12. **V-type mixer** gives 2 splits + 2 recombinations per revolution. > 13. **Double-cone mixer** is gentler and used for fragile crystals. > 14. **Sigma mixer** is for pastes — open trough, two S-blades, different speeds. > 15. **Banbury mixer** is the most intense paste/rubber mixer — enclosed, ram-pressurized. > 16. **Over-mixing** causes *re-segregation*; find $t_{\text{opt}}$ from the $M_L$ vs. $t$ curve. > 17. **Plug flow assumption** does not apply; particle motion in mixers is **random**. > 18. **Pharmaceutical importance:** Blend Uniformity (BU) testing on 10 samples is mandatory by USP/FDA. > 19. **Segregation causes:** differences in particle size, density, shape, and resilience — common during discharge. > 20. **Nauta / conical screw mixers** are ideal for cohesive powders and heat-sensitive materials.

topics.title: Mixing Performance and Effectiveness
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mixing Performance and Effectiveness

# Mixing Performance and Effectiveness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic directly addresses **Course Outcome CO3** — *"Apply the principles of size reduction, mixing, and particle separation to equipment design."* It also bridges into the lab-scale evaluation of mixers, a frequent 14-mark question in End Semester Examinations.

## 1. Core Definition and Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)

**Mixing Performance** is the quantitative assessment of *how well* a mixing device blends two or more components into a homogeneous state, evaluated using statistically-defined parameters. **Mixing Effectiveness** is a broader engineering metric that combines the *quality* of mixing with the *time* and *power* required to achieve it.

Mathematically, mixing performance is measured using:

$$\text{Mixing Index} \quad M = \frac{\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma^{2}}{\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma_{R}^{2}}$$

where $\sigma_0^{2}$ is the variance of a completely segregated system, $\sigma^{2}$ is the actual variance of the mixture under study, and $\sigma_{R}^{2}$ is the variance of a perfectly random mixture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions of Mixing Index (Lacey, 1954):**
> - $M = 1 \;\Rightarrow\;$ Perfectly random (ideal) mixture
> - $M = 0 \;\Rightarrow\;$ Completely segregated (no mixing)
> - $0 < M < 1 \;\Rightarrow\;$ Partially mixed state

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salt in Soup" Picture

Imagine you drop a spoonful of salt into a pot of clear soup.

- **At t = 0:** Salt sits as a clump at the bottom — *completely segregated*. Sampling gives wildly different concentrations. This is $\sigma_0^{2}$.
- **At t = 5 s:** Salt is partially dissolved, but uneven — *partial mixing*. Sampling still shows fluctuation. This is $\sigma^{2}$.
- **At t = 30 s (after stirring):** Salt is uniformly dissolved — *random mixture*. Sampling gives nearly identical concentrations everywhere. This is $\sigma_{R}^{2}$.

The **Mixing Index** is essentially asking: *"How far has the spoon journeyed from the clump to uniform distribution?"* — a normalized journey between 0 (start) and 1 (destination).

### 1.3 Engineering Importance

| Industrial Sector | Why Mixing Performance Matters |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Tablet uniformity, drug bioavailability |
| **Food Processing** | Consistent flavor, texture, color |
| **Polymer Reactors** | Heat and mass transfer uniformity |
| **Paints & Coatings** | Pigment dispersion, color consistency |
| **Wastewater Treatment** | Coagulation efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A mixer is *not* automatically "good" because it consumes high power. A small, slow mixer achieving $M = 0.95$ in 60 s is more effective than a large, fast mixer achieving $M = 0.95$ in 90 s — **because effectiveness = quality / (time × power)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Statistical Description of Mixedness

A mixture of two components A and B (with A having overall fraction $p$ and B having fraction $1-p$) can be described by drawing samples of size $n$. Each sample yields a local concentration $x$. The statistical spread of $x$ values defines the **quality of mixing**.

### Variance Hierarchy

| State | Variance Symbol | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Completely Segregated | $\sigma_0^{2}$ | $p(1-p)$ | All samples are pure A or pure B |
| Completely Random | $\sigma_{R}^{2}$ | $\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}$ | Sample composition fluctuates by chance |
| Actual Mixture | $\sigma^{2}$ | Experimental | Lies between $\sigma_0^{2}$ and $\sigma_{R}^{2}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Size Rule:** The sample size $n$ must be chosen to be of the **order of the desired product scale**. Sampling at molecular scale gives $\sigma_{R}^{2} \to 0$, making the index meaningless. The "scale of scrutiny" governs the index.

## 2.2 Danckwerts' Two-Fold Description (1952)

P.V. Danckwerts refined mixing theory by separating it into two **independent** statistical concepts:

### A. Intensity of Segregation ($I$)

$$I = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\sigma_0^{2}}$$

- $I = 1 \Rightarrow$ completely segregated
- $I = 0 \Rightarrow$ perfectly random
- **Intensity answers:** *"How big is the concentration difference between adjacent regions?"*

### B. Scale of Segregation ($S$)

$$S = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(r) \, dr$$

where $R(r)$ is the spatial autocorrelation coefficient between two points separated by distance $r$.

- **Scale answers:** *"How large are the unmixed clumps?"*
- A mixture with many fine clumps but high local contrast has **small scale** but **high intensity**.
- A mixture with few large regions of nearly-pure A and B has **large scale** but possibly **lower intensity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Confused Point:** *Intensity* and *Scale* are **orthogonal** (independent). A process can reduce scale (break clumps) without reducing intensity (sharpness of contrast), and vice versa. **Lacey's Mixing Index conflates both** into a single number — this is why Danckwerts' description is theoretically superior.

## 2.3 Power Consumption in Mixing

### Power Number ($N_p$)

The dimensionless power consumption of an impeller:

$$N_p = \frac{P}{\rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}}$$

where $P$ = power (W), $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³), $N$ = impeller speed (rev/s), $D$ = impeller diameter (m).

### Mixing Reynolds Number

$$Re_m = \frac{\rho \, N \, D^{2}}{\mu}$$

### Froude Number (for unbaffled tanks, surface vortexing)

$$Fr = \frac{N^{2} \, D}{g}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma^{2}}{\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma_{R}^{2}}$ | Lacey Mixing Index | Quality assessment |
| 2 | $I = \sigma^{2}/\sigma_0^{2}$ | Intensity of Segregation | Local contrast |
| 3 | $S = \int_0^{\infty} R(r)\,dr$ | Scale of Segregation | Clump size |
| 4 | $\sigma_0^{2} = p(1-p)$ | Variance of segregation | Two-component system |
| 5 | $\sigma_{R}^{2} = p(1-p)/n$ | Variance of randomness | Sample size n |
| 6 | $N_p = P / (\rho N^{3} D^{5})$ | Power Number | Mixer power |
| 7 | $Re_m = \rho N D^{2}/\mu$ | Mixing Reynolds Number | Flow regime |
| 8 | $Fr = N^{2}D/g$ | Froude Number | Surface vortex |
| 9 | $N_p \, Re_m = K_p$ (laminar) | Power–Reynolds relation | Viscous regime ($Re < 10$) |
| 10 | $N_p \approx \text{const}$ (turbulent) | $N_p$ plateau | Baffled tank ($Re > 10^{4}$) |
| 11 | $N \cdot t_m = K_t$ | Dimensionless mixing time | Time scaling |
| 12 | $E = \dfrac{M}{P \cdot t_m}$ | Mixing Effectiveness Index | Overall metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Power-Curve Regimes (Rushton Chart):**
> - **Laminar** ($Re_m < 10$): $N_p \cdot Re_m = K_p$ (slope = $-1$ on log-log plot)
> - **Transitional** ($10 < Re_m < 10^{4}$): Curve bends
> - **Turbulent** ($Re_m > 10^{4}$): $N_p$ becomes constant (typically 5–6 for Rushton turbine in baffled tank)

## 2.5 Mixing Time and Its Correlations

Mixing time $t_m$ is the time to reach a specified degree of uniformity (often 95%, i.e., $M = 0.95$).

For **turbulent regime** in baffled tanks, an empirical correlation:

$$N \cdot t_m = K \left(\frac{D}{T}\right)^{-2}$$

where $T$ = tank diameter, $D$ = impeller diameter, $K$ = geometric constant (≈ 4 for turbines).

## 2.6 Mixing Effectiveness — The Unified Metric

A practical engineering definition:

$$E = \frac{M_{final}}{P \cdot t_m}$$

A mixer with $E = 0.001$ W⁻¹s⁻¹ is "10× more effective" than one with $E = 0.0001$ W⁻¹s⁻¹, assuming both achieve the same $M_{final}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lacey's Mixing Index from First Principles

### Step 1 — Define Sampling

Draw $N_s$ samples, each containing $n$ particles. Let $x_i$ = fraction of component A in sample $i$. The true overall fraction of A is $p$.

The mean of $x_i$:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N_s}\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} x_i \approx p$$

The sample variance:

$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{N_s - 1}\sum_{i=1}^{N_s}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Variance of Completely Segregated State

If every sample is purely A or purely B, then each $x_i$ is either 1 or 0.

$$\sigma_0^{2} = \frac{1}{N_s}\left[ N_A(1-p)^{2} + N_B(0-p)^{2} \right]$$

With $N_A/N_s = p$ (fraction of A-rich regions) and $N_B/N_s = 1-p$:

$$\sigma_0^{2} = p(1-p)^{2} + (1-p)p^{2} = p(1-p)\left[(1-p) + p\right]$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_0^{2} = p(1-p)}$$

### Step 3 — Variance of Completely Random State

For a random draw of $n$ particles, each particle is independently A with probability $p$. The sample fraction $x$ follows a binomial distribution with mean $p$ and variance $p(1-p)/n$. Averaging over many samples:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{R}^{2} = \frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

### Step 4 — Construct a Normalized Progress Variable

We want a number that equals 0 at the segregated state and 1 at the random state. Define:

$$M = \frac{\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma^{2}}{\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma_{R}^{2}}$$

**Verification of Boundary Conditions:**

- At $\sigma^{2} = \sigma_0^{2}$ (no mixing): $M = 0/(\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma_{R}^{2}) = 0$ ✓
- At $\sigma^{2} = \sigma_{R}^{2}$ (perfect mixing): $M = (\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma_{R}^{2})/(\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma_{R}^{2}) = 1$ ✓
- For $0 < \sigma^{2} < \sigma_{R}^{2}$: $0 < M < 1$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** In real practice, samples are drawn of finite size $n$. Thus $\sigma_R^{2}$ is not zero (it is $p(1-p)/n$), and $M$ can never reach exactly 1. This is an *instrumental limit*, not a property of the mixture itself.

## 3.2 Derivation of Power Number for Laminar Regime

Consider a Couette-flow analogy: an impeller drags fluid in a viscous shear field. For a flat-blade impeller in laminar flow, the power required equals viscous dissipation.

### Step 1 — Shear Stress in Creeping Flow

For a fluid with viscosity $\mu$ being sheared at rate $\dot{\gamma} \sim N$ in a region of thickness $\delta \sim D/10$ around the impeller:

$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma} = \mu N$$

### Step 2 — Force on Impeller Surface

Force equals stress times area $A \sim D^{2}$:

$$F \sim \tau A = \mu N D^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Power = Force × Tip Velocity

Tip velocity $v_{tip} = \pi N D$:

$$P = F \cdot v_{tip} \sim \mu N D^{2} \cdot N D = \mu N^{2} D^{3}$$

### Step 4 — Form Power Number

$$N_p = \frac{P}{\rho N^{3} D^{5}} \sim \frac{\mu N^{2} D^{3}}{\rho N^{3} D^{5}} = \frac{\mu}{\rho N D^{2}} = \frac{1}{Re_m}$$

Therefore, in laminar regime:

$$\boxed{N_p \cdot Re_m = K_p \quad \text{(constant, depends on impeller geometry)}}$$

For a Rushton turbine in laminar flow, $K_p \approx 70$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Power Consumption Calculation

**Given Data (typical KTU problem):**

- Tank diameter $T = 1.0$ m
- Impeller diameter $D = 0.33$ m (i.e., $D/T = 1/3$)
- Fluid: water at 25°C, $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s
- Impeller speed $N = 3$ rev/s (180 rpm)
- Baffled tank, six-blade Rushton turbine

### Step 1 — Compute Mixing Reynolds Number

$$Re_m = \frac{\rho N D^{2}}{\mu}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_m &= \frac{(998)(3)(0.33)^{2}}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} \\
     &= \frac{(998)(3)(0.1089)}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} \\
     &= \frac{326.05}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} \\
     &= 3.66 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Identify Regime

$$Re_m = 3.66 \times 10^{5} > 10^{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Turbulent flow}$$

### Step 3 — Look Up Power Number

For a six-blade Rushton turbine in a baffled tank at $D/T = 1/3$, from the standard Rushton correlation chart:

$$N_p \approx 5.5$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Power

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= (5.5)(998)(3)^{3}(0.33)^{5} \\
  &= (5.5)(998)(27)(0.33)^{5}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $0.33^{5}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
0.33^{2} &= 0.1089 \\
0.33^{3} &= 0.035937 \\
0.33^{5} &= 0.33^{3} \times 0.33^{2} = 0.035937 \times 0.1089 = 0.003914
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= (5.5)(998)(27)(0.003914) \\
  &= (5.5)(998)(0.10567) \\
  &= (5.5)(105.46) \\
  &\approx 580 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Mixing Time (Turbulent Correlation)

For a baffled turbine in turbulent regime with $D/T = 1/3$:

$$N \cdot t_m = 4 \left(\frac{D}{T}\right)^{-2} = 4 \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{-2} = 4 \times 9 = 36$$

$$t_m = \frac{36}{N} = \frac{36}{3} = 12 \; \text{s}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Mixing Effectiveness

Assuming $M_{final} = 0.95$ (95% mixing):

$$E = \frac{M_{final}}{P \cdot t_m} = \frac{0.95}{(580)(12)} = \frac{0.95}{6960} \approx 1.36 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{W}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Mixing Evaluation Framework

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete evaluation methodology** of mixing performance. Note how the three independent pillars — Quality (Statistical), Power (Hydrodynamic), and Time (Kinetic) — converge into a single Effectiveness Index.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mixing Operation Initiated] --> B[Define System Boundaries]
    B --> C{Choose Evaluation Pillar}

    C --> D1[Quality Pillar]
    C --> D2[Power Pillar]
    C --> D3[Time Pillar]

    D1 --> E1[Draw N Samples]
    E1 --> F1[Measure Local Concentration xi]
    F1 --> G1[Compute Sample Variance sigma squared]
    G1 --> H1[Compute sigma naught squared and sigma R squared]
    H1 --> I1[Lacey Mixing Index M]
    H1 --> I2[Danckwerts Intensity I]
    H1 --> I3[Danckwerts Scale S]

    D2 --> E2[Measure Impeller Speed N]
    E2 --> F2[Measure Torque tau]
    F2 --> G2[Compute Power P = 2 pi N tau]
    G2 --> H2[Compute Power Number Np]
    E2 --> J2[Compute Mixing Reynolds Number Re m]
    J2 --> K2{Identify Flow Regime}
    K2 --> L1[Laminar: Np Re m = Kp]
    K2 --> L2[Transitional: Np f Re m]
    K2 --> L3[Turbulent: Np constant]

    D3 --> E3[Decolorization or Tracer Method]
    E3 --> F3[Record Time to 95 percent Uniformity]
    F3 --> G3[Compute Mixing Time t m]

    I1 --> M[Final Effectiveness Computation]
    I2 --> M
    I3 --> M
    H2 --> M
    G3 --> M

    M --> N[Mixing Effectiveness Index E = M divided by P times t m]
    N --> O{Pass Design Criteria?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Approve Mixer Configuration]
    O -->|No| Q[Iterate: Change N, D, or Geometry]
    Q --> A

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style P fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style Q fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Scale vs. Intensity of Segregation

This diagram illustrates the *orthogonality* of Danckwerts' two concepts. Notice how mixtures A and B can have the same intensity but very different scales, and vice versa.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Case1["Mixture X: High Intensity + Large Scale"]
        A1[Pure A Region] -->|Sharp Boundary| A2[Pure B Region]
        A2 --> A3[Pure A Region]
    end

    subgraph Case2["Mixture Y: High Intensity + Small Scale"]
        B1[A Strip] --> B2[B Strip]
        B2 --> B3[A Strip]
        B3 --> B4[B Strip]
    end

    subgraph Case3["Mixture Z: Low Intensity + Large Scale"]
        C1[Mostly A] -->|Diffuse Boundary| C2[Mostly B]
    end

    Note["Same M value can arise from vastly different microstructures.
    Lacey index hides this information; Danckwerts separates it."]

    Case1 --> Note
    Case2 --> Note
    Case3 --> Note

    style Note fill:#fffacd,stroke:#888800,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Power-Curve Regime Chart (Rushton-Type Impeller)

A textual representation of the standard $\log N_p$ vs. $\log Re_m$ chart:

| Region | $Re_m$ Range | Behavior | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laminar | $< 10$ | Steep negative slope | $N_p = K_p / Re_m$ |
| Transition | $10$ to $10^{4}$ | Curved transition | Empirical curve |
| Turbulent | $> 10^{4}$ | Plateau (constant) | $N_p \approx 5.5$ (Rushton, $D/T = 1/3$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** Once $Re_m > 10^{4}$, increasing impeller speed gives **no power-number benefit** — power scales as $N^{3}$, so doubling speed increases power eightfold. This is the operating regime of *most industrial stirred tanks*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the Mixing Index as proposed by Lacey. State the boundary conditions and explain what values indicate perfect mixing and complete segregation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Lacey Mixing Index $M$ is a statistical measure of the degree of mixing in a two-component system, defined as:

$$M = \frac{\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma^{2}}{\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma_{R}^{2}}$$

where $\sigma_0^{2}$ is the variance of a completely segregated mixture (= $p(1-p)$), $\sigma^{2}$ is the variance of the actual mixture, and $\sigma_{R}^{2}$ is the variance of a completely random mixture (= $p(1-p)/n$, $n$ being sample size).

**Boundary conditions:**
- $M = 1$: Perfectly random mixture (best possible mixing for that sample size).
- $M = 0$: Completely segregated mixture (no mixing has occurred).
- $0 < M < 1$: Partially mixed state — practically, $M \geq 0.95$ is usually the design target.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for boundary conditions.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between the Intensity of Segregation and the Scale of Segregation as defined by Danckwerts.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Danckwerts (1952) decomposed the concept of mixedness into two **independent** statistical measures:

1. **Intensity of Segregation ($I$):** It measures the *magnitude of concentration difference* between neighboring regions. It is defined as $I = \sigma^{2}/\sigma_0^{2}$. It ranges from 1 (segregated) to 0 (random). It answers the question *"how contrasty are the clumps?"*

2. **Scale of Segregation ($S$):** It measures the *spatial size* of the unmixed regions. It is defined as $S = \int_0^{\infty} R(r)\, dr$, where $R(r)$ is the autocorrelation coefficient. It has units of length and answers the question *"how big are the clumps?"*

A mixer may reduce scale (by breaking up clumps) without reducing intensity (the local contrast remains sharp), and vice versa. Thus, both must be reported for complete description.

*(1.5 marks each.)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice Set 1]`

**A pharmaceutical mixing tank is fitted with a six-blade Rushton turbine impeller. The following data are given:**

- Tank diameter: $T = 1.5$ m
- Impeller diameter: $D = 0.5$ m
- Impeller speed: $N = 2.5$ rev/s
- Liquid density: $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³ (glycerol-water blend)
- Liquid viscosity: $\mu = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- Tank is fully baffled

**(a) Calculate the power consumption of the impeller and identify the flow regime. (7 marks)**

**(b) If a tracer study shows that the system reaches 95% mixedness in 22 seconds, compute the mixing effectiveness index assuming $M_{final} = 0.95$. (7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate Mixing Reynolds Number (2 marks)**

$$Re_m = \frac{\rho N D^{2}}{\mu}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_m &= \frac{(1050)(2.5)(0.5)^{2}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
     &= \frac{(1050)(2.5)(0.25)}{0.005} \\
     &= \frac{656.25}{0.005} \\
     &= 1.3125 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Flow Regime (1 mark)**

$$Re_m = 1.3125 \times 10^{5} > 10^{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Turbulent flow}$$

**Step 3 — Power Number for Baffled Rushton Turbine (1 mark)**

For a standard six-blade Rushton turbine in a fully baffled tank with $D/T = 0.5/1.5 = 1/3$:

$$N_p = 5.5 \quad \text{(from standard Rushton correlation)}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Power (3 marks)**

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= (5.5)(1050)(2.5)^{3}(0.5)^{5} \\
  &= (5.5)(1050)(15.625)(0.03125)
\end{aligned}$$

Computing step-by-step:

- $(2.5)^{3} = 15.625$
- $(0.5)^{5} = 0.03125$
- $(15.625)(0.03125) = 0.48828$
- $(1050)(0.48828) = 512.7$
- $(5.5)(512.7) \approx 2820 \; \text{W}$

$$\boxed{P \approx 2.82 \; \text{kW}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define Mixing Effectiveness (2 marks)**

$$E = \frac{M_{final}}{P \cdot t_m}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values (3 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{0.95}{(2820)(22)} \\
  &= \frac{0.95}{62040} \\
  &= 1.531 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{W}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on Effectiveness (2 marks)**

This value indicates that for every watt-second of energy input, the mixer achieves an additional $1.531 \times 10^{-5}$ units of mixing index. To compare mixers, $E$ values must be computed under the same $M_{final}$ criterion. The current $E$ suggests moderate effectiveness — the high viscosity ($\mu = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) and large impeller diameter relative to power input limit the energy efficiency. A helical or anchor impeller would be more effective for this viscous regime.

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.53 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{W}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}}$$

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice Set 2]`

**A solid-solid mixing study is conducted in a V-blender. The overall composition is 40% component A (by mass) and 60% component B. Ten samples of size $n = 50$ particles each are drawn and analyzed. The local mass fractions of A in the ten samples are:**

$$x_i = \{0.42, \; 0.38, \; 0.41, \; 0.39, \; 0.40, \; 0.43, \; 0.37, \; 0.41, \; 0.39, \; 0.40\}$$

**(a) Calculate the Lacey Mixing Index $M$. Comment on the quality of mixing. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the same mixer is then operated at a higher RPM and the new sample variances yield $M = 0.99$, while power consumption rises to 1.8 kW and mixing time drops to 8 s, compare the two states using the Effectiveness Index. (7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values (1 mark)**

- Overall fraction of A: $p = 0.40$
- Sample size: $n = 50$
- Number of samples: $N_s = 10$

**Step 2 — Compute Sample Mean (1 mark)**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{10}\sum x_i = \frac{0.42+0.38+0.41+0.39+0.40+0.43+0.37+0.41+0.39+0.40}{10}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{4.00}{10} = 0.40 \quad \checkmark \; (\text{matches expected } p)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sample Variance $\sigma^{2}$ (2 marks)**

$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{N_s - 1}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$$

Deviations squared:

| $x_i$ | $(x_i - 0.40)$ | $(x_i - 0.40)^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.42 | +0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 0.38 | -0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 0.41 | +0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 0.39 | -0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 0.40 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| 0.43 | +0.03 | 0.0009 |
| 0.37 | -0.03 | 0.0009 |
| 0.41 | +0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 0.39 | -0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 0.40 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| **Sum** | | **0.0030** |

$$\sigma^{2} = \frac{0.0030}{9} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Reference Variances (1 mark)**

$$\sigma_0^{2} = p(1-p) = (0.40)(0.60) = 0.24$$

$$\sigma_{R}^{2} = \frac{p(1-p)}{n} = \frac{0.24}{50} = 4.8 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Lacey Index (2 marks)**

$$M = \frac{\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma^{2}}{\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma_{R}^{2}} = \frac{0.24 - 3.333 \times 10^{-4}}{0.24 - 4.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$M = \frac{0.23967}{0.2352} \approx 1.019$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error (and Valuation Penalty):** A mixing index *greater than 1* indicates that the sample variance $\sigma^{2}$ is **lower than the random-mixture variance** $\sigma_{R}^{2}$. This is statistically possible when **sample size $n$ is large relative to the mixer scale of scrutiny** — i.e., the samples are *too big* and average over multiple clumps. The correct interpretation here is: **the chosen sample size is inappropriate for this mixture scale**. The student must redo the analysis with $n$ matching the *desired product scale* (e.g., $n = 5$ or 10).

*Correctly applying a smaller sample size:* If we re-interpret $n = 5$ (sampling unit matching tablet scale), $\sigma_{R}^{2} = 0.24/5 = 0.048$, and:

$$M = \frac{0.24 - 3.333 \times 10^{-4}}{0.24 - 0.048} = \frac{0.2397}{0.192} \approx 1.25$$

Even then $M > 1$, indicating the mixture is essentially at perfect random mixing for that scale. The interpretation is: **the V-blender has produced an excellent mixture at the scale of $n = 5$ particles**.

---

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $E$ for Original State (2 marks)**

From Part (a) (taking $M \approx 0.95$ as design target for original case):

Power $P_1$ not given for original case — but we are told $M_1 \approx 0.95$, $t_{m,1}$ must be inferred. Assuming the original ran with $P_1 = 1.2$ kW and $t_{m,1} = 15$ s (typical values from the problem context):

$$E_1 = \frac{0.95}{(1200)(15)} = \frac{0.95}{18000} = 5.28 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{W}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E$ for New State (2 marks)**

$$E_2 = \frac{0.99}{(1800)(8)} = \frac{0.99}{14400} = 6.88 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{W}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compare (3 marks)**

$$\frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{6.88 \times 10^{-5}}{5.28 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.30$$

**Conclusion:** Operating at higher RPM increases effectiveness by **30%**. Although power consumption rose by 50% (from 1.2 kW to 1.8 kW), the mixing time dropped by ~47% (from 15 s to 8 s), and the final mixedness improved from 95% to 99%. The combined benefit yields a net 30% gain in engineering effectiveness. However, the diminishing return warns against further RPM increase — at some point, additional speed will only increase power ($P \propto N^{3}$) without proportional reduction in $t_m$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on Mixing Performance Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sample size $n$:** Writing $M = 1$ without acknowledging that $\sigma_R^2 = p(1-p)/n$ depends on $n$. KTU expects students to identify the *scale of scrutiny*.
>
> 2. **Conflating Lacey Index with Danckwerts:** Lacey gives a single number; Danckwerts splits it into intensity and scale. Examiners reward explicit mention of both frameworks.
>
> 3. **Wrong Power Number for Unbaffled Tanks:** If baffles are absent, the *Froude Number* $Fr = N^2D/g$ must be checked. At $Fr > 0.1$, surface vortexing creates air entrainment and $N_p$ drops sharply. Many students forget this.
>
> 4. **Using $N$ in rpm instead of rev/s:** Power formulas require $N$ in rev/s. Using rpm gives a power value off by a factor of $(60)^{3} = 216{,}000$. **This is the single biggest silent error.**
>
> 5. **Mixing Time Correlation Domain:** $N \cdot t_m = K$ correlations are valid only in turbulent regime for baffled tanks. In laminar regime, $t_m$ depends strongly on $Re_m$.
>
> 6. **No Units in Final Answer:** Always state $P$ in W (or kW), $E$ in W⁻¹s⁻¹, $M$ as dimensionless. KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

## Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Mixing Performance and Effectiveness**

- ☐ **Lacey Mixing Index:** $M = (\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma^{2})/(\sigma_0^{2} - \sigma_R^{2})$; $0 \leq M \leq 1$.
- ☐ **Reference Variances:** $\sigma_0^{2} = p(1-p)$ (segregated); $\sigma_R^{2} = p(1-p)/n$ (random).
- ☐ **Sample size $n$ defines the scale of scrutiny** — choose it carefully.
- ☐ **Danckwerts:** Intensity $I = \sigma^{2}/\sigma_0^{2}$; Scale $S = \int_0^{\infty} R(r)\, dr$.
- ☐ **Intensity and Scale are independent** — Lacey hides this.
- ☐ **Power Number:** $N_p = P/(\rho N^{3} D^{5})$.
- ☐ **Mixing Reynolds Number:** $Re_m = \rho N D^{2}/\mu$.
- ☐ **Froude Number (unbaffled):** $Fr = N^{2} D/g$ — check if $> 0.1$.
- ☐ **Three regimes:** Laminar ($Re_m < 10$): $N_p Re_m = K_p$. Turbulent ($Re_m > 10^{4}$): $N_p = $ const.
- ☐ **Always use $N$ in rev/s**, not rpm, in power formula. Conversion: $N_{rev/s} = N_{rpm}/60$.
- ☐ **Mixing time correlation (turbulent):** $N \cdot t_m = K(D/T)^{-2}$.
- ☐ **Effectiveness:** $E = M/(P \cdot t_m)$ — higher is better; compares competing mixer designs.
- ☐ **Common error trap:** $M > 1$ implies sample size mismatch with scale of scrutiny.
- ☐ **Common error trap:** $N_p$ drops at high $Fr$ in unbaffled tanks due to vortex formation.
- ☐ **Industrial target:** $M \geq 0.95$ is the typical design goal for acceptable mixedness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixers for Noncohesive and Cohesive Solids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT302 - PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mixers for Noncohesive and Cohesive Solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solid–Solid Mixing (Blending)** is a unit operation in particle technology that involves the intermixing of two or more particulate solids — distinguishable by composition, size, shape, density, or colour — to produce a homogeneous mixture in which every small sample drawn from the bulk has the same composition as the gross mixture.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 4), mixing equipment is broadly classified based on the **flow behaviour of the solids** being handled:

- **Noncohesive (free-flowing) solids** — particles that flow easily under gravity, exhibit negligible interparticle adhesion, and resist agglomeration (e.g., dry sand, salt, sugar, grains, plastic pellets).
- **Cohesive (non-free-flowing) solids** — particles that tend to stick together due to interparticle forces, surface moisture, van der Waals forces, or electrostatic attraction, often forming agglomerates or pastes (e.g., wet clay, cement, dough, rubber compounds, fine pigments).

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 Context (KTU 2024):** This topic directly addresses CO4 — "Select size reduction and size separation equipment for chemical processes" and indirectly supports CO3 — "Apply particle technology concepts to industrial equipment design."

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Perspective)

Imagine you have a bowl containing **black and white rice grains**. If you simply tilt the bowl, the grains slide freely over each other — this is mixing **noncohesive solids** (the particles are like marbles that don't stick).

Now imagine mixing **wet flour and water** to make dough. The flour particles cling to each other, resisting flow, and you need a strong tool to push, fold, and shear them — this is mixing **cohesive solids** (the particles behave like sticky putty).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The choice of mixer is dictated by the **flowability** of the solid, NOT by the size of the particles. Even very fine dry powders (e.g., talc) are noncohesive, while coarse wet solids (e.g., moist sand) are cohesive.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics

- **Mixing Index (Lacey's Index, I):** Dimensionless, ranges from **0** (complete segregation) to **1** (perfect mixing).
- **Mixing Time (tₘ):** Measured in **seconds (s)** or **minutes (min)**.
- **Power Number (Nₚ):** Dimensionless, used in agitator power calculations.
- **Froude Number (Fr):** Dimensionless, important for tumbling mixers.
- **Standard Density of Solids (ρₛ):** Reported in **kg/m³** or **g/cm³**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanisms of Solid Mixing

Mixing of solids occurs through three primary mechanisms (often operating simultaneously):

1. **Convective Mixing** — Bulk movement of groups of particles from one location to another (achieved by paddles, ribbons, screws). Analogous to stirring a liquid.
2. **Diffusive Mixing** — Random motion of individual particles across newly formed surfaces (achieved in tumbling mixers where cascading particles create random distribution).
3. **Shear Mixing** — Application of slip planes and shear forces that break up agglomerates and disperse cohesive particles (achieved in sigma blade and muller mixers).

### 2.2 Classification of Mixers

**A. Mixers for Noncohesive (Free-Flowing) Solids:**

| Mixer Type | Operating Mechanism | Best Suited For |
|------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Tumbling (Drum, Double-Cone, V-type, Cube) | Diffusive | Free-flowing dry powders, granules |
| Ribbon Blender | Convective + Shear | Dry powders, crystalline solids |
| Paddle Mixer | Convective | Low-viscosity dry blends |
| Horizontal Pan Mixer | Convective + Diffusive | Small batches of free-flowing solids |
| Air (Pneumatic) Mixer | Diffusive (fluidization) | Large volumes of fine dry powders |
| Screw Mixer | Convective | Continuous mixing of free-flowing solids |

**B. Mixers for Cohesive (Non-Free-Flowing) Solids:**

| Mixer Type | Operating Mechanism | Best Suited For |
|------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Sigma Blade Mixer | Shear | Pastes, doughs, cements, putty |
| Double-Arm Kneader | Shear + Convective | Heavy pastes, rubber, clay |
| Pug Mill | Shear + Cutting | Sticky, plastic, fibrous masses |
| Pan (Muller) Mixer | Shear + Compressive | Foundry sand, ceramics, refractories |
| Intensive Internal Mixer (Banbury) | High Shear | Rubber, plastics, PVC |
| Continuous Z-arm Mixer | Shear | Dough, adhesives |

### 2.3 Why and How Each Type Operates

- **Tumbling Mixers (e.g., Double-Cone, V-Type):** When rotated, particles cascade and split, then merge and split again. This continuous splitting-and-folding is mathematically analogous to card-shuffling and produces a **statistical random mixture** with a binomial variance.
- **Ribbon Mixers:** The outer helical ribbon pushes material in one direction, the inner ribbon in the opposite direction. This creates a counter-current flow that ensures axial and radial homogenization.
- **Sigma Blade Mixers:** The two blades, shaped like the Greek letter **Σ**, rotate at different speeds (typically 1:2 ratio). The differential speed creates intense **shear at the interface**, breaking down agglomerates.
- **Muller Mixers:** Heavy wheels roll and slide over a stationary bed of material. The combination of compression and shear makes them ideal for breaking up lumps and incorporating moisture.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-frequency KTU exam topics**. Master each equation and its boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $S^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - p)^2$ | Variance of mixture based on $N$ samples, $x_i$ is concentration of key component in sample $i$, $p$ is mean concentration. | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $S_0^2 = p(1-p)$ | Variance of **completely segregated** mixture (binary) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $S_r^2 = \dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}$ | Variance of **random mixture** for sample size $n$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $I = \dfrac{S_0^2 - S^2}{S_0^2 - S_r^2}$ | **Lacey's Mixing Index**; $I = 1$ ideal; $I = 0$ segregated | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $M = 1 - \dfrac{S}{S_0}$ | **Kramers Mixing Index**; alternate mixing measure | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{M_\infty - M_0}{M_\infty - M}\right) = kt$ | First-order rate of mixing; $M$ = mixing degree at time $t$ | Time in s |
| 7 | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$ | Power consumption by agitator impeller; $N_p$ = power number, $D$ = impeller diameter | Power in W |
| 8 | $\tau_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5}$ | Definition of power number | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{ribbon} = P_{lifting} + P_{friction}$ | Ribbon mixer total power (lifting dominates) | W |
| 10 | $Fr = \dfrac{N^2 D}{g}$ | Froude number for tumbling/centrifugal mixers | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $t_m = K\left(\dfrac{D}{T}\right)^a \cdot N^b$ | Mixing time correlation; $T$ = vessel diameter, $K, a, b$ are empirical | s |
| 12 | $\sigma_t = \sqrt{p(1-p)}$ | Standard deviation of binary completely segregated mixture | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
